To support these efforts, police patrolling parties are instructed to keep a continuous track of vulnerable locations and maintain constant communication with the central control room, ensuring that real-time information flows seamlessly between the field and the command center. Wherever the situation demands, police pickets, which are small, stationary groups of police officers, shall be established at strategic points to ensure that all voters have free and fearless access to cast their votes. The Commanders and Assistant Commanders of the Central Armed Police Forces, often deployed for large-scale security operations, must be provided with a comprehensive list of such vulnerable locations. In scenarios where these forces arrive in advance to establish area domination, which means securing the region to prevent unlawful activities, special attention must be directed toward these identified pockets. As a confidence-building measure, these commanders are required to personally visit such vulnerable areas on the day of the poll to reassure the local population of their safety and the state's commitment to protecting their democratic rights.

If any obstruction is encountered by these security commanders, they are duty-bound to take immediate note of the incident and inform the relevant electoral officials without delay. These officials include the Returning Officer, District Election Officer, Superintendent of Police, Commissioner of Police, Election Observer, or Sector Officer. It is crucial that the time of this intimation is recorded meticulously to establish a clear timeline of events and responses. Similarly, if any complaint is received or if information is gathered from any source regarding obstruction or threats against any voter, the local administration is mandated to inquire into the matter immediately without any procrastination. The Returning Officers must aggregate all inputs regarding mass-scale intimidation, threats, or obstructions and factor these findings into their final post-poll reports. Election Observers play a pivotal role in this oversight mechanism and must devote their full attention to this issue, verifying conditions at every stage, both before the poll and on the polling day itself. They are required to submit periodic reports to the Election Commission, with a special mention of these vulnerability issues included in their final comprehensive report. Beyond observational duties, observers should perform an intelligent analysis of Form 17A, which is the statutory document recording the total number of voters who cast their votes, alongside the marked copy of the electoral roll used at the polling stations. This scrutiny, often ordered by the Commission after the poll, helps cross-verify turnout figures against the list of actual voters to detect discrepancies indicative of malpractice.

In locations where there is a history of Electronic Voting Machines being looted or broken, the deployment of Central Armed Police Forces at those specific polling stations is mandatory to prevent recurrence. The Election Commission has issued strict directives stating that the accountability of various police and civil officials for vulnerability mapping and follow-up actions at every stage must be clearly defined with reference to each polling station and constituency. This ensures that every official knows their specific responsibilities regarding the safety of voters in sensitive areas. Severe disciplinary action will be initiated against any police or civil official found guilty of dereliction of duty, which refers to the failure to perform one's official responsibilities, in this regard. This strict accountability framework is designed to eliminate negligence and ensure that the machinery of the state functions efficiently to protect the sanctity of the electoral process.

Regarding the infrastructure of polling arrangements, the concept of auxiliary polling stations is introduced to manage voter load and ensure orderly conduct. Before every revision of the electoral roll, which is the periodic update of the voter list, polling stations should be rationalized based on the expected additions to the roll. The objective of this rationalization is to ensure that after the final publication of the roll, no single polling station serves more than fifteen hundred voters. Adhering to this limit helps obviate, or remove the need for, setting up auxiliary polling stations on the eve of the polls, thereby simplifying logistics. However, in cases where it becomes absolutely necessary due to an unexpected surge in voter numbers or geographical constraints, auxiliary polling stations may be established subject to specific conditions. An auxiliary polling station is essentially an overflow facility created to accommodate excess voters from a main polling station. Such a station shall retain the same serial number as the original polling station but will be distinguished by a suffix such as A, B, or C. As far as practicable, these auxiliary stations should be located within the same building or premises as the original station to minimize confusion and logistical complexity. An auxiliary station may be located in a separate building only when it is unavoidable due to the non-availability of suitable rooms within the original premises. Even in such cases, the auxiliary station must remain within the same general area as the original station. Crucially, a separate serial number shall never be assigned to an auxiliary polling station, even if it is housed in a different building. It must always carry the serial number of the original station with the appropriate suffix, reflecting the fact that both the original and auxiliary stations serve electors listed in the same part of the electoral roll, thus maintaining the integrity of the voter list segmentation.

The procedure for listing polling stations is governed by strict administrative protocols to ensure clarity and precision. The draft list of polling stations must be drawn up using the prescribed form as detailed in Annexure 3 of the relevant election guidelines. This standardized form ensures uniformity in data collection and reporting across all constituencies. A critical component of this process is the demarcation of the polling area, which refers to the precise geographical boundary defining which voters fall under the jurisdiction of a specific polling station. This demarcation must be done clearly and unambiguously to prevent any overlap or confusion regarding where a voter is supposed to cast their ballot. Clear boundaries are essential for effective resource allocation, security planning, and the accurate management of the electoral roll, ensuring that every eligible voter knows exactly which station they belong to and that officials can effectively monitor turnout and address any localized issues that may arise during the election process.

The comprehensive documentation of polling areas within an Assembly Constituency requires a meticulous and systematic approach to ensure that every eligible voter can easily identify their designated voting location. The foundational record for this process is a detailed list that explicitly states the name of every village included in the polling area alongside the specific count of registered voters residing in that village. This information must be presented in distinct columns corresponding to each individual polling station, creating a clear link between geographic location and voter volume. To further enhance clarity and accessibility, a map of the entire Assembly Constituency must be prepared, visually marking the locations of all polling stations. Wherever technological resources allow, this physical map should also be converted into a digital format to facilitate easier distribution, analysis, and updates. The overarching goal of providing such a clear and detailed description of the polling area is to ensure that an ordinary voter, regardless of their educational background or familiarity with administrative procedures, can unequivocally determine exactly which polling station they must visit to cast their ballot. This eliminates confusion on election day and streamlines the democratic process. To maintain consistency and uniformity across the entire nation when filling out these critical data columns, specific instructions serve as a guiding framework for election officials.

The first column of the proforma is dedicated to the serial numbering of polling stations, which must follow a strict geographical logic rather than a random assignment. The numbering sequence must commence from the north-western corner of the constituency and proceed in a zigzag pattern across the landscape until it reaches the south-eastern corner. This methodical approach ensures that adjacent polling stations have consecutive numbers, aiding in spatial orientation and logistical planning. In scenarios where a specific part of the electoral roll contains only one polling station, the serial number assigned to that polling station must be identical to the part number of the electoral roll that covers the area served by that station. This alignment simplifies cross-referencing between the physical location and the voter list. However, in more densely populated areas where a single part of the electoral roll requires multiple polling stations to accommodate the volume of voters, the numbering system adapts by using suffixes. In these cases, the base part number is retained, and letters such as A, B, C, and so forth are appended to distinguish the different stations within that same electoral part, ensuring a logical hierarchy in the identification system.

The second column focuses on the locality, which is defined as the specific name of the area where the polling station is physically situated. This requires precise naming conventions to avoid ambiguity. Special attention is required when the polling station is housed in a temporary structure, such as a tent or a prefabricated shed erected specifically for the election. In such instances, simply stating the general area is insufficient; the description must clearly indicate the exact site chosen for the temporary structure, perhaps referencing nearby permanent landmarks or street intersections to ensure voters can locate it without difficulty. Moving to the third column, the requirement is to provide the full, unabbreviated name of the building housing the polling station. The use of abbreviations is strictly discouraged because they can lead to misinterpretation by voters who may not be familiar with local shorthand. If a large building hosts more than one polling station, the location within that building must be specified with extreme precision. Officials should use descriptive terms such as North Wing, South Wing, East Block, or Ground Floor Hall to differentiate between the stations, ensuring that voters entering the building know immediately which direction to proceed.

Column four mandates the recording of the physical area of the polling station room or hall, measured specifically in square meters. This metric is crucial for assessing whether the space is adequate to manage voter flow, maintain social distancing if necessary, and house voting equipment securely. There is a standard expectation regarding the minimum size of these venues; specifically, if a polling station is located in a room or hall that has an area of less than twenty square meters, this deviation from the norm must be justified. The official responsible for the selection must furnish a detailed reason for choosing such a small space in the Remarks column of the proforma opposite the relevant entry. These reasons might include a lack of available larger buildings in remote areas or unique geographical constraints, but they must be documented for review. Similarly, the fifth column addresses the flow of movement within the polling venue by asking whether the location possesses a separate entrance and a separate exit. If the answer is affirmative, the word Yes is recorded. This separation is vital for maintaining an orderly queue and preventing congestion where incoming voters might clash with those who have just voted. If a separate entrance and exit are not feasible, the official must provide a reasoned explanation in this column detailing why it was impossible to locate the polling station in a facility that offers this architectural advantage.

The sixth column serves as a comprehensive geographic identifier, requiring a granular list of the areas served by the polling station. This includes the names of villages, blocks, wards, streets, specific localities, and even house numbers if the area is small enough to be defined by them. Crucially, this column must also cite the part number of the electoral roll that corresponds to these geographic designations. This creates a direct link between the physical address of a voter and their entry in the official voter list, reducing the likelihood of errors during the verification process at the polling booth. Column seven specifies the demographic scope of the polling station, indicating whether it is open to all registered voters or if it is designated exclusively for men or exclusively for women. While most stations are mixed, certain cultural or security considerations in specific regions may necessitate gender-segregated polling stations, and this status must be clearly declared. The eighth column is a quantitative record that must contain the total number of voters assigned to that specific polling station based on the final, certified electoral roll of the constituency. This figure is essential for resource allocation, determining the number of ballot papers needed, and staffing the booth with an appropriate number of election officials.

Column nine deals with the accessibility of the polling station relative to the voters it serves. It requires an indication of the distance a voter must travel to reach the station, but specifically in cases where this distance exceeds the maximum permissible limit of two kilometers. The standard guideline is that no voter should have to travel more than two kilometers to cast their vote, so any instance exceeding this threshold must be highlighted. This data helps the Election Commission identify gaps in coverage and plan for additional mobile polling stations or transport assistance if needed. The tenth column acts as a catch-all for exceptions and justifications. In situations where it is not practically possible to conform to the Commission's standard directions regarding the ideal location or the minimum area of a polling station, broad and detailed reasons must be provided in this column. These explanations are intended for the consideration of the higher authorities, such as the Election Commission, to understand the ground realities that forced the deviation. Additionally, this column allows the District Election Officer or the Returning Officer to add any other remarks they deem necessary to provide context to the data presented.

At the conclusion of each list of polling stations, summary statistics must invariably be displayed to provide a macro-level view of the electoral arrangement. These totals include the aggregate number of electors in the entire constituency, the total count of polling stations proposed for that constituency, and the calculated average number of voters per polling station. These figures allow supervisors to quickly assess whether the workload is evenly distributed and if the number of stations is sufficient to handle the voter density without causing excessive waiting times. Furthermore, the textual list must be accompanied by a map drawn to scale, which serves as a visual counterpart to the tabular data. This map must depict all villages and, in urban areas, all wards or localities. Ideally, the number of voters in each village or locality should be written directly on the map itself to provide immediate visual context. If the map becomes too cluttered to display these numbers legibly, a separate statement annexed to the map should provide this data. The map must also clearly mark the precise places selected as locations for the polling stations. Finally, the map should delineate the area served by each polling station, indicated by the same serial numbers used in the list. These numbers should be arranged systematically on the map, preferably beginning from the north-western corner and proceeding in a zigzag fashion to the south-eastern corner, mirroring the logic used in the first column of the data list. Consistency between the map serial numbers and the electoral roll part numbers is essential for seamless coordination.

Throughout the preparation of these lists and maps, the use of abbreviations should be avoided as far as possible to ensure universal understanding. In rare cases where abbreviations are unavoidable due to space constraints or common usage, they must be fully explained in a legend or footnote. Similarly, if local terms or dialect words are used to describe buildings, landmarks, or geographic features in the list, these terms must also be explained to ensure that officials from outside the immediate locality or voters who have moved away can still understand the descriptions. This attention to linguistic clarity is part of the broader mandate to make the electoral process transparent and accessible. The final stage of this preparatory work involves the publication of the list of polling stations in draft form. Under Section 25 of the Representation of the People Act, 1951, a key piece of legislation governing elections in the region, the District Election Officer bears the statutory responsibility to provide a sufficient number of polling stations for every constituency. This duty applies specifically to constituencies where the whole or the greater part of the area lies within the officer's jurisdiction. However, this power is not absolute; the establishment of these stations requires the prior approval of the Election Commission, ensuring centralized oversight and adherence to national standards. The law acknowledges that determining the district in which the greater part of a constituency lies should not ordinarily be difficult, implying that geographic boundaries are usually clear-cut, but it establishes a legal framework to resolve any ambiguities should they arise, thereby guaranteeing that every voter falls under the administrative care of a specific District Election Officer responsible for their polling arrangements.

In the complex administration of electoral processes, situations frequently arise where the geographical boundaries of a Parliamentary constituency do not align neatly with the administrative borders of districts. A Parliamentary constituency is a large electoral area that sends a representative to the national legislature, and it is often composed of several smaller units known as Assembly Constituencies, which are used for state-level elections. Consider a scenario where a single Parliamentary constituency comprises eight such Assembly Constituencies. If four of these smaller constituencies are located within one administrative district and the remaining four are situated in a different district, it becomes administratively challenging to determine definitively which district contains the greater part of the Parliamentary constituency. This ambiguity cannot be left unresolved, as clear jurisdictional responsibility is essential for the smooth conduct of elections. In such instances of geographical overlap or equal division, the authority to make the final determination rests with the Chief Electoral Officer. This senior official acts as the primary administrator for elections at the state level and must resolve the question by examining specific criteria. The Chief Electoral Officer should base their decision on the physical location of the headquarters of the Returning Officer, who is the official responsible for managing the election process within that specific Parliamentary constituency. Furthermore, the decision should take into account quantitative data, such as the number of registered voters residing in the portions of the constituency that fall within each respective district, or alternatively, the total population figures for those specific areas. Once this determination is made, the Chief Electoral Officer must formally communicate the decision to the District Election Officers of all districts involved in the split. The District Election Officer of the district that the Chief Electoral Officer has identified as containing the greater part of the constituency then assumes full responsibility. This officer becomes accountable for providing and managing polling stations for the entire Parliamentary Constituency, ensuring that every voter within that large area has an assigned place to cast their ballot, regardless of whether their specific neighborhood lies in the primary district or the secondary one.

It is also entirely possible, and indeed common, for a Parliamentary constituency to have a configuration where the majority of its component Assembly constituencies fall within one district, while only a small portion or specific segments extend into one or more other neighboring districts. In these cases, a collaborative approach is required to ensure uniformity and efficiency in the arrangement of voting facilities. The polling stations that have been established or proposed by the District Election Officer of the other district or districts, where the minor portions of the constituency lie, must be adopted in their entirety by the District Election Officer of the main district. This means that the officer responsible for the major part of the constituency takes over the administrative control of these external polling stations for the purpose of the Parliamentary election. They become the official polling stations for that Parliamentary Constituency, and the primary District Election Officer is required to manage them as if they were located within their own jurisdiction. This consolidation prevents fragmentation of responsibility and ensures that there is a single point of accountability for the logistical setup of the entire electoral area. Once this initial allocation and adoption of polling stations are complete, the administrative process moves to a stage of transparency and public consultation. The District Election Officer, often working in tandem with the Returning Officer, must prepare a draft list of these proposed polling stations based on the guidelines and decisions previously outlined. This draft list is a critical document that maps out exactly where citizens will vote. To uphold the principles of democratic openness, this draft must be published for general information. The publication must be conducted in the language or languages in which the electoral roll for that constituency is maintained, ensuring that all voters can understand the information regardless of their linguistic background. The purpose of this publication is to invite objections and suggestions from the public, allowing citizens to point out errors, propose better locations, or raise concerns about accessibility. The law mandates that a specific deadline be set for receiving these inputs, and the period allowed for the public to respond must not be less than seven days, providing a reasonable window for community engagement.

To ensure that the notification reaches the widest possible audience, the notice regarding the publication of the draft list of polling stations, along with details on where the list can be physically inspected, must be disseminated through local newspapers. This media coverage serves as a formal invitation for written objections and suggestions, which will be collected and carefully considered by the election authorities. Beyond general public notification, there is a specific requirement to engage directly with political stakeholders. Copies of the draft lists must be supplied to the local branches of all recognized political parties, ensuring that organized political groups have the opportunity to review the arrangements. Additionally, these copies must be sent to the sitting members of the House of the People, which is the lower house of the national parliament, and to the members of the Legislative Assembly representing the concerned constituencies. In situations where the House of the People or the Legislative Assembly has been dissolved prior to the election, the documents should instead be provided to the ex-members who previously held those seats. This ensures continuity and leverages the local knowledge of experienced legislators. Following the distribution of these documents, the District Election Officer is obligated to convene a meeting. At this gathering, party representatives and legislators are called to discuss the draft list and review the suggestions that have been received from the public and various organizations. The process is designed to be inclusive; any bona fide person who intends to become a candidate in the upcoming election and wishes to participate in these discussions must be allowed to attend the meeting. This inclusion ensures that prospective candidates have a voice in the logistical planning that will affect their campaigns and their constituents.

After the meeting has taken place and all viewpoints have been heard, the District Election Officer must exercise their judgment to make a final decision on the suggestions received. Based on the merit of the arguments presented and the practical realities on the ground, the officer should amend the draft list where necessary to correct errors or improve accessibility. Once these adjustments are made, the draft list of polling stations for the constituency is considered finalized at the district level. The next step involves forwarding this finalized list, accompanied by a detailed map showing the locations, to the Election Commission. This transmission is not direct but must be routed through the Chief Electoral Officer of the State, who acts as an intermediary reviewer. The submission must include a scrutiny sheet and a certificate, both of which must be completed in the specific formats prescribed in Annexure 4 and Annexure 5 of the relevant regulations. These documents serve as formal verification that due process has been followed. A crucial linguistic requirement applies at this stage: if the list is forwarded to the Commission in a language other than English, it must be accompanied by a complete translation in English. This ensures that the national body can review the documents regardless of the regional language used locally. However, it is important to note that there is no mandatory requirement to print the final approved list of polling stations in English for public distribution. The primary obligation is to publish in the local language of the electorate. That said, there is no objection to producing English copies if they are required for official internal use by government departments or if members of the public specifically request them. The Chief Electoral Officer, upon receiving the package from the district, will conduct their own scrutiny of the list and enclosures. After this review, the Chief Electoral Officer forwards the materials to the Election Commission for final approval, attaching their own comments and observations.

The Election Commission, which is the apex constitutional body responsible for administering elections in the country, will then consider the proposed list of polling stations. The Commission has the authority to approve the list as submitted or to mandate changes if they deem it necessary for the fairness or efficiency of the election. Once the Commission reaches a decision, their approval is communicated directly to the District Election Officer, with a copy sent to the Chief Electoral Officer for their records. Receipt of this approval does not mark the end of the district officer's duties. On the contrary, the District Election Officer must once again meticulously check the list to ensure that there are no clerical errors and must incorporate any specific changes or modifications suggested by the Commission during their review process. Only after these final adjustments are made is the list considered truly final and ready for public release. The finally approved list of polling stations must then be published according to strict directives. For an Assembly Constituency, the District Election Officer is responsible for publishing the list of polling stations that they have provided, but this action can only be taken after obtaining the previous approval of the Election Commission. The method of publication involves making a copy of the list available for public inspection on the official website of the Chief Electoral Officer, thereby leveraging digital technology for broad access. In addition to the online presence, a physical notice in the specific format given in Annexure 6 must be displayed prominently. This notice must be posted at the office of the District Election Officer and also at the office of the Returning Officer of that specific constituency. This dual approach of digital and physical display ensures that all voters, regardless of their access to the internet, are informed of their designated polling station, thus completing the rigorous cycle of planning, consultation, approval, and notification that underpins the integrity of the electoral infrastructure.

The District Election Officer bears a significant responsibility to ensure transparency and accessibility in the electoral process by making copies of the relevant sections of the polling station list, accompanied by the official notice in the prescribed format, available for public inspection. This duty must be fulfilled to the greatest extent practicable across a wide network of government offices and local administrative centers. These locations include the offices of the Collector, who serves as the chief revenue and executive officer of the district, as well as the District Magistrate and Sub-divisional Magistrate who oversee law and order and administration at district and sub-district levels respectively. The list must also be accessible at the offices of Revenue Divisional Officers, Judges and Munsif Courts which handle civil judicial matters, Prant Officers who manage regional revenue divisions, and Tahsildars or Amildars who are responsible for land revenue collection at the taluka or circle level. Furthermore, Deputy Tahsildars, Sub-Registrars who manage property documentation, and local Police Stations must maintain these records for public view. The requirement extends deep into rural governance structures, mandating availability at the offices of Mauzadars, Sarpanches who lead village councils, Union Panchayat Ghars which serve as village council halls, Union Boards, District Boards, Municipal Committees governing urban local bodies, and Notified Area Committees managing transitional urban zones. The District Election Officer retains the discretion to designate additional places and methods for dissemination if deemed necessary and suitable to ensure maximum public reach. Upon such publication, the document officially becomes the definitive list of polling stations for that specific constituency, establishing the legal framework for where voting will occur. Once this list is published, the authority of the District Election Officer to alter it becomes strictly limited; they may only correct printing errors or clerical mistakes, such as typographical faults or data entry slips, but cannot make substantive changes to the content or location of polling stations. In scenarios where elections are being held simultaneously for both the House of the People, which is the lower house of the national parliament, and the Legislative Assembly, which is the state legislature, the Returning Officer responsible for the Parliamentary Constituency is not required to publish the list a second time, except for display at their own office, thereby avoiding redundancy. However, in cases where an election is being held solely for the House of the People without a concurrent state assembly election, the Returning Officer must proceed with the full publication of the list. Before the final version of the list is released to the public, specific technical data entries must be removed to protect sensitive operational details. Specifically, the data found in columns four, five, eight, nine, and ten of Annexure three, along with summary statistics at the bottom of the list detailing the total number of registered voters, the total count of polling stations, and the calculated average number of voters assigned to each polling station, must be deleted. This ensures that the public list serves its purpose of informing voters of locations without revealing internal logistical metrics that could be misused. Additionally, the list of polling stations for any Assembly Constituency must be published in the same language or languages used for the electoral roll of that constituency, ensuring that the local population can read and understand the information in their primary tongue.

Regarding modifications to the established list, strict protocols govern how changes are handled following the initial publication. Any adjustment resulting from fluctuations in the number of registered voters within a specific polling area, which often occurs due to the periodic revision and updating of electoral rolls, must be formally reported to the Election Commission solely for their information and record-keeping. More significant alterations involve the physical relocation of polling stations, which may become necessary under specific compelling circumstances. For instance, a move to a new building or site is warranted if the owner of the originally proposed venue has subsequently become a contesting candidate in the election, or if they are known to hold strong sympathies for a particular candidate or political party, as this could create a perception of bias or intimidate voters. Relocation is also mandatory if the original building has been damaged or rendered unusable due to natural calamities such as floods, earthquakes, or storms. All such proposed changes regarding location must be reported to the Election Commission for their explicit approval before implementation. Once the lists have received formal approval from the Commission, the threshold for making further changes based on requests from political parties or private individuals becomes exceptionally high. Requests to shift a polling station from one village to another or from one site to another within the same village should only be entertained in extremely exceptional cases where there are overriding considerations of public convenience that justify the disruption. If the District Election Officer or Returning Officer finds merit in such a request, they are obligated to first consult with representatives of other political parties and all contesting candidates to gauge consensus and address concerns. Only after this consultation process should they formulate recommendations to submit to the Commission. It is imperative to note that the District Election Officer is strictly prohibited from unilaterally changing the location of any polling station that has already been approved by the Commission without obtaining prior approval from the Commission itself. Violating this protocol is a serious infraction, as any unauthorized change has the potential to compromise the integrity of the election process and could ultimately result in the election being declared void by judicial or quasi-judicial authorities. In situations where changes are truly inevitable and must be executed, they must be referred to the Commission for prior sanction, fully publicized to ensure voter awareness, and all contesting candidates and political parties must be notified in writing to maintain procedural fairness.

Distinct procedures apply to changes in the nomenclature or naming convention of the building housing a polling station, provided the physical location remains unchanged. After the list of polling stations for a constituency has been approved, if the name of the building changes due to administrative upgrades or reclassification, such as a Primary School being upgraded to a Middle School or similar institutional renaming, these cases do not require formal referral to the Election Commission for approval since the actual voting venue has not moved. However, even though approval is not needed, the Commission must still be informed of such nominal changes to keep their records accurate. Furthermore, transparency dictates that all political parties and contesting candidates must be informed in writing about these name changes to prevent any confusion among voters or agents on election day. The distribution of the finalized list is governed by clear mandates to ensure all stakeholders have equal access to information. As soon as reasonably possible after the list of polling stations has been finally published, a copy of this definitive document must be supplied free of cost to every recognized political party, specifically those to whom draft versions of the lists were previously provided during the consultation phase. Additionally, every individual contesting candidate in an election is entitled to receive three copies of the polling station list for their respective constituency free of charge. These copies must be delivered immediately after the last date permitted for the withdrawal of candidatures, ensuring candidates have the final data before campaigning intensifies. Beyond free distribution to key stakeholders, copies of the list must also be made available for sale to the general public at a fixed, regulated price for any person who demands them, promoting broader civic engagement. The District Election Officer is also responsible for supplying the necessary number of copies to law enforcement leadership, specifically the Superintendent of Police, Senior Superintendent of Police, or Commissioner of Police, depending on the jurisdictional hierarchy, to aid in security planning. Operational copies must be furnished to the Returning Officer and any Assistant Returning Officers designated for the Parliamentary or Assembly Constituency to facilitate the conduct of the poll. Finally, one master copy of the finalized list must be transmitted to the Chief Electoral Officer of the state or union territory to complete the archival and oversight chain of command.

The comprehensive management of polling stations serves as a foundational element in the administration of free and fair elections, ensuring that the democratic process remains accessible, predictable, and secure for all eligible voters. To enhance transparency and public access to information, the complete list of designated polling stations is frequently published on the official website of the Chief Electoral Officer, allowing citizens to verify their voting locations well in advance. This practice supports the broader directive issued by the Election Commission regarding the establishment of polling stations for future general elections and bye-elections. Under the existing instructions of the Commission, there is a strong emphasis on the principle of permanence concerning the location of polling stations. The rationale behind this policy is to provide voters with a clear and consistent understanding of where they must proceed to cast their ballots. By maintaining relatively permanent locations, the electoral authority ensures that voters are not subjected to the confusion or logistical burden of traveling to different venues for different elections, thereby fostering greater voter participation and reducing administrative errors. Furthermore, the Commission has mandated that electoral rolls, which are the official registers of eligible voters, must be prepared specifically according to polling station boundaries. This means that each distinct part of the electoral roll corresponds precisely to a well-defined geographic area assigned to a specific polling station, creating a direct and organized link between the voter list and the physical voting location.

Once a proposed list of polling stations is submitted to the Commission and receives formal approval under the provisions of Section 25 of the Representation of the People Act of 1951, this approved document becomes the definitive list of polling stations for that particular constituency. The Representation of the People Act of 1951 is the primary legislation in India that governs the conduct of elections to the Parliament and State Legislatures, providing the legal framework for everything from voter eligibility to the resolution of election disputes. When a future general election or bye-election is scheduled to take place within that constituency, the District Election Officer, often working in conjunction with the Returning Officer who is responsible for conducting the election in that specific area, must undertake a thorough review of the previously approved list. This examination is crucial to determine whether any additions or alterations to the existing list are necessary, particularly in light of revisions made to the electoral rolls prior to the upcoming election. If, after careful consideration, it is determined that no changes or modifications to the approved list are required and the same list is proposed for adoption in the forthcoming election, the law stipulates that no further fresh approval from the Commission is needed. In such scenarios, the list does not need to be referred back to the Commission for re-approval, which streamlines the administrative process. However, to maintain oversight and ensure procedural compliance, the Commission must be formally informed of this decision to retain the existing list at least two weeks before the last date for the withdrawal of candidatures, which is a critical deadline in the election timeline after which candidates can no longer withdraw their names from the contest.

Conversely, if the District Election Officer proposes to adopt the previously approved list of polling stations but with specific modifications or updates for the said election, a more rigorous consultative process is triggered. In these instances, the District Election Officer is required to convene a meeting involving representatives from the local branches of all recognized political parties as well as sitting legislators. This consultation ensures that all major political stakeholders have an opportunity to voice their concerns or suggestions regarding changes to polling locations, thereby upholding the principles of fairness and bipartisanship. Following these consultations, the District Election Officer must compile a fresh composite proposal that incorporates the feedback received and submit this updated plan to the Commission through the office of the Chief Electoral Officer, who acts as the principal officer responsible for the preparation and conduct of elections in the state. Parallel to the logistical arrangement of polling stations is the critical exercise of Security and Law and Order Analysis, which must be carried out in every district of the state under the direct leadership of the District Election Officer. This analysis is a proactive measure designed to identify potential risks, vulnerabilities, and security challenges that could disrupt the electoral process. To execute this task effectively, the District Election Officer must garner all necessary support from the police force and other relevant government officers, including the Returning Officers and Sector Officers or Sector Magistrates, who are responsible for overseeing security in specific geographical sectors of the constituency.

In the context of security planning, it is imperative that the Returning Officer thoroughly studies the latest Manual on Vulnerability Mapping. This manual contains the most current instructions, methodologies, and standardized formats required for reporting risk assessments to the Commission. Vulnerability mapping is a strategic tool used to identify areas that are prone to electoral malpractices, violence, or coercion, allowing authorities to deploy resources accordingly. The latest edition of this manual is typically available for download from the Commission's official website, ensuring that all field officers have access to uniform guidelines. A key outcome of this security analysis is the identification of Critical Polling Stations, which are voting locations deemed to be at high risk based on a set of objective criteria established by the electoral authority. The District Election Officer and Returning Officer must adhere strictly to these criteria when flagging such stations. One primary indicator is the presence of vulnerable pockets within the jurisdiction of a polling station or the location of the station itself within an area already identified as vulnerable due to historical tensions or criminal activity. Another significant metric involves analyzing past voting patterns; specifically, polling stations where voter turnout exceeded ninety percent in the previous election and where more than seventy-five percent of the votes cast were in favor of a single candidate are considered suspicious, as such skewed results may indicate booth capturing or undue influence.

Additionally, polling stations that recorded extremely low turnout, defined as less than ten percent, are also classified as critical, as this may suggest voter intimidation or boycotts enforced by anti-social elements. Any polling station where a re-poll was conducted in the past due to the vitiation of the poll process is automatically flagged. Vitiation refers to the corruption or impairment of the election process, often caused by serious electoral offences such as booth capturing, which is the illegal seizure of a polling booth by armed groups to stuff ballot boxes or prevent genuine voters from casting their votes. This criterion applies to any election for the Lok Sabha, the lower house of India's Parliament, or the State Legislative Assembly that has occurred within the last five years. Similarly, any polling station where incidents of violence took place on polling day, leading to the filing of a First Information Report or FIR with the police, during any parliamentary or state assembly election in the preceding five years, must be included in the critical list. An FIR is a formal document prepared by the police when they receive information about the commission of a cognizable offence, serving as a legal record of the incident. Furthermore, statistical anomalies regarding the voter list are taken into account; specifically, polling stations exhibiting a percentage of Absentee, Shifted, and Dead voters that is higher than the constituency average are marked as critical. The constituency average is calculated by dividing the total number of such invalid or outdated voter entries in the entire constituency by the total number of polling stations, providing a baseline for comparison.

Information regarding any abnormal law and order situation in a particular area, such as ongoing communal tensions or gang wars, is also factored into this assessment. The District Election Officers and Returning Officers are mandated to consider all these diverse inputs collectively to finalize the list of critical polling stations that will require additional security measures and monitoring. To ensure an unbiased and comprehensive evaluation, the Election Commission Observers, who are independent officials deputed to oversee the election process, must also be consulted during the finalization of this list. The specific response protocols to be followed concerning vulnerable villages, hamlets, or electoral segments have been detailed in official correspondence, specifically referencing the Commission's letter numbered 464 divided by L and O slash 2023 slash EPS open parenthesis VM close parenthesis dated the 21st of June 2023, as well as the latest editions of the Manual of Vulnerability Mapping and the Manual of Force Deployment. These documents provide the operational blueprint for handling high-risk scenarios. Once polling stations are identified as critical based on the indicators listed above, one or all of several stringent measures shall be implemented as directed by the Commission to safeguard the integrity of the vote.

First and foremost, all critical polling stations must have a deployment of Central Armed Police Forces, commonly known as CAPF. The CAPF comprises various federal police organizations in India that operate under the Ministry of Home Affairs and are tasked with maintaining internal security. Their presence at these sensitive locations is intended to safeguard the polling stations to the maximum possible extent, deterring any attempts at intimidation or violence. Secondly, the finalized list of all such critical polling stations must be shared with the Commanding Officers and Assistant Commanding Officers of the deployed CAPF units. This ensures that the military-style command structure is fully aware of the specific locations requiring heightened vigilance, allowing them to keep a close watch on these stations throughout the election period. Thirdly, as a civil measure to enhance transparency and provide real-time monitoring, the deployment of webcasting or videography systems, along with any other advanced monitoring technology, shall be positioned at these polling stations. Webcasting involves the live streaming of video footage from the polling booth to a central server, allowing election officials and observers to monitor the proceedings remotely and instantly detect any irregularities. These combined measures of physical force deployment and technological surveillance create a robust security apparatus designed to neutralize threats and ensure that every voter in even the most volatile areas can exercise their franchise without fear or favor.

The conduct of free and fair elections requires a robust framework of monitoring and infrastructure to ensure the integrity of the voting process and the comfort of the electorate. A primary component of this framework is the implementation of webcasting, which involves the live video streaming of proceedings from polling stations to allow for remote, real-time oversight by election authorities and observers. This measure is mandatory for all locations designated as critical polling stations, which are typically areas identified as having a higher risk of malpractice or logistical challenges. Furthermore, the requirement extends to at least fifty percent of the total number of polling stations, including any auxiliary or temporary stations established to handle overflow, with the rule being applied based on whichever figure yields a greater number of locations. In addition to the internal broadcasting of the voting hall, it is imperative that critical events occurring in the immediate vicinity and surroundings of the polling station are also captured on video. This comprehensive videography ensures that the entire ecosystem of the polling location, from the queue formation outside to the ballot casting inside, remains transparent and accountable. To further strengthen these monitoring capabilities, election commissions may deploy a Micro Observer within critical polling stations. This individual acts as an additional civil measure, serving as an extra set of eyes on the ground to enhance the scrutiny of procedures and deter any potential irregularities. The Presiding Officer, who is the senior official in charge of a critical polling station, must receive specific and detailed briefings prior to the election day. A key focus of this briefing is the rigorous verification of voter identity documents, specifically the Electors Photo Identity Card, commonly known as EPIC, or any other approved identification document. The officer must ensure that the status of these verifications is accurately recorded in the remarks column of Form 17A, which is the official register documenting every vote cast. To support these operational protocols, the Election Commission has compiled a comprehensive Manual on Force Multipliers and Civil Measures. This document, available in its latest edition on the Commission's official website, outlines strategies for maximizing the effectiveness of security and administrative personnel. It also contains detailed information regarding non-Central Armed Police Force measures tailored for critical polling stations, providing local authorities with the necessary guidelines to maintain order and efficiency without relying solely on central paramilitary forces.

Parallel to the monitoring mechanisms, the Election Commission has issued strict directives concerning the physical infrastructure of polling locations through the concept of Assured Minimum Facilities. These facilities represent a baseline standard of amenities that must be present at every polling station to facilitate a smooth, hassle-free, and convenient voting experience for citizens while enabling polling personnel to discharge their duties efficiently. The responsibility for implementing these standards lies with the Chief Electoral Officers and District Election Officers, who are mandated to conduct a complete gap analysis. This analytical process involves assessing the current state of each polling station against the required standards to identify deficiencies in infrastructure. Following this assessment, these officers must collaborate with relevant state authorities to permanently install or upgrade the necessary facilities. This is not a temporary arrangement for the election day but a long-term initiative to improve civic infrastructure. To ensure compliance, a multi-tiered verification exercise is conducted well in advance of the elections, as scheduled in the official election planner. Field officials, specifically Sector Officers, are required to physically visit every polling station within their jurisdiction to evaluate whether the location is fit for use. Above them in the hierarchy, Returning Officers, who oversee larger constituencies, must personally verify at least twenty percent of the polling locations to ensure that the assessments by lower-level officials are accurate and that standards are being met. This layered approach to inspection guarantees that no location is overlooked and that the conditions on the ground match the administrative records.

One of the fundamental requirements for a polling station is its physical accessibility, starting with its location within a building. Polling stations must be established on the ground floor of a structure or at road level. This stipulation is crucial for facilitating access for aged electors and persons with disabilities who may find it difficult or impossible to climb stairs. The overarching goal of the electoral geography planning is to ensure that no voter is required to travel more than two kilometers to reach their designated polling station. However, the Commission recognizes that rigid adherence to distance norms may not be feasible in all terrains. In sparsely populated hilly regions or dense forest areas, where population density is low and terrain is difficult, this two-kilometer rule may be relaxed. In such exceptional cases, to prevent voters from having to walk unduly long distances over challenging topography, polling stations may be established for a smaller number of electors than the usual normative strength. This flexibility ensures that the ease of travel and the specific topography of the region are given due consideration, prioritizing voter convenience over strict numerical quotas. Special attention is also paid to hilly areas where polling stations might be located on elevated levels relative to the surrounding roads. In these scenarios, it must be ensured that if a polling station is situated at a higher elevation, the approach road leading to it passes at the same level as the station entrance, thereby eliminating the need for voters to climb steep inclines immediately before voting. Furthermore, the flow of human traffic must be managed effectively by providing separate entrances and exits for voters. This separation prevents congestion and maintains an orderly queue. In instances where a building has only a single doorway, a functional separation can be achieved using temporary fencing constructed from locally available materials such as bamboos and ropes, positioned in the middle of the doorway to create distinct entry and exit lanes based on local conditions.

Accessibility for persons with disabilities and senior citizens is further reinforced by the mandatory provision of ramps at all polling stations. A permanent ramp with a maximum gradient of one unit of vertical rise for every twelve units of horizontal length, expressed as a ratio of 1:12, must be installed. This specific slope is designed to be gentle enough for wheelchair users and those with limited mobility to ascend safely without assistance. This requirement stems from the directions of the Honorable Supreme Court of India, which emphasized the right of every citizen to vote with dignity. The Supreme Court also directed that wide publicity be given to the availability of these ramps so that voters are aware of the facilities provided for them. Hydration is another critical aspect of voter welfare, particularly in regions with hot climates or during long waiting times. Therefore, there should preferably be a permanent arrangement of tap facilities providing drinking water at every polling station. As an alternative where permanent plumbing is unavailable, large drinking water pots with sufficient capacity to last the entire day of the poll must be provided. To maintain hygiene, a sufficient number of environment-friendly disposable glasses should be made available for voters to use. The drinking water facility should ideally operate on a self-service basis to empower voters, but wherever possible, the administration should arrange for volunteers or daily wage earners to actively serve water to voters standing in the queue, ensuring that no one suffers from dehydration while waiting to exercise their franchise.

Inside the polling station, the working environment for both officials and voters must be conducive to efficiency and comfort. Adequate furniture, including tables, chairs, and benches, must be provided for the polling parties and the polling agents who represent the various political candidates. These furnishings allow the officials to manage documents and Electronic Voting Machines properly and enable agents to observe the process without fatigue. Additionally, specific seating arrangements in the form of adequate chairs or benches must be available at each polling station exclusively for disabled, pregnant, or senior citizen electors, allowing them to rest while waiting their turn. Proper lighting and electricity arrangements are vital for the technical operation of the voting equipment and the clarity of the process. It must be ensured that the interior space of the voting compartment, where the voter marks their choice, is sufficiently lighted to allow for clear visibility. However, great care must be taken when setting up Electronic Voting Machines and Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail units to ensure that light sources do not fall directly onto the voting compartment, which could compromise the secrecy of the vote or cause glare on the machine screens. If the existing lighting is inadequate, suitable additional lighting arrangements must be installed for each compartment. Strict prohibitions are in place regarding the type of lighting used; halogen lamps or high-wattage bulbs of 200 watts or more are strictly avoided directly above or in front of the voting compartment to prevent excessive heat and harsh glare. Finally, if the location has an electrical power connection, a thorough check of the condition of switches, bulbs, and fans must be conducted prior to the election to ensure that the environment remains cool and well-lit throughout the long hours of polling.

In the comprehensive planning and execution of democratic elections, ensuring uninterrupted power supply at every polling station is a fundamental requirement for maintaining order, security, and operational efficiency. However, in certain geographical contexts, providing a standard electrical connection, even on a temporary basis, may prove impossible due to challenging terrain features or the excessive distance from the nearest electrical grid infrastructure. In such scenarios where the main power network cannot be accessed, election authorities are mandated to secure alternative lighting solutions to guarantee that the polling environment remains adequately illuminated throughout the voting day. This involves the procurement and deployment of non-electric or self-contained light sources such as Petromax pressure lamps, which operate using kerosene fuel to produce bright light, traditional lanterns, and modern battery-operated LED lights or charger-based illumination systems. Furthermore, for locations requiring more substantial power generation to run multiple devices or larger areas, the hiring of portable generator sets becomes essential. The primary objective of these measures is to ensure that no polling station operates in darkness, thereby facilitating the smooth conduct of voting procedures, enabling officials to verify documents accurately, and ensuring the safety and comfort of both voters and staff regardless of the remote or difficult nature of the location.

Complementing the physical infrastructure, the implementation of proper signage serves as a critical navigational aid for the electorate. Clear, visible, and informative signs must be erected in the immediate vicinity of and directly at each polling station location. These signs are not merely directional but serve an educational and facilitative role by guiding voters through the layout of the premises. They must explicitly indicate the location of various essential facilities available within the compound, such as accessibility ramps designed for individuals with mobility challenges, restroom facilities for men and women, sources of safe drinking water, and the specific location of the Voter Assistance Booth. This systematic approach to signage reduces confusion, minimizes congestion, and ensures that voters, particularly those who may be elderly, first-time voters, or unfamiliar with the area, can locate necessary services without undue stress or delay. The presence of such guidance markers is a key component of voter-friendly election management, fostering an inclusive environment where every citizen can exercise their franchise with dignity and ease.

Sanitation facilities constitute another pillar of polling station preparedness, with strict guidelines governing the provision of toilets for male and female voters. At every polling station location, there must be separate toilet facilities available in sufficient numbers to accommodate the expected volume of voters from both genders. The protocol dictates a hierarchical approach to fulfilling this requirement based on the existing infrastructure. Ideally, two permanent structures, known locally as pucca toilets, should be available and designated separately for men and women. In situations where two permanent toilets are not present, the administration must earmark one existing pucca toilet exclusively for female voters to ensure their privacy and security, while a temporary toilet structure may be constructed or installed to serve male voters. In cases where no permanent toilet infrastructure exists at all, the election commission mandates the provisioning of mobile toilet vans, which are self-contained sanitation units that can be transported to the site. Special considerations are made for hilly regions or areas with difficult topography where transporting large vehicles like toilet vans is impractical; in these instances, appropriate temporary toilet arrangements suitable for the terrain must be established. To maintain hygiene standards throughout the lengthy voting hours, one employee or daily wage earner must be specifically deployed at each location to clean and maintain the toilets continuously. Furthermore, these facilities must be designed or adapted to be fully accessible for Persons with Disabilities and senior citizens, ensuring that physical barriers do not prevent any voter from utilizing these essential amenities.

To protect voters and staff from harsh weather conditions, particularly intense sunlight or rain, adequate shading arrangements are mandatory at every polling station. A tented arrangement featuring a canopy of sufficient size, with minimum dimensions specified as fifteen feet by fifteen feet, must be erected to serve as a designated waiting area. This shaded space is crucial for the comfort of voters who may need to queue before entering the polling booth. Within this waiting area, sufficient seating in the form of chairs or benches must be provided, with priority allocation for vulnerable groups including ladies, senior citizens, nursing mothers, differently abled voters, and any children accompanying them. This provision acknowledges the physical limitations of these groups and ensures they do not have to stand for prolonged periods under direct exposure to the elements. Additionally, recognizing that many voters bring young children who may become restless or tired during the voting process, there must be a proper arrangement for a creche or childcare facility at each polling station location. This dedicated space allows children to rest or play safely while their parents or guardians fulfill their civic duty. To manage this facility effectively, one trained attendant or volunteer must be deployed specifically to supervise and care for these children, providing reassurance to parents and maintaining a calm atmosphere within the polling station complex.

Information dissemination at the polling station is governed by statutory requirements, specifically Rule 31 of the Conduct of Elections Rules, 1961, which mandates the prominent display of voter facilitation posters. To fulfill these legal obligations and to provide accurate, relevant information that enhances voter awareness, the Election Commission has directed that four distinct kinds of posters must be displayed at all polling stations. These posters serve as an official source of truth regarding the voting process, candidate information, and behavioral codes, ensuring that every voter has access to standardized information before casting their vote. Integral to this information ecosystem is the establishment of a Voter Assistance Booth at every polling station location. This booth acts as the primary help desk, staffed by a team comprising the Booth Level Officer and other designated officials. Their specific role is to assist voters in navigating the electoral roll, helping them correctly identify their assigned polling booth number and their unique serial number within the roll of that specific booth. Setting up this booth requires advance preparation by the Returning Officer, who must ensure the availability of basic furniture such as tables and chairs for the staff, a small covered canopy or tent to provide shade, and prominent signage. This signage typically takes the form of a back-drop banner clearly indicating "VOTER ASSISTANCE BOOTH" along with directional signs to guide voters to the spot. A key technological tool deployed at these booths is the Alphabetic Roll Locator, a system generated via the ERO Net platform. This tool organizes voter names according to the English alphabet, allowing for rapid searching so that voters can easily find their names and ascertain their serial numbers in the Electoral Roll without manual sifting through voluminous paper records.

Beyond administrative and logistical support, the health and safety of voters and polling personnel are paramount, necessitating the availability of comprehensive medical resources at every location. It is mandatory to ensure that a sufficient number of Medical Kits or First-Aid kits are present at each polling station. These kits must be well-stocked with a wide array of medicines and medical items, including gauze pads of standard sizes such as four by four inches, large gauze pads for significant wounds, adhesive bandages for minor cuts, rolling bandages for wrapping injuries, Oral Rehydration Salts for treating dehydration, triangular bandages for slings or immobilization, wound cleaning agents, scissors for cutting dressings, blankets for shock or warmth, tweezers for removing foreign objects, adhesive tape, latex gloves for hygiene, resuscitation equipment for emergency life support, as well as masks and sanitizers for infection control. These supplies are intended for emergency use by both voters and polling staff. To ensure the appropriate use of these materials, the District Election Officer must prepare a standard list of medicines and user instructions in consultation with the District Chief Medical Officer. During summer months, when the risk of heat-related illnesses increases, every polling party must be supplied with Oral Rehydration Salts for their own consumption and for distribution to any voter suffering from heat stroke. Educational materials, such as a handbill detailing the "Dos and Don'ts" in case of heat stroke, should be prepared and distributed to all polling parties. Furthermore, public appeals may be issued advising electors to carry wet towels to protect against dehydration and recommending that women avoid bringing children to polling stations during extreme hot weather conditions to minimize health risks. To enhance medical responsiveness, the District Election Officer shall requisition the services of paramedical staff from the offices of the Chief District Medical Officers. One such paramedical staff member must accompany every mobile Patrol Unit and Sector Officer in their vehicles, equipped with essential medicines. These mobile patrol units are instructed to visit every polling station at least once every hour, preferably more frequently, ensuring that instant professional medical assistance is available to any voter in distress, supplementing the basic first aid facilities already present with the polling parties.

The management of voter flow and the assistance of vulnerable populations rely heavily on the deployment of volunteers. Young volunteers from organizations such as the National Cadet Corps, the National Service Scheme, and the Scout and Guide movements should be deployed specifically to manage queues and maintain order among voters. These volunteers play a vital role in identifying Persons with Disabilities and senior citizen voters, assisting them in reaching the polling station, and providing wheelchairs if required. Persons with Disabilities can request wheelchair facilities in advance by registering on the Saksham-ECI application, a digital platform designed to facilitate accessible voting. The volunteers are responsible for guiding these electors up to the entrance of the polling station, after which the responsibility transitions to the internal polling staff who assist them inside the voting enclosure. A specific demographic criterion is applied to these volunteers, stipulating that their age should be less than eighteen years, leveraging the energy and community spirit of youth to support the democratic process while ensuring they operate under the supervision of adult election officials. This structured involvement of youth organizations not only aids in the logistical smoothness of the election but also fosters a sense of civic engagement and responsibility among the younger generation.

The Chief Electoral Officer holds the primary responsibility for preparing a comprehensive and detailed set of guidelines outlining acceptable and prohibited behaviors for all volunteers involved in the election process. This document, often referred to as a code of conduct or a list of dos and don'ts, must be meticulously crafted to ensure clarity and adherence to ethical standards. Once prepared, it is imperative that these volunteers receive thorough briefings and structured training sessions conducted by the District Electoral Officer or the Returning Officer. These training programs are essential to equip volunteers with the necessary knowledge to perform their duties effectively while maintaining the integrity of the electoral process. Furthermore, logistical arrangements must be made to provide adequate food and sustenance for all volunteers who are deployed at polling stations on the day of the election. Ensuring that volunteers are well-fed is not merely a matter of comfort but a critical operational requirement to maintain their energy levels and focus throughout the long hours of duty, thereby contributing to the smooth functioning of the polling activities.

In addition to volunteer management, specific provisions must be established to facilitate the mobility of vulnerable groups of voters on poll day. Senior citizens, individuals with visual impairments, those with locomotive disabilities affecting their movement, and other infirm voters must be provided with free passes to utilize public transportation systems. This measure is designed to remove financial and logistical barriers that might otherwise prevent these citizens from exercising their democratic right to vote. In scenarios where public transport options are unavailable or insufficient in a particular area, authorities are mandated to hire private vehicles to serve this purpose. The service must be door-to-door in nature, meaning that the voter should be picked up directly from their residence and dropped back at the same location after they have successfully cast their vote. This end-to-end support system ensures that physical limitations or lack of access to transport do not disenfranchise any eligible voter, reinforcing the principle of inclusive democracy.

Effective queue management at polling stations is another critical component of election administration, requiring a structured approach to organize the flow of voters. There should be three distinct queues established at every polling station: one dedicated exclusively to male voters, a second for female voters, and a third specifically reserved for senior citizens and persons with disabilities. To balance the flow and account for historical voting patterns, the protocol dictates that for every two female electors allowed to enter the polling booth, one male elector should be permitted. However, flexibility and compassion are paramount; persons with disabilities or infirmities must be granted priority entry, allowing them to bypass the standard queues entirely without having to wait. Alongside priority entry, all necessary assistance required by these individuals must be readily available within the polling station premises. Similarly, pregnant women and mothers holding infants in their arms should also be given precedence over other voters in the queue to minimize their physical strain and waiting time. To further enhance comfort, chairs should be placed at intermittent intervals along the queuing areas, particularly at polling stations known to experience long lines, enabling voters to rest for a few minutes while waiting their turn.

The Chief Electoral Officer and District Electoral Officer bear the responsibility of generating adequate publicity regarding these special facilities extended to senior citizens, persons with disabilities, infirm voters, pregnant women, and mothers with children. Public awareness campaigns should include a strong appeal to the general electorate to cooperate with these initiatives. Voters who are able-bodied and in good health should be explicitly requested not to exploit these provisions to jump the queue; rather, these privileges should be utilized strictly on a need basis. It is crucial to foster a culture of empathy and civic responsibility where voters waiting in line maintain an open mind and are willing to assist fellow citizens suffering from disabilities or age-related and health-related challenges. Presiding Officers and other field officers deployed at polling stations must receive clear instructions to enforce these guidelines and facilitate this cooperative environment. Based on historical data, past voting patterns, and the accumulated experience of field staff, authorities must proactively identify polling stations or locations that are prone to developing long queues. Dynamic tracking of queue lengths on poll day should be carried out continuously by Sector Officers to monitor the situation in real-time. Whenever congestion is detected, immediate decongestion measures must be initiated, such as deploying additional polling staff, mobilizing volunteers, or distributing tokens to manage the crowd systematically. Reserve staff and vehicles should be kept on standby, ready for rapid deployment to any polling station experiencing excessive queues to provide immediate relief to the existing polling team. Additionally, members from organizations such as the National Cadet Corps and the National Service Scheme may be mobilized to assist in managing the queues, leveraging their discipline and organizational skills to maintain order.

Technological integration plays a pivotal role in modernizing the voting process and enhancing efficiency through tools like the Booth App. This application is an integrated module of the larger ENCORE ecosystem, which stands for Enabling Communications on Real-time Operations. The Booth App facilitates the rapid and accurate identification of voters by scanning encrypted QR codes present on Voter Information Slips and cross-referencing them with the digital marked copy of the electors list. This technological intervention significantly reduces waiting times in queues, accelerates the overall polling process, and ensures the error-free recording of voter turnout data at two-hour intervals with minimal human intervention. By automating verification, the potential for manual errors is drastically reduced, and the transparency of the process is enhanced. Beyond technology, the concept of Model Polling Stations introduces additional attractive features tailored to local culture and specific requirements, as per directions issued under the Systematic Voters Education and Electoral Participation program. These model stations serve as benchmarks for excellence and voter friendliness. Environmentally conscious practices are also mandated; polling stations should be constructed using eco-friendly, recyclable, and biodegradable materials, striving to be as plastic-free as possible. Provisions for environmentally friendly waste management must be established to ensure that the electoral process leaves a minimal ecological footprint.

To further expedite the voting procedure, several operational measures must be strictly followed. Printed blank voter slips, which are distinct from the Voter Information Slips distributed to electors prior to election day, must be available with all polling parties to ensure faster operations inside the polling station. These slips aid in the internal documentation and workflow efficiency.
