A critical component of this mandate involves the detection and investigation of suspected cases of paid news, which refers to the practice where media outlets publish favorable content about a candidate in exchange for payment without disclosing it as an advertisement. The timeline for accounting for these paid news cases is strictly defined, starting from the specific date on which a candidate files their nomination papers. The committee is also required to actively consider and investigate any paid news cases that are referred to it by Expenditure Observers, who are officials tasked with tracking campaign spending.

Upon identifying instances of paid news or unaccounted political publicity, the MCMC must formally intimate the Returning Officer, who is the senior election official responsible for conducting the election in a specific constituency. This intimation triggers the issuance of official notices to the candidates involved, demanding that they include the actual expenditure incurred for the published matter in their official election expense accounts. In scenarios where the actual amount paid cannot be determined or verified, the committee calculates a notional expenditure based on standard rates established by the Department of Information and Public Relations, known as DIPR rates. If these specific rates are unavailable for a particular type of media or region, the committee may alternatively utilize the rates set by the Directorate of Advertising and Visual Publicity, or DAVP. It is a crucial legal point that this inclusion of expenditure in the candidate's account is mandatory regardless of whether the candidate actually disbursed any funds to the channel or newspaper; the mere act of publication benefiting the candidate creates a financial liability under election law. A copy of every such notice issued to a candidate must also be marked to the Expenditure Observer to ensure transparency and coordinated oversight. Furthermore, the monitoring scope extends to political advertisements in all other media forms concerning candidates, whether these advertisements are overt and clearly identified or covert and disguised as regular content. This monitoring is viewed through the lens of expenditure control and includes any publicity, advertising, or appeal made by the candidate themselves, on their behalf, by their Star Campaigners who are high-profile party leaders authorized to campaign extensively, or by any other associates, provided the intent is to impact the candidate's electoral prospects.

When examining advertisements in print media, the MCMC must determine whether the advertisement was published with the consent or knowledge of the candidate. If such consent or knowledge is established, the cost of the advertisement must be accounted for within the total election expenses of the candidate or candidates involved. However, if the investigation reveals that the advertisement was published without the authority or consent of the candidate, the matter shifts from an expenditure violation to a potential criminal offense. In such unauthorized cases, action may be initiated for the prosecution of the publisher for violating Section 171H of the Indian Penal Code, which specifically penalizes the publication of election-related matter without proper authorization. Another vital compliance check involves verifying that all election pamphlets, posters, handbills, and similar documents carry the name and address of both the publisher and the printer, as strictly required under Section 127A of the Representation of the People Act of 1951. This legal provision ensures accountability and traceability of printed political material. If any printed material is found lacking these mandatory details on its face, the MCMC is obligated to bring this violation to the immediate notice of the Returning Officer for further necessary legal action. For the specific purposes of Section 127 of the Representation of the People Act, the definition of an "other document" is interpreted broadly to explicitly include paid news, thereby subjecting such covert advertisements to the same disclosure requirements as traditional printed materials. To facilitate the efficient certification of political advertisements across large areas, the Returning Officer of a Parliamentary Constituency is empowered to co-opt additional members to assist in the certification process. The number of these co-opted members should correspond to the number of districts falling within that Parliamentary Constituency, ensuring that there is a wider range of opinions and equal representation from all districts in the matter of certification.

Operational reporting is a key function of the district-level MCMC, which is required to submit a daily report to the designated Accounting team. This report must also be copied to the Returning Officer and the Expenditure Observer and must follow a prescribed format, typically detailed in annexure 12 of the official Expenditure Guidelines. The report must cover each candidate individually and detail the expenditure incurred by the candidate on election advertising, as well as the actual expenditure incurred for publishing news items that have been substantiated by necessary documents furnished by the candidate. Additionally, it must include notional expenditures as computed by the committee in assessed cases of paid news where actual payment proofs are absent. At a higher administrative level, an Additional or Joint Chief Electoral Officer's Committee on Certification is established to handle pre-certification applications. This committee comprises specific high-ranking officers, including the Additional or Joint Chief Electoral Officer who serves as the Chairperson, and the Returning Officer of any Parliamentary constituency located in the capital city of the State. The committee also includes one expert who must be an officer not below the rank of a Class-I officer, requisitioned specifically from the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting to provide technical media expertise. Furthermore, an intermediary or social media expert, chosen by the Chief Electoral Officer subject to specific eligibility criteria, is included to address the complexities of digital campaigning. This committee is responsible for considering applications for pre-certification submitted by all registered political parties that have their headquarters in that State or Union Territory, as well as by all organizations, groups of persons, or associations that maintain their registered offices within that same State or Union Territory.

The State level MCMC operates as the apex body for media monitoring within the state and comprises a distinguished panel of officers. The Chief Electoral Officer of the State serves as the Chairman of this committee. The membership includes an ADG or Director level officer from the Press Information Bureau or the Broadcast Engineering Corporation present in the State, who acts as the Nodal officer for the MCMC and is nominated by the Director General of the relevant zone in the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting, Government of India. Alternatively, the committee may co-opt one expert as deemed necessary. Any Observer appointed directly by the Election Commission of India is also a member, providing an independent layer of oversight. An officer of the Indian Information Service, holding a rank equivalent to Under Secretary or Deputy Secretary and posted in the State or Union Territory, represents the media department of the Government of India; this role is distinct from the previously mentioned expert. The panel may also include an independent citizen or journalist nominated by the Press Council of India if such a nomination is made. The Additional or Joint Chief Electoral Officer in charge of Media functions as the Member Secretary of the committee, managing its administrative operations. Like the lower committee, the State level body also includes an intermediary or social media expert chosen by the Chief Electoral Officer based on eligibility criteria. The State level MCMC is vested with two primary sets of functions to ensure the integrity of the electoral process. First, it is responsible for deciding on appeals filed against decisions made by both the District level committees and the Additional or Joint Chief Electoral Officer Committees regarding the certification of advertisements, acting in accordance with the Commission order dated 15th April 2004. Second, it examines all cases of paid news that come before it on appeal against the decisions of the District MCMC. Additionally, the State level committee has the power to take up cases suo-motu, meaning on its own motion without waiting for an appeal, and in such instances, it shall direct the concerned Returning Officers to issue formal notices to the candidates involved, thereby enforcing financial accountability and legal compliance across the entire electoral framework.

The procedural framework for handling appeals regarding the certification of political advertisements establishes a clear division of labor within the regulatory bodies responsible for election oversight. Specifically, the task of reviewing appeals related to advertisement certification is assigned to a designated subset of committee members, identified in the governing directives as those falling under categories a, b, c, and g. These individuals are tasked with the nuanced review of content approval disputes to ensure consistency and adherence to legal standards. In contrast, matters pertaining to Paid News, which refers to the unethical practice where media outlets publish or broadcast news items that favor a particular political candidate or party in exchange for monetary compensation or other benefits without disclosing the financial transaction, are reserved for the full assembly of the State Media Certification and Monitoring Committee, commonly abbreviated as State MCMC. This distinction ensures that the more complex and potentially scandalous issues of covert paid propaganda receive the collective scrutiny of the entire committee rather than a sub-group. Furthermore, a specific appellate mechanism exists for decisions made at the state level concerning Paid News. If any stakeholder wishes to challenge a ruling issued by the State level MCMC regarding a Paid News case, the appeal must be directed to the Election Commission of India, which serves as the supreme authority in electoral matters within the country. Conversely, if complaints regarding Paid News are submitted directly to the Election Commission without first passing through lower channels, the Commission does not adjudicate them immediately but instead forwards these cases to the relevant State level MCMC for initial consideration and fact-finding, thereby maintaining the hierarchical integrity of the grievance redressal system.

The selection and qualification criteria for the Social Media Expert, a critical role within both District and State level MCMCs, are rigorously defined to ensure technical competence and administrative reliability. The guidelines stipulate that the intermediary expert or social media expert appointed to these committees should preferably be a serving government officer, subject to meeting specific eligibility requirements designed to guarantee expertise and rank. In the scenario where the candidate is a government officer, the individual must hold a rank not below that of a Sub-Divisional Magistrate, often referred to as an SDM, which is a senior administrative position in the Indian bureaucracy responsible for law and order and revenue administration at the sub-district level. Additionally, such an officer must possess a minimum of five years of practical experience working specifically within the Information Technology department, cell, or social media cell of the government, ensuring they have hands-on familiarity with digital governance tools. Alternatively, if the appointed expert is not a government officer and is instead a private individual from the civilian sector, the eligibility bar is raised to compensate for the lack of inherent bureaucratic accountability. Such a private individual must hold a master's degree in the field of Information Technology or a closely related discipline. Beyond academic credentials, they are required to demonstrate at least ten years of professional experience working within the IT department, cell, or social media cell of either the central or state government. This extensive tenure requirement ensures that even private experts have a deep, long-term understanding of government operations, social media platform mechanics, and the digital ecosystem. Crucially, regardless of whether the expert is a government officer or a private citizen, they must be vetted for their background and neutrality, ensuring they have no conflicts of interest or partisan biases that could compromise the fairness of the election monitoring process.

The functional responsibilities of the Social Media Expert are comprehensive and integral to the smooth operation of the MCMC in the digital age. This expert is mandated to assist the committee in the critical task of certifying political advertisements that candidates and parties propose to publish on various social media platforms, acting as a technical advisor to determine compliance with election laws. Their duties extend to proactively scanning social media platforms to identify suspected cases of Paid News, utilizing their specialized knowledge to detect subtle forms of undisclosed promotional content that might evade general observation. A key administrative function involves assisting the MCMC in compiling and submitting a daily report to the Accounting team, with copies simultaneously sent to the Returning Officer, known as the RO, and the Expenditure Observer. This report details the expenditure incurred by candidates on election advertisements across social media platforms, ensuring financial transparency and adherence to spending limits. The expert also serves as the primary point of contact for addressing queries and complaints related to advertisements on social media, providing technical clarification and resolution pathways. Furthermore, they are responsible for managing the overall coordination between the MCMC and digital intermediaries or social media platforms, facilitating communication and enforcement actions. It is the duty of the Social Media Expert to ensure that all Election Laws are strictly adhered to in matters concerning digital advertising, and they play a pivotal role in assisting the MCMC in handling violation cases that arise on social media. As a member of the State level MCMC, the expert contributes to deciding appeals originating from the District level or the Additional and Joint Chief Electoral Officer Committees regarding advertisement certification. They are also tasked with examining all Paid News cases that come up on appeal against District MCMC decisions or cases that the State committee chooses to investigate suo-motu, meaning on their own motion without a formal complaint.

The scope of pre-certification for political advertisements has been significantly expanded to cover a wide array of media formats beyond traditional broadcasting. While political advertisements telecast on television and cable networks or cable channels have long been subject to scrutiny, subsequent instructions from the Election Commission have brought additional mediums under the ambit of mandatory pre-certification. These newly included formats encompass advertisements displayed in cinema halls, audio content aired on radio including private Frequency Modulation or FM channels, audio-visual displays situated in public places, advertisements published in electronic newspapers or e-newspapers, and the use of bulk Short Message Service or SMS and voice messages. Since social media platforms and internet websites are legally and technically defined as forms of electronic media, the Commission's directives for pre-certification explicitly apply to these digital domains as well. Consequently, any political advertisement proposed for dissemination across all forms of electronic media, including dynamic audio-visual displays in public spaces, requires prior certification from the concerned committee. However, it is important to distinguish that this pre-certification mandate does not apply to static physical media such as flex hoardings, wall paintings or wallpapers, and printed pamphlets. Despite being exempt from the pre-certification process, these static materials must still strictly adhere to the provisions of the Model Code of Conduct and other relevant election-related laws and guidelines. These regulatory directions are applicable throughout the entire territory of India and are not restricted solely to the period commencing from the official announcement of an election until the completion of the election process. This implies that the pre-certification of political advertisements is a year-round activity, functioning continuously regardless of whether an election is imminent. Therefore, political advertisements intended for television, cable networks, cinema halls, public audio-visual displays, e-newspapers, social media, and internet websites must be certified by the appropriate committee during the entire calendar year, not just during election seasons. In contrast, for specific mediums such as radio including private FM channels, bulk SMS, and voice messages, the instructions regarding pre-certification are applicable only during the designated election period.

Beyond its primary function of pre-certifying advertisements, the Media Certification and Monitoring Committee bears the responsibility of examining complaints and cases involving Paid News published in both print and electronic media. The committee is also tasked with scanning all media channels during the election period to enforce media-related regulations established under the Representation of the People Act, a foundational statute governing the conduct of elections in India. To ensure effective implementation of these duties at the grassroots level, it is imperative that Returning Officer Level Teams are properly constituted and function efficiently, acting as the operational arm of the broader monitoring framework. The MCMC possesses the discretionary authority to refuse certification for any advertisement it deems unfit for telecast, broadcast, or publication. This power allows the committee to act as a gatekeeper, preventing the dissemination of content that violates legal norms, contains misinformation, or otherwise undermines the integrity of the electoral process. By integrating technical expertise through the Social Media Expert role and maintaining a robust appellate structure, the regulatory framework aims to create a transparent, fair, and legally compliant media environment for political campaigning in India.

The electoral framework in India establishes a clear and hierarchical pathway for addressing grievances related to the certification of election materials, ensuring that decisions are subject to appropriate scrutiny while maintaining the integrity of the process. An appeal can be formally lodged against the determinations made by both the District level committee of the Media Certification and Monitoring Committee, often abbreviated as MCMC, and the State level certification committee, which operates at the additional or joint Chief Executive Officer level. These appeals are directed to the State level MCMC, an authority headed by the Chief Executive Officer of the respective state, who serves as the primary appellate body for such administrative decisions within the state jurisdiction. However, the legal structure places a definitive limit on this administrative review process to ensure finality and judicial oversight at the highest level. Specifically, only the Honorable Supreme Court of India possesses the jurisdiction to entertain any further appeals against the orders issued by the State level MCMC concerning pre-certification matters. This exclusivity underscores the gravity of pre-certification disputes and reserves the ultimate interpretative authority for the nation's highest court. Furthermore, during the critical period of elections, it is imperative that all candidates and political parties seeking certification for their advertisements are explicitly informed that their content must strictly adhere to the provisions of the Model Code of Conduct. This code acts as a set of guidelines issued by the Election Commission to regulate the behavior of political parties and candidates, ensuring free and fair elections by preventing the misuse of official position and maintaining a level playing field, thereby integrating ethical standards directly into the advertising approval workflow.

A fundamental logistical component of conducting free and fair elections involves the meticulous preparation and distribution of working copies of the electoral rolls, which serve as the master list of eligible voters for each polling station. For every single polling station established, exactly four working copies of the electoral roll must be prepared, regardless of whether the election is a standalone event or part of simultaneous elections involving multiple bodies. This fixed number ensures consistency and prevents confusion or resource wastage. The distribution of these four copies follows a specific operational logic designed to cover all essential functions at the polling booth. The first copy is assigned to the First Polling Officer, whose primary responsibility is the identification of electors as they arrive to cast their votes; this officer uses the list to verify voter identity against the registered entries. The second copy is designated for the Presiding Officer, who holds overall charge of the polling station and requires independent access to the voter list to supervise the process and resolve disputes. The third copy is intended for circulation among polling agents, who represent the various candidates and political parties, or for public exhibition, allowing stakeholders to observe the voting process transparently and verify that only eligible voters are participating. The fourth copy is maintained as a reserve at the polling station, serving as a backup in case any of the other copies are damaged, lost, or become illegible during the long hours of polling. It is crucial to note that even in the complex scenario of simultaneous elections, where voters may be casting ballots for different constituencies or levels of government on the same day, the requirement remains strictly limited to these four copies per polling station to streamline operations.

Beyond the standard working copies, additional safeguards are implemented to prevent fraudulent voting practices, particularly those involving individuals who are no longer eligible or present in the constituency. After the distribution of Voter Information Slips to the registered voters, the Booth Level Officers, known as BLOs, are tasked with preparing a specialized document called the ASD list. This acronym stands for Absentees, Shifted, and Dead, representing three categories of electors whose names might still appear on the main electoral roll but who are effectively ineligible to vote at that specific location. Absentees refer to voters who have moved away temporarily or permanently without updating their registration, Shifted denotes those who have relocated to a different constituency, and Dead refers to deceased individuals whose names have not yet been removed from the rolls due to administrative delays. This ASD list must be furnished to the Presiding Officer alongside the main voters list to act as a critical filter against bogus voting, which is the act of casting votes in the name of non-existent or ineligible persons. To further fortify the system against impersonation, where an individual pretends to be someone else to vote, a rigorous set of special measures is mandated for any elector whose name appears on this ASD list but who nonetheless presents themselves at the polling station to vote.

The procedure for handling voters found on the ASD list is detailed and multi-layered to ensure absolute verification of identity. First, the list of ASD voters must be prepared specifically for each polling station, ensuring that every Presiding Officer receives a separate and distinct list of Absentee, Shifted, and Dead electors relevant only to their specific booth. On the day of the poll, if any elector whose name appears on this sensitive list arrives to vote, they are subjected to heightened scrutiny. Such an individual is required to produce their EPIC, which stands for Electors Photo Identity Card, as proof of their identity. If the EPIC is unavailable, they must present any one of the alternative photo identity documents that have been permitted by the Election Commission, such as a passport, driving license, or PAN card. The Presiding Officer is personally responsible for verifying this identification document to ensure it matches the person standing before them. Once verified, the details of this interaction must be properly registered by the concerned Polling Officer in the official register of voters, known as Form 17A, which serves as the permanent legal record of who voted at the station. Following this verification, the First Polling Officer has the duty to inform the polling agents present at the booth about the arrival of an ASD elector by reading out the individual's name loudly. This public announcement ensures transparency and allows the agents representing different candidates to witness and potentially challenge the verification if they suspect foul play.

The documentation requirements for ASD electors are even more stringent than for regular voters to create an indelible record of their participation. In addition to signing their name in the "signature" column of the Register of Voters, Form 17A, these electors must also provide a thumb impression. This requirement for a thumb impression is mandatory and applies in addition to the signature, even in cases where the elector is fully literate and capable of signing their name normally. This dual method of biometric and written identification serves as a robust deterrent against future disputes regarding whether the correct person voted. Furthermore, a formal declaration must be obtained from these ASD electors using a specific format provided by the Commission. In this declaration, the elector solemnly affirms under oath that they are indeed the same person whose name appears at a specific serial number and part number of the existing electoral roll for their constituency, noting the qualifying date used for the roll's preparation, such as the first day of January, April, July, or October. Crucially, the declaration includes a statement acknowledging that the elector is aware that impersonation at elections constitutes a serious electoral offence punishable under Section 171D of the Indian Penal Code, which deals specifically with the crime of personation at an election. This legal warning serves to impress upon the voter the severity of lying about their identity. The form is completed with the thumb impression of the elector and their name, followed by a certification section where the Presiding Officer signs to confirm that the declaration was made and subscribed by the named elector in their presence, recording the number and name of the polling station and the date.

To ensure accountability and facilitate post-election scrutiny, the Presiding Officer is required to maintain a comprehensive record of all such incidents. At the conclusion of the polling process, the Presiding Officer must issue a certificate stating exactly how many electors from the ASD list were allowed to vote after undergoing proper scrutiny. This certificate is to be kept securely with Form 17A, ensuring that election officials and courts can review these specific cases during any subsequent election petitions or audits. In polling stations where videography or photography is being conducted as part of enhanced monitoring measures, these specific electors from the ASD list must be photographed, and their visual records must be preserved alongside their paperwork. This visual evidence provides an additional layer of security and proof of identity. Furthermore, Micro Observers, who are independent officials deployed to monitor the conduct of polls at a granular level, have a specific mandate to ensure that these instructions regarding Absentee, Shifted, and Dead electors are meticulously followed. They are required to make a specific mention of their observations regarding these procedures in their official reports, thereby creating an external check on the Presiding Officer's actions. Finally, to guarantee that all staff are fully aware of these complex protocols, the Presiding Officer must be thoroughly briefed in accordance with the Election Commission's latest instructions. This briefing must specially cover the procedures to be followed for electors appearing on the ASD list, ensuring that there is no ambiguity or deviation from the prescribed safety measures at the polling stations.

The effectiveness of all these procedural safeguards ultimately relies on the competence and preparedness of the human resources managing the election, necessitating a rigorous approach to the training of polling staff. It is expected that the staff assigned to polling duties have already undergone initial training during earlier polling rehearsals, which serve as dry runs for the actual election day. However, given the high stakes and the potential for technical issues, it is essential to brush up their knowledge and practical experience through additional rehearsals as necessary. These refresher sessions should place a special emphasis on the operation of Electronic Voting Machines, commonly known as EVMs, and the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail systems, or VVPATs, which are critical technologies used to record and verify votes electronically while providing a paper slip for voter confirmation. All Presiding Officers and Polling Officers must be given hands-on training if they have not received it previously, ensuring they are physically comfortable with the equipment and can troubleshoot minor issues without disrupting the flow of voting. To foster trust and transparency, candidates should be invited to attend these rehearsals, and they should be encouraged to secure the attendance of their prospective polling agents. These joint sessions provide a valuable opportunity to explain the vital points that polling agents need to remember during the actual poll, such as how to observe the sealing of machines, how to monitor the voting process, and what to do in case of objections. Additionally, a specific prohibition is placed on the conduct of Mock Polls using EVMs and VVPATs at Distribution Centres after the receipt of polling materials. Mock Polls are test runs performed to demonstrate that the machines are functioning correctly and have zero memory of previous tests, but conducting them at distribution centers after materials have been issued could compromise the security seal or the chain of custody. Therefore, staff must be explicitly instructed to avoid this practice at those locations, reserving mock polls for the appropriate time and place, typically at the polling station before the actual voting commences, to maintain the sanctity and security of the electoral equipment.

To ensure the integrity, efficiency, and smooth operation of the electoral process, it is imperative that comprehensive, intensive, and hands-on training be provided to every individual involved in polling and counting duties. This includes all personnel responsible for the preparation, operation, and post-poll sealing of Electronic Voting Machines, commonly known as EVMs, and Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail units, referred to as VVPATs. The training must cover every aspect of handling these devices, from their initial setup before voting begins to their secure sealing after the conclusion of both polling and counting activities. Officers who are tasked with preparing these machines must, upon completion of their work, issue a formal certificate attesting that they have configured the equipment strictly in accordance with the official instructions issued by the election authorities. Furthermore, this certificate must include a declaration that the officer has thoroughly inspected the machines and fully understands that they will be held personally accountable and responsible for any lapses or errors discovered in the functioning of the devices later on. This measure ensures a high degree of accountability and diligence among the technical staff managing the voting infrastructure.

During the training sessions, specific and detailed instructions must be conveyed to the polling personnel regarding the correct application of indelible ink. Indelible ink is a special semi-permanent ink used to mark the fingers of voters to prevent multiple voting, and it is crucial that it is applied properly to maintain the sanctity of the vote. All Presiding Officers, who manage the polling stations, and Sector Magistrates, who oversee broader areas, may be required to sign a certificate confirming that they have received adequate training in the operation of EVMs and VVPATs. This certification serves as a formal acknowledgment that they are confident in their ability to operate these machines during the actual election and that they are fully aware of the consequences of failure, including being held responsible for any operational lapses that occur under their supervision. Additionally, all Presiding Officers and Polling Officers must be explicitly instructed to verify that every elector, at the time of casting their vote, provides either their full signature or their thumb impression on the Register of Electors, which is officially designated as Form 17A. It is a strict rule that under no circumstances should an elector who possesses a left thumb be permitted to use any other finger to provide their mark on this register; the left thumb is the mandatory standard to ensure uniformity and prevent fraud.

The Election Commission has issued specific directives stating that police officers and other police personnel deployed for election-related duties must also undergo specialized training. It is essential to ensure that these instructions are strictly complied with and that appropriate training programs are conducted for all law enforcement staff involved in maintaining order and security during the elections. Beyond the polling staff and police, training must also be imparted to Zonal Officers and Micro-Observers to enable them to discharge their supervisory and monitoring duties effectively. The training of polling parties on EVM and VVPAT systems is a critical component of election readiness. Presiding Officers and Sector Magistrates must approach this training with the utmost seriousness to guarantee correct operation on polling day. Similarly, Polling Officers must be vigilant in applying indelible ink on the left finger of each elector exactly as prescribed by the Commission, ensuring that the mark is visible and durable enough to serve its purpose throughout the voting hours.

Regarding educational materials for candidates, there is no objection to a candidate printing a dummy ballot paper that features their own name and election symbol to indicate the position where these will appear on the actual ballot paper used in the election. However, such dummy documents must not contain the names or symbols of any other contesting candidates within the same constituency to avoid confusion or misrepresentation. These dummy ballot papers may be printed in various colors such as brown, yellow, or grey, but they are strictly prohibited from being printed in pink or white, and they must not resemble the genuine ballot paper in either size or color. This distinction is vital to prevent voters from mistaking sample materials for official voting documents. Furthermore, candidates and political parties are permitted to prepare dummy Ballot Units for the purpose of educating voters about how to cast their votes. These mock units can be constructed from materials like wood, plastic, or plywood boxes and should be approximately half the size of the official Ballot Units. They may be painted in brown, yellow, or grey to distinguish them from real machines. These educational tools can include provisions to display the serial number, name, and symbol of the candidate, similar to the dummy ballot paper, and may even feature a battery-operated button and a lamp that lights up when pressed to simulate the voting experience without actually recording a vote.

In terms of physical infrastructure near polling stations, specific regulations govern the establishment of candidates' election booths. Only one table and two chairs are permitted at each such booth, along with an umbrella or a small tent measuring no more than ten feet by ten feet to provide protection from weather conditions. These booths must be located outside the two-hundred-meter periphery of the polling station premises to maintain a neutral zone around the voting area. Each booth is allowed to display one party flag and one banner featuring party symbols or photographs. The size of the banner used in these offices must not exceed four feet by eight feet; however, if local laws prescribe a smaller maximum size for banners or hoardings, then the lower size mandated by local law shall prevail. The setting up of such booths is also subject to obtaining valid permission from the relevant local authority. Political parties and candidates are authorized to issue unofficial identity slips to voters to assist them in locating their details on the electoral roll. These slips must contain only specific information: the name and serial number of the voter in the electoral roll, the part number of the electoral roll, and the serial number and name of the polling station.

The design and content of these identity slips are strictly regulated to prevent them from becoming tools for campaigning. The slips must be printed on white paper and are forbidden from containing the name of the candidate, the name of their political party, or their election symbol. Furthermore, the slips must not include any slogans or any exhortation urging voters to vote for a specific party or candidate. Including such content would constitute canvassing within the polling station environment, which is legally impermissible. The circulation of any slip containing slogans or exhortations within two hundred meters of the polling station is also considered canvassing and is prohibited under election law. The acceptable content for slips issued by candidates is limited to factual logistical details, specifically the name of the Parliamentary Constituency or Assembly Constituency, the polling station number and name, and location details to help the voter find the correct venue. By adhering to these rigorous standards for training, equipment handling, dummy materials, and booth management, the election machinery ensures a fair, transparent, and orderly democratic process where every vote is cast securely and every participant understands their responsibilities.

The Voter Information Slip serves as a critical administrative document within the electoral framework, designed specifically to assist registered voters in locating their enrollment details and identifying their designated polling station. This slip contains essential data points including the voter's serial number within the specific electoral roll, the part number of the roll where their name appears, and the full name of the voter. The primary function of this document is logistical rather than identificatory; it is distributed by the District Administration to ensure that every eligible citizen knows exactly where they are enrolled to vote and what their unique sequence number is within that specific location's registry. It is imperative to understand that while this slip provides necessary directional information, it does not serve as a standalone proof of identity. A voter cannot use the Voter Information Slip by itself to cast a ballot at the polling booth; instead, it must be presented alongside other government-issued photo identification documents mandated by election laws. To maintain linguistic consistency and accessibility for the local populace, the Election Commission has issued strict instructions that these slips must be printed only in the languages in which the official electoral roll for that particular assembly constituency is published. This ensures that voters can read and understand their enrollment details in their native or preferred regional language, thereby reducing confusion and enhancing the inclusivity of the electoral process.

The physical production and distribution of the Voter Information Slip are governed by rigorous standards to ensure durability, clarity, and security. The slips must be printed on high-quality paper to prevent tearing or degradation during handling, and the dimensions are standardized to half the size of an A4 sheet, measuring approximately eight inches by six inches. This specific size is chosen to make the document compact enough for easy storage by voters while remaining large enough to display clear, legible entries. The responsibility for overseeing the printing process lies with the District Election Officer and the Electoral Registration Officer, who must implement all possible safeguards to prevent fraud, counterfeiting, or unauthorized alterations. Only a single set of these slips is permitted to be printed for the registered electors of each Assembly Constituency, and this sole edition must be duly authenticated by the Electoral Registration Officer before being handed over to Booth Level Officers for distribution. The inclusion of a Quick Response code, commonly known as a QR code, on the slip represents a significant technological advancement in election management. This two-dimensional barcode contains approved information identical to what is printed on the slip and facilitates rapid searching and matching of elector details when scanned using the official Booth Application or the Voter Helpline App. This digital integration allows poll officials to verify voter details quickly and accurately, streamlining the check-in process at polling stations while maintaining the integrity of the data.

The distribution mechanism for the Voter Information Slip is a highly structured operation involving multiple layers of accountability and verification. The process begins with the preparation of a pre-printed register of voters, which is provided to each Booth Level Officer along with the corresponding slips for the electors within their assigned polling station area. The Returning Officer is tasked with creating a detailed schedule for this distribution, and a copy of this timeline must be shared with political parties, their appointed Booth Level Agents, and contesting candidates or their representatives, all of whom must acknowledge receipt of this schedule. This transparency ensures that all stakeholders are aware of when and how the slips will be delivered, minimizing suspicions of bias or irregularity. The actual handover of the slip is restricted to specific recipients; the Booth Level Officer must deliver the document either directly to the registered voter or to an adult member of the voter's family who is also a registered voter. Bulk distribution methods, such as leaving stacks of slips at public places or handing them out indiscriminately, are strictly prohibited to prevent misuse or loss. Furthermore, the distribution window is tightly regulated, commencing on the last day of nomination filing and concluding at least five clear days before the actual date of the poll. This timeline ensures that voters have adequate time to locate their polling stations while preventing last-minute chaos or manipulation.

Accountability during the distribution phase is enforced through a mandatory acknowledgment system. When a Booth Level Officer delivers a Voter Information Slip, they are required to obtain either the signature or the thumb impression of the recipient as formal proof of delivery. This record-keeping measure creates an audit trail that verifies which voters have received their slips and protects against claims of non-delivery. Once the distribution period concludes, the Booth Level Officer must deposit the updated register, containing all collected acknowledgments, with the Electoral Registration Officer three days prior to the polling day. Any slips that remain undistributed after the cutoff date must be immediately returned by the Booth Level Officer to the Returning Officer or Assistant Returning Officer of the respective Assembly Constituency. These undistributed documents are then placed in sealed covers and kept under safe custody to prevent unauthorized access. The Returning Officer is further obligated to compile an alphabetical list of all undistributed slips for each part or polling station, ensuring a comprehensive record of missing deliveries. Crucially, once these slips are returned to the Returning Officer, no further distribution is permitted under any circumstances, closing the loop on the logistical chain and securing the materials against potential electoral malpractice.

Legal consequences for the mishandling of Voter Information Slips are severe, reflecting the document's status as sensitive election material. Any unauthorized possession, distribution, or tampering with these slips is considered a direct violation of the Representation of the People Act of 1951 and relevant sections of the Indian Penal Code. Individuals found guilty of such offenses face punishable penalties that may include imprisonment, substantial fines, or both, depending on the gravity of the violation. This strict legal framework acts as a deterrent against attempts to manipulate voter information or disrupt the electoral process. In addition to standard procedures, special provisions have been established to ensure the inclusive participation of Persons with Disabilities, particularly those with visual impairments or blindness. Aligned with the Strategy Framework for Accessible Election launched in 2018, the Election Commission mandates the issuance of Accessible Voter Information Slips equipped with Braille features. These specialized slips are provided to visually impaired voters in addition to the normal printed slips, enabling them to independently read and verify their enrollment details. This initiative underscores the commitment to wholesome constructive participation, ensuring that physical disabilities do not hinder a citizen's ability to engage actively and confidently in the democratic process. By integrating tactile reading capabilities into the voter information system, the administration removes barriers and empowers all sections of society to exercise their franchise with dignity and autonomy.

The regulations governing the use of motor vehicles for electioneering purposes are designed to ensure transparency, fairness, and order during the electoral process while preventing the misuse of resources to unduly influence voters. Under the current framework, there is no numerical cap on the total number of vehicles a candidate may utilize for campaigning activities prior to the actual day of voting. This flexibility allows candidates to reach voters across vast geographical areas effectively. However, this freedom is strictly conditioned upon a mandatory disclosure and authorization protocol that must be completed before any campaigning activities commence. A candidate is legally obligated to submit comprehensive details regarding every vehicle intended for campaign use, along with a precise specification of the geographical areas where these vehicles will operate. These submissions must be directed to the District Election Officer, who serves as the primary regulatory authority in the district, or to other subordinate officers who have been specifically authorized by the District Election Officer to handle such matters. Upon receiving these details, the authorized officer conducts a necessary scrutiny process to verify the legitimacy and compliance of the proposed vehicles. Only after this verification is complete will an official permit be issued. It is a critical requirement that the original physical copy of this permit, not a photocopy or digital reproduction, must be prominently displayed on the windscreen of the vehicle. The dimensions of this permit document are regulated to ensure it is large enough to be easily visible and legible from a considerable distance, allowing election officials and law enforcement to quickly identify authorized campaign vehicles during patrols. The content of the permit is also standardized and must explicitly include the registration number of the vehicle, the specific date on which the permit was issued, the full name of the candidate sponsoring the vehicle, and the designated area where the vehicle is authorized to conduct campaigning.

Candidates and their support teams are required to adhere strictly to the instructions issued by the Election Commission regarding the regulation of vehicular traffic throughout the election period, with particular emphasis on the day of the poll. These traffic regulations are implemented to cordon off polling areas from general vehicular traffic, a measure specifically intended to prevent candidates and their supporters from transporting voters to polling stations in arranged vehicles, which could constitute bribery or undue influence. Permits for vehicles used by candidates, their election agents, and other essential personnel are issued strictly according to the norms prescribed by the Commission. On the day of the poll, the rules become significantly more restrictive to maintain the sanctity of the voting process. For elections to the House of the People, also known as the Lok Sabha or the lower house of the national parliament, each contesting candidate is entitled to a specific allocation of vehicles. This allocation includes one vehicle for the personal use of the candidate throughout the entire parliamentary constituency. In addition to this, the candidate is permitted one vehicle for the exclusive use of their election agent within the same parliamentary constituency. Furthermore, recognizing the administrative complexity of larger constituencies, the rules allow for one additional vehicle for the use of the election agent, campaign workers, or party workers in each of the assembly segments that comprise the parliamentary constituency. This tiered approach ensures that the candidate's team can manage operations across different sub-regions without flooding the area with unregulated transport.

In the context of elections to the State Legislative Assembly, which governs individual states or union territories, the vehicle entitlements on the day of the poll are adjusted to fit the smaller scale of the constituency. Each contesting candidate in a state assembly election is entitled to one vehicle for their own personal use within that specific constituency. Additionally, they are granted one vehicle for the use of their election agent. Beyond these two, the candidate is allowed one further vehicle to be used by their campaign workers or party workers. The authority responsible for issuing these specific poll-day permits shifts from the District Election Officer to the Returning Officer, who is the official in charge of conducting the election for that particular constituency. Candidates must furnish the particulars of the vehicles they intend to use on poll day directly to the concerned Returning Officer and must ensure that the issued permits are displayed on the windscreens of these vehicles just as they are during the broader campaign period. A crucial aspect of these regulations is the principle of equality and non-discrimination based on status. No other vehicles are permitted to be used by leaders of political parties, including sitting Ministers, workers, agents, or sympathizers of any candidate. No exceptions are made to this rule irrespective of the high status, political rank, or influence of the candidate or their associates. This ensures that no individual gains an unfair advantage through the deployment of unauthorized fleets of vehicles on the sensitive day of voting.

The process for obtaining permits from the District Election Officer before the campaign begins requires the submission of detailed documentation. The candidate must provide specific data points including the full registration details of the vehicle, valid fitness certificates which certify that the vehicle is mechanically safe for road use, and the names and personal particulars of both the owners and the drivers of the vehicles. Alongside these technical and personal details, the candidate must clearly define the areas in which the vehicles will be deployed for campaigning purposes. Once the permit is issued, the original document must remain affixed to the windscreen, meeting the visibility dimension standards previously noted. These vehicles remain subject to random checks and verification by any competent authorities, including Flying Squads and Static Surveillance Teams, which are mobile and stationary units deployed by the election administration to monitor compliance and prevent malpractice. It is imperative to understand that vehicles can only be legally used for electioneering after obtaining the necessary permits through the proper channels involving the candidate or their election agent. To ensure financial accountability, the District Election Officer is required to convey the details of all vehicles used for campaigning by candidates to the Election Expenditure Observers. These observers are independent officials tasked with monitoring the financial transactions of candidates to ensure that the costs associated with hiring, fueling, and maintaining these vehicles are accurately recorded and included in the candidate's official expenditure account, thereby preventing hidden spending that exceeds legal limits.

Any vehicle found being used for campaigning without due authorization or a valid permit is immediately deemed unauthorized. The use of such a vehicle is considered a violation committed on behalf of the candidate and may attract penal provisions under Chapter IX A of the Indian Penal Code, which deals specifically with offences relating to elections. These offences can carry significant legal consequences, including fines and imprisonment. Consequently, any unauthorized vehicle identified during the campaign must be immediately taken out of the campaigning exercise to halt the violation. Conversely, vehicles that have been duly deployed for the election campaign in accordance with the intimation given by the candidates or their election agents to the District Administration are granted a degree of protection; they should not be requisitioned by the administration for government duties during the election period, ensuring that the campaign logistics are not disrupted by state demands. Finally, the regulations make a specific provision for recognized political parties. Upon the request of such a party, the District Election Officer shall issue a permit for one vehicle to be used by a district-level office bearer of that recognized political party. This provision applies to office bearers who are not designated as star campaigners, allowing them to visit multiple assembly constituencies within the district for electioneering purposes, thus facilitating coordinated party efforts while maintaining strict control over the number of vehicles in motion.

The regulatory framework governing election logistics mandates that any permit issued for campaign vehicles must contain specific, verifiable details to ensure transparency and accountability. This document must explicitly state the registration number of the vehicle, the full name of the political party utilizing it, the specific administrative district in which it operates, a detailed route map outlining the intended path of travel, and a day-by-day movement plan that tracks the vehicle's location over time. Furthermore, the permit must clearly indicate the exact duration or period for which it remains valid. To facilitate immediate visual verification by authorities on the ground, these permits are required to be printed in distinct colors, allowing police officers and other officials to quickly identify authorized vehicles from a distance. Procedural compliance dictates that an attested copy of this permit must be firmly affixed to the windshield of the vehicle where it is clearly visible, while the original document must remain in the possession of the driver at all times. This original copy serves as the primary proof of authorization and must be produced upon demand by the police, other competent government authorities, or specialized election monitoring teams such as Flying Squads and Static Surveillance Teams. From a financial perspective, strict accounting rules apply to the costs associated with obtaining and maintaining these permits. All expenditures related to these vehicles must be recorded against the general account of the political party itself, rather than being charged to the individual election expense accounts of specific candidates, thereby preventing the inflation of individual spending limits. It is explicitly clarified that these regulations are not limited to general elections but are equally applicable during bye-elections held in any specific constituency to fill vacant seats. The District Administration bears the responsibility of maintaining vigilant oversight to prevent the misuse of private vehicles by candidates, their appointed agents, political party leaders, or supporters. Such misuse encompasses a range of illicit activities, including the transportation of anti-social elements with the deliberate intent of instilling fear or intimidation among the electorate, as well as the smuggling of illegal arms and ammunition designed to create public disturbances or violence. If any vehicle, whether owned by a political party, a candidate, or a private individual, is discovered to be involved in such unlawful acts, it becomes the mandatory duty of the local administration to immediately impound the vehicle. Once seized, the vehicle shall not be released back to its owner until the entire election process has been fully concluded. In addition to the seizure of the vehicle, comprehensive legal action must be initiated in accordance with existing laws against the vehicle owner, any occupants found inside, and the candidate or political party implicated in orchestrating these illegal activities. During the critical phase of filing nominations, strict spatial restrictions are enforced to maintain order around election offices. A maximum of only three vehicles are permitted to enter the designated perimeter extending one hundred meters from the offices of the Returning Officer or Assistant Returning Officer. This restricted zone of one hundred meters must be clearly marked and demarcated by the Returning Officer or the District Election Officer to ensure that all participants are aware of the boundary.

To guarantee adherence to statutory requirements as outlined in Rule 31 of the Conduct of Election Rules established in 1961, and to provide enhanced facilitation and awareness for voters arriving at polling locations on the day of the vote, specific informational materials must be prominently displayed. Four distinct Voter Facilitation Posters are required to be positioned near the entrance of every polling station. These posters serve critical educational functions and include the first poster detailing specific Polling Station Details, the second listing all Candidates contesting in that area, the third outlining the essential Do's and Don'ts for voter behavior, and the fourth providing information on Approved Identification Documents along with step-by-step instructions on how to cast a vote. The visual presentation of these materials is strictly regulated; the color scheme, graphic design, and physical dimensions of each poster must align exactly with the latest instructions issued by the Election Commission of India to ensure uniformity and clarity across all polling locations. The broader operational manual dedicates a significant chapter to the various technical aspects of voting materials, specifically covering Postal Ballots, electronically transmitted Postal Ballots, standard ballot papers used in Electronic Voting Machines, and tendered ballot papers reserved for specific contingencies. Within the section on Postal Ballot Papers, there is a crucial emphasis on entitlement, requiring the Returning Officer of an Assembly Constituency or the Assistant Returning Officer of a Parliamentary Constituency to possess a clear and precise understanding of exactly which categories of electors are legally qualified to receive postal ballots. The classes of electors entitled to vote via postal ballot include Service Voters, excluding those who have chosen the proxy voting option known as Classified Service Voters, as well as other specific categories of electors granted this right, Special Voters, and the spouses of persons falling into the aforementioned service and special voter categories. Additionally, electors who are currently subjected to preventive detention, voters who are assigned to election duty on poll day, and any notified voters identified under Section 60(c) of the Representation of the People Act of 1951 are eligible. The framework also includes Absentee Voters employed in Essential Services, Absentee Voters who are Senior Citizens, Absentee Voters who are Persons with Disabilities, and Absentee Voters affected by COVID-19, subject to notifications by the Commission.

To provide further clarity on these classifications, a Service Voter is defined as any person described in clause (a) of Section 60 of the Representation of the People Act, 1951. This definition encompasses members of the armed forces of the Union, members of any force to which the provisions of the Army Act of 1950 have been applied whether with or without modifications, and members of an armed police force of a State who are deployed for service outside the boundaries of that specific State. It also includes individuals employed by the Government of India in posts located outside the country, such as Ambassadors of India stationed abroad and their supporting staff. The names of these individuals are registered in the final part of the electoral roll, and those Service Voters who have formally opted for proxy voting are designated as Classified Service Voters. A Special Voter refers to any individual holding a government office that has been officially declared by the President to be an office to which the provisions of sub-section (4) of Section 20 of the Representation of the People Act, 1950 apply, as well as the spouse of such a person, provided they have been registered as an elector based on a statement made under sub-section (5) of that same section. An Elector subjected to Preventive Detention is defined as any person who is currently being held under preventive detention orders issued under any law that is in force at the time. These detailed definitions ensure that election officials can accurately determine eligibility and distribute the correct voting materials to those citizens who are unable to physically visit a polling station due to their service obligations, health conditions, legal status, or employment requirements, thereby upholding the democratic right to vote for all segments of the population.

The concept of a voter on election duty encompasses a specific category of individuals who, despite being registered electors entitled to vote in their respective constituencies, are unable to cast their ballots at their designated polling stations because they are actively engaged in the administration and security of the election process itself. This definition broadly includes Polling Agents, who represent political parties at polling stations to ensure fairness; Polling Officers, who manage the logistical operations of voting; and Presiding Officers, who hold overall responsibility for the conduct of elections at a specific booth. Furthermore, this classification extends to security personnel deployed to maintain order and any other public servants officially appointed for election-related tasks within the constituency. Because these individuals are physically present at locations other than their home polling stations due to their official obligations, the legal framework provides them with alternative mechanisms to exercise their franchise. It is important to note that this designation also applies to observers appointed by the Election Commission as well as the staff members who accompany them during their monitoring duties, recognizing that their presence at various polling sites precludes them from voting at their own registered locations.

Distinct from those on active duty is the category known as notified electors, which refers to a class of voters specifically identified and declared by the Election Commission under the authority granted by clause c of Section 60 of the Representation of the People Act of 1951. This legislation serves as the foundational law governing elections in India, and pursuant to its provisions, the Commission has established criteria for voters who may cast their votes via postal ballot, a method allowing them to vote by mail rather than in person. The Commission has specified that this class includes electors who are above the age of eighty years, acknowledging the mobility challenges faced by the elderly, as well as electors with physical disabilities who are already marked or flagged as such in the official database of electoral rolls maintained by the State or Union Territory. Additionally, this provision covers individuals who are suspected of having or are confirmed to be affected by Covid-19, ensuring public health safety does not disenfranchise voters, and absentee voters who are employed in essential services and formally request to vote by postal ballot. These groups are permitted to vote by post in general elections or bye-elections within their respective States or Union Territories, in accordance with the rules outlined in Part-III A of the Conduct of Elections Rules 1961, as amended from time to time.

While the provisions for postal ballots are extensive, there are specific clarifications regarding eligibility for certain individuals residing abroad or associated with diplomatic missions. Domestic servants who accompany Diplomatic Officers from India to foreign postings are explicitly not entitled to the concession of voting by postal ballot. Similarly, the sons and daughters of Government of India employees who reside with their parents at the place of posting outside India are also excluded from this facility. However, the spouse of a service voter, which traditionally referred to military or government personnel stationed away from home, is fully entitled to this voting facility. It is crucial to highlight a significant legal update brought about by the Election Laws Amendment Act of 2021, dated December 29, 2021, which modernized the language of the statute by substituting the word wife with the gender-neutral term spouse, thereby ensuring that husbands of service voters are also included in this entitlement. Furthermore, the definition of voters on election duty has been pragmatically expanded to include support staff such as drivers, helpers, and cleaners who are actually employed on vehicles that have been requisitioned specifically for election purposes. These individuals, though not necessarily holding officer ranks, are integral to the election machinery and may be provided with the facility of voting by postal ballot due to their inability to reach their home polling stations.

The logistical planning for these special voting arrangements requires careful assessment by the Returning Officer, who is the senior official responsible for the conduct of elections in a constituency. This officer must accurately estimate the requirement for postal ballot papers by considering several key factors. First, they must account for the total number of polling personnel appointed for election duty, including all categories of officers and agents. Second, they must include the number of drivers, helpers, cleaners, and other vehicle staff who qualify as voters on election duty. Third, the assessment must incorporate the number of police personnel who will be deployed on election duty and consequently unable to vote at their regular polling stations. Finally, the Returning Officer must add a sufficient reserve quantity to this total to accommodate any last-minute changes or unforeseen circumstances, ensuring that no eligible voter is denied a ballot due to a shortage of materials. This meticulous calculation is vital to the integrity and smooth functioning of the electoral process.

Regarding the physical form and linguistic presentation of the ballot papers, there are distinct specifications for two primary types: postal ballot papers designed for Service Voters and those intended for other categories of electors entitled to vote by post. For Service Voters, the ballot paper must display the names of the candidates along with their party affiliations, printed in both the official language of the State and in English to ensure clarity and accessibility. A notable difference for this category is that the election symbol, which is the visual logo representing a political party, is not to be printed on the postal ballot for Service Voters. However, photographs of the candidates must be included to aid identification. Alongside the candidate's name, the name of the political party that has set up the candidate must be printed directly below it, and in the case of independent candidates who do not belong to any party, the word Independent must be clearly printed. The width of the ballot paper, when formatted in a single column, should range between four inches and six inches, depending on what is considered necessary for legibility and design.

In contrast, the postal ballot papers issued to other categories of electors, such as the elderly, disabled, or those on election duty who are not service voters, contain additional visual aids. For these voters, the election symbols allotted to each candidate are printed on the ballot paper in addition to the photographs of the candidates. The textual particulars on these ballots must also be printed in the official language of the State and in English, provided English is not already the sole official language. Unlike the ballots for Service Voters, the party affiliation is not required to be printed on the ballot paper for these categories, as the election symbol serves as the primary identifier of the political party. The dimensions remain consistent, with the width of a single-column ballot paper falling between four and six inches. On these ballots, the election symbol is strategically placed between the photograph of the candidate and the space allocated for the voter to mark their choice. Furthermore, for these categories of electors, the symbol representing the None of the Above option, as specified by the Commission, must be printed on the postal ballot paper in the column designated for election symbols, offering voters the choice to reject all candidates. Despite these differences in layout and content, a common requirement for both types of postal ballot papers is the mandatory inclusion of photographs of all candidates, ensuring that visual identification remains a standard feature across all postal voting materials.

The physical design and layout of election ballot papers are governed by strict regulatory standards to ensure clarity, uniformity, and the integrity of the voting process. A fundamental requirement involves the inclusion of candidate photographs on the ballot panel. Specifically, a photograph of each candidate must be printed within the designated panel that contains the candidate's name. This image is positioned on the right-hand side of the candidate's name, situated precisely between the textual name and the election symbol assigned to that candidate, or alternatively, between the name and the column reserved for marking a vote or preference, depending on the specific format of the ballot. To maintain consistency across all voting materials, the dimensions of these photographs are rigidly defined; every photograph printed on the ballot paper must measure exactly two centimeters in breadth and two point five centimeters in height. These specifications ensure that voters can easily identify candidates visually while maintaining a standardized appearance for all official election documents.

In accordance with sub-rule one of Rule twenty-two of the Conduct of Elections Rules established in nineteen sixty-one, the Election Commission has issued specific directives regarding the preparation of postal ballot papers. These special ballots are designed for voters who are deployed on election duty during elections for the House of the People, which is the lower house of the national parliament, or for the Legislative Assembly of any state within the country. A critical structural feature of every postal ballot paper is the attachment of a counterfoil. This counterfoil serves as a stub or receipt portion that remains attached to the main ballot until it is processed. It is located at the very top of the ballot paper, and its vertical depth is regulated such that it ordinarily does not exceed fifteen centimeters. The information printed on this counterfoil is strictly limited to the English language to ensure uniform understanding by election officials regardless of the local linguistic context. The counterfoil contains several specific elements arranged in a precise order. At the very top, there must be a dedicated space allocated for stitching, which allows multiple ballot papers to be bound together securely. Immediately below this stitching area, a solid black border with a width of one centimeter runs across the top of the counterfoil. Directly beneath this black border, the specific particulars of the election are printed, providing essential context about the poll being conducted.

Further details on the counterfoil include the text stating Electoral Roll Part Number and Serial Number of Elector. These words are printed one below the other and can be positioned on either the left-hand side or the right-hand side of the counterfoil, whichever is deemed more convenient for the printing layout. Additionally, the unique serial number of the ballot paper itself must be printed on the left-hand side, appearing either on the front or the back of the counterfoil as determined by the printing authorities. A crucial security and separation feature is a block of lines measuring one centimeter in height, which includes a perforated rule or a series of disjointed straight lines. This feature acts as a tear-off line, physically separating the ballot paper from the counterfoil once the vote has been cast and verified. On the main body of the ballot paper, immediately below this perforated separation line, there is another block of lines also measuring one centimeter in height. Regarding the overall dimensions, when a postal ballot paper is printed in a single column format along with its counterfoil, the total width must fall between four inches and six inches. The exact width within this range is decided by the Chief Electoral Officer, who determines the most convenient size for efficient printing and handling.

The layout of the candidate list on the ballot paper follows specific rules to accommodate varying numbers of contestants. At the bottom of the list of candidates, there is always a dedicated panel containing the words none of the above, allowing voters to reject all contesting candidates if they choose. However, when the number of contesting candidates exceeds eight, the format of the postal ballot paper changes significantly to accommodate the larger list. In such scenarios, the ballot paper is printed in two or more columns, with the number of columns dependent on the total count of candidates. As a general guideline, a single column should not contain more than nine entries, which includes the candidates plus the none of the above option. Under no circumstances should a single column exceed fifteen entries. When multiple columns are used, the total width of the ballot paper and its counterfoil adjusts based on the number of columns required. To visually distinguish between these columns, each one is separated from the adjacent column by a shaded vertical area that is half an inch wide and extends from the top to the bottom of the candidate list.

The ordering of candidates on the multi-column ballot paper is strictly sequential and derived from Form 7A, which is the official list of contesting candidates. The names and election symbols appear in the exact same order as they are listed in Form 7A, although the specific headings categorizing candidates into three groups found in that form are omitted from the ballot paper itself. The names are printed vertically down the columns. The sequence begins with the candidate listed at serial number one in Form 7A, who appears as the first entry in the first column. If each column is designed to hold nine names, then the candidate listed at serial number ten in Form 7A becomes the first candidate in the second column, and this pattern continues for subsequent columns. The placement of the none of the above panel is also calculated based on this sequence. For instance, if there are ten contesting candidates and each column holds nine names, the none of the above panel would appear as the eleventh item, placing it as the second entry in the second column. In cases where the total number of entries, including candidates and the none of the above option, is not perfectly divisible by the number of slots per column, the remaining empty panels on the rightmost side of the ballot paper are completely shaded to indicate that they are void and not to be used. It is also explicitly stated that no election symbols are to be printed on postal ballot papers, distinguishing them from standard electronic or physical booth ballots.

Finally, the header section on the front face of the ballot paper contains vital identification details regarding the election and the constituency. Printed at the very top, these particulars include the serial number and the official name of the Parliamentary Constituency or the Assembly Constituency, as applicable. These names correspond exactly to those listed in the Delimitation of the Parliamentary and Assembly Constituencies Order, which is the legal document defining electoral boundaries. There is a specific provision for States or Union Territories that constitute an entire Parliamentary Constituency on their own.
