In such unique cases, since their names do not appear as separate entries in the Delimitation Order, the name of the State or Union Territory itself is used as the name of the Parliamentary Constituency on the ballot paper. This ensures that every voter, regardless of their location or the specific administrative structure of their region, receives a ballot paper that accurately reflects their specific electoral district and the legal framework governing their representation.

The physical design and printed content of a postal ballot paper are governed by strict regulations to ensure clarity, security, and the correct identification of the specific election for which the vote is being cast. A critical component of this identification process involves the printing of the year in which the election is taking place. Immediately following this year, a specific legend or abbreviation must be printed to distinguish the nature of the electoral event. If the ballot paper is intended for use in a general election, which is a regularly scheduled election to fill all or most seats in a legislative body, the abbreviation Genl will be printed after the year. Conversely, if the ballot paper is designed for a bye-election, which is a special election held to fill a vacant seat that has become empty between general elections due to resignation, death, or disqualification of a member, the abbreviation Bye will be printed instead. This distinction is vital for administrative tracking and ensures that votes are counted within the correct electoral cycle. Furthermore, to explicitly identify the document as a mechanism for remote voting rather than in-person polling, the words Postal Ballot Paper must be endorsed or printed directly below the entry containing the year and the election type legend.

To provide concrete examples of how these formatting rules are applied in practice, one can examine specific illustrations for different types of elections and legislative bodies. For a general election to the House of the People, which refers to the lower house of the national parliament, the postal ballot paper is printed on white paper. The text on such a document would read with the constituency number and name, such as 8-Rampur PC, followed by the year and the general election indicator, for instance, slash 2004 Genl, and finally the endorsement Postal Ballot Paper. In the scenario of a bye-election for the same House of the People, the format remains similar but the indicator changes; a ballot for the Rampur constituency in 2005 would display 8-Rampur PC followed by slash 2005 Bye and the standard Postal Ballot Paper endorsement. When the election concerns the Legislative Assembly, which is the state-level legislative body, the color of the paper changes to pink to visually differentiate it from parliamentary elections. A general election ballot for the Almora Assembly Constituency in 2004 would therefore appear on pink paper with the text 11-Almora AC followed by slash 2004 Genl and the postal ballot endorsement. Similarly, a bye-election for the same assembly constituency in 2005 would feature the text 11-Almora AC followed by slash 2005 Bye on pink paper. These visual and textual cues serve as immediate identifiers for election officials and voters alike.

The layout of the candidates on the ballot paper follows a structured panel system designed to prevent confusion and ensure equal visibility for all contesting individuals. The name of each candidate, along with their assigned election symbol which serves as a visual identifier for illiterate or semi-literate voters, shall be inscribed within a distinct rectangular area known as a panel. To ensure clear separation between competing candidates and to prevent accidental marking across boundaries, these panels are separated from one another by a shaded area that measures exactly three centimeters in width. This neutral space acts as a buffer zone. At the very bottom of the list of candidates, there is a mandatory thick black border measuring one centimeter in width, which serves as a visual terminator for the candidate list section. Immediately following the panel that contains the name and particulars of the last candidate on the ballot, there must be an additional panel dedicated to the None of the Above option. This provision allows electors who do not wish to support any of the contesting candidates to formally exercise their right to reject all options, thereby registering a dissenting vote without abstaining from the election process entirely. The words None of the Above must be written in the same language or languages that are used for printing the names of the candidates, ensuring linguistic consistency and comprehension for the voter. The physical dimensions of this None of the Above panel must be identical to the size of the panels used for the actual candidates, granting it equal prominence on the ballot. In cases involving postal ballot papers for specific categories of electors entitled to vote by post, the official election symbol designated by the Election Commission for the None of the Above option must be printed on the ballot paper in the column specifically reserved for election symbols, aligning it with the textual option.

Language requirements for postal ballot papers vary depending on the category of the voter, particularly distinguishing between service electors and other groups. Service electors typically include members of the armed forces, police forces serving outside their home state, and government employees posted abroad. For these individuals, the names of the contesting candidates and their party affiliation, if they belong to a recognized political party, must be printed in the official language of the State or Union Territory concerned. Additionally, if English is not the official language of that specific State or Union Territory, the information must also be printed in English to ensure broad comprehensibility. In instances where the ballot paper features text in multiple languages, the particulars written in the official local language must appear first, positioned above or before the English translation. However, despite the multilingual presentation of candidate details, the name of the constituency itself must be printed in English only to maintain a standardized geographic reference across the nation. Furthermore, the counterfoil, which is the detachable stub attached to the ballot paper used for record-keeping and verification purposes, must have all its particulars printed in English only. For other categories of electors who are entitled to vote by postal ballot but are not classified as service voters, the rules differ slightly. Their ballot papers must be printed in the official language of the State and also in English if English is not the official language. However, for these non-service categories, it is not required to print the party affiliation of the candidates on the ballot paper, although the election symbols of the candidates must still be printed to aid in identification.

Administrative control and security features are integrated into the physical production of the ballot papers through serial numbering and bundling procedures. The serial number of each individual ballot paper must be printed on the left-hand side of the document. This number can be placed either on the front or on the back of the paper, depending on what is most convenient for the printing and handling process, provided it remains consistent and legible. Once printed, the postal ballot papers are stitched together into convenient bundles. A crucial security requirement in this process is that the serial numbers within these bundles must be consecutive, creating an unbroken chain of numbered documents. It is strictly ensured that the serial number printed on the main part of the ballot paper matches identically with the serial number on its corresponding counterfoil. This matching system is essential for auditing purposes, allowing election authorities to verify that every issued ballot is accounted for and to detect any potential fraud or duplication.

The color coding of postal ballot papers serves as an immediate visual classification system based on the level of government being elected. As previously noted in the examples, the regulations mandate that postal ballot papers for parliamentary elections, which determine the composition of the national legislature, shall be printed on white colored paper. In contrast, postal ballot papers for assembly elections, which determine the composition of state legislatures, shall be printed on pink colored paper. This chromatic distinction helps prevent the accidental mixing of ballots from different tiers of government during the sorting and counting phases, adding a layer of error prevention to the electoral workflow.

The preparation and dispatch of what is referred to as ETPB, or Electors' Postal Ballot Papers, follow a targeted distribution logic based on the status of the voter within the electoral roll. Postal ballot papers must be sent to all service electors whose names appear in the last part of the electoral roll. This specific section of the roll is dedicated to voters who are away from their ordinary residence due to service obligations. However, this distribution is contingent upon the voter not having opted for proxy voting. Proxy voting is a mechanism where a voter appoints another person to cast a vote on their behalf at a polling station. Service electors who have formally appointed such a proxy are classified as Classified Service Voters, often abbreviated as CSVs. In the case of these Classified Service Voters, the physical postal ballot paper is not sent to them because the voting right has been delegated; instead, their appointed proxy will go to the designated polling station to cast the vote in person. This system ensures that every eligible service member has a method to vote, either through the mail via the postal ballot paper or through a representative at the polls, but prevents the possibility of a single voter casting two votes through both methods. The entire process, from the specific wording of legends like Genl and Bye to the color of the paper and the language of the candidate list, is meticulously engineered to uphold the integrity, accessibility, and accuracy of the democratic process for voters who cannot be physically present at their home polling stations.

The administration of elections for service voters, who are individuals employed in specific government services such as the armed forces or police and are often stationed away from their home constituencies, requires a specialized procedural framework to ensure their democratic rights are upheld despite geographical constraints. A critical component of this framework is the assignment of a specific polling station that corresponds to the area covering the house address of the service voter, ensuring that their vote is counted within the correct electoral jurisdiction. Comprehensive details regarding this allocation process are provided in subsequent sections of the relevant electoral chapter. A significant modernization of this process was introduced through an amendment to the Conduct of Elections Rules, 1961, enacted via an official notification dated October 21, 2016. This regulatory update authorized the transmission of postal ballot papers to service voters using electronic means, a method specifically designated by the Election Commission of India for the categories of personnel defined under sub-clause two of clause three of rule eighteen. When a postal ballot paper is transmitted through these digital channels, the existing legal provisions found in the original rule as well as rules twenty-two, twenty-four, and twenty-seven apply with necessary modifications to accommodate the electronic nature of the transaction, ensuring that the integrity and security standards of physical ballots are maintained in the digital realm. Pursuant to the second proviso of sub-rule one of Rule 23 of the Conduct of Elections Rules, 1961, the Election Commission has established a precise protocol for how these postal ballot papers must be transmitted electronically to service voters. The Returning Officer, who is the senior official responsible for conducting the election in a specific constituency, is mandated to transmit a specific set of documents electronically to facilitate this process. These documents include the actual Postal Ballot Paper on which the voter will mark their choice, Form 13-A which serves as a formal declaration by the elector affirming their identity and eligibility, a specific label designated as Form 13-B intended for the inner envelope known as Cover A, and another label designated as Form 13-C for the outer envelope known as Cover B. Additionally, the transmission must include Form 13-D, which contains detailed instructions to guide the elector through the voting process, as well as any required blank pages necessary for the completion of the forms.

The timeline for preparing and distributing these materials is strictly regulated to ensure synchronization with the overall election schedule. Immediately after the list of contesting candidates is finalized following the conclusion of the period allowed for the withdrawal of candidatures, the Returning Officer must initiate the preparation of postal ballot papers specifically for service voters. It is mandatory for the Returning Officer to arrange for the uploading of these postal ballot papers and all associated documentation onto the Electronically Transmitted Postal Ballot System, commonly referred to as ETPBS, by the day immediately following the last date permitted for the withdrawal of candidatures. To guarantee the integrity of this digital dispatch, the most senior Observer appointed by the Election Commission and posted at the district headquarters is required to personally monitor the entire operational process. This observer must submit a detailed report to the Commission as soon as the electronic dispatch of postal ballots intended for service voters has been successfully completed. Furthermore, an Assistant Returning Officer, who is deputed specifically to supervise the issuance of postal ballot papers to service electors, must adhere to a strict set of verification and record-keeping duties. This officer is required to write the letters PB against the entry of each elector in the marked copy of the electoral roll, serving as a visual indicator that a postal ballot paper has been issued to that individual, while explicitly avoiding the recording of the specific serial number of the ballot paper on the roll itself to maintain secrecy. A proper and accurate account of every postal ballot paper issued to each service voter must be meticulously maintained in a dedicated issue register. Additionally, the specific part of the electoral roll that relates to service voters must be sealed in a separate packet, with a brief descriptive note recorded on the exterior of this packet, which is then placed in the safe custody of the Returning Officer to prevent unauthorized access or tampering.

The mode of transmission for these Electronically Transmitted Postal Ballots involves a series of secure digital actions initiated once the Central Administration Officer activates the Returning Officer operations regarding the release of ballot papers. Upon activation, the Returning Officer gains access to the system to perform several critical activities essential for the election workflow. The officer must enter or view specific data points related to the election of their constituency, adhering to the official election schedule, which includes the description of the election, the state code of the constituency, the type of constituency whether it is an Assembly Constituency or a Parliamentary Constituency, the unique constituency number, the name of the constituency, the date of the election, and the official address of the Returning Officer, all of which are necessary for the proper mailing and dispatch of the marked ballots back to the election office. The officer must also enter or view the specific download window, defined by a start date and time and an end date and time, during which the postal ballots are available for retrieval. Furthermore, the Returning Officer must provide a cut-off date and time by which the marked postal ballots must be received at their office to be counted validly. The system allows the officer to load a template of the Postal Ballot Paper that is specific to their constituency, view and approve a sample Postal Ballot generated by the system to ensure accuracy, and finally, generate a secure password or Personal Identification Number along with password-protected Postal Ballots in bulk for all service voters associated with their constituency.

The final stage of this process involves the downloading and printing of the Electronically Transmitted Postal Ballot Papers by the receiving military or paramilitary units. The officer in charge of records, the unit officer concerned, or the nodal officer who is competent to handle these documents must download the files using a secure One-Time Password mechanism to ensure authentication. The Election Commission has issued direct instructions to Unit level Officers, communicated through the Nodal Officers of the various Forces, stating that the downloading of the ETPB along with all other connected papers uploaded by the Returning Officers must be completed no later than the eighth day after the last date for the withdrawal of candidatures. Once the files are downloaded, if the service voter in question is in a position to print the ETPB themselves, they are permitted to do so using the unique PIN that will be transmitted to them separately. In cases where the voter cannot perform the printing themselves, the Unit Officer or Nodal Officer assumes the responsibility of printing the ETPB and must hand over the printed ballot along with printed copies of Form 13-A, the labels for Form 13-B and Form 13-C, and the instructional document Form 13-D directly to the service voter concerned. It is imperative that the officer in charge of records, the unit officer, or the nodal officer ensures that the ETPB and all accompanying documents meant for a particular service voter are handed over exclusively to that specific individual and to no one else, thereby preserving the sanctity and secrecy of the vote. To complete the package, two envelopes are supplied to each service voter: a smaller envelope designed to hold Form 13-B and a larger envelope designed to hold Form 13-C, facilitating the secure return of the voted ballot to the Returning Officer.

The logistical framework for enabling service voters to participate in elections begins with the proactive responsibilities of the concerned District Election Officer, who is mandated to supply a sufficient quantity of both inner and outer envelopes well in advance of the voting period. These envelopes must strictly adhere to specified technical standards, most notably that they must be white in color to ensure uniformity and ease of processing within the electoral system. The Record Officer, Unit Officer, or Commandant serves as the primary distribution point, responsible for handing over a matched pair consisting of one inner and one outer envelope to each service voter deployed under their command. This handover process is not merely a transactional exchange but a documented event; the Unit Officer or Nodal Officer is legally required to maintain a meticulous record of every Electronically Transmitted Postal Ballot, commonly referred to as an ETPB, that is issued to a service voter. This ledger acts as a critical chain-of-custody document, ensuring accountability from the moment the ballot leaves the election office until it is cast. The integrity of this record is paramount, necessitating that it be preserved within the respective military or paramilitary unit for a minimum duration of five years. This extended retention period is designed to facilitate potential audits, allowing the records to be produced for inspection by any competent authority or presented as evidence before courts of law should any legal dispute arise regarding the issuance or handling of these sensitive voting materials.

Upon receipt of the ETPB kit and its accompanying documentation, the service voter must follow a precise sequence of actions to ensure their vote is valid and secure. The first step involves marking the ballot paper itself, where the voter indicates their choice by placing either a cross mark or a tick mark against the name of their preferred candidate, strictly following the instructions outlined in Form 13D. Once the selection is made, the voter must carefully place the marked ballot paper inside the smaller of the two provided envelopes. This inner envelope must then be securely sealed using gum or adhesive, and a specific label designated as Form 13B must be pasted onto its surface. A crucial security feature of this process involves the serial number of the ballot paper; the voter is required to note this unique identifier on the Form 13B label in the space provided, unless this number has already been pre-printed on the label by the authorities. This linkage between the ballot serial number and the envelope label helps prevent fraud and ensures that each ballot can be traced back to its specific issuance if necessary. Following the sealing of the inner envelope, the voter must complete a formal declaration found in Form 13A. This document serves as an affidavit confirming the voter's identity and eligibility, which must be signed by the voter and subsequently attested by a designated officer within their unit who is authorized to verify such declarations.

The final stage of packaging involves placing two distinct items into the larger outer envelope: the sealed inner envelope containing the marked ballot, which bears the Form 13B label, and the completed and attested declaration form, known as Form 13A. Once both documents are safely inside, the larger envelope must be closed and sealed with gum to prevent tampering during transit. The voter must then affix the label for Form 13C onto this outer envelope and sign in the specific area marked for the sender's signature, thereby taking personal responsibility for the dispatch. The completed Form 13C envelope is then dispatched back to the Returning Officer through whatever postal means are available to the service member at their location. A significant facilitation in this process is that no postage stamp is required to be affixed to the Form 13C envelope if it is posted from within India, as the electoral system covers these costs to remove financial barriers to voting. To ensure timely delivery, Chief Electoral Officers are instructed to coordinate with the General Manager of the local Post Office, directing them to accept these envelopes for speed post delivery to the designated address, with all associated charges being borne directly by the Chief Electoral Officer or District Election Officer office.

As the election timeline progresses and the Returning Officer begins to receive the polled ballot papers from service voters and other electors entitled to vote via postal ballot, a rigorous monitoring protocol is activated. The Returning Officer is obligated to generate a daily report that details the exact number of postal ballots received on that specific day, while also providing a cumulative total of all ballots received since the commencement of the counting period. This report is submitted to the Election Observer assigned to the constituency, ensuring transparent oversight of the influx of votes. When the Observers eventually depart from the constituency after the polling concludes, they are required to include in their final report to the Election Commission a specific figure indicating the total number of postal ballots received up to the date of their departure. This layered reporting mechanism ensures that there is a continuous, verified audit trail of ballot receipt, minimizing the risk of lost votes or administrative discrepancies.

In addition to the standard postal ballot procedure, the electoral framework provides a specialized facility for certain categories of service voters, specifically those belonging to the Armed Forces and members of forces governed by the Army Act of 1950. These individuals are granted the unique option to choose between voting via postal ballot or appointing a proxy to cast their vote on their behalf. Service voters who exercise the option to vote through a representative are officially categorized as Classified Service Voters. To utilize this proxy voting facility, a Classified Service Voter must appoint an individual who is a resident of the specific geographical area covered by the relevant constituency. The appointed proxy must meet strict eligibility criteria, including being at least eighteen years of age and possessing full qualification for registration as an elector, meaning they must not be disqualified due to criminal conviction, unsoundness of mind, or other legal impediments. The formal appointment of a proxy is executed using a specific document known as Form 13F, which is referenced in Annexure 22 of the election guidelines.

Once a proxy appointment is made, it carries significant longevity and convenience for the service voter; the appointment remains valid for all future elections indefinitely, provided that the person making the appointment continues to hold the status of a service voter. This standing arrangement persists until the service voter explicitly revokes the appointment, or until the appointed proxy passes away. Recognizing that circumstances may change, the Classified Service Voter retains the right to revoke an existing appointment and designate a new proxy in the event of the previous proxy's death or for any other reason deemed sufficient by the voter. Such revocation and subsequent re-appointment must be formally documented using Form 13G, also found in Annexure 22. Upon receiving official intimation that a Classified Service Voter has appointed a proxy, the Returning Officer is required to update the electoral roll by marking the letters CSV against the name of that service voter in the final section of the roll. This notation serves as a clear indicator to polling officials that the elector has authorized another person to cast a vote on their behalf. For a proxy to be eligible to vote in a specific election, the intimation of the proxy's appointment must reach the Returning Officer no later than the last date fixed for filing nominations for that particular election, ensuring that all administrative preparations can be completed in time.

The administrative burden on the Returning Officer extends to maintaining comprehensive and separate lists of all Classified Service Voters and their corresponding proxies, including their complete residential addresses. These lists must be compiled in the specific form and manner prescribed by the Election Commission, with the standardized format provided in Annexure 23 serving as the mandatory template. This centralized registry allows for efficient management and verification of proxy votes. Furthermore, after the deadline for filing nominations has passed, the Returning Officer must undertake the task of preparing sub-lists organized by polling station. These sub-lists categorize all Classified Service Voters and their proxies based on the specific polling station where the proxy will cast the vote. The format prescribed by the Commission for maintaining these polling station-wise sub-lists is enclosed as Annexure 24, ensuring uniformity across all constituencies. This detailed segmentation facilitates smooth operations on election day, allowing polling agents to quickly verify the credentials of proxies and ensure that every classified service voter's right to participate in the democratic process is honored through their chosen representative.

The administrative procedures governing the compilation and utilization of electoral rolls involve specific mechanisms to ensure that every eligible voter, including those tasked with conducting the election itself, can exercise their democratic franchise. When finalizing the documentation for a specific polling station, supplementary sub-lists must be appended to the end of the primary section of the electoral roll that corresponds to that particular polling location. This combined document, consisting of the original part of the roll plus the newly added sub-lists, constitutes the official marked copy of the electoral roll for use at that station. It is crucial to note a specific restriction regarding proxy voting; if a person registered as a service voter has already appointed a proxy to cast a vote on their behalf, they are ineligible to be issued a postal ballot paper, thereby preventing duplicate voting methods for the same individual.

A significant portion of election administration focuses on the issuance of postal ballot papers to individuals who are deployed on election duty. These individuals, often referred to as voters on election duty, include a wide array of personnel essential to the functioning of the electoral process. The core principle guiding this provision is that any person appointed to election duties who finds themselves unable to cast their vote at their home polling station due to their work obligations is entitled to alternative voting facilities. These facilities generally come in two forms: an Election Duty Certificate or a Postal Ballot. The determination of which facility a worker receives depends entirely on the geographical relationship between their place of enrollment as a voter and the location where they are assigned to work. If an election worker is deployed within the same constituency where they are registered as a voter, they are entitled to receive an Election Duty Certificate. This certificate serves as a special authorization allowing them to cast their vote at the specific polling station where they are currently performing their duties, rather than traveling back to their home booth. Conversely, if the assignment places the worker in a different constituency from the one where they are enrolled, they are entitled to a Postal Ballot, which allows them to vote remotely and send their ballot back through secure channels.

The categories of personnel covered under these provisions are extensive and encompass nearly everyone involved in the logistical and security aspects of an election. This includes members of polling parties who directly manage the voting process, as well as supervisory roles such as Sector Officers and Zonal Officers who oversee multiple polling stations. Senior officials like Returning Officers and Assistant Returning Officers, who bear significant legal responsibility for the conduct of elections in their areas, are also included. Furthermore, the administrative staff working in the offices of the District Elections Officer, the Deputy District Election Officer, and the Returning Officer are covered, along with employees stationed in control rooms and other election-related command centers. The scope extends to micro-observers who monitor the fairness of the process, all police personnel providing security, and members of home guards. Even support staff such as drivers, conductors, and cleaners of vehicles engaged specifically for election transportation work are eligible if their duties prevent them from visiting their enrolled polling station. Additionally, polling agents representing various political candidates are recognized as voters on election duty for this specific purpose, acknowledging that their presence at polling stations is mandatory and prevents them from voting elsewhere.

The rationale behind facilitating postal balloting for these workers is rooted in the necessity of maintaining a transparent and inclusive electoral process. The Election Commission has mandated that facilitation must be conducted with complete transparency, ensuring that all stakeholders are involved at every stage of the process. During major general elections, such as those for the Lok Sabha, the lower house of the parliament, the majority of polling staff, including police forces and vehicle crews, are typically drawn from within the same parliamentary constituency where they are deployed. Consequently, a large majority of these workers can utilize the Election Duty Certificate to vote locally. However, recognizing that a significant number of personnel will inevitably be deployed outside their home constituencies, the system provides the postal ballot mechanism to ensure their right to vote is not forfeited due to professional obligations. To manage this effectively, a comprehensive database of individuals eligible for postal ballots must be prepared well in advance of the election dates.

This preparatory database is a critical tool for election management and must contain detailed and accurate information about each eligible person. Beyond basic personal details, the database is required to capture specific electoral data, including the number and name of the Assembly Constituency where the person is enrolled, the part number of the electoral roll, and the specific serial number assigned to them within that part. A vital component of this record is the EPIC number, which stands for Electors Photo Identity Card number, a unique identifier assigned to every registered voter in the country. Capturing this number ensures precise identification and prevents errors in ballot issuance. The database must also include fields to record the location and address of facilitation centers designated for postal balloting. These facilitation centers are strategically chosen to be the same locations identified for imparting training to election staff, thereby optimizing logistics and resource usage. If an individual is called upon to attend multiple training sessions, the database must be updated to reflect the history of all such trainings. To verify the electoral enrollment details of any person, officials can utilize search facilities available on the websites of all Chief Electoral Officers. These digital tools allow for searches based on the unique EPIC number or by using the individual's name and surname, ensuring that the data populated in the database is accurate and up-to-date.

The responsibility for creating and maintaining these databases lies primarily with the District Election Officer, who acts as the chief coordinator for electoral activities within a district. Specifically, the District Election Officer must prepare a dedicated database for employees assigned to polling parties and other critical election duties such as sector officers, zonal officers, and micro-observers. This database is all-encompassing and must include every person appointed to any kind of election duty who, by virtue of that duty, cannot cast their vote at their enrolled polling station. The creation of this list is not merely a clerical task but a fundamental safeguard of democratic rights, ensuring that the machinery of the election does not disenfranchise the very people operating it. By systematically identifying these voters, capturing their unique electoral identifiers, and assigning them to appropriate voting mechanisms based on their deployment location, the election administration upholds the principle that no eligible vote should be lost due to the demands of public service. The entire process, from the initial compilation of sub-lists to the final issuance of postal ballots, is designed to integrate seamlessly into the broader electoral framework, ensuring that the marked copies of electoral rolls are accurate and that every voter on duty has a clear, authorized path to participate in the selection of their representatives.

When police personnel are assigned to perform duties during an election, they occupy a unique dual status as both law enforcement officers responsible for maintaining order and as registered voters entitled to exercise their democratic franchise. To manage this complex situation effectively, the Superintendent of Police, often referred to as the SP, or another designated competent authority within the district, is mandated to establish and maintain a comprehensive database containing the records of all police staff deployed for election-related tasks. This registry is not limited to regular police officers but explicitly includes members of the home guard if they too are called upon to serve in an election capacity. The creation of this database is a critical administrative step that goes beyond merely listing names; it requires the meticulous population of specific electoral details for each individual. These details include the number and official name of the Assembly Constituency where the officer is registered as a voter, as well as the specific part number and serial number corresponding to their entry in the official electoral roll. By compiling this information centrally, the administration ensures that every officer's voting rights can be tracked and facilitated regardless of their deployment location.

Once the database is established, the Superintendent of Police must formulate a detailed deployment plan for all police officials within the district well in advance of the election date. This forward planning is essential because it allows the administration to determine precisely which constituency each officer will be serving in during the poll. At this planning stage, it becomes clear whether an officer is being posted within the same constituency where they are registered as an elector or if they are being assigned to a different constituency entirely. This distinction is legally significant because it dictates the method by which the officer will cast their vote. Officers who are posted outside the constituency where they are registered as voters are eligible to vote through a mechanism known as the Postal Ballot. The Postal Ballot is a special provision that allows individuals who cannot physically visit their home polling station due to official duty, illness, or other valid reasons to cast their vote via mail or a designated secure channel. To oversee and coordinate all activities related to enabling these police personnel to exercise their right to vote, whether through the Postal Ballot or via an Electronic Voting Machine at a designated center, the Superintendent of Police is required to appoint a specific individual known as a Nodal Officer. This Nodal Officer serves as the primary point of contact and coordination for all logistical matters concerning the voting process for duty-bound staff.

The procedural framework for applying to vote while on duty involves specific standardized documents. The Superintendent of Police or the appointed Nodal Officer must provide Form 12A to those personnel who will be voting at an Electronically Transmitted Postal Ballot System center, often abbreviated as EDC, or Form 12 to those who require a standard Postal Ballot. These forms act as the official application through which police personnel request the necessary arrangements to cast their votes. It is the responsibility of the Superintendent of Police or the Nodal Officer to ensure that these applications, specifically Forms 12 and 12A, are completed accurately with all relevant electoral roll details, duly signed by the police personnel, and transmitted to the concerned Returning Officer. The Returning Officer is the senior official responsible for conducting the election in a specific constituency. A strict timeline governs this submission; the forms must reach the Returning Officer at least seven days prior to the actual date of the poll. This lead time is crucial because it allows the Returning Officer to make the necessary entries in the marked copy of the electoral roll, which is the official record used to track who has voted, and to issue the Postal Ballot or arrange for the EDC facility accordingly.

This systematic approach to facilitating voting rights extends beyond the police force to include other categories of workers drafted for specific election-related duties, such as drivers, conductors, cleaners, and other support staff essential for the logistics of the election. For these groups, a Nodal Officer may also be appointed to manage their voting arrangements. The Nodal Officer is tasked with ascertaining the enrollment details of these individuals, which includes identifying the number and name of their constituency, the part number, and the serial number of their entry in the electoral roll. Based on their deployment location relative to their registration, these workers are provided with the appropriate form. If a driver or conductor is posted to work within the same constituency where they are enrolled as a voter, they are given Form 12A to facilitate voting at an EDC. Conversely, if they are posted in a different constituency from where they are registered, they are provided with Form 12 to apply for a Postal Ballot. Just as with the police force, the Nodal Officer must ensure that these completed and signed applications are forwarded to the relevant Returning Officer no later than seven days before the polling date. This ensures that the marked electoral roll can be updated and the necessary voting materials issued in time for the election.

The physical process of voting for a person on election duty involves receiving the postal ballot, recording their vote on the document in a secure manner, and returning it to a designated facilitation center. Arrangements for this entire workflow, including the distribution of ballots and the collection of voted papers, must be made available at these facilitation centers to ensure convenience and security. A significant aspect of this procedure involves the issuance of pre-filled Form 12 documents. Form 12 is specifically intended for all persons who are assigned election duty outside the constituency where they are enrolled to vote. To streamline the process and reduce errors, Form 12 can be pre-filled with key information about the elector, including their full name, the number and name of their Assembly Constituency, and the part number and serial number indicating where they appear in the electoral roll. This vital information is drawn directly from the database of employees that has been prepared by the District Election Officer, commonly known as the DEO. The District Election Officer is the chief coordinator for all election activities within a district. Furthermore, software systems developed under the direction of the Chief Electoral Officer, or CEO, who oversees elections at the state level, can be utilized to print these pre-filled forms directly from the employee database, thereby enhancing efficiency and accuracy.

To maintain the integrity of the data, a specific note must be printed at the bottom of every pre-filled Form 12. This note instructs the employee to carefully review the pre-populated details and to make any necessary corrections if the information provided is inaccurate. This step empowers the voter to verify their own data before submitting the application. The distribution of these forms follows a clear chain of command; Form 12 documents destined for police personnel are distributed through the Superintendent of Police or the specific Nodal Officer appointed for this purpose. Similarly, Form 12 forms for drivers, conductors, and cleaners of vehicles used in election operations are distributed through the Nodal Officer or the officer in charge of transportation. Once the forms are distributed, the collection of the signed documents becomes a priority. The signed Form 12 must be collected along with supporting documentation, specifically a copy of the appointment letter which serves as proof that the individual has been officially drafted for election duty, and a photocopy of their EPIC, which stands for Electors Photo Identity Card. The requirement for the EPIC copy is a safeguard designed to ensure that no ineligible person is issued a Postal Ballot, thereby preventing fraud.

The timeline for collecting these signed forms is rigorous but allows for some flexibility to accommodate operational realities. Ideally, efforts should be made to collect all signed Form 12 documents before the last date for the withdrawal of candidature, which is a statutory deadline in the election calendar after which the list of candidates is finalized. However, the guidelines recognize that logistical challenges may arise, and thus provision is made for scenarios where forms could not be collected by this deadline. In such cases, the forms should still be collected as soon as possible even after the withdrawal date has passed. Once collected, the signed Form 12 documents are the responsibility of the District Election Officer, who must forward them to the concerned Returning Officers within the district. This transmission triggers the final stage of the process, which is the actual issuance of the Postal Ballots to the eligible voters. Finally, the system accounts for other categories of voters who may require similar assistance. The District Election Officer is tasked with filling in the information regarding the specific facilitation center assigned to each employee within the central database. This ensures that every individual on election duty knows exactly where to go to receive their ballot, cast their vote, and return it securely, thus upholding the democratic principle that official duty should never disenfranchise a citizen.

In the comprehensive framework of election administration, a critical component involves the management of postal ballots for voters who are simultaneously assigned to election duty. To ensure these individuals can exercise their franchise without conflict, the standard protocol mandates that every designated training centre must also function as a facilitation centre. This dual-purpose arrangement is strategically designed so that voters on election duty can receive their postal ballot papers and cast their votes immediately upon arriving for their mandatory training sessions. This process is timed to occur after the official printing of the postal ballots has been completed, ensuring that the physical materials are available when the personnel gather. The scope of this mandate is broad and inclusive, covering all police personnel deployed for election security, as well as the drivers, conductors, and cleaners of any vehicles utilized during the election period. These essential workers are required to attend at least one training session specifically organized to facilitate the postal balloting process. In scenarios where the nature of the duties requires multiple training sessions for specific categories of employees, the administration must ensure that the opportunity to cast a postal ballot is provided during every single session. This redundancy is a vital safeguard; it ensures that if an employee misses the chance to vote in an earlier session due to scheduling conflicts or oversight, they retain the full ability to cast their ballot in any subsequent training session they attend, thereby maximizing voter participation among election staff.

The logistical chain for preparing and distributing these ballots begins with the Returning Officer, who holds the primary responsibility for generating the actual Postal Ballot papers. This action is triggered only for those individuals who have successfully submitted a signed FORM-12, which serves as the formal application or declaration of intent to vote by post. The Returning Officer is instructed to execute this preparation with utmost urgency, ideally immediately after the bulk printing of postal ballots is concluded, to minimize delays in the delivery chain. Once the ballots are prepared, the Returning Officer must verify the specific location and address of the facilitation centre assigned to the concerned employee. Based on this verification, the ballot is dispatched to the Officer-in-Charge of that specific facilitation centre, who acts as the local custodian responsible for handing the document to the employee. The routing of these ballots depends heavily on jurisdictional boundaries. If the facilitation centre is located within the same geographic area under the jurisdiction of the Returning Officer who issued the ballot, the document is sent directly to the Officer-in-Charge of that centre. Similarly, if the centre lies outside the immediate jurisdiction of the issuing Returning Officer but remains within the same district, the ballot is still sent directly to the relevant Officer-in-Charge within that district. This direct transmission method is preferred to avoid unnecessary bureaucratic layering; specifically, the protocol dictates that the ballot should not be routed through the District Election Officer in such intra-district cases, as this would introduce avoidable delays.

Under normal operational circumstances, there should be no requirement to transport postal ballots outside the boundaries of a district, as the deployment logic generally assigns election duty to employees within their own home districts. However, the system accounts for exceptional circumstances where cross-district deployment becomes unavoidable. In such rare instances where a postal ballot must be sent to a facilitation centre located in a different district, the coordination mechanism shifts to a higher administrative level. The District Election Officer of the originating district is tasked with coordinating directly with the District Election Officer of the destination district to ensure the secure and timely transfer of the ballot. Regardless of the distance or complexity of the logistics, a fundamental rule remains absolute: a voter on election duty must receive their postal ballot, record their vote, and return the completed document exclusively at the designated facilitation centre. All necessary arrangements to support this end-to-end process must be established and maintained at the facilitation centre itself, creating a self-contained voting environment within the training venue.

To maintain the integrity and order of this specialized voting process, the District Election Officer is empowered to appoint a senior officer to serve as the in-charge of postal balloting at each facilitation centre. This appointed official bears the full weight of responsibility for overseeing all aspects of the balloting procedure at that location. The operational guidelines for these centres include strict protocols regarding transparency and political observation. All recognized political parties must be formally informed in writing regarding the schedule of facilitation activities at these centres. Furthermore, these parties are granted the right to depute representatives to witness the entire facilitation process, ensuring that the proceedings are conducted fairly and without bias. To accommodate this voting activity within the training schedule, a minimum of two hours must be earmarked in every training session specifically for the facilitation of postal balloting. While two hours is the baseline, the administration retains the flexibility to extend this duration if the volume of voters or other logistical needs demand more time. Crucially, the actual casting of postal ballots is scheduled to take place only after the instructional portion of the training has concluded. Consequently, representatives of political parties are permitted to enter the facilitation centre only after the training sessions are over and the balloting process has officially commenced.

Provisions are also made to accommodate representatives of individual candidates, allowing them to sit and observe the facilitation process. However, their role is strictly limited to observation; they are expressly forbidden from interfering with the proceedings in any manner. The officer in-charge of the facilitation centre possesses the authority to maintain order and, if any person attempts to disrupt or interfere with the process, that officer has the power to order the immediate removal of such an individual from the premises. To protect the sanctity of the vote, the facilitation centre must be equipped with voting compartments that mirror those found in regular polling stations. These private booths are essential to ensure that employees can mark their postal ballots in complete secrecy, free from observation or coercion. The number of compartments can be increased based on the expected turnout to prevent bottlenecks. Additionally, the centre must be stocked with adequate supplies of glue or gum. This is a technical necessity for the sealing of the envelope, known technically as Cover-A or FORM-13B, which must be securely sealed immediately after the marked postal ballot is placed inside it. The cycle is completed when the voter returns this sealed envelope at the same centre where they received the ballot.

A critical legal requirement in this process involves the attestation of the voter's identity and declaration. Under existing election laws, every person casting a postal ballot must submit a declaration in FORM-13A, and this document must be attested by a gazetted officer. A gazetted officer refers to a high-ranking government official whose appointment is published in the government gazette, granting them the legal authority to verify identities and authenticate documents. To fulfill this statutory obligation, the District Election Officer must ensure that at least one gazetted officer is present and on duty at every facilitation centre throughout the voting period. Specifically, Group A and Group B officers within the state hierarchy, who have been officially authorized to attest true copies of certificates and testimonials, are eligible to perform this function. Their role is to verify the identity of the voter by examining their official identity documents and then signing the declaration to confirm that the person casting the ballot is indeed the registered voter they claim to be. This layer of verification adds a robust security measure to the postal balloting system, ensuring that only eligible election duty personnel cast votes and that their declarations are legally valid.

Before the formal process of attestation can be considered complete, the responsible official must conduct a meticulous and multi-step verification of the ballot paper documentation to ensure strict adherence to electoral integrity standards. This preliminary check is a critical safeguard in the voting procedure, designed to prevent invalidation of votes due to clerical errors or omissions. The officer must first examine the specific section of the declaration form dedicated to the serial number of the ballot paper, often referred to as the SI number or Sl. No. It is imperative that this unique identification number, which tracks the specific physical ballot issued, has been clearly and legibly entered into the designated space provided within the declaration document. Failure to record this number renders the chain of custody for that specific ballot incomplete. Following the confirmation of the serial number entry, the officer must verify the presence of the elector's signature. This signature serves as the primary biometric affirmation that the voter has reviewed the contents of the declaration and accepts responsibility for the information provided. Furthermore, the document must explicitly state the postal address of the elector. This address is not merely a formality but a crucial data point used for correspondence, verifying residency requirements, and ensuring that the postal ballot reaches the correct destination if it is being mailed rather than handed over in person. Only when all three of these elements—the filled serial number, the voter's signature, and the recorded postal address—are present and accurate may the officer proceed to the next stage of the process.

Once the initial verification of the voter's inputs is successfully concluded, the attesting officer must execute their own portion of the authentication protocol to officially validate the document. This step transforms the document from a mere application into a legally recognized instrument of the election process. The officer is required to append their full signature, ensuring that it is distinct and matches the signature on file for that official, alongside the current date on which the attestation is taking place. The inclusion of the date is vital for establishing the timeline of the voting process and ensuring that the ballot was processed within the legally mandated windows. In addition to signing and dating, the officer must provide clear identification of their authority. This can be achieved in one of two ways: either by writing out their official designation and the full address of their office in their own handwriting, or by affixing an official stamp that contains this same information. The designation clarifies the rank and role of the official, confirming they have the jurisdiction to attest such documents, while the office address provides a physical location for accountability and potential audit trails. This dual layer of verification, combining the voter's details with the officer's certified credentials, creates a robust framework for preventing fraud and ensuring that every postal ballot is accounted for by a specific authorized individual at a specific time and place.

The distribution of postal ballots at a Facilitation Centre represents a highly controlled phase of the electoral workflow, managed directly by the Officer in-charge who acts as the gatekeeper for these sensitive materials. When a voter arrives at the centre to collect their ballot, the officer does not simply hand over the document; instead, they must first retrieve the specific postal ballot that was previously received from the Returning Officer, the senior official responsible for the conduct of the election in that constituency. The ballot intended for a specific voter is matched against the voter's identity through a rigorous verification process. The primary method for this identification is the presentation of the EPIC, which stands for Electors Photo Identity Card, a specialized document issued by the election commission containing the voter's photograph, name, and unique identity number. However, recognizing that not all voters may possess this specific card at the moment of collection, the regulations allow for flexibility by accepting any other valid photo identity document issued by a competent government authority. This ensures that the right to vote is not denied due to the lack of a single specific document, provided the alternative ID reliably confirms the person's identity. The officer must visually compare the photograph and details on the presented ID with the individual standing before them and cross-reference this with the records associated with the postal ballot in their possession.

Upon successful verification of the voter's identity, the final administrative step involves documenting the transfer of the ballot from the state to the citizen. The Officer in-charge is mandated to obtain the signature of the elector, which serves as a formal token or receipt acknowledging that the voter has physically received their postal ballot. This acknowledgment is recorded in a dedicated register maintained specifically for this purpose, creating a permanent written record of the transaction. The format of this register is strictly standardized to capture all necessary data points for future auditing and reconciliation. The register includes a column for a running serial number, which provides a sequential count of all ballots issued at that centre, ensuring no gaps in the numbering sequence that could indicate lost or unaccounted ballots. Another essential column records the Part Number, which refers to the specific geographical segment or division of the electoral roll to which the voter belongs, helping to organize voters by locality. The register also requires the entry of the Electoral Roll Serial Number of the elector, which is the unique index number assigned to that voter in the master list of eligible voters for their constituency. To further secure the process, there is a dedicated section for recording the details of the identification document produced by the elector, such as the ID type and its number, linking the physical receipt of the ballot to the specific proof of identity used. Finally, the register captures the signature or thumb impression of the elector, serving as the ultimate proof of delivery, and includes a remarks column where the officer can note any anomalies, special circumstances, or additional observations relevant to that specific issuance. This comprehensive logging system ensures that every single postal ballot issued can be traced from the Returning Officer to the Facilitation Centre and finally into the hands of the verified voter, maintaining the sanctity and transparency of the democratic process.

The maintenance and security of electoral registers constitute a foundational element of democratic integrity, requiring strict adherence to prescribed legal protocols. Specifically, the register in question must be sealed using the exact same methodology and security measures applied to the Register maintained in Form 17A, ensuring a uniform standard of tamper-evidence across all statutory documents. Once sealed, this register is not to be stored in isolation but must be kept alongside other statutory covers as explicitly mandated under rule 93 subsection 1 of the relevant election regulations. This co-location ensures that all critical election documents are subject to the same chain of custody and security oversight. Furthermore, transparency is maintained through the provision that a copy of this specific register may be furnished to any candidate who formally requests it, provided such a demand is made after the date of the poll within the respective constituency. This allows candidates to verify the administrative actions taken regarding voter management post-election while preventing interference during the active voting period.

A distinct procedure exists for voters who are assigned to election duty on the day of the poll, recognizing that these individuals cannot visit their designated polling stations to cast votes in person. Such voters are entitled to receive a postal ballot, which serves as their mechanism for exercising their franchise remotely. The process requires the voter on election duty to receive this postal ballot, record their vote upon it in secrecy, and return the completed document at a designated facilitation center. Comprehensive arrangements to support this workflow, including the physical setup and staffing, must be established at every facilitation center to ensure smooth operations. The timeline and procedural framework for issuing these postal ballots are governed by Rules 20, 23, and 27 of the Conduct of Elections Rules 1961. These rules detail the provisions for how voters on election duty must intimate their need for a postal ballot, the step-by-step procedure for the issuance of the ballot paper, and the strict deadlines by which the voted ballot must be returned to be counted.

To prevent the serious electoral offense of double voting, a rigorous tracking system is embedded within the issuance process. Whenever a postal ballot paper is issued to a voter on election duty, the electoral roll number of that elector must be entered on the counterfoil of the ballot paper. This counterfoil acts as a stub or record retained by the election officials. Simultaneously, in the marked copy of the electoral roll that is set apart specifically for conducting the poll at the physical polling station, the letters PB must be clearly marked against the name of that elector. The abbreviation PB stands for Postal Ballot and serves as a visual indicator to polling staff that a postal ballot has already been issued to this individual. This marking is crucial because it ensures that if the elector were to somehow appear at a polling station, they would be identified as having already received a postal ballot and would therefore be prohibited from casting a second vote on an Electronic Voting Machine or via a regular ballot paper. This synchronization between the issued postal ballots and the marked electoral roll is the primary safeguard against duplicate voting.

Once the postal ballots have been successfully issued to all eligible voters on election duty and these individuals have cast their votes at the facilitation center, the Returning Officer has a specific subsequent duty. The Returning Officer must transmit the relevant sections of the electoral roll to the concerned Presiding Officers at the various polling stations. This transmission allows the Presiding Officers to mark the names of electors who have already voted via postal ballot, ensuring they are not allowed to vote again at the polling station on the day of the poll. After the polling process concludes for the day, this marked copy of the electoral roll must be sealed and placed into safe custody to preserve the integrity of the record. It is a clear legal inference drawn from these provisions, particularly clause a of sub-rule 5 of Rule 23 of the Conduct of Elections Rules 1961, that once the marked copy of the electoral roll is prepared and ready to be sent to the Presiding Officers, the window for issuing any further postal ballot papers closes immediately. Consequently, no ballot paper can be issued after this specific stage of preparing the marked copy for supply to the presiding officers, as doing so would break the synchronization required to prevent double voting.

The actual process of postal balloting involves a series of precise steps designed to guarantee both the secrecy of the vote and the authenticity of the voter. Upon receiving the postal ballot, the voter must proceed to a voting compartment to mark their choice in complete secrecy, mirroring the privacy afforded in a physical polling booth. The voter is then required to write the serial number of the postal ballot paper in the space provided within the declaration form known as Form 13A, as well as on the envelope that will contain the voted ballot, unless these numbers have already been pre-filled by officials. Following this, the marked postal ballot must be placed inside a smaller inner envelope, designated as Form 13B or Cover A, which is specifically meant for this purpose. The voter must seal this inner envelope properly to ensure the contents remain confidential. The next step involves the voter signing the declaration in Form 13A and writing their postal address of registration as it appears in the electoral roll, along with their name in the designated spaces. Crucially, this declaration must then be attested by a gazetted officer, a government official of a certain rank authorized by law to verify identities. During this attestation, the polled postal ballot, now sealed inside the inner envelope, must not be shown to the officer; the officer only verifies the identity and signature on the declaration form, not the voting choice itself.

After the declaration is signed and attested, the voter places the sealed inner envelope containing the marked ballot, along with the duly signed and attested Form 13A declaration, into a larger outer envelope known as Form 13C or Cover B. This outer envelope is also sealed by the voter. The voter then proceeds to cast this complete package into the Facilitation Ballot Box. The facilitation ballot box is typically a large steel trunk equipped with a single opening at the top, designed specifically for the deposit of these postal ballot envelopes at the facilitation center. Before the casting of ballots begins for the day, strict transparency protocols are observed. The empty facilitation ballot box is opened and displayed to all persons present, including candidates or their agents, to demonstrate that it contains no prior ballots. Once verified as empty, the box is sealed by the officer in charge of the Facilitation Centre. Every voter on election duty must cast their postal ballot into this sealed box only after completing the marking and sealing procedures described previously. It is important to note that this facilitation ballot box is deployed only on the second or subsequent days of training at the respective training venues, aligning with the schedule of voters on election duty.

The final phase of this daily process involves the sorting of the collected postal ballots. After all postal ballots for the day have been cast into the box, the officer in charge of the Facilitation Centre opens the box in the presence of representatives of political parties. This presence of party representatives serves as an independent witness to the integrity of the count and handling process. All postal ballots are carefully removed from the box, and once emptied, the interior of the box is shown again to the said representatives of political parties to confirm that no ballots remain inside and that the extraction was complete. This meticulous sequence of sealing, witnessing, opening, and verifying ensures that every stage of the postal balloting process for voters on election duty is conducted with maximum transparency, security, and adherence to the statutory requirements laid out in the election laws.

The procedural framework for managing postal ballots in the context of parliamentary and assembly constituency elections involves a meticulously structured sequence of actions designed to ensure transparency, accountability, and accuracy throughout the entire process. Upon receipt at a designated facilitation centre, all postal ballot envelopes undergo an initial sorting phase where they are categorized strictly according to the specific Parliamentary or Assembly Constituency to which they belong. This geographical and administrative segregation is critical because it aligns the voting materials with the correct electoral rolls and returning officers responsible for those specific regions. Once the envelopes are sorted by constituency, the total count of postal ballot envelopes received for each individual Parliamentary or Assembly Constituency must be meticulously recorded. This data entry is not performed on loose sheets but is formally entered into a standardized document known as Statement in Format-1. This statement serves as the primary ledger for tracking the volume of votes cast at that specific facilitation centre and is maintained exclusively for this statutory purpose. The creation of this record ensures that there is an immediate, verifiable paper trail documenting exactly how many ballots have been processed for every single constituency represented at the centre.

To further reinforce the integrity of the election process and build trust among stakeholders, the protocol mandates the active participation of political party representatives who are present at the facilitation centre. These representatives are formally requested to affix their signatures on the official register where the ballot counts are logged. This act of signing serves as a witness verification, confirming that the numbers recorded are accurate and observed by unbiased third parties representing the diverse political spectrum. Following this verification step, copies of the relevant pages from this signed register are provided to the representatives. This distribution of information ensures that all political entities have immediate access to the same data regarding the number of ballots processed, thereby preventing disputes over figures and promoting a transparent environment where all parties can independently verify the proceedings. After the counting and recording phases are complete, all postal ballot envelopes belonging to a single Parliamentary or Assembly Constituency are consolidated and placed together into a large, dedicated envelope specifically intended for that constituency. This physical bundling prevents any mixing of ballots between different constituencies, which is essential for maintaining the sanctity of the vote count for each distinct electoral area.

The external labeling of these large consolidated envelopes is subject to strict clarity requirements to avoid any confusion during transit or subsequent handling. The name of the Facilitation Centre where the processing took place, the specific date on which the facilitation occurred, and the precise number of postal ballots contained within the package must be clearly and legibly written on the exterior of the envelope. In addition to these logistical details, the envelope must also prominently display the number and name of the Parliamentary or Assembly Constituency for which the contents are meant. This dual labeling system ensures that even if the envelope is separated from its accompanying documentation, the destination and contents remain immediately identifiable. Once sealed and labeled, this envelope containing the ballots is prepared for dispatch to the Returning Officer of the concerned Parliamentary or Assembly Constituency. The Returning Officer is the statutory authority responsible for the conduct of the election in that specific area and is the only person authorized to open and count these ballots. To guarantee the security of the transmission, the envelope is sent along with a copy of the relevant Statement in Format-1 through a special messenger appointed specifically for this high-security task. The protocol dictates that this messenger must hold a government rank not below that of a Deputy Tahsildar, ensuring that the individual transporting the votes is a senior and accountable government official capable of maintaining the chain of custody.

A crucial component of the modernization and oversight of this electoral procedure is the mandatory requirement for videography of the entire postal balloting process. Every step, from the initial sorting of envelopes to the final sealing and handover to the special messenger, is captured on video. This continuous visual record acts as an immutable audit trail, allowing authorities to review the proceedings in case of any allegations of malpractice or procedural errors. It serves as a deterrent against misconduct and provides concrete evidence that the established rules were followed diligently. Furthermore, specific regulations govern the voting rights of officials deployed on election duty. It is imperative to note that once a postal ballot paper has been issued to a voter who is on election duty, that individual is legally bound to vote only through the postal ballot mechanism. This rule applies even if the official is subsequently exempted from duty or is not ultimately deployed on election day. The issuance of the postal ballot effectively locks in the method of voting for that individual, preventing them from switching to in-person voting at a polling station, which helps eliminate the risk of double voting or administrative confusion regarding the status of their vote.

The monitoring and reporting hierarchy for the postal balloting process extends beyond the immediate facilitation centre to include district and state-level oversight mechanisms. The officer in charge of each Facilitation Centre is required to prepare a daily return using a standardized document known as Format-2 on every day that facilitation services are provided. This daily report details the number of ballots cast and is transmitted to the District Election Officer, commonly referred to as the DEO. The role of the DEO is to compile these individual returns from all facilitation centres within their specific district. This compilation happens every single day until the facilitation period for postal ballots concludes in that district. By aggregating the data daily, the DEO maintains a real-time overview of postal voting trends and volumes across the entire district. Once the district-level compilation is complete, a copy of this return is sent every day to the Chief Electoral Officer, who operates at the state level. The Chief Electoral Officer then undertakes the massive task of compiling the returns from all districts to create a comprehensive statement for the entire State in Format-2. This state-wide consolidation provides a holistic view of the postal ballot progress across all constituencies and districts.

The flow of information continues upward and outward to ensure maximum transparency and regulatory compliance. The Chief Electoral Officer sends a copy of the compiled State-level Format-2 to the Election Commission every day until the facilitation process is fully completed across the State. This daily reporting keeps the central regulatory body informed of the election's progress and allows for immediate intervention if any anomalies are detected in the data. Simultaneously, the Chief Electoral Officer is mandated to send a copy of the daily Format-2 return to all recognized political parties. This parallel distribution ensures that political organizations have the same level of access to aggregate data as the election authorities, fostering an environment of openness and allowing parties to monitor the overall pace and volume of postal voting. The Format-2 document itself is a detailed district-wise statement that captures specific data points including the name of the State, the name of the District, the numbers and names of the Parliamentary or Assembly Constituencies involved, and the specific date of facilitation. It lists the facilitation centres by serial number and records two distinct metrics for the total number of postal ballots cast: the count for that specific date and the cumulative total up to that date. This distinction between daily and cumulative figures allows analysts and observers to track the rate of voting over time and verify that the totals are increasing logically without discrepancies. The rigorous adherence to these formatting, reporting, and logistical protocols ensures that the complex operation of collecting and securing postal ballots from government employees and other eligible voters is conducted with the highest standards of integrity, precision, and public trust.

The administrative framework for managing election totals and the secure handling of postal ballots involves a rigorous hierarchy of accountability and specific logistical protocols designed to maintain the integrity of the democratic process. When compiling election results, the data is aggregated first at the district level and subsequently at the state level to ensure accurate reporting across different tiers of governance. A critical component of this process is the storage of postal ballots once they have been received by the Returning Officer, who is the senior election official responsible for conducting the election in a specific area. For each Assembly Constituency, which represents a local legislative district, the Returning Officer is mandated to store the envelopes containing these postal ballots alongside a copy of the relevant statement prepared in a standardized document known as Format-1.
