This storage must take place in a special strong room that is constructed specifically for this purpose, ensuring that the ballots are kept in a highly secure environment protected from unauthorized access, tampering, or environmental damage until the counting phase begins.

In the context of simultaneous elections, where voting for both Parliamentary constituencies, which elect members to the national legislature, and Assembly constituencies occurs on the same day, distinct procedures are established to prevent the mixing of ballots intended for different levels of government. The postal ballot papers pertaining to Parliamentary Constituencies must be returned directly to the Returning Officers of those specific constituencies and not to Assistant Returning Officers, who serve as subordinate officials assisting in the election management. To facilitate this separation during simultaneous elections, all required documentation forms, specifically identified as Forms 13A, 13B, 13C, and 13D, must be prepared and dispatched in two distinct sets. One set of these forms relates exclusively to the Parliamentary election, while the other set pertains to the Assembly election. Each of these sets is placed into its respective covering envelope, technically referred to as Form 13C, with the address of the concerned Returning Officer clearly specified on the exterior to ensure correct routing. To further distinguish between the two types of postal ballot papers and minimize the risk of human error during sorting and processing, a color-coding system is implemented for the covers used in Forms 13B and 13C. Specifically, the covers designated for an Assembly election are to be printed in pink, whereas the covers intended for a Parliamentary election are to be printed in green. It is also a mandatory requirement that the full official designation of the Returning Officer along with their complete office address be either printed or legibly written on the covers in Form 13C to guarantee precise delivery and identification.

Special provisions exist for the issuance and management of postal ballot papers for specific categories of voters, including special voters, electors under preventive detention, and voters deployed on election duty. The printing of these ballot papers must be executed expeditiously to ensure timely delivery, given the unique circumstances of these voters. It is important to note that the names of special voters and electors held under preventive detention will not appear in the final section of the standard electoral roll. However, if these individuals are eligible to vote, their names will have been entered in one of the other sections of the electoral roll corresponding to their constituency. The appropriate Government authority bears the responsibility of identifying these individuals; within fifteen days of the official calling of an election, the government must ascertain and formally intimate the Returning Officer regarding the names of any electors under preventive detention. This intimation must include their residential addresses, their electoral roll numbers, and specific particulars concerning their places of detention. Furthermore, an elector who is under preventive detention retains the right to personally provide this same information to the Returning Officer within the same fifteen-day window following the calling of the election. For a special voter who wishes to exercise their franchise via post, there is a requirement to notify the Returning Officer at least ten days prior to the scheduled date of the poll. Upon receiving such intimation for either a special voter or an elector under preventive detention, the Returning Officer is obligated to dispatch a postal ballot paper to that individual. The issuance of these ballots is governed strictly by the provisions laid down in rule 23 of the Conduct of Election Rules, 1961, which provides the legal basis for remote voting in these exceptional cases.

Another significant category of voters facilitated by postal ballots comprises Absentee Voters in the Essential Service category, often abbreviated as AVES. This provision stems from clause (c) of Section 60 of the Representation of the People Act, 1951, a primary legislation governing elections in India. Based on due consideration of operational necessities and in consultation with the Government, the Election Commission of India periodically notifies specific categories of persons employed in essential services who qualify for this facility. Individuals employed in essential services are defined as those who, due to the nature of their duties on the day of the poll, are unable to be physically present at their assigned polling stations to cast their votes in person. Consequently, the facility of voting by postal ballot is extended to these groups through notifications issued by the Election Commission from time to time. An illustrative list of such essential services includes employees of the Airport Authority of India, the Food Corporation of India, the Indian Railway system, the Press Information Bureau, Doordarshan (the public television broadcaster), All India Radio, the Electricity Department, the Health and Family Welfare sector, State Transport Corporations, the Food and Civil Supply department, BSNL (a telecommunications provider), media persons who have been authorized by the Election Commission for poll day coverage, and the Fire Service. It is crucial to understand that this list of essential services is not static; it is finalized by the Commission after obtaining views from the Chief Electoral Officer of the concerned State, meaning the specific eligible professions may vary from one state to another depending on local requirements. Notably, in a communication dated September 2, 2019, the Commission proposed to the Ministry of Law and Justice that persons employed in the aviation sector, train operations, the shipping sector, long-distance government road transport or corporation buses, fire services, traffic management, and media personnel authorized for election coverage should be treated as absentee voters. This proposal recognizes that these individuals are often prevented from reaching their polling stations due to the compulsions of their service conditions, which require them to be active during election periods.

To avail themselves of the postal ballot facility, an Absentee Voter belonging to the essential services category must submit a formal application to the Returning Officer using a specific document designated as Form-12D, in which they must provide all requisite personal and professional particulars. A key verification step in this process involves the Nodal Officer, a designated official appointed by the organization employing the voter. For applicants in the essential services category, their application seeking the postal ballot facility must be verified and endorsed by this Nodal Officer to confirm their eligibility and employment status. Strict timelines govern the submission of these applications; they must reach the Returning Officer during a specific window that begins on the date of the announcement of the election and extends up to five days following the date of the formal notification of the election concerned. This structured approach ensures that while the rights of voters who are indispensable to public services and safety are protected, the administrative machinery can efficiently process, verify, and distribute ballot papers without compromising the schedule or security of the overall election process. The entire system reflects a balance between accessibility for disenfranchised groups due to duty or detention and the stringent security measures required to uphold the sanctity of the vote.

Under the amended legal frameworks established by Rule 27E and Rule 27-I of the Conduct of Elections Rules, 1961, the entire process regarding the issuance of postal ballot papers to absentee voters and the subsequent return of these ballots after voting has been strictly codified to follow the specific directions issued by the Election Commission. This regulatory foundation ensures that citizens who are unable to visit their designated polling stations due to the nature of their employment can still exercise their democratic right to vote. In alignment with these statutory provisions, the Election Commission has formulated detailed procedures and comprehensive guidelines specifically designed to facilitate postal ballot voting for a distinct category of individuals known as persons on essential service. For the sake of administrative convenience and clarity within official documentation, this group is referred to as Absentee Voters on Essential Service, or AVES. These rules create a structured pathway that allows members of the armed forces, police forces, and other government employees deployed on critical duties to participate in elections without being physically present at their home constituencies on polling day.

The execution of these regulations places significant responsibility on the Returning Officer, who acts as the chief electoral administrator for a specific constituency. The first critical step in this process involves ensuring adequate publicity so that all eligible electors are fully aware of the facility being provided to them. Transparency and accessibility are paramount, which is why Form 12D, the official application document required for requesting a postal ballot, must be prominently displayed on the website of the District Election Officer. Furthermore, this form must be made readily available to the Nodal Officer of the relevant organization, such as a military unit or police department, who is tasked with disseminating the information and documents to the concerned electors within their workforce. This dual-channel approach ensures that no eligible voter is left uninformed due to a lack of access to digital resources or organizational communication gaps. Once the applications begin to arrive, the Returning Officer must meticulously process each Form 12D submitted. Upon receipt of these applications, the officer is mandated to compile a comprehensive list of all Absentee Voters on Essential Service whose applications have been received within the stipulated timeframe and are found to be in proper order. This list must be drawn up using a prescribed format to maintain uniformity and ease of verification across different constituencies.

Following the compilation of the list, the Returning Officer must undertake a rigorous verification process to ensure the integrity of the electoral roll. This involves cross-referencing the details provided by the applicants in Form 12D against the master electoral roll to confirm that the particulars match exactly and that the individuals are indeed registered as valid electors in that constituency. This step is crucial for preventing fraud and ensuring that only legitimate voters are granted the postal ballot facility. Only those electors whose particulars are verified as correct with reference to the official electoral roll will be issued a postal ballot paper in accordance with the standing instructions. Once the final list of absentee voters in this category who will be voting through postal ballot papers is finalized, a specific administrative marker must be applied to the official records. The entry consisting of the letters PB, which stands for Postal Ballot, shall be entered in the marked copy of the electoral roll against the names of such electors. This notation serves as an internal control mechanism to prevent these individuals from attempting to vote again at a regular polling station, thereby upholding the principle of one person, one vote.

Regarding the physical instruments of voting, the postal ballot paper designed for Absentee Voters on Essential Service must adhere to strict standards of consistency. The form and language of the postal ballot paper issued to these absentee voters shall be identical to the postal ballot papers used for voters who are on election duty. This standardization ensures that all postal voters, regardless of whether they are working for the election machinery or serving in essential services elsewhere, receive the same clear instructions and ballot design, minimizing confusion and ensuring that the vote is cast correctly. To manage the complexity of this operation, a dedicated Assistant Returning Officer, commonly abbreviated as ARO, shall be assigned to handle all work related to postal ballot voting by Absentee Voters. This appointment ensures that there is a single point of accountability and expertise overseeing the entire lifecycle of the postal voting program for this specific group. It shall be the sole responsibility of this designated ARO to carry forward the postal voting programme from the initial stage of receiving applications in Form 12D through to the final collection of ballots, taking all necessary actions to ensure the process runs smoothly and securely.

The location where the actual voting takes place is governed by specific legal provisos and sub-rules. As per the second proviso to Rule 27E, the issuance of postal ballot papers to absentee voters must occur in the manner specified by the Election Commission. Furthermore, Sub-rule 3 of Rule 27F explicitly provides that the recording of votes by an Absentee Voter shall take place in a center specifically designated by the Election Commission. In constituencies where there are Absentee Voters in the essential service category who have been found eligible for the postal ballot facility, the Returning Officer is required to identify a suitable venue and room to serve as the hub for these activities. This location will function as the place for issuing postal ballot papers to such Absentee Voters, facilitating their voting process, and subsequently collecting the completed postal ballot papers. For this purpose, the Returning Officer must identify a suitable location to be officially designated as the Postal Voting Centre, or PVC, for the absentee voters. The selection of this venue is subject to geographical constraints; it must be situated within the territorial boundaries of the constituency. Moreover, the location should be chosen, as far as practically possible, towards the central part of the constituency area. This strategic placement is intended to ensure that the traveling distance for the voters to reach the Postal Voting Centre is not unduly long, thereby reducing the logistical burden on service personnel who may have limited time or transport options.

Effective communication is vital to the success of this voting mechanism, and strict protocols exist for intimating applicants about the logistics of casting their votes. All persons who have applied for a postal ballot paper under the AVES category must be duly intimated with specific details. This notification must include the full address of the Postal Voting Centre venue where the postal ballot voting is to be arranged, the specific dates on which the postal ballot voting facility will remain open, and the precise hours fixed for postal ballot voting. The method of delivering this intimation is flexible to accommodate modern communication technologies while maintaining traditional safeguards. The intimation may be given through Short Message Service, or SMS, on the mobile phone number wherever the same has been mentioned in the application in Form 12D. In cases where a mobile number is not available or as a supplementary measure, the intimation may be given by post and or through the Booth Level Officer, known as the BLO, who is the grassroots-level election official responsible for a specific polling booth area. In addition to direct communication with the voters, this information should also be widely disseminated through the Nodal Officer of the organization concerned, via advertisements in local newspapers, and through the official website of the District Election Officer to ensure maximum reach.

The instructions provided to the voters must be explicit regarding the operational parameters of the facility. It should be clearly explained to the absentee voters who have applied for postal ballot voting that they may come for casting their vote on any of the three days during the hours fixed for the purpose, offering flexibility to those with demanding service schedules. Furthermore, it must be emphasized that they are required to produce their service identity card as proof of eligibility upon arrival at the center. A critical restriction that must be communicated is that those who have been approved for voting through this specific facility can only vote at such designated Postal Voting Centres and not in any other manner, such as at a general polling station or via mail from a remote location. This restriction is enforced to maintain the chain of custody and security of the ballot. Finally, all necessary arrangements at the facilitation centre shall be made to assist the voter, including those on election duty who opt for a postal ballot, to record their vote in secret and to return the Postal Ballot securely to the Returning Officer. These arrangements include providing private voting compartments, secure ballot boxes, and trained staff to guide the voters through the procedure, ensuring that the sanctity of the election is preserved while accommodating the unique needs of those serving the nation in essential capacities.

In the administration of electoral processes, particularly concerning the specialized mechanism of postal voting, it is a fundamental requirement that all contesting candidates within a specific constituency are afforded prior notification regarding the operational details of the Postal Voting Centre, commonly referred to as the PVC. This advance intimation serves a critical democratic function, ensuring transparency and allowing candidates the opportunity to exercise their right to appoint authorized representatives, known as agents, whose sole purpose is to observe and witness the proceedings within these centers. The communication sent to these candidates must be formal and written, explicitly stating that they have the prerogative to deploy agents for monitoring the postal voting activities. Should a candidate or their designated election agent wish to avail themselves of this right, the appointment must be executed through a written instrument bearing the signature of either the candidate directly or their officially recognized election agent. To standardize this process and ensure legal validity, the administrative framework permits the use of Form-10, which is the established format typically utilized for appointing polling agents at regular polling stations, to be adapted and employed for the appointment of agents specifically for the Postal Voting Centre. This ensures consistency in documentation and clarity in the chain of authorization across different types of voting venues.

The operational efficiency of any Postal Voting Centre is contingent upon the deployment of adequate staffing levels, which must be meticulously calculated based on the anticipated volume of voters belonging to the Absentee Voter from Essential Services, or AVES, category who are registered to cast their ballots at that specific location. The term AVES refers to individuals such as members of the armed forces, police forces, and government officials posted outside their home constituencies who are unable to vote in person on election day and thus rely on postal mechanisms. To maintain the integrity of the process, every single postal voting centre is mandated to have at least one gazetted officer present on the premises. A gazetted officer is a high-ranking public official whose signature carries significant legal weight and is required here specifically to attest, or formally certify, the declaration made by the elector in Form-13A. This attestation acts as a verification layer to prevent fraud. While one such officer is the minimum requirement, the number of gazetted officers and supporting staff may be increased proportionally depending on the total number of electors expected at a particular center to prevent bottlenecks and ensure smooth operations.

The duties and functions assigned to the staff operating within the Postal Voting Centre are comprehensive and strictly defined to safeguard the sanctity of the vote. The primary responsibility involves the issuance of the postal ballot paper along with all associated envelopes and documentation, specifically Form-13A which is the declaration form, Form-13B which is the smaller sealing envelope, and Form-13C which is the larger covering envelope. However, before any of these materials are handed over, the identity of the voter must be rigorously verified. It is important to note that while Form-13D, which contains detailed instructions for voters, does not need to be distributed individually to every elector to save resources, a copy must be made available within the PVC for reference should any elector request to see it. Instead of handing out individual instruction sheets, the staff is required to verbally brief the electors on the necessary requirements and procedures. Furthermore, to aid visual understanding, a large chart clearly indicating the step-by-step procedure for voting must be prominently displayed within the center for the information and guidance of all electors.

The protocol for verifying the identity of an elector is a crucial checkpoint that occurs before the issuance of the postal ballot paper. Upon presentation, the name of the elector and the specific identification document produced for verification must be recorded in a dedicated register maintained exclusively for this purpose. This register serves as an official log of attendance and identity confirmation. In addition to recording these details, the staff must obtain the signature or thumb impression of the elector in this register to provide biometric or written proof of their presence. A standardized format for maintaining this register is provided in Annexure-B of the election guidelines. Concurrently, to prevent double voting and to track participation, a tick mark must be placed against the name of the elector in the master list of AVES voters, serving as an immediate visual indicator that the person has successfully cast their vote. Following the voting process, the counterfoil, which is the detachable stub containing the serial number and part number of the elector, must be carefully detached from the ballot paper. These counterfoils are then kept in safe custody and are destined to be sent to the Returning Officer, the senior official responsible for the conduct of the election in that constituency, along with the envelopes containing the actual polled postal ballots.

Before casting their vote, electors must receive a clear and thorough briefing from the staff regarding the entire procedure and the formalities they are obligated to observe. This briefing covers several critical steps to ensure the ballot is valid. First, the elector must be instructed on how to make the necessary declaration in Form-13A and the imperative need to get this declaration attested by the gazetted officer present in the PVC. Second, they must be guided to enter the unique serial number of their postal ballot paper in two specific locations: on the declaration Form-13A and on the smaller envelope designated as Form-13B. This dual entry creates a link between the voter's declaration and their specific ballot. Third, the staff must explain the correct manner of marking the vote, which involves placing either a cross mark or a tick mark clearly against the name of the candidate of their choice, avoiding any other markings that could invalidate the ballot. Fourth, the elector must be shown how to properly fold the marked ballot paper and place it inside the smaller envelope, Form-13B, and how to securely close and seal this envelope. Fifth, the procedure requires the elector to place the duly filled, signed, and attested declaration form (Form-13A) along with the sealed smaller envelope containing the marked ballot into the larger covering envelope, Form-13C. Finally, the elector must be instructed to close this larger envelope and drop it into the secure ballot box kept within the PVC, thereby completing the voting act.

The temporal framework for the operation of the Postal Voting Centre is strictly regulated to ensure uniformity and sufficient time for all eligible absentee voters. The PVC shall remain open for a period of up to three consecutive days in each constituency. The scheduling of these three days is subject to a specific constraint: the entire three-day window must be concluded at least three days prior to the date fixed for the general poll in the constituency. This buffer period is essential to allow for the transportation and counting of postal ballots alongside regular ballots on election day. During each of these three operational days, the center must remain open to the public from 9 AM in the morning until 5 PM in the evening, providing an eight-hour window daily for voters to attend.

At the close of polling hours on each day, a rigorous protocol for securing the votes must be followed. All envelopes in Form-13C that have been deposited in the drop box during the day must be retrieved and sealed together in a larger packet. This packet must be clearly superscribed, or labeled on the outside, with the title "Postal Ballots in PVC." To ensure traceability and accountability, specific particulars must be clearly written on the outer surface of this packet. These details include the name and number of the constituency, the specific name and location of the Postal Voting Centre, the exact date on which the votes contained within were cast, and the total count of Form-13C envelopes enclosed in the packet. In parallel, the counterfoils of the postal ballots, which were detached earlier, must also be collected and sealed in a separate, distinct packet. This second packet must be superscribed with the label "Counter foils of PBs in PVC," where PB stands for Postal Ballots. Similar to the ballot packet, the name and number of the constituency and the dates on which this packet was sealed must be entered on its exterior.

Once the packets containing the polled postal ballots and the corresponding counterfoils are securely sealed and labeled, they must be dispatched to the Returning Officer on the same evening that the polling closed. This immediate transfer minimizes the risk of tampering or loss and ensures that the chain of custody is maintained without interruption. Furthermore, to uphold the principle of transparency and bipartisan oversight, the signatures of the agents of the candidates who were present and observing the proceedings in the PVC must be obtained at the end of each day of voting. These signatures serve as an acknowledgment by the candidate representatives that they witnessed the sealing and packing process. The specific format for recording these daily observations and signatures is provided in Annexure C of the election guidelines, ensuring that there is a standardized record of agent presence and concurrence with the day's closing procedures.

At the conclusion of the designated three-day voting period, a strict protocol governs the handling and custody of all election materials utilized within the Postal Voting Centre, commonly referred to as the PVC. The Assistant Returning Officer, who holds direct operational responsibility for the specific PVC, is mandated to collect every item of material that was supplied to the centre for the conduct of the poll. This collection process must be thorough and encompass all documents, stationery, and equipment used during the voting days. Once collected, these materials cannot remain at the temporary voting venue but must be securely transported to the main headquarters of the election administration. The retention of these items at the headquarters is not arbitrary but follows a standard protocol established for the specific category of material in question, ensuring chain of custody, security, and proper archival or disposal procedures as dictated by election laws. This step is critical for maintaining the integrity of the electoral process and ensuring that no sensitive materials, such as marked ballot papers or voter registers, are left vulnerable at the temporary site after operations cease.

Regarding the participation ofAbsentee Voters, specific rules apply to those who are registered to vote at a PVC but fail to appear in person during the scheduled voting window. If an Absentee Voter does not present themselves at the PVC to cast their vote, there is absolutely no requirement or provision for the election authorities to subsequently send a postal ballot to that individual via regular mail. The procedure prescribed for this specific category of voters, often classified under special service categories such as armed forces or government employees on duty, is exclusively limited to voting in person at the designated Postal Voting Centre. There is no alternative method available to them; they cannot vote by mail from their location nor can they request a ballot be sent to them after missing their appointment at the PVC. This restriction ensures that the special voting mechanism remains controlled and verifiable within the secure environment of the polling station, preventing potential issues related to ballot secrecy or coercion that might arise from unsupervised postal voting outside the centre.

To facilitate the smooth conducting of elections within a Postal Voting Centre, each PVC must be fully equipped with a comprehensive set of necessary materials prior to the commencement of polling. The provisioning of these items is meticulous and calculated based on the number of Absentee Voters assigned to that specific centre. First and foremost, the centre must possess a sufficient quantity of postal ballot papers. This quantity is determined by matching the exact number of assigned Absentee Voters, with an additional surplus of ten percent added to the total. This buffer is a crucial contingency measure designed to address unforeseen circumstances where ballot papers might become damaged, torn, or incorrectly marked, thereby requiring the issuance of a fresh ballot paper under specific regulatory provisions such as rule 26(2). Without this surplus, a damaged ballot could disenfranchise a voter, so the extra supply ensures that every eligible voter has a functional ballot available to them.

In addition to the ballot papers themselves, the inventory must include a sufficient stock of specific envelope forms, namely Form 13A, Form 13B, and Form 13C. These forms serve distinct purposes in the sealing and transmission of votes, acting as the secure vessels that protect the voter's choice and identity during the transport phase. To enable the physical act of voting, the centre must be supplied with pens specifically intended for marking the vote on the ballot paper. These writing instruments must be reliable and provided in adequate numbers to prevent delays. Furthermore, glue is required for the secure closing of the various envelopes, ensuring that once a ballot is sealed, it cannot be tampered with before it reaches the counting centre. An ink-pad is also a mandatory item, typically used for applying indelible ink to the fingers of voters or for official stamping purposes to prevent multiple voting attempts and to authenticate documents.

The physical infrastructure of the voting process requires a dedicated box into which the envelopes containing the marked ballot papers are dropped. This ballot box serves as the primary secure repository for cast votes within the PVC and must be robust and sealable. Administrative tracking is maintained through a register specifically designed to obtain the signatures of electors at the moment they are issued their ballot paper. This register creates a legal record of who received a ballot, serving as a check against fraud. Essential to the verification process is the List of Absentee Voters assigned for voting in that specific PVC. This list must be provided with three extra copies to ensure that the Presiding Officer, polling agents, and reserve records all have access to the authorized voter roll. This document, often referenced as Annexure-A, contains detailed information including the serial number, name of the elector, part number, serial number within the part, and the EPIC number, which stands for Electors Photo Identity Card number, a unique identifier for each voter.

To ensure the privacy and sanctity of the vote, material for setting up a voting compartment is required. These compartments provide a private space where voters can mark their ballots without observation or pressure from polling staff, candidates, or agents. Logistics for post-voting transmission require large envelopes designed for sending the sealed ballots, along with the counterfoils of the ballot papers, to the Returning Officer on a daily basis. Counterfoils are the stubs attached to the ballot papers that remain with the election officials, allowing for reconciliation between issued ballots and cast votes. The supply list also includes general stationery items that may be required for various administrative tasks throughout the voting days. Finally, appropriate furniture must be arranged for the polling staff, the agents representing the candidates, and for the construction of the voting compartments, ensuring a functional and orderly environment for all participants.

The documentation associated with the Postal Voting Centre includes several annexures that serve as official records. Annexure-A functions as the master list of absentee voters in the AVES category, which typically refers to specific service categories entitled to vote through postal ballot at the PVC. This document records the name of the election, the name of the constituency, and the venue of the PVC. It lists voters by serial number, name, part number, serial number in the part, and their EPIC number. Crucially, this document requires the signature of the Returning Officer to validate the list, along with the date. It also bears the signatures and names of the Presiding Officer, recorded with dates, to attest to the accuracy and handover of the list. This multi-signature requirement establishes accountability at the highest levels of the local election management team.

Annexure-B serves as the log of absentee voters who have actually been issued a postal ballot at the PVC. Like the master list, it details the name of the election, the constituency, and the venue. This document acts as a real-time record of ballot distribution, ensuring that every ballot issued is accounted for against the master list of eligible voters. Complementing this is Annexure-C, which records the presence and details of the agents of candidates who are observing the proceedings at the PVC. Transparency in elections is maintained by allowing candidate agents to witness the process, and this annexure captures their names, the specific candidate they represent, and their signatures. This ensures that political parties have verified representation at the polling station to monitor fairness and adherence to procedure.

A further detailed register, often integrated into the daily workflow, tracks the specific actions of each voter. This record includes the serial number and name of the elector, cross-referenced with their serial number in the list of electors assigned to the PVC. It documents the specific document produced by the voter for identification purposes, verifying their identity before a ballot is issued. The date on which the voter cast their vote is recorded, along with the signature or thumb impression of the elector. The inclusion of a thumb impression is vital for voters who may be illiterate or unable to sign, ensuring inclusivity while maintaining a biometric-style record of participation. This level of detail in the register provides a robust audit trail that can be reviewed in case of any disputes regarding voter eligibility or turnout.

An important procedural update noted in the guidelines specifies that a separate list should be pre-printed for each day of postal voting. Since the voting period spans three days, maintaining a single cumulative list could lead to confusion or errors in tracking daily turnout. By generating distinct lists for day one, day two, and day three, election officials can better manage the flow of voters, reconcile daily ballot counts more accurately, and isolate any issues to a specific day if they arise. This segmentation of data enhances the granularity of election monitoring and simplifies the end-of-day reporting process where materials are sent to the Returning Officer. The combination of these detailed material requirements, strict procedural rules regarding non-attendance, and comprehensive documentation protocols forms a rigorous framework designed to uphold the integrity, secrecy, and efficiency of the postal voting process for absentee voters. Every element, from the ten percent surplus of ballots to the daily segregation of voter lists, is engineered to minimize human error and maximize the security of the democratic exercise.

The procedural framework governing the issuance of Election Duty Certificates to voters who are simultaneously serving as election officials is a critical component of ensuring democratic integrity while accommodating the logistical realities of conducting large-scale elections. This specific mechanism is grounded in the provisions of sub-rule two of rule twenty within the Conduct of Elections Rules established in nineteen sixty-one. The core objective of this regulation is to address the unique situation faced by public servants, such as Polling Officers, Presiding Officers, and other government employees assigned to election duties within the very same constituency where they are registered as eligible voters. Under normal circumstances, a voter must cast their ballot at a specific polling station assigned to their residential address within the electoral roll. However, when an individual is deployed to work at a polling station on the day of the election, they cannot leave their post to travel to their home polling station to vote. To resolve this conflict between duty and franchise, the rules provide a special pathway that allows these individuals to vote in person at the location where they are working, provided they follow a strict application and verification process.

The process begins when a voter who has been assigned election duty within their own constituency decides they wish to exercise their right to vote in person rather than through alternative means like postal ballots. This individual must submit a formal application using a specific document known as Form Twelve A. This application must be directed to the Returning Officer, who is the senior official responsible for the conduct of the election in that particular constituency. Timing is a crucial element of this procedure; the application must reach the office of the Returning Officer at least four days prior to the scheduled date of the poll. However, the rules also provide a degree of flexibility, allowing the Returning Officer to accept applications submitted within a shorter timeframe if specific circumstances warrant such leniency. Upon receipt of the application, the Returning Officer has the responsibility to verify the claims made by the applicant. The officer must be fully satisfied that the applicant is indeed a public servant currently engaged in election duty within the constituency and that their name appears on the electoral roll of that same area. Once this verification is successfully completed, the Returning Officer issues an official document called the Election Duty Certificate, which corresponds to Form Twelve B. This certificate serves as a legal authorization, permitting the holder to cast their vote at any polling station within the constituency where they happen to be deployed on the actual day of the election, effectively overriding the standard requirement to vote only at their designated home booth.

To ensure the security and traceability of these certificates, the production of Form Twelve B is subject to strict administrative controls. These certificates must be printed in sufficient quantities to meet the anticipated demand, and this printing can be carried out either centrally by the Chief Electoral Officer of the state or regionally by the District Electoral Officer, depending on what is deemed most convenient and efficient for the administration. A vital security feature of these certificates is that each one must bear a unique serial number, ensuring that every issued certificate can be individually tracked and accounted for, thereby preventing fraud or duplication. When examining the structure of the application form, Form Twelve A, one finds specific columns designed to capture essential details about the applicant's dual role. The form requests information regarding the number and name of the polling station within the constituency where the applicant has been posted for election duty. Additionally, it requires the applicant to provide the serial number and part number of the electoral roll section in which their name is registered as a voter. These details are necessary to cross-reference the individual's status as both an employee and an elector.

However, a practical challenge arises in the completion of Form Twelve A due to the timing of administrative assignments. While certain static data points, such as the assembly constituency name, the part number of the electoral roll, the specific serial number of the voter within that roll, and the Electors Photo Identity Card number, are already available in the existing employee databases, the specific assignment of a polling station is often not determined until later in the planning process. The allocation of specific polling stations to election staff is conducted through a system of randomization designed to prevent bias and ensure fairness. Typically, the final assignment of a specific polling station to an individual occurs only after the third round of this randomization process is completed. Consequently, at the time when the voter on election duty needs to submit their Form Twelve A application, they may not yet know the exact polling station where they will be deployed. Recognizing this logistical reality, the rules explicitly state that the applicant is not required to fill in the details regarding the specific polling station at the time of application. This provision prevents unnecessary delays and confusion, allowing the application process to proceed based on the known variables while leaving the specific deployment location to be updated internally once the randomization is finalized.

A critical safeguard embedded in this system is the method used to prevent double voting. Since a voter on election duty is being authorized to vote at a potentially different location than their home booth, there must be a mechanism to ensure they do not vote twice—once using the Election Duty Certificate and once at their original polling station. To achieve this, the rules mandate that once an Election Duty Certificate is issued, the letters EDC must be clearly indicated against the name of that person in the marked copy of the electoral roll. The marked copy of the electoral roll is the version of the voter list used at polling stations on election day, which contains annotations indicating who has already voted. By marking the entry with EDC, the officials at the voter's home polling station are alerted that this individual has been issued a certificate to vote elsewhere. This annotation ensures that if the person attempts to vote at their original entitlement station, they will be denied, thus preserving the principle of one person, one vote. With this certificate in hand, the voter on election duty can legally exercise their franchise in person at the polling station where they are actually deployed to perform their duties, seamlessly integrating their civic right with their professional obligation.

The administrative workflow extends beyond just the general public servants to include specific protocols for polling staff and law enforcement personnel, recognizing the distinct chains of command and database structures involved. In the case of general polling staff, the issuance of appointment orders and the drafting of individuals onto election duty typically occur after the first round of randomization. At this stage, the administration ensures that a copy of Form Twelve A is sent to the concerned staff members. Depending on the status of the digital records, this form may be pre-filled with data generated directly from the employee database, containing all the known electoral particulars, or it may be a blank form if the database has not yet been populated with the specific electoral roll details. This ensures that every staff member has the necessary documentation to apply for the certificate without having to manually research their own voter registration details.

For police personnel, who are also classified as voters on election duty but operate under a different hierarchical structure, specific arrangements are made by the Superintendent of Police or other competent officers within the district. The Superintendent of Police is responsible for maintaining a comprehensive database of all police personnel serving in the district. This database is not limited to service records but must also include detailed elector information for each officer. Specifically, it must populate fields such as the number and name of the Assembly Constituency where the officer is registered to vote, as well as the Part Number and Serial Number of the electoral roll where their name appears. This integration of electoral data into the police administrative database is crucial for facilitating the voting process. The Superintendent of Police is also tasked with preparing the deployment plan for police officials well in advance of the election date. At the stage of creating this deployment plan, it becomes clear whether a police officer will be working within the same constituency where they are registered as a voter or in a different constituency altogether. This distinction determines the method by which they will vote. Those police personnel who are posted to duty within their own registered constituency are eligible to vote using the Election Duty Certificate mechanism described earlier. Conversely, those who are deployed to constituencies outside their own registration area are not eligible for the EDC for in-person voting in the host constituency; instead, they become eligible to vote through a Postal Ballot, which allows them to mail their vote back to their home constituency.

To manage these complex logistics for the police force, the Superintendent of Police must appoint a dedicated Nodal Officer. This individual serves as the central coordinator for all activities related to facilitating the exercise of franchise by police personnel, whether through the postal ballot system or the Election Duty Certificate route. The Nodal Officer acts as the liaison between the police department and the election commission machinery. It is the responsibility of the Superintendent of Police or this designated Nodal Officer to distribute the appropriate application forms to the police personnel. Specifically, Form Twelve A is provided for those seeking an Election Duty Certificate, while Form Twelve is provided for those who need to apply for a Postal Ballot. This distribution ensures that every officer has access to the correct paperwork based on their deployment status. Furthermore, the Superintendent or the Nodal Officer bears the responsibility of ensuring that these applications are completed correctly. They must verify that the electoral roll details are duly entered into the forms and that each form is signed by the respective police personnel. Once verified, these applications must be forwarded to the concerned Returning Officer of the relevant constituency. A strict deadline applies here as well; these applications must reach the Returning Officer at least seven days before the date of the poll. This earlier deadline, compared to the four-day window for general public servants, accounts for the additional coordination required within the police hierarchy and ensures that the Returning Officer has ample time to issue the EDC or Postal Ballot and make the necessary entries in the marked copy of the electoral roll to prevent duplicate voting.

The physical act of voting for those utilizing the postal ballot system, particularly among the police and other designated groups, follows a structured protocol to maintain secrecy and security. A voter on election duty who is eligible for a postal ballot will receive their ballot paper through the established chain of command or at a designated facilitation center. The individual must then record their vote on the ballot in secret, seal it according to the prescribed instructions, and return it to the facilitation center. Arrangements for this entire process, including the distribution of ballots, the provision of private spaces for marking votes, and the collection of sealed ballots, must be made at these facilitation centers. These centers serve as secure hubs where the integrity of the postal voting process is maintained. Similar administrative structures are replicated for other categories of workers appointed for specific election-related duties, such as drivers, conductors, cleaners, and support staff. For these groups, a Nodal Officer may also be appointed to oversee the process. Just like with the police and general polling staff, the enrollment details for these individuals, including the number and name of their constituency, the part number of their electoral roll section, and other identifying information, must be accurately captured and processed to ensure they can either obtain an Election Duty Certificate or a Postal Ballot depending on the location of their duty relative to their voter registration. This comprehensive approach ensures that no eligible voter is disenfranchised due to their commitment to serving the election process, while simultaneously upholding the rigorous standards of electoral security and preventing any possibility of fraudulent double voting.

The procedural framework for managing voting rights of government personnel drafted for election duty is a critical component of ensuring democratic integrity and administrative efficiency. When individuals such as drivers, conductors, and other support staff are officially assigned to work during an election, their status as voters must be carefully managed so that their professional obligations do not disenfranchise them. The first step in this process involves the Nodal Officer, who acts as the central coordinating authority responsible for verifying the identity and registration details of every person selected for duty. This officer must ascertain the specific serial number of each individual's entry within the official electoral roll, which is the comprehensive list of all registered voters in a given area. Once these details are confirmed, the Nodal Officer provides the appropriate application forms based on the location of the assigned duty relative to the voter's home constituency. If the official is posted to perform duties within the same constituency where they are already registered to vote, they are issued Form 12A, which serves as an application for an Election Duty Certificate. Conversely, if the official is deployed to a different constituency far from their place of registration, they are provided with Form 12, which is an application for a Postal Ballot. This distinction ensures that voters can exercise their franchise either through a special certificate at their duty station or via mail if they are geographically displaced from their home polling area.

The timeline for processing these applications is strictly regulated to guarantee that all necessary documentation is completed well before voting begins. The Nodal Officer bears the responsibility of ensuring that all submitted Forms 12 and 12A, which must include fully entered electoral roll details and bear the signature of the drafted employee, are transmitted to the relevant Returning Officer no later than seven days prior to the date of the poll. The Returning Officer is the senior official in charge of conducting the election in a specific constituency, and receiving these documents early is vital. This lead time allows the Returning Officer to make the required annotations in the marked copy of the electoral roll, which is the master document used at polling stations to track who has voted. Only after these entries are made can the Election Duty Certificate or the Postal Ballot be legitimately issued. For those receiving a postal ballot, the procedure mandates that the voter receives the ballot paper, records their vote in private, and returns the completed document to a designated facilitation center. These centers are specially established locations equipped to handle the secure collection and processing of votes from election officials, ensuring that the chain of custody for the ballot is maintained and that the vote is counted correctly.

Administrative efficiency requires that the marking of the code EDC, which stands for Election Duty Certificate, in the marked copies of the electoral roll be executed as soon as possible after officials submit their Form 12A applications. There is a specific provision noting that Returning Officers should not insist on the inclusion of the Polling Station Number in the Form 12A application at the initial stage. This flexibility is necessary because the preparation of the marked electoral roll cannot be delayed until the final issuance of the certificates. Once an official has successfully submitted a Form 12A application, their voting method becomes restricted; they are authorized to cast their vote only by using the Election Duty Certificate, often referred to procedurally as Form 12B, at the specific polling station where they are assigned to work. If an official has not been allocated a specific polling station, they may utilize the certificate at a nearby polling station within the same constituency. This rule prevents double voting and ensures that the individual is accounted for in the system as a duty-bound voter rather than a regular constituent voter. Furthermore, this protocol extends to higher-ranking officials such as Zonal Magistrates or Sector Officers. Even if such an official is deployed in a parliamentary constituency where they are themselves a registered voter, they must be issued an Election Duty Certificate. This enables them to exercise their right to vote at any polling station falling within the specific zone or sector to which they have been allotted for supervisory duties, thereby integrating their civic duty with their professional deployment.

The determination of whether a specific piece of polling personnel is assigned to a fixed polling station or held in reserve is finalized immediately following the second randomization process. Randomization is a security measure used to assign staff to locations in a unpredictable manner to prevent bias or collusion. At this precise stage, the total number of staff members destined for deployment inside polling stations within the Parliamentary Constituency becomes known. It is imperative that officials who qualify for the Election Duty Certificate are briefed about these facilities during their very first training session. As part of this preparatory phase, a copy of Form 12A must be distributed to all officials requisitioned for election duty along with their formal duty orders. These officials are instructed to complete and sign the form when they report for their first training class. A key logistical detail addressed in the guidelines is that officials are not required to mention the serial number and name of the specific polling station where they will be working on Form 12A at this early juncture. This omission is intentional and practical, as the specific posting location is often unknown before the third randomization of polling personnel takes place. Requiring this information prematurely would cause unnecessary delays and confusion.

Accuracy in data entry is paramount when completing Form 12A. In scenarios where the form is generated automatically from a central database, the official must carefully review the pre-printed electoral roll details. They are tasked with correcting any errors found in critical fields such as the Assembly Constituency Number and Name, the Part Number and Serial Number of their entry in the electoral roll, and their EPIC number, which is the Electors Photo Identity Card number serving as a unique identifier. If the form is not pre-generated and must be filled out manually, the official is required to write all details legibly to avoid processing errors. Recognizing that many officials may not memorize their specific part number and serial number from the electoral roll, the administration must provide full assistance to help them locate this information. To facilitate this, physical or digital copies of the electoral rolls are organized and kept at separate counters, arranged by assembly segment. A sufficient number of support staff are stationed at these counters to assist election duty officials in finding their names and corresponding entry numbers quickly. This support system ensures that every polling personnel can accurately complete the particulars relating to their electoral roll entries on Form 12A without hindrance.

To maintain the integrity of the issuance process and prevent fraud, the submission of Form 12A must be accompanied by specific supporting documents. The official must attach a copy of their official duty order, which proves they have been legally drafted for election work, and a photocopy of their EPIC card. These documents serve as verification measures to ensure that no ineligible person is mistakenly issued an Election Duty Certificate. The collection of these completed and signed forms occurs during the first training class, where they are gathered and sorted according to assembly segments to streamline subsequent processing. The District Election Officer, who oversees elections at the district level, is responsible for making immediate arrangements to forward all received Form 12A applications to the concerned Returning Officer. Upon receipt, the Returning Officer proceeds to prepare the Election Duty Certificate for every individual who has submitted a duly signed and complete application. This preparation must be concluded well before the second training session or facilitation day. The objective is to have the physical Election Duty Certificates ready for delivery to the electors exactly when they gather for their second round of training or facilitation. Finally, during the creation of the Election Duty Certificate, specific formatting rules apply to the identification numbers. The Part Number of the voter's entry in the electoral roll must be suffixed after the Serial Number in the designated space on the certificate format. For instance, if an elector has a serial number of 4 and belongs to part 5 of section 25, the entry would be formatted as 4 I 5/25, ensuring a standardized and unambiguous record that links the certificate directly to the master electoral roll.

At the stage where Electronic Dispatch Cards, commonly referred to as EDCs, are distributed to election staff, it is imperative that a rigorous and proper account of every single card issued be maintained in a dedicated register. This register serves as the primary legal record for tracking the movement and custody of these voting instruments. The process requires that the full names of all polling personnel who are issued an EDC be clearly entered into this log, alongside the unique serial number assigned to each specific card. This dual entry of name and serial number ensures that there is no ambiguity regarding which individual holds which device. Furthermore, to validate the transaction and confirm receipt, the signature of the polling personnel must be obtained directly against their respective entry in the register at the exact moment the EDC is handed over to them. This signature acts as a formal acknowledgment of responsibility for the card. In scenarios where individuals have submitted an application for an EDC but fail to collect the device on the designated second day of training or facilitation sessions, they should not be denied the facility. Instead, these individuals may be instructed to collect their EDC from the Returning Officer, often abbreviated as RO, or the Officer-in-Charge on a subsequent date that is determined at the discretion of the Returning Officer. This flexibility ensures that all eligible staff can participate regardless of scheduling conflicts during the initial training phase. Additionally, any leftover personnel who have not yet received their cards can be provided with an EDC at the time the polling party is being dispatched to their assigned locations, ensuring no eligible voter among the staff is left out due to logistical timing.

Special provisions exist for polling personnel who are kept as reserves rather than being assigned to a fixed station immediately. If these reserve personnel are deployed to duty at any specific polling station on the actual day of the poll, they are entitled to cast their vote using the EDC within that same polling station where they are working. For other staff members who remain on reserve and are not assigned to any particular polling station, the regulations allow them the flexibility to vote at any polling station located near the place where the reserve personnel are currently stationed. This proximity-based rule facilitates convenience and operational efficiency for mobile staff. Upon arrival at the polling stations, the Presiding Officers, who are the senior officials in charge of the booth, have a critical duty to brief the polling agents. These agents represent the various political candidates and parties. The briefing must explicitly cover the facility provided to polling personnel allowing them to vote in that specific station using an EDC. It is also vital to make it unequivocally clear to these agents that once an EDC has been issued to a staff member, that individual can vote only on the basis of that EDC and cannot use any other method or standard voter identification for that specific election event. This prevents double voting and maintains the integrity of the electoral roll.

When a polling staff member presents an EDC to cast their vote at a polling station, the Presiding Officer is legally bound to follow a strict set of actions as mandated by rule 35A of the Conduct of Election Rules, 1961. The first step in this procedure is to obtain the signature of the person producing the EDC directly on the card itself, serving as immediate proof of usage. Following this, the Presiding Officer must ensure that the name and the electoral roll details, exactly as mentioned in the EDC, are entered at the very end of the marked copy of the electoral roll. This marked copy is the version of the voter list that has been annotated to show who has already voted. Along with the name, the particulars of the electoral roll details must be recorded. Each such entry made for an EDC voter must be serially numbered consecutively, continuing directly after the last serial number of the existing entries in the relevant part of the electoral roll. This sequential numbering integrates the EDC voters into the official count without disrupting the original order of the registered electorate. Once these entries are made, the physical EDC shall be retained by the first polling officer or the Presiding Officer, effectively taking the card out of circulation for the remainder of the voting process. Thereafter, the person is allowed to proceed to the voting compartment and cast their ballot by following the usual standard procedure applicable to all voters.

The documentation of these votes extends to Form 17A, which is the official register of voters maintained at the polling station. In the specific column meant for the serial number of the elector in the electoral roll, the officer must meticulously mention the serial number, the part number, and the name of the Assembly Segment of the EDC holder. An Assembly Segment refers to the specific geographic constituency or district for which the election is being held. To illustrate this with a concrete example, if the EDC holder is originally enrolled at Serial Number 415 in Part Number 25 of an Assembly Segment named 'XYZ', the entries in column 2 of Form 17A for that individual would be recorded as '415/25/XYZ'. This format provides a complete reference trail back to the voter's home registration. Furthermore, in the remarks column of Form 17A, the phrase "EDC voter" must be distinctly written to categorize the nature of the vote. Regarding the timing of the vote, polling personnel utilizing the EDC facility should ideally cast their vote during hours when polling agents are present in the polling station to ensure transparency and observation. The only exception to this rule is in cases where no polling agent turns up at all, in which case the staff may vote to avoid disenfranchisement. In the specific case of reserve polling personnel, they should be strongly advised to vote as soon as possible at the beginning of the polling day. This urgency is necessary because they may be required later for duty or might need to be shifted to different polling stations to meet emergent situations or staffing shortages, and their presence at the booth is prioritized for operational needs.

Administrative accountability continues through the Presiding Officer's Diary, specifically referenced as Annexure-52. The Officer must note the total number of electors who voted upon production of an EDC against the relevant item in this diary, creating a daily log of such occurrences. This data feeds into the final accounting forms. In Item Number 1 of Form 17C, Part-I, which deals with the total number of electors assigned to the Polling Station, the figures must reflect a composite total. This total comprises the number of electors as per the relevant part of the standard electoral roll plus the additional electors who voted on production of an EDC. The presentation of these figures should show the actual number of electors assigned as per the total number in that part of the electoral roll first, followed immediately by the count of votes polled on production of an EDC. For instance, if the total number of electors normally assigned to a polling station is 1125 and 9 votes were cast on production of an EDC, then in Form 17C it shall be explicitly shown as "1134 (1125 + EDC 9)". This mathematical clarity ensures that the inclusion of EDC voters does not inflate the perceived size of the constituency but rather accounts for the temporary addition of polling staff voters.

The physical security of the used EDCs is paramount. The Presiding Officer shall gather all the EDCs produced and surrendered in the polling station and place them in a separate, dedicated envelope. On the exterior of this envelope, the Officer must clearly mention the number and name of the polling station as well as the total count of EDCs contained inside. This envelope shall then be securely sealed and returned along with the other election material to the collection center, ensuring a chain of custody. Upon arrival at the collection centre, all envelopes containing EDCs should be kept separately from other documents and materials. These envelopes should later be stored in a separate trunk or secure container to prevent mixing or loss, preserving them for potential audits or verification. Finally, the regulations address the contingency of a repoll, which is a situation where voting must be conducted again in a specific polling station due to irregularities or disruptions. In the event of any repoll in any polling station, the electors who previously voted in that polling station on the basis of the EDC shall be issued a fresh EDC. This new issuance enables them to exercise their franchise once again during the repoll at the concerned polling station, ensuring that their right to vote is preserved even in the event of procedural resets.

When an election official known as an EDC or Election Duty Certifier casts a vote at a specific polling station and there is subsequently a need to repoll or verify their details, the identity and relevant information of that individual can be ascertained by opening the secure envelope in which the EDC documents for that particular polling station are stored. This procedure ensures transparency and accountability in the electoral process. The District Election Officer, commonly referred to as the DEO, bears the primary responsibility for coordinating all activities related to this process, ensuring that every step is executed smoothly and in accordance with established legal frameworks. In certain regions of the country, specifically the states of Mizoram and Nagaland, as well as the Union Territories of Andaman and Nicobar Islands, Chandigarh, Ladakh, Lakshadweep, and Puducherry, the administrative structure is unique because each of these areas comprises only a single parliamentary constituency. Due to this singular constituency status, every category of voter who is assigned election duty in these regions will be issued only an EDC, simplifying the logistical requirements compared to areas with multiple constituencies. However, in states where simultaneous elections are conducted for both the Lok Sabha, which is the lower house of India's parliament, and the Legislative Assembly, which is the state-level legislative body, the protocol differs significantly. In such scenarios, all voters engaged in election duty, including polling personnel, police officers, drivers, cleaners, videographers, and any other category of individuals serving on election duty, will be issued only a Postal Ballot Paper. This specific arrangement is necessary because, under existing randomization norms regarding the posting of duties, these individuals are often assigned roles within their Parliamentary Constituency but outside their specific Assembly Constituency. Without this special provision, they would theoretically receive one EDC for the Parliamentary Constituency election and a Postal Ballot for the Assembly Constituency election, creating logistical complexities. Therefore, issuing only the Postal Ballot Paper streamlines the voting process for these duty-bound individuals.

The framework for voting through postal ballots has been expanded to include absentee voters belonging to vulnerable categories, specifically senior citizens, persons with disabilities, and those suspected of or affected by Covid-19. This expansion is grounded in the Conduct of Elections Amendment Rules of 2019 and 2020, read in conjunction with a pivotal decision made by the Election Commission on the sixteenth of July, 2020. These legal instruments have collectively granted the facility of voting via postal ballot paper to three distinct groups. The first group comprises senior citizens who are above the age of eighty years, recognizing the physical challenges they may face in traveling to polling stations. The second group includes persons with disabilities who have been officially flagged as such in the electoral roll, ensuring that mobility or sensory impairments do not disenfranchise them. The third category encompasses individuals who are either suspected of having contracted Covid-19 or have been confirmed as affected by the virus, a measure introduced to prevent the spread of infection while safeguarding democratic participation. The process for applying for this postal ballot facility requires an absentee voter to submit a formal application to the Returning Officer, who is the chief electoral authority for the specific constituency in question. This application must be made using a designated document known as Form-12D, wherein the applicant must provide all requisite personal and electoral particulars. It is critically important that this application reaches the office of the Returning Officer within five days following the date on which the election is officially notified. This strict timeline ensures that the electoral machinery has sufficient time to process requests and distribute materials before the actual polling day.

According to the amended provisions found under Rule 27E and Rule 27-1 of the Conduct of Elections Rules of 1961, the issuance of postal ballot papers to absentee voters and the subsequent return of these ballots after voting must be carried out in a manner directed by the Election Commission. Acting on this authority, the Commission has laid down detailed procedures and guidelines to facilitate voting for senior citizens, persons with disabilities, and those impacted by Covid-19. A specific provision exists for electors who are hospitalized due to Covid-19 in a hospital within the state or are undergoing home or institutional quarantine because of the virus. If medical advice dictates that such an individual cannot personally cast their vote at a polling station, and if they make a formal request for a postal ballot, the concerned Returning Officer, upon being satisfied regarding the genuineness of the application, is mandated to provide the postal ballot. The Returning Officer must also arrange for the delivery of the ballot to the elector and ensure its collection back from the said elector before the date fixed for the poll in that constituency. This entire logistical arrangement is to be made in coordination with a designated Nodal Officer for Covid-19. Furthermore, any application for a postal ballot from such electors, submitted via Form-12D, must be accompanied by a copy of a certificate or instructions issued by competent health authorities. This documentation must clearly demonstrate that the applicant is either hospitalized within the state or is in quarantine, whether at home or in an institution, due to Covid-19. The format for this certificate is standardized based on specific Election Commission instructions to maintain uniformity and prevent fraud.

The operational execution of this system involves the Booth Level Officer, or BLO, who plays a crucial role at the grassroots level. The BLO is tasked with visiting the houses of the concerned electors within the polling station area, as identified and provided by the Returning Officer, to deliver Form-12D directly to them. In instances where an elector is not available at their residence during the initial visit, the BLO is required to share their contact details and schedule a revisit to collect the form within five days of the election notification. The elector retains the choice to opt for or decline the postal ballot facility, a decision which is recorded in an acknowledgement section attached to Form-12D. If the elector chooses to opt for the postal ballot, the BLO must collect the filled-in Form-12D from the elector's residence within the stipulated five-day window and deposit it immediately with the Returning Officer. The BLO is also responsible for depositing all acknowledgement forms, regardless of the elector's choice, along with the completed Form-12D submissions, to the Returning Officer. This entire process is supervised by the Sector Officer, who operates under the overall supervision of the Returning Officer to ensure compliance and efficiency. As per specific guidelines issued by the Election Commission, the Returning Officer is authorized to deploy specialized polling teams. These teams are responsible for delivering the postal ballots to the eligible absentee voters on pre-informed dates and subsequently collecting the voted ballots, ensuring that they are securely deposited with the Returning Officer well before the counting begins. Upon receipt of applications in Form-12D, the Returning Officer initiates the next phase of administration by drawing up a comprehensive list of all absentee voters falling into the three specified categories, utilizing a standardized format to track and manage the distribution and retrieval of postal ballots effectively.

The procedural framework for managing absentee voting involves specific categories of electors designated as AVSC, AVPD, and AVCO, provided their applications have been submitted within the stipulated deadlines and contain all necessary information in the correct format. These acronyms represent distinct groups of voters who are unable to visit polling stations on election day due to specific circumstances, such as service obligations or health-related restrictions. A critical milestone in the electoral timeline is the final date set for filing nominations for the election. On this specific date, the electoral roll, which serves as the official list of all registered voters, is effectively frozen. This freezing mechanism ensures stability and prevents any further additions or deletions of names from the register until the entire election process is concluded. During this phase, the Returning Officer, often abbreviated as RO, who acts as the primary administrator responsible for conducting the election in a specific constituency, must undertake a rigorous verification process. The RO is tasked with confirming that every individual applying for a postal ballot is indeed a registered elector. Furthermore, the officer must meticulously cross-reference the personal details provided by the applicants in Form I2-D, a specific document used to request absentee voting privileges, against the data contained in the existing frozen electoral roll to ensure absolute accuracy. Once the Returning Officer verifies that the particulars of an elector match the official records, that individual is authorized to receive a postal ballot paper. This issuance applies specifically to those classified under the categories of AVSC and AVPD. However, the procedure differs slightly for the third category, known as AVCO, which refers to voters absent due to COVID-19 infection or exposure. Before issuing Form 12D to these individuals, the Returning Officer must validate a medical certificate issued by a competent authority. This authority must be an entity duly appointed by the State Government or the Union Territory administration, and the certificate must explicitly state that the elector is currently undergoing either home quarantine or institutional quarantine as a direct result of COVID-19.

To maintain an accurate audit trail and prevent double voting, the Returning Officer is required to make specific annotations in the marked copy of the electoral roll. Against the names of electors who have been approved for postal ballots, the officer must enter the code PB. This marking serves as a visual indicator that a postal ballot paper has been dispatched or issued to that specific voter.
