It is important to note that while the PB entry is mandatory, the serial number of the actual ballot paper issued is not recorded in the roll itself, preserving a layer of secrecy regarding the specific ballot assigned to the voter. Concurrently, strict measures are enforced to ensure the integrity of the single-vote principle. The Returning Officer must guarantee that any elector who has already been issued a postal ballot is strictly prohibited from casting a vote in person at any physical polling station. Regarding the physical characteristics of the voting materials, the postal ballot paper designed for these Absentee Voters must be identical in form, layout, and language to the postal ballot papers used for voters who are away on official election duty. This standardization ensures uniformity across different types of absentee voting and prevents any confusion or perceived bias in the design of the ballot.

The logistics of delivering these ballots and collecting the marked votes involve a structured deployment of polling officials. Separate teams of poll officers are constituted specifically for this purpose. Each team must consist of at least two officials, and there is a rank requirement stipulating that at least one member of the pair must hold a rank or level equivalent to or higher than that of an official typically appointed as a polling officer for a standard Polling Station within the State. The total number of such teams deployed is not fixed but is instead determined dynamically based on the volume of Absentee Voters who have been issued postal ballot papers. The primary function of these teams is to physically visit the electors at the residential addresses provided in their Form 12D applications. This home visit facility is designed to facilitate the voting process for those who cannot travel to a polling booth. To ensure the electors are available and prepared for this visit, they must be informed in advance regarding the scheduled date and the approximate time of the officials' arrival. This notification process utilizes modern communication methods where possible; if the elector has provided a mobile phone number in their application, the intimation is sent via Short Message Service, commonly known as SMS. In instances where a mobile number is not available, the notification is delivered through traditional post or conveyed by the Booth Level Officer, a local official familiar with the voters in that specific area.

Protocols are also established for situations where an elector is unavailable during the scheduled visit. If the team arrives at the given address and finds the elector absent, they are required to leave a notice indicating the time of a subsequent attempt and then return for a second visit. This two-attempt rule balances the need to facilitate voting with the practical limitations of official resources. If the elector remains absent even during the second visit, the officials are under no obligation to make further visits or take additional action for that specific case. Transparency in this process is maintained by informing all contesting candidates about the schedule of visits for both the delivery and collection of postal ballots. Candidates are granted the right to depute their authorized representatives, which may include Booth Level Agents known as BLAs, to observe the entire process, provided they give prior intimation to the Returning Officer. To optimize efficiency, the master list containing the names of all AVSC, AVPD, and AVCO voters is divided and distributed among the various teams of polling officials. This division is executed strategically so that each team is assigned a list of persons residing in a geographically compact area, minimizing travel time and maximizing the number of voters served per day.

Upon arriving at the elector's residence, the visiting polling official must first ascertain and verify the identity of the elector before handing over the postal ballot paper. This step is crucial to prevent impersonation. Following successful identification, the name of the elector and details of the identification document produced are entered into a dedicated register, often referred to as Annexure-2 in official documentation. The elector is then required to provide their signature or thumb impression in this register as proof of receipt and participation. Simultaneously, a tick mark is placed against the elector's name in the master list of AVSC, AVPD, and AVCO voters to officially record that the person has cast their vote. The counterfoil of the ballot paper, which contains the serial number and part number associated with the elector, is detached and kept in safe custody by the team to ensure the chain of custody is maintained. The responsibilities of each polling team extend beyond mere delivery; they are tasked with issuing the postal ballot to every assigned Absentee Voter, providing a comprehensive briefing on the voting procedure, and ensuring that the elector casts their vote without any external influence. The officials must rigorously uphold the secrecy of the voting process throughout all activities conducted at the elector's address. However, provisions are made for inclusivity; if an elector is unable to cast their vote independently due to blindness or other physical infirmities, they are permitted to seek the assistance of any adult person of their choice to help them mark and submit their ballot, ensuring that disability does not disenfranchise any voter.

When briefing electors about the voting procedure and the formalities they must observe, the polling official is required to clearly explain several critical steps to ensure the validity and secrecy of the postal ballot. The first step involves the elector making a formal declaration using a specific document known as Form 13A. This form serves as a sworn statement confirming the voter's identity and eligibility to cast a postal vote. It is mandatory that this declaration be attested, or officially verified, by the poll officer who is present at the time. Following the completion of this declaration, the elector must enter the unique serial number of their postal ballot paper in two distinct locations: once on the aforementioned Form 13A and again on a smaller envelope designated as Form 13B. This dual recording of the serial number creates a crucial audit trail that links the voter's declaration to their specific ballot without revealing their choice. The official must then instruct the voter on the correct manner of marking their vote. The voter is permitted to indicate their choice by placing either a cross mark or a tick mark directly against the name of the candidate they wish to support. No other symbols or writings are allowed on the ballot paper to prevent invalidation. Once the vote is marked, the elector must carefully fold the ballot paper to conceal the marking and place it inside the smaller envelope, Form 13B, which must then be securely closed. The next phase involves combining all documents into a larger protective envelope, known as Form 13C. The elector must place the fully completed, signed, and attested declaration from Form 13A along with the sealed smaller envelope containing the marked ballot into this larger envelope. Finally, the elector closes the larger envelope and hands it over directly to the poll officer, completing the casting process.

The authority to attest the declaration found in Form 13A is specifically granted to the poll officer when dealing with certain categories of absentee voters, identified by the codes AVSC, AVPD, and AVCO. These codes generally refer to voters who are absent due to service obligations, physical disabilities, or confinement, though the specific definitions are managed internally by election authorities. When performing the attestation, the poll officer is not merely signing the document; they are legally required to write out their full name in legible script and explicitly state their designation as 'poll officer' to establish their official capacity. In special circumstances involving a person affected by an infectious disease such as COVID-19 who is currently receiving treatment in a hospital, the standard protocol is adapted to ensure safety and accessibility. In such cases, the Medical Officer who is attending to the patient is also authorized to attest the declaration. This provision ensures that health protocols do not disenfranchise voters who are isolated due to illness, allowing a trusted medical professional to verify the voter's identity and intent while maintaining necessary health safeguards.

Once the vote has been successfully cast via the postal ballot and the final package in Form 13C is prepared and sealed, the responsibility shifts to the polling officials to collect these envelopes. This collection is a systematic process designed to maintain the chain of custody for every ballot issued. To facilitate these operations effectively, each team of polling officials must be equipped with a comprehensive kit of materials before departing for their assignments. This kit includes a verified list of electors falling under the AVSC, AVPD, and AVCO categories to ensure that only eligible individuals are visited. The team must carry an adequate supply of postal ballot papers to cover all scheduled visits, along with the requisite sets of envelopes including the smaller Form 13B and the larger Form 13C. Essential stationery items such as pens for writing and ink-pads for capturing thumb impressions are vital, particularly for voters who may be unable to sign their names due to physical limitations. Additionally, glue sticks must be provided to ensure that envelopes can be sealed securely without the need for moisture, which might damage documents or spread infection. All collected envelopes containing voted ballots and their corresponding counterfoils, which serve as receipts and records, must be stored in a sufficiently large canvas bag. This bag acts as a secure container to keep the sensitive electoral materials safe during transport. Any other essential stationeries deemed necessary for the smooth execution of the duty should also be included in the team's inventory.

Given the sensitive nature of handling electoral materials and visiting voters in various locations, robust security measures are mandated. Police security cover must be provided to accompany the poll officers throughout their visits to ensure their safety and to prevent any potential tampering or intimidation. Furthermore, to enhance transparency and provide an objective record of the proceedings, arrangements for videography must be made at the addresses of the AVSC, AVPD, and AVCO electors. A dedicated videographer is required to accompany the polling team to capture the entire process, from the explanation of procedures to the handing over of the sealed envelope. However, this requirement for documentation comes with a strict caveat: it must be ensured that the secrecy of the vote is never violated during the videography exercise. The camera must not capture the marked ballot paper or any indication of how the voter cast their vote. The focus of the recording should remain strictly on the procedural compliance and the interaction between the official and the voter, safeguarding the fundamental principle of the secret ballot while ensuring accountability.

The logistical planning of these visits is subject to strict timelines to ensure that all postal votes are received and processed before the main polling day. Visits by the teams of poll officers to the residences or locations of AVSC, AVPD, and AVCO electors must be planned and executed in such a manner that the entire operation is completed one day prior to the date fixed for the poll in that specific constituency. For example, if the general election polling is scheduled to take place on the 10th day of a month, the postal voting process for these absentee categories must be fully concluded by the 9th day of that same month. This buffer day is critical for the transportation, sorting, and secure storage of the ballots before they are counted alongside regular votes. Additionally, for poll officers who are deputed to visit AVCO electors, who may include those in quarantine or isolation due to health crises, appropriate protection equipment must be provided. This includes Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) kits to protect both the officials and the voters. The provision of such equipment should be done in close consultation with the Nodal Health Officer responsible for AVCOs, ensuring that the latest health guidelines are followed and that the risk of disease transmission is minimized during the electoral process.

At the conclusion of each day's visits to the addresses of AVSC, AVPD, and AVCO electors, a rigorous deposit and accounting procedure is initiated. The envelopes in Form 12C, which contain the marked postal ballot papers, along with the counterfoils of the ballot papers that bear the signature or thumb impression of the electors and other identifying particulars, must be collected by the Assistant Returning Officer (ARO) designated specifically for postal voting by absentee voters. The ARO plays a pivotal role in the chain of custody, making immediate arrangements to have these materials delivered to the headquarters of the Returning Officer (RO) for safe custody. This centralization ensures that the ballots are stored in a high-security environment under constant surveillance. The ARO is also responsible for maintaining a detailed record that indicates the exact number of ballots sent out and collected on each day of the operation. This daily log is not merely an internal record; it must be shared with the Returning Officer on a daily basis to allow for real-time monitoring of the progress and to detect any discrepancies immediately. Finally, to ensure fairness and transparency in the electoral contest, all contesting candidates will be duly informed about these guidelines issued by the Election Commission. This notification allows candidates and their agents to be aware of the procedures being followed, thereby fostering trust in the integrity of the postal voting mechanism and the overall election process.

Within the administrative framework of any election, specifically in the office designated for each Returning Officer, it is a mandatory procedural requirement to assign a responsible official to manage the logistics and verification processes related to postal voting. This individual should ideally be selected from the pool of Assistant Returning Officers to ensure a high level of competence and authority. The primary duty of this assigned officer is to remain available at all times with a current copy of the electoral roll, which serves as the master list of all registered voters. Their presence is critical to assist any person who has been drafted for election duty, allowing them to approach the office and verify whether their name appears correctly on the electoral roll. This verification step is essential because only those whose names are properly listed are eligible to participate in the electoral process, either as voters or as workers. Furthermore, this same responsible officer must be deputed to travel to the training venues where election personnel gather for their briefings. At these locations, the officer acts as the receiving authority for Form 12, a specific document that individuals on election duty must complete and submit to apply for a postal ballot paper. It is a fundamental rule that postal ballot papers intended for polling personnel must be issued strictly in person, ensuring a direct chain of custody and preventing unauthorized distribution. To maintain rigorous accountability, a separate register must be established and maintained exclusively for tracking the issuance of postal ballot papers to each distinct category of electors. This register functions as an audit trail and must contain several specific columns of information to ensure no voter is disenfranchised or allowed to vote twice. The first column must record the full name of the person who has been drafted for election duty and who has subsequently applied for a postal ballot paper using Form 12. The second column requires the name and the unique identification number of the Assembly Constituency in which that person is currently serving their election duties. The third column must indicate clearly, with a yes or no response, whether the individual is already registered in the electoral roll of the constituency where they are serving. If the answer to the previous query is affirmative, the fourth column must detail the specific Part Number and the serial number assigned to that person within the electoral roll of the constituency, providing a precise locational reference within the massive database of voters. The fifth column is dedicated to recording whether a postal ballot paper has actually been issued to the applicant and confirming that a necessary entry has been made in the marked copy of the electoral roll against the name of the person; this entry must include the specific date on which the ballot was issued and the date the record was updated. The sixth column tracks the return process, noting whether all persons who collected a postal ballot have successfully returned their completed ballot paper to the designated facilitation center after recording their vote. The final column is reserved for remarks, allowing officers to note any anomalies, special circumstances, or additional observations relevant to the transaction. This entire register must be prepared strictly adhering to the format provided in Annexure 25 of the election guidelines to ensure uniformity across all jurisdictions.

Once the distribution phase is complete, a critical security procedure known as the sealing of the marked copy of the electoral roll must be undertaken. This process involves securing the marked copy of the electoral roll, which includes not only the main list but also the sub-lists detailing polling station-wise CSVs, which refer to the lists of voters entitled to vote by post, as well as the records of any proxies appointed to vote on behalf of others. This sealing action can only occur after every single postal ballot paper for voters who are entitled to vote by post and every Election Duty Certificate has been officially issued. Once sealed, this document must be arranged for delivery to the concerned Presiding Officer, who will utilize it as the authoritative marked copy of the electoral roll at the specific polling station on election day. It is of paramount importance that this specific version of the electoral roll, which bears the recorded letters PB indicating a postal ballot has been issued and EDC indicating an Election Duty Certificate has been granted, is the exact copy used at the polling station alongside the polling station-wise list of CSVs. The necessity of this strict protocol cannot be overstated; if an incorrect or unmarked copy were used, there would be a significant risk that a voter who has already cast their vote via postal ballot or through an election duty certificate could inadvertently be allowed to vote again in person at the polling station, thereby violating the principle of one person, one vote. Simultaneously, the Returning Officer is responsible for the secure handling of the counterfoils, which are the stubs or duplicate parts of the postal ballot papers that remain after the actual ballot is issued to the elector. These counterfoils must be placed in a separate packet, securely sealed to prevent tampering, and kept in the safe custody of the Returning Officer to serve as a permanent record of issuance.

In scenarios where a voter encounters issues with their initial documents, provisions exist for the issuance of a second set of postal ballot papers in exchange for the first set, but this is permitted only under very strict conditions to prevent fraud. The Returning Officer may authorize the release of a second postal ballot paper, along with all other connected paperwork, only if the voter physically returns the entire original set of papers that were previously issued to them. Furthermore, the voter must satisfy the Returning Officer that the spoiling of the first set of papers occurred through genuine inadvertence or accidental error, rendering them completely incapable of being used for voting. Mere dissatisfaction with how the ballot was filled out is not sufficient grounds; the damage must be such that the ballot is unusable. Upon receipt of the spoiled documents, the Returning Officer is obligated to immediately cancel the returned papers to ensure they cannot be reintroduced into the system. These cancelled papers must then be sealed in a dedicated packet, with the serial numbers of the specific postal ballot papers clearly noted on the exterior of the packet, creating a transparent record of the exchange and destruction of the compromised materials.

The integrity of the postal voting system relies heavily on the proper attestation of the declaration form, a legal requirement mandated by Sub-rule 2 of Rule 24 of the Conduct of Elections Rules, 1961. This rule stipulates that any elector wishing to vote by post must sign a declaration in Form 13-A, and this signature must be executed in the physical presence of, and attested by, specific authorized officials. The list of competent authorities for this attestation includes a stipendiary magistrate, who is a paid judicial officer; or, in the case of a service voter belonging to the armed forces or similar establishments, an officer appointed by the Commanding Officer of the unit or establishment to which the voter belongs. For citizens employed by the Government of India in posts located outside the country, the attestation must be performed by the concerned diplomatic or consular representative, such as an ambassador or consul. Within the domestic jurisdiction, the list extends to an officer holding a rank not below that of Deputy Secretary to the Government of India or a state government, as well as any Gazetted Officer, which refers to a senior public servant whose appointment is notified in the official gazette, or the Presiding Officer of the polling station where the voter is posted. Additionally, the Superintendent of a jail or the Commandant of a detention camp is authorized to attest declarations for voters under their supervision. Finally, any other officer specifically designated for this purpose by the Election Commission of India may also perform this function. Despite these clear regulations, there have been allegations that candidates and their supporters engage in malpractice by collecting postal ballot papers from voters before the vote has been recorded on them. In such schemes, the declaration is signed and attested later, sometimes by obliging attesting officers who violate the law by attesting signatures even when the electors are not present in person. To guard against such potential malpractices, the Election Commission has formally requested all State Governments and High Courts to alert every stipendiary magistrate and insist that the formal legal requirements regarding the positive identification of the elector and the strict attestation of their signature on the declaration be followed without exception whenever they are called upon to perform this duty. It is also crucial for the Returning Officer to note that under current laws, honorary magistrates, who serve in an unpaid or ceremonial capacity, are no longer considered competent to attest any postal ballot paper, and any attestation by such an individual would render the ballot invalid.

In the intricate framework of electoral administration, specific distinctions are drawn regarding the authority of various public officials to validate legal documents. A fundamental rule establishes that an individual serving as a member of the Parliament or a member of a State Legislature does not automatically qualify as a Gazetted Officer for the specific purpose of attesting declarations related to postal ballot papers. While members of these legislative bodies hold high public office, the designation of a Gazetted Officer typically refers to a government official whose appointment is published in the official government gazette and who possesses specific statutory powers to verify identities and authenticate documents. This exclusion ensures that the attestation process remains within the purview of designated administrative officers who are specifically empowered and trained for such verification tasks, thereby maintaining a clear chain of custody and legal validity for the sensitive voting materials used by absent voters.

The logistical machinery required to manage the return of postal ballots through the mail system involves a highly coordinated arrangement between the election administration and the national postal service. The Chief Electoral Officer, who serves as the top election authority at the state level, is mandated to establish a formal agreement with the Postal Department to facilitate the secure and timely return of these ballots. Under this arrangement, the Postal Department is instructed to nominate exactly one post office for each Parliamentary Constituency and each Assembly Constituency. This nominated post office acts as the dedicated collection and dispatch hub for that specific geographic area. Its primary responsibility is to ensure that all postal ballots received within its jurisdiction are delivered every single day to the respective Returning Officer. The Returning Officer is the key official responsible for conducting the election in a particular constituency, and this daily delivery mechanism is critical to ensuring that votes cast by mail are not delayed in transit and are available for processing well before the counting begins.

To maintain strict temporal discipline in this process, a fixed cutoff time for the delivery of these ballots is established at the office of the Returning Officer. This deadline is set at 3 pm every day throughout the election period. This consistent schedule allows the election staff to process incoming ballots systematically each afternoon. However, an exception is made for the day on which the votes are actually counted. On counting day, the urgency is significantly higher, and the delivery time is moved to early morning. The postal service must deliver the ballots before 8 am, or alternatively, before whatever other time has been officially fixed for the commencement of the counting proceedings at the designated counting centre for that Assembly constituency. This adjustment ensures that every possible vote delivered by post is included in the count before the tallying process begins, preventing any disenfranchisement due to minor delays on the final day. To enable this precise coordination, the list of all counting centres along with the specific addresses of the Returning Officers' offices must be communicated in writing by the Chief Electoral Officer to the Postal Department, ensuring that the mail carriers have accurate destination data.

Transparency and the observation of the electoral process by stakeholders are paramount principles in democratic elections. Consequently, all recognized political parties and all candidates who are contesting the election must be informed in writing regarding the schedule for the receipt of postal ballots. They are explicitly notified that they, or their authorized representatives, have the right to remain present at the location during the specific time window when the post office delivers the postal ballots. This provision allows party agents and candidate observers to witness the handover of sensitive voting materials firsthand, thereby building trust in the integrity of the system. To facilitate access for the postal staff who are performing these deliveries, especially on the high-security counting day, a special pass must be issued to the nominated employee of the Postal Department. This pass grants them authorized entry into the counting centre, which is otherwise a restricted zone, solely for the purpose of delivering the accumulated postal ballots.

Upon the arrival of the postal ballots, a rigorous procedure is initiated to document the transaction. The bundles of ballots delivered by the post office are not simply accepted; they are counted immediately in the presence of the representatives from the political parties and the candidates who are observing the process. This joint counting at the point of receipt serves as a verification step to ensure that the number of packets or envelopes handed over by the postal worker matches the expectations and records. Once the count is verified by all present parties, an official acknowledgement stating the exact number of postal ballots received is generated and given to the post office representative. This document serves as a receipt, releasing the postal service from further liability for those specific items. A copy of this acknowledgement is then meticulously preserved in the official records maintained by the Returning Officer, creating a permanent audit trail. Furthermore, the numerical data regarding the number of postal ballots received is entered into a specific daily log known as the daily return in format-3. This standardized form is used to track the flow of ballots day by day. To ensure an additional layer of accountability and evidence, the entire process of receiving and counting these incoming postal ballots is videographed, creating a visual record that can be reviewed later if any disputes arise.

The physical security of the ballots once they are inside the election office is managed through a strict storage protocol. The Returning Officer is required to take all the postal ballots received from the post office on any given day and place them into a separate envelope designated specifically for that day. This segregation by date helps in tracking the timeline of receipt and isolating any potential issues to a specific day's batch. On the exterior of this envelope, the date is clearly written along with the specific label words "Postal Ballots Received by Post." This labeling ensures that the contents are immediately identifiable without needing to open the secure package. Once sealed and labeled, this envelope is not left in general office areas; it is immediately transferred to the strong room designated for postal ballots. The strong room is a high-security vault or room with restricted access, designed to protect voting materials from tampering, theft, or damage. This transfer happens every day immediately after the post has been received and processed, ensuring that the ballots spend the minimum amount of time outside of maximum security confinement.

Monitoring and reporting the influx of postal ballots involve a multi-tiered hierarchy of documentation and communication. The Returning Officer is tasked with preparing a detailed return of the postal ballots received, not only from the direct post but also from facilitation centres, which are help desks set up to assist voters in submitting their ballots. This data is compiled into a document referred to as Statement-3. The officer must prepare this statement every single day until the period for receiving postal ballots from facilitation centres officially closes. Additionally, the number of postal ballots received specifically through the post is also entered into the return on Statement-3, and this tracking continues right up until the day of counting. This dual entry ensures comprehensive coverage of all ballot sources. The dissemination of this data is equally systematic. The Returning Officer must send a copy of the return in format-3 every day to the Chief Electoral Officer. This transmission is routed through the District Election Officer of the district, adding a supervisory layer to the reporting chain. Simultaneously, to keep the competitors in the election informed, a copy of the return in format-3 is also sent to all the candidates contesting in that specific constituency. This ensures that every candidate has real-time knowledge of how many postal ballots are entering the system.

At the state level, the Chief Electoral Officer aggregates this information to create a statewide picture of postal ballot reception. The Chief Electoral Officer compiles the data received from all constituencies into a master version of format-3 every day. This compiled state-level report is then transmitted to the Election Commission, the supreme body governing the conduct of elections in the country. This allows the central authority to monitor trends, identify anomalies, and oversee the smooth functioning of the postal ballot mechanism across the entire state. In keeping with the spirit of transparency, the Chief Electoral Officer also sends a copy of this compiled format-3 to all recognized political parties. This broad distribution of data ensures that major political entities are aware of the volume of postal ballots being processed statewide, allowing them to deploy their resources and observers effectively. The document known as format-3, which is central to this entire reporting ecosystem, contains specific fields for recording dates, constituency details, numbers of ballots received from various sources, and cumulative totals, serving as the backbone of the administrative tracking system for postal voting.

The procedural framework governing the handling of postal ballots in scenarios where the vote counting location differs from the Returning Officer's headquarters is designed to ensure maximum transparency, security, and chain-of-custody integrity. In specific electoral circumstances, the physical act of counting votes for a Parliamentary or Assembly Constituency may take place at a designated counting centre that is distinct from the official headquarters of the Returning Officer. When such a separation of locations occurs, strict protocols mandate the transfer of all postal ballots intended for that specific constituency to a dedicated strong room located within the counting centre itself. This transfer operation is scheduled to occur exactly one day prior to the actual day designated for counting votes. To facilitate oversight and maintain trust in the electoral process, the Returning Officer is legally obligated to issue a formal written notification to all contesting candidates, explicitly stating the precise time at which this critical transfer of ballot materials will be executed. Upon the appointed time, the strong room housing the postal ballots at the originating location must be opened only in the direct presence of the candidates or their duly authorized representatives. This requirement ensures that no unauthorized access occurs before the materials are secured for transport. Once the room is accessed under observation, every single postal ballot is carefully collected and placed into a large, robust steel box. This container serves as the primary secure vessel for the transit phase. The sealing of this steel box is a ceremonial and legal necessity that must also be performed in the immediate view of the candidates and their representatives, thereby providing visual confirmation that the contents are complete and untouched.

Following the sealing of the container, the steel box containing the sensitive postal ballots is transported to the strong room designated for postal ballots at the counting centre. This movement is not conducted casually but is undertaken under the strict guard of armed personnel from the Central Paramilitary Forces, ensuring physical security against any potential threats or tampering during transit. To further reinforce the transparency of this operation, candidates and their representatives are granted the explicit right to follow the vehicle carrying the sealed box throughout its journey to the counting centre. This allowance enables them to witness the entire logistical chain personally. Upon arrival at the counting centre, the box is once again placed inside the specific strong room allocated for postal ballots, and this action is repeated in the presence of the candidates and their agents. Once the box is safely stored inside, the strong room itself is sealed. As documentary proof of their presence and witness to the sealing procedure, the signatures of the candidates and their representatives are formally recorded. Furthermore, to prevent any possibility of interference while the ballots remain in storage overnight, candidates and their representatives are permitted to maintain a continuous watch over the strong room. The District Election Officer is responsible for providing reasonable facilities to accommodate these observers during their vigil. The entirety of this complex process, from the opening of the initial strong room to the final sealing at the counting centre, is subject to mandatory videography, creating an immutable visual record of all actions taken.

On the actual day designated for counting, the Returning Officer proceeds to open the sealed strong room and retrieves all postal ballots along with copies of the relevant pages from the registers that were received from various facilitation centres. These materials are brought to the specific table where the counting of postal ballots will commence. Before the actual counting of votes begins, a crucial preliminary step known as tallying is performed to verify the volume of ballots received. The envelopes delivered from the facilitation centres are opened individually, and the number of postal ballots discovered inside each envelope is meticulously counted. This physical count is then cross-referenced and tallied against the numbers recorded in the copies of the relevant register pages that accompanied the shipment from the facilitation centre. The results of this verification exercise are displayed to the candidates and their election agents prior to the commencement of the vote counting, ensuring consensus on the quantity of ballots to be processed. In a similar manner, the register documenting postal ballots that were received directly by post is also presented to the candidates and their agents for inspection and verification.

Regarding the custody of postal ballot papers that are returned within the legally prescribed timeframe, the Returning Officer bears the responsibility of keeping all covers containing these ballots in safe custody. The rules stipulate that postal ballot papers must be returned to the Returning Officer no later than the specific hour fixed for the commencement of the counting of votes for that constituency. To maintain an accurate and auditable account of these materials, the total number of postal ballot papers received within the deadline must be duly entered into a specialized register maintained specifically for this purpose. This register serves as the definitive log to ensure that no ballot paper is lost, misplaced, or unaccounted for during the pre-counting phase. The entries in this register are required to be made on a day-to-day basis as receipts occur, and at the conclusion of each day, these entries must be countersigned by either the Returning Officer or the Assistant Returning Officer to validate their accuracy and official status.

In contrast to timely returns, specific procedures govern postal ballot papers that are returned late. The Returning Officer is instructed not to decline or refuse acceptance of any cover containing a postal ballot paper even if it is delivered after the expiry of the fixed hour for its return. However, while the physical cover must be accepted, any postal ballot paper received in its cover too late, defined as arriving after the hour fixed for the commencement of counting or on any subsequent date, cannot be included in the count and must be rejected. For every such late receipt, the Returning Officer must note the exact date and time of receipt on the cover itself. These late covers are strictly prohibited from being opened and must be kept together as a separate collection for future reference should the need arise. Although the ballots inside are not counted, the serial number or identifier of each postal ballot received late must still be noted in the register to maintain a complete record of all issued materials. It is important to clarify that the ballot papers contained within these late covers do not need to be included in the total number of rejected votes reported in the Return of Election using Form 21-E. Furthermore, the final result sheet, known as Form 20, does not provide any designated space to display the number of postal ballot papers that were excluded from the count solely because they were received after the prescribed hour and date, effectively separating administrative tracking from the final vote tally.

To support the efficacy of this entire system, the Director-General of Posts has been formally requested to instruct all postal authorities regarding the provisions of law that establish it as their legal duty to arrange for the prompt and efficient delivery of every cover containing a postal ballot paper to the correct addressee. This directive is essential to ensure that the system of postal balloting functions satisfactorily and that voters who rely on mail-in voting are not disenfranchised by logistical delays. Under the prevailing rules, postal ballot papers are permitted to be returned to the Returning Officer under the classification of Service Unpaid. This means that the postage costs for returning the ballot are not borne by the voter but are instead collected upon delivery. Consequently, the Returning Officer is required to pay cash against each such delivery when the ballots arrive, ensuring that financial barriers do not impede the return of valid votes. This comprehensive set of procedures balances the need for strict security and chain-of-custody documentation with the practical necessities of logistics and legal compliance, aiming to preserve the sanctity of the electoral process while accommodating voters who cast their ballots via post.

In the administration of electoral processes, particularly concerning the logistics of postal ballot distribution and return, financial practicality dictates that it would be highly inefficient and operationally burdensome for a Returning Officer to settle cash payments repeatedly each time a batch of ballot covers is delivered. To streamline this fiscal procedure, it is strongly recommended that the Returning Officer establish a dedicated deposit account at the post office facility nearest to their office or the designated location where they receive these critical electoral materials. By maintaining such an account, the postal authorities are empowered to automatically deduct or adjust the applicable postage charges against the existing deposit balance whenever a delivery of covers occurs, thereby eliminating the need for transactional cash exchanges and ensuring a seamless flow of materials. This system ensures continuity and reduces administrative friction during the high-pressure period of an election. Furthermore, specific considerations apply to voters serving in foreign capacities, known as Foreign Service Voters. In standard circumstances, the issue of their postal ballot papers being returned to the Returning Officer marked as Service Unpaid does not arise because the office where the voter is currently stationed is responsible for affixing the necessary postage stamps prior to dispatch. However, a distinct protocol exists for instances where these ballot papers are transported via a diplomatic bag. In such cases, the covers may arrive at the Returning Officer's desk bearing the endorsement Service Unpaid, reflecting their mode of transport rather than a lack of postage payment. It is incumbent upon the election officials to maintain a meticulous account or register of all such covers received through this diplomatic channel to ensure proper tracking and accountability. Regarding the financial responsibility for redirected postal ballot papers sent to Service Voters, any expenditure incurred for obtaining a certificate of posting for these redirected items is to be funded directly from the grants allocated to the Ministry of Defence, ensuring that the election commission's budget is not burdened by these specific defense-related logistical costs. To guarantee the integrity and timeliness of the voting process, the Director-General of Posts issues mandatory instructions to all postal authorities under their command. These instructions emphasize the imperative of prompt delivery of all postal ballot papers to the Returning Officer, specifically on the day of polling. This directive overrides normal operational schedules, meaning that postal deliveries must proceed irrespective of whether the day falls on a Sunday or coincides with an official post office holiday, recognizing that the sanctity of the election timeline supersedes regular weekly rest periods or public holidays.

Transitioning to the technical specifications and design standards for ballot papers used in conjunction with Electronic Voting Machines, often abbreviated as EVMs, strict regulatory frameworks govern their physical and visual attributes. On every individual Balloting Unit of an EVM, which is the interface where the voter casts their vote, a physical ballot paper strip must be displayed in the designated space provided for this purpose. This requirement is grounded in Rule 49B of the relevant election rules, which stipulates that the ballot paper, referred to technically as a label within sub-rule four of that same regulation, must contain specific particulars and be printed in the language or languages explicitly specified by the Election Commission. In adherence to updated instructions issued by the Commission to enhance voter recognition and transparency, the ballot papers displayed on the Balloting Unit must now include not only the previously mandated details but also a photograph of each contesting candidate. The placement of this photograph is precisely defined; it must be printed within the panel allocated for the candidate's name and must appear on the right-hand side of the name, situated specifically between the candidate's printed name and their election symbol. This spatial arrangement ensures that the voter can visually correlate the name, the image, and the party symbol without confusion. The dimensions of the photograph are strictly regulated to maintain uniformity across all machines, with the printed image required to measure exactly two centimeters in breadth and two point five centimeters in height. The Commission has further detailed the exact form and linguistic requirements for these ballot papers to ensure consistency nationwide.

The physical dimensions of the ballot paper vary slightly depending on the manufacturer of the Electronic Voting Machine, reflecting minor engineering differences between the two primary state-owned manufacturers, ECIL and BEL. For machines manufactured by ECIL, which stands for Electronics Corporation of India Limited, the total length of the ballot paper strip is standardized at four hundred and sixty millimeters, while its width is fixed at one hundred and forty millimeters. Conversely, for machines produced by BEL, or Bharat Electronics Limited, the length of the ballot paper is marginally longer at four hundred and sixty-one point five millimeters, though the width remains consistent at one hundred and forty millimeters. These precise measurements are critical to ensure that the paper feeds correctly through the mechanical components of the Balloting Unit without jamming or misalignment. At the very top of the ballot paper, a specific horizontal space is reserved for indicating the essential particulars of the election and the name of the constituency. The dimensions of this header space differ based on the machine manufacturer; for ECIL machines, this area measures twelve point seven millimeters in length by one hundred and forty millimeters in width, whereas for BEL machines, the height of this section is reduced to seven point five millimeters while maintaining the same width. Within this designated top section, specific printing protocols are enforced. On the top left-hand corner, the unique serial number of the ballot paper must be printed to facilitate tracking and audit trails. On the top right-hand corner, a sheet number is to be printed, but only in scenarios where the ballot paper extends across more than one sheet, a situation that arises when the number of contesting candidates exceeds fifteen. Additionally, the core particulars of the election must be printed within this top space. These particulars include the serial number and the full name of the Parliamentary Constituency or the Assembly Constituency, as applicable to the specific election, along with the year in which the election is taking place and the nature of the election, such as whether it is a general election or a bye-election. Crucially, these specific election particulars must be printed in the English language alone to ensure a standardized reference point across diverse linguistic regions.

To clarify the format of these election particulars, specific examples illustrate the required notation. For a General Election to the House of the People, which is the lower house of the parliament, the text should read in the format of the constituency number followed by the name and the abbreviation for Parliament Constituency, the year, and the abbreviation for General, such as one-New Delhi PC dot two thousand four-Genl. In the case of a Bye-Election to the House of the People, triggered by a vacancy between general elections, the format adjusts to include a slash and the word Bye, for instance, one-New Delhi PC dot slash two thousand five-Bye. Similarly, for a General Election to the Legislative Assembly of a state, the format would be three hundred and four-Etawah AC dot slash two thousand four-Genl, where AC denotes Assembly Constituency. For a Bye-Election to the Legislative Assembly, the format follows as three hundred and four-Etawah AC slash two thousand five-Bye. There are provisions for flexibility in layout based on the number of candidates. If the number of contesting candidates is fewer than fifteen, the aforementioned particulars may be printed either at the very top or at the bottom-most portion of the ballot paper, specifically in the space originally intended for a sixteenth candidate, whichever is deemed more convenient from a printing and logistical perspective. However, if the number of contesting candidates is fifteen or more, necessitating a multi-sheet ballot or a denser layout, these particulars may alternatively be printed in a vertical column running down the left-hand side of the ballot paper. In this vertical configuration, a thick dividing line must separate this column of election details from the main panels containing the serial numbers, names, photographs, and symbols of the contesting candidates, as well as the option for None of the Above, commonly known as NOTA. Finally, immediately below the space provided at the top for printing the election particulars, a thick black horizontal line must be printed to visually demarcate the header from the candidate list. The thickness of this line is also manufacturer-specific, measuring two point one millimeters for ECIL machines and one point zero zero millimeters for BEL machines, ensuring clear visual segmentation for the voter.

The design and layout of official ballot papers for elections involve a highly structured set of regulations to ensure clarity, fairness, and uniformity for every voter. Below the designated starting line on the ballot paper, there must be printed specific details for each individual who is contesting the election. These details include the unique serial number assigned to the candidate, their full name, a clear photograph of their face, and the specific political symbol that has been officially allotted to them by the election authorities. Each of these candidates is presented within a distinct rectangular section known as a panel, which serves as a separate visual container for their information. This arrangement ensures that voters can easily distinguish between different contenders. Immediately following the panel that contains the name and symbol of the very last candidate on the list, there is a mandatory additional panel situated directly below it. This final panel bears the words "None of the Above," often abbreviated as NOTA. This option allows voters to express a preference for none of the listed candidates. Alongside these words, the specific symbol designated by the Election Commission for the "None of the Above" option must be printed, granting it the same visual status as any political party symbol.

The physical dimensions of these candidate panels are strictly regulated and vary slightly depending on the manufacturer of the voting machines with which the ballot paper is used. In cases where the Electronic Voting Machines are manufactured by ECIL, which stands for Electronics Corporation of India Limited, each candidate panel must measure exactly 25.4 millimeters in length and 140 millimeters in width. However, if the machines are produced by BEL, or Bharat Electronics Limited, the dimensions adjust slightly to a length of 27.5 millimeters while maintaining the same width of 140 millimeters. These precise measurements are critical to ensure that the paper ballots align perfectly with the electronic sensors or display mechanisms of the voting equipment. To further enhance visual separation and prevent confusion, the panels for individual candidates are divided from one another by a thick black line. The thickness of this dividing line also depends on the machine manufacturer. For ECIL-manufactured units, the black line separating the panels must be 2.1 millimeters thick, whereas for BEL-manufactured units, the line is slightly thinner at 1.00 millimeter. These visual cues help voters clearly identify where one candidate's section ends and the next begins.

The order in which candidates appear on the ballot paper is not arbitrary but follows a strict sequential logic based on the official list of contesting candidates. The names must be arranged on the ballot in the exact same order in which they appear on the master list filed with the election officials. Regarding the capacity of a single sheet of ballot paper, there is a limit to how many candidates can be displayed legibly. No more than fifteen candidates may be arranged on one single sheet. The "None of the Above" option is always positioned immediately after the name of the last candidate on that sheet. If the total number of contesting candidates, including the "None of the Above" option, amounts to sixteen or fewer, the space remaining below the final panel on the sheet is left completely blank. The only exception to this blank space rule is if specific particulars regarding the election, such as the constituency name or election date, are required to be printed at the bottom of the page as per other regulatory clauses. However, elections often feature a large number of participants exceeding the capacity of a single sheet. In scenarios where the number of contesting candidates surpasses fifteen, the ballot paper must be expanded and printed across two sheets of the previously mentioned standard size and dimensions. If the number of candidates grows even larger, exceeding thirty-one individuals, the ballot expands to three sheets. Should the number of contesting candidates exceed forty-seven, the ballot paper extends to four sheets. Through this modular expansion, the system is capable of accommodating up to 383 candidates distributed across 24 balloting units, ensuring that even crowded electoral fields can be managed without compromising the format.

When a ballot paper requires multiple sheets due to a high number of candidates, the distribution of names follows a precise numerical sequence to maintain order. On the second sheet of a multi-page ballot, the names of the contesting candidates ranging from serial number 17 to serial number 32 are printed. The third sheet continues this sequence, displaying candidates from serial number 33 to serial number 48. Any candidates numbered 49 and onwards are printed on the fourth sheet. This logical progression ensures that voters can follow the serial numbers seamlessly from one page to the next. In situations where the total number of candidates does not fill an entire sheet, such as having fewer than 32 candidates total, the space below the panel of the last contesting candidate on the second sheet, specifically after the "None of the Above" option, is kept blank. This same rule applies to the third and fourth sheets; any unused space at the bottom of a sheet that is not the final sheet of the ballot remains empty to avoid confusion. To help voters and election officials navigate these multi-sheet documents, each individual sheet must clearly indicate its position in the sequence. In the dedicated space provided for election particulars on each sheet, the sheet number must be printed in bold words and letters, using labels such as "Sheet No. 1", "Sheet No. 2", and so forth. This labeling prevents pages from being separated or misordered during the voting process.

Language consistency and numerical formatting are also vital components of the ballot design. The names of all contesting candidates, as well as the "None of the Above" option, must be printed in the same language or languages that were used to prepare the official list of contesting candidates. This ensures that the ballot reflects the linguistic demographics and official notifications of the specific region. Furthermore, the serial number assigned to each candidate must be displayed using the international form of Indian numerals, which are the standard digits recognized globally (0, 1, 2, 3, etc.), rather than traditional regional numeral scripts, to ensure universal readability. Within each candidate's panel, the layout of information is standardized for quick recognition. The serial number, the name of the candidate, and their photograph are printed on the left-hand side of the panel. Conversely, the political symbol allotted to the candidate is printed on the right-hand side. The photograph plays a crucial role in voter identification and is placed within the panel specifically designated for the candidate's name. Visually, the photograph appears on the right side of the candidate's name but sits between the name and the symbol, acting as a bridge between the text and the icon. The size of this photograph is strictly controlled, measuring 2 centimeters in breadth and 2.5 centimeters in height, providing a clear but compact image of the contender.

The "None of the Above" panel serves a democratic function by providing a formal mechanism for voters to reject all available candidates. This panel is located immediately after the panel containing the name, photograph, and symbol of the last candidate on the list. It contains the words "None of the Above" along with its specified symbol, designed specifically for electors who wish to exercise their right to not vote for any of the candidates in the fray. The text in this panel must be written in the same language or languages used for the candidate names to maintain consistency. The physical size of the "None of the Above" panel is identical to the panels used for the candidates, giving it equal visual weight. For instance, if there are twelve candidates contesting an election, the ballot will feature a thirteenth panel dedicated to "None of the Above" with its symbol, and the corresponding button or selection area for this thirteenth panel must remain active and open for voting. In cases where there are sixteen candidates, the system requires an additional Balloting Unit to be attached to the first unit to accommodate the "None of the Above" panel. A key operational rule dictates that when more than one Balloting Unit is utilized due to a large number of candidates, the "None of the Above" panel appears only once, situated in the very last Balloting Unit, directly below the panel for the final candidate on the entire list. This ensures that the option to reject all candidates is available exactly once per voter, regardless of how many sheets or units are required to list all participants.

In the administration of democratic elections, specific protocols govern the physical production and handling of ballot papers to ensure integrity, clarity, and operational efficiency. Unless the Election Commission issues a specific directive overriding standard procedure for a particular case, there is a established color-coding system for the paper stock used in printing these critical documents. For elections to the Parliament, which is the supreme legislative body at the national level, the ballot papers must be printed on white paper. Conversely, for elections to the Assembly, which refers to the state or provincial legislative bodies, the ballot papers are required to be printed on pink paper. This visual distinction serves as an immediate identifier for election officials and polling agents, helping to prevent the accidental mixing of ballots intended for different levels of government within the same electoral event.

Following the determination of paper color, extreme attention must be paid to the technical precision involved in the printing process, particularly when these papers are designed to be affixed onto electronic Balloting Units. The ballot papers are not merely standalone sheets but are integral components of the voting machine interface. They must be printed in such a precise manner that, once fixed onto the designated space on the Balloting Unit, the graphical elements align perfectly with the machine's physical structure. Specifically, the dividing line printed below the section containing the particulars of the election, such as the constituency name and election type, as well as the dividing lines situated below the panel of each individual candidate, must fall into complete alignment with the corresponding grooves or engraved lines on the surface of the Balloting Unit. These grooves are mechanical guides that help voters identify where to press or where the candidate information ends. If the printed lines do not match these engraved guides, it could cause confusion for voters or mechanical issues during the voting process. Consequently, utmost care is mandatory during the printing phase. To verify that the ballot papers have been correctly printed with the necessary dimensional accuracy, the technicians at the printing press must be shown the actual Balloting Unit they will be attached to. These technicians should be instructed to take exact measurements of the specific spaces allocated for printing the election particulars and the candidate panels, as well as the precise thickness of the dividing lines. This collaborative verification ensures that the final printed product fits the hardware exactly, maintaining the seamless integration between the paper ballot and the electronic unit. Furthermore, logistical efficiency is considered in the binding process; it is explicitly stated that ballot papers destined to be fixed onto Balloting Units do not need to be stitched into bundles, likely to facilitate easier handling and application by the technical teams at the polling stations.

A distinct category of ballot papers exists for exceptional circumstances known as tendered ballot papers. Under Rule 49P of the Conduct of Elections Rules enacted in 1961, a specific procedure is mandated for situations where an individual claiming to be a registered elector arrives at a polling station to vote, only to discover that another person has already cast a vote using that elector's identity. This scenario suggests potential impersonation or administrative error. In such cases, if the individual can satisfactorily answer questions regarding their identity posed by the Presiding Officer, who is the official in charge of the polling station, they are not permitted to vote through the standard electronic voting machine. Instead, they are supplied with a tendered ballot paper. This rule stipulates that the design and the language or languages used on the tendered ballot paper must be those specified by the Election Commission. The Commission has clarified that the tendered ballot paper must share the exact same design and be printed in the same language or languages as the standard ballot paper used for display on the Balloting Unit. Essentially, the visual content and layout of the tendered ballot and the machine-attached ballot are identical to ensure the voter receives the same information regardless of the voting method. The sole physical distinction is that the words "Tendered Ballot Paper" must be clearly stamped on the reverse side of each such ballot. This marking allows election authorities to segregate and account for these votes separately during the counting process, as they are recorded in a separate register rather than being tallied directly by the machine.

The logistics of election management require a rigorous assessment to determine the total quantity of ballot papers that must be printed. This calculation is not arbitrary but depends on several variable factors including the total number of polling stations to be established, the number of voting machines scheduled for use including reserve machines kept for emergencies, and the specific allocation of tendered ballot papers for each station. The total requirement is derived through a multi-step basis. First, one ballot paper is required for display on each individual Balloting Unit. Therefore, the baseline number of papers needed for this primary purpose is exactly equal to the total count of voting machines to be deployed across the constituency, inclusive of any reserve machines held in readiness. Second, a contingency supply for tendered votes is calculated. Each polling station may be supplied with twenty ballot papers to be utilized as tendered ballot papers if the situation described earlier arises. The total requirement for these tendered papers is computed by multiplying the number of polling stations in the constituency by this allocation figure. Third, to account for unforeseen contingencies, an additional buffer is required. Beyond the sum of the papers needed for the machines and the initial tendered allocation, an extra ten percent of that total number must be printed. This surplus is vital to meet various potential issues such as the mutilation or tearing of papers while they are being fixed onto the Balloting Units, the discovery of defective prints that render a paper unusable, or the need to supply additional tendered ballot papers to specific polling stations where unexpected numbers of challenged voters appear. These additional supplies are typically distributed through zonal or sector officers who oversee clusters of polling stations.

Finally, the organizational arrangements for the printing of these ballot papers follow a clear chain of command and communication. The responsibility for making arrangements to print the ballot papers required for a Returning Officer's constituency lies with the Chief Electoral Officer, who oversees the electoral process for the broader region, just as they do for other constituencies. For this process to commence without delay, it is imperative that the Returning Officer, who manages the election within a specific constituency, furnishes the Chief Electoral Officer with the official list of contesting candidates. This list must be provided in the prescribed format, known as Form 7A, immediately after it has been finalized. The finalization occurs after the last hour fixed by law for the withdrawal of candidatures and after the allotment of unique symbols to each candidate, which helps illiterate voters identify their preferred choice. Speed is of the essence in this transmission; the list should be sent to the Chief Electoral Officer via the fastest means of communication available at the time, such as fax, electronic mail, or by dispatching a special messenger to deliver the document physically. Once the printing is completed, the Returning Officer must also make special arrangements to collect the ballot papers from the printing press immediately. The security of these documents is paramount from the moment they leave the press. Therefore, the arrangements for transporting the ballot papers from the printing facility to the Returning Officer's headquarters must involve the use of a closed vehicle to prevent unauthorized access or tampering, accompanied by a proper security guard to ensure the safe and intact delivery of the sensitive electoral materials.

The Returning Officer bears the critical responsibility of maintaining the absolute integrity and security of all ballot papers from the moment they are received until they are required for the actual polling process. This duty necessitates that every single ballot paper be stored under strict lock and seal within a designated treasury or secure strongroom. This measure ensures that no unauthorized individual can access, tamper with, or remove any voting material before the election day. The security protocol extends beyond mere storage; it includes a rigorous phase of verification while the ballot papers are still being manufactured at the printing press. During this production stage, the Returning Officer must arrange for special inspections to confirm that the printed materials accurately reflect the official election data. Specifically, these checks ensure that the names of every contesting candidate are spelled correctly, that their official photographs are clearly and accurately reproduced, and that the election symbols allotted to each candidate by the Election Commission of India are printed without error. It is vital that the impression of these symbols matches exactly with the master designs approved by the Election Commission. To facilitate this precision, soft copies of the approved symbol designs can be obtained from authorized personnel responsible for designing these electoral icons.

Once the printed ballot papers are delivered from the press to the Returning Officer, a second layer of scrutiny is applied through a detailed counting and inspection process. The primary objective here is to reconcile the quantity of papers supplied by the printer with the actual number received and counted at the Returning Officer's office, ensuring there is no discrepancy in the total volume. During this physical examination, if any ballot paper is found to be defective, such as having smudged ink, torn edges, or printing errors, or if a paper is discovered to have a duplicate serial number or is missing a serial number entirely, specific procedural steps must be followed immediately. The Returning Officer is required to clearly record the details of every such defective or irregular paper in an official register. Furthermore, transparency is maintained by publishing a notice regarding these discrepancies on the notice board of the Returning Officer's office as well as at the distribution center where polling materials are handed out. To ensure all stakeholders are informed, a copy of this notice must also be sent formally to each of the contesting candidates, thereby keeping the entire electoral field aware of any issues with the voting materials before the poll commences.

Regarding the safe custody of undistributed ballot papers, there are instances where certain ballots remain with the Returning Officer after the primary allocation to polling stations has been completed. These undistributed papers are often reserved to be used as tendered ballot papers, which are issued to voters whose names appear on the register but who find that their vote has already been cast fraudulently, or for other exigencies that may arise on the day of the poll. On election day, these surplus ballots must be kept at a central, highly secure location under the direct supervision of the Returning Officer so they can be rapidly dispatched to any polling station that faces an unexpected shortage. Strict safeguards are mandatory to ensure that these undistributed papers remain exclusively in the custody of the Returning Officer or one of their Assistant Returning Officers who has been specifically nominated for this task. Under no circumstances should these sensitive documents fall into unauthorized hands. After the election concludes, any remaining undistributed or surplus ballot papers must be placed inside a sturdy steel trunk or similar secure receptacle. This container is then either transmitted to the Chief Electoral Officer or retained in the treasury under the specific orders of the Chief Electoral Officer, pending their eventual destruction in accordance with the procedures outlined in Chapter 19 of the relevant election manuals.

The preparation for commissioning Electronic Voting Machines, commonly known as EVMs, and the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail, or VVPAT, systems requires meticulous preliminary planning and assessment by the Returning Officer. Well before the election date, the officer must have accurately calculated the total requirement of EVMs and VVPAT units, including a sufficient number of reserve machines to handle any technical failures or unforeseen demands within the constituency. These machines must be procured through the appropriate channels, typically via the Chief Electoral Officer of the state or the District Election Officer of the respective district. Immediately after the final list of contesting candidates is finalized and published, the Returning Officer can determine the exact number of Ballot Units needed for each polling station. This calculation is based on the technical capacity of the hardware, where a single Ballot Unit can accommodate up to fifteen candidates, with an additional row reserved for the None of the Above option, known as NOTA. Consequently, the Returning Officer must perform an immediate reassessment of the Ballot Units required and procure any additional units without delay to ensure full coverage.

A significant evolution in election technology was introduced through the amendment in the Conduct of Elections Rules in 2013, specifically after rule 49A. This amendment added a proviso allowing for the attachment of a specialized printer with a drop box to the voting machine. This device, designed and approved by the Election Commission of India, creates a physical paper record of every vote cast electronically. This system is technically referred to as the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail or VVPAT. Following the directives of the Election Commission, the use of VVPAT in conjunction with EVMs has become mandatory at all polling stations for all elections to the House of the People, which is the lower house of the Indian Parliament known as the Lok Sabha, as well as for all State Legislative Assembly elections. This integration ensures that voters have a tangible verification of their vote while maintaining the efficiency of electronic counting.

The assessment of the specific quantity of EVM and VVPAT components required for a constituency follows a structured logical basis to ensure no polling station is left without equipment. First, regarding the Control Unit, which serves as the brain of the voting machine and records the votes, exactly one Control Unit must be supplied to each polling station. Therefore, the total number of Control Units needed is directly equal to the total number of polling stations established within that constituency. Second, the requirement for Ballot Units, which display the candidate names and buttons for voting, is dependent entirely on the number of contesting candidates in that specific election. The scaling is systematic: if the number of candidates including the NOTA option ranges from three to sixteen, only one Ballot Unit is required. If the number of candidates falls between seventeen and thirty-two, two Ballot Units must be linked to the Control Unit. For thirty-three to forty-eight candidates, three Ballot Units are necessary, and for forty-nine to sixty-four candidates, four Ballot Units are required, with this pattern continuing proportionally for larger fields of candidates. Finally, regarding the VVPAT machines, the rule mandates that one VVPAT unit must be supplied to each polling station alongside the EVM. Thus, the total requirement for VVPAT units is also exactly equal to the number of polling stations provided in the constituency, ensuring that every location has the capability to generate a paper audit trail for every vote cast.

In the comprehensive administration of electoral processes, specific protocols govern the quantification and reservation of essential voting hardware to ensure seamless operations at polling stations. Beyond the baseline calculation of Control Units and Ballot Units required for actual voting activities, a mandatory reserve stock must be maintained to address unforeseen contingencies or logistical challenges. This reserve must constitute no less than twenty percent of the total Control Units and Ballot Units designated for use. Furthermore, a higher threshold applies to Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail machines, commonly known as VVPATs, which serve as the physical verification mechanism for electronic votes; for these devices, at least thirty percent of the total inventory must be held in reserve. These percentages represent minimum standards, and the overseeing Election Commission retains the authority to direct an increase in these reserve figures based on unique geographical conditions, such as difficult terrain, remote locations, or areas prone to natural disruptions that might hinder the rapid deployment of replacement equipment. In scenarios where simultaneous elections are conducted for both the House of People, which is the lower house of the national parliament, and the Legislative Assembly of a state, the logistical requirements become more complex, necessitating two entirely separate sets of hardware. One complete set comprising Ballot Units, Control Units, and VVPATs must be dedicated exclusively to the elections for the House of People, while a distinct and separate set of the same components must be allocated for the state Legislative Assembly elections. This segregation ensures that votes cast for national representatives are not commingled with those cast for state representatives, maintaining the integrity and distinct counting procedures required for each legislative body.

Regarding the utilization of Electronic Voting Machines and VVPAT units for public awareness campaigns and the training of election officials, strict guidelines are enforced to prevent any compromise of electoral data or voter privacy. Only those units that have been certified as fully functional and okayed by the Field Level Checking process, often abbreviated as FLC OK, are permitted for use in training and awareness activities. It is imperative that before any machine is deployed for these educational purposes, a thorough verification is conducted to ensure that no data from previous elections remains stored within the device memory.
