To further safeguard against confusion or the accidental recording of test votes as real ballots, dummy symbols are used on the ballot papers and within the VVPAT displays during these sessions, ensuring that no actual candidate names or party symbols are present. The status of these specific EVMs and VVPATs, which may comprise up to ten percent of the total inventory, must be explicitly marked as Awareness within the Election Management System, a digital database used to track the lifecycle and status of all voting equipment. A transparent list detailing the serial numbers and specifications of these machines designated for awareness purposes must be formally provided to all National and State political parties as well as to individual candidates to ensure full visibility. Physically, these units are distinguished by the affixing of a bright yellow sticker clearly bearing the word Awareness, serving as an immediate visual indicator that the machine is not part of the active polling pool. These designated training units must be stored in secure facilities and accessed only by authorized officials deputed by the District Election Officer. These officials must undergo comprehensive training on the technical functioning of both EVMs and VVPATs, and a detailed logbook must be maintained to record every instance of usage, movement, and handling of these devices to ensure accountability.

The initial phase of equipment allocation, known as the first randomization, is a critical procedural step managed by the District Election Officer to ensure the impartial distribution of Control Units, Ballot Units, and VVPATs to various Assembly Constituencies or Assembly Segments. This process must be conducted according to a fixed schedule and, crucially, must take place in the presence of representatives from all National and State recognized political parties to guarantee transparency and build trust in the system. The randomization itself is executed within the Election Management System software, which algorithmically selects Control Units, Ballot Units, and VVPATs to match the specific polling day requirements of each constituency, including the previously mandated reserve quantities. Any surplus equipment that exceeds the immediate needs of the constituencies is retained at the District Election Officer level to be deployed on a need basis should emergencies arise. Once assigned, a green-colored sticker is affixed to each Control Unit, Ballot Unit, and VVPAT destined for a specific constituency. This sticker serves as a formal label clearly indicating the name of the State or Union Territory, along with the specific number and name of the Assembly Constituency or, in the case of Parliamentary Constituencies, the relevant segment. Additionally, the word Election is written on the stickers of Control Units and Ballot Units assigned specifically for polling duties to differentiate them from training units. It is important to note that during this first randomization phase, no current identification codes linking specific machines to specific polling stations are generated; the allocation is only at the constituency level. Following this procedure, a comprehensive list detailing which specific machines are assigned to which constituencies for polling, and which are set aside for training and awareness, must be distributed to every recognized political party, with a written acknowledgement obtained to confirm receipt. Even if a party representative is unavailable to attend the randomization in person, the list must be promptly sent to the party office, and a receipt must be secured. A copy of this finalized list is subsequently provided to all candidates after the last date for the withdrawal of candidatures has passed. Upon completion of this stage, the Returning Officer of the respective constituency assumes physical charge of the randomly allotted equipment, recording the receipt in the Election Management System via a dedicated mobile application. These machines are then transported under proper police escort to the strong room designated for the Returning Officer or Assistant Returning Officer. During the sealing of this strong room, representatives of political parties are invited to remain present and are permitted to affix their own seals on the locks, adding an additional layer of security and mutual oversight. Inside the strong room, a logbook is maintained to track all entries and exits, and the facility is guarded twenty-four hours a day, seven days a week, by a minimum of one section of police personnel, with Closed Circuit Television cameras covering both the strong room interior and the surrounding corridor to deter any unauthorized access.

The second randomization represents the final and most granular stage of equipment allocation, occurring after the first randomization but before the machines are prepared with candidate data for the actual poll. The primary objective of this second randomization is to allocate the already constituency-assigned EVMs and VVPATs to specific polling stations within that constituency, thereby breaking any potential predictability regarding which machine will be used at which location. The Returning Officer is responsible for fixing the date for this preparation process, often referred to as candidate setting, and must intimate this date in writing to all candidates, requesting their presence or that of their authorized representatives. This date is scheduled as early as possible following the last date for the withdrawal of candidatures and the finalization of the ballot paper layout to minimize the time machines spend in storage before the election. On the appointed date, the second randomization is performed within the Election Management System prior to the physical loading of candidate symbols onto the Ballot Units. This digital process ensures that the Control Units, Ballot Units, and VVPATs are randomly matched to specific polling stations, ensuring that no pattern can be discerned or manipulated. Once this allocation is complete, a detailed list is generated specifying which Control Units, Ballot Units, and VVPATs are assigned to which specific polling stations, alongside a separate list identifying the units kept in reserve at the polling station level. This comprehensive documentation is distributed to all candidates, and a written acknowledgement is obtained from each to confirm they have received the information. This rigorous two-tier randomization process, combined with multi-party oversight at every stage, forms the backbone of the technical security measures designed to uphold the sanctity, fairness, and credibility of the electoral exercise.

The meticulous preparation of Electronic Voting Machines, commonly known as EVMs, and their accompanying Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail units, or VVPATs, specifically during the critical phase of candidate setting, requires a rigorous adherence to established legal and procedural protocols designed to ensure transparency and integrity in the electoral process. The journey begins with the issuance of a formal written invitation that conveys the detailed schedule for these activities to all contesting candidates. This document serves not merely as a notice but as a mandatory summons, explicitly stating that the candidates themselves or their duly authorized representatives must be physically present to oversee the entire preparation workflow. Their role is active rather than passive; they are required to participate in the mock poll process, which is a trial run conducted to verify that the machines are functioning correctly and recording votes as intended. Following this participation, it is imperative that these representatives obtain a written acknowledgement confirming their presence and involvement, thereby creating an auditable trail of oversight. At this specific stage of the procedure, the Control Units, referred to as CUs, and the Ballot Units, known as BUs, along with the VVPAT devices that have been randomly earmarked for specific polling stations through previous randomization exercises, must be positively identified. This identification is achieved by matching the unique polling station numbers assigned to each device. Once matched, these corresponding units are kept together as a pair and physically tagged to ensure that the Control Unit, which stores the vote data, remains inseparably linked to its specific Ballot Unit and VVPAT printer throughout the election cycle, preventing any possibility of mixing or tampering.

A crucial aspect of this preparatory phase involves a second layer of randomization to further enhance security and unpredictability in the allocation of machines. This second randomization is conducted by different authorities depending on the type of election being held. In the context of General Elections or Bye-Elections for the State Legislative Assembly, the responsibility falls upon the Returning Officer of the specific Assembly Constituency concerned. Conversely, for General Elections or Bye-Elections to the Lok Sabha, which is the lower house of the national parliament, the Returning Officer of the Parliamentary Constituency takes charge. In scenarios where simultaneous elections are held for both the Lok Sabha and the State Legislative Assembly, a dual approach is adopted wherein the Returning Officer of the Assembly Constituency manages the process for the state assembly seats, while the Returning Officer of the Parliamentary Constituency handles the parliamentary seats. There is also a provision for exceptional cases regarding General or Bye-Elections to the Lok Sabha, where an Assistant Returning Officer may be empowered to conduct this randomization if circumstances dictate. This layered approach to randomization ensures that no single individual or entity can predict or manipulate which machine will be deployed at which polling station, thus safeguarding the sanctity of the vote.

Following the final date by which candidates may withdraw their nominations, the process moves into the commissioning phase of the EVMs and VVPATs. The Returning Officer is tasked with drawing up a comprehensive schedule for this commissioning, which must be executed in separate halls dedicated to each assembly constituency or segment to maintain organizational clarity and security. Just as in the earlier preparation stage, written intimation must be sent to all candidates informing them of the schedule, urging their presence to oversee the commissioning, and inviting them to actively participate in the mock poll process. Their signatures must be recorded in a register maintained specifically for this purpose, as outlined in the appropriate annexure of the official EVM-VVPAT manual. The Returning Officer has the discretion and duty to inform each candidate or their election agent about the specific number of representatives they are permitted to bring to the venue. This limit is not arbitrary but is calculated based on the total number of EVMs and VVPATs being prepared, ensuring that the hall does not become overcrowded while still allowing for adequate representation. The entirety of this preparation process is conducted under the watchful eyes of independent observers and is videographed in its entirety to create an immutable visual record of the proceedings.

It is a fundamental principle of electoral administration that the timeline of the election cannot be held hostage by the absence of political participants. Therefore, if for any reason none of the candidates or their agents appear at the designated date and time specified in the notice, the Returning Officer is strictly instructed not to postpone the preparation of the EVMs and VVPATs. The process must proceed without delay to adhere to the constitutional timeline of the election. Even in the complete absence of candidates or their agents, the Returning Officer must move forward with the commissioning. However, to maintain transparency despite the absence, public announcements must be made through the public address system installed within the building before the preparation begins, declaring the commencement of the activity for the record. If a candidate, their election agent, or a person duly authorized in writing by the candidate arrives late, they are not barred from the process; instead, they are allowed to witness the proceedings from the point of their arrival onwards, ensuring that latecomers are still afforded the opportunity for oversight without disrupting the scheduled workflow. When candidates and their agents are present, the Returning Officer bears the responsibility of explaining the detailed procedure that will be followed for preparing the voting machines, demystifying the technical steps for the political stakeholders involved.

In the specific context of Parliamentary Elections, the hierarchy of supervision is clearly defined, with the Assistant Returning Officer supervising the preparation of EVMs for each assembly segment within the larger parliamentary constituency. The physical arrangements within the premises where the EVMs and VVPATs are prepared are subject to extremely stringent security protocols. A large preparation hall is requisite, capable of accommodating the vast array of electronic machines, the officers nominated by the District Election Officer, the engineers from the concerned manufacturing companies, and the representatives of the candidates. To facilitate real-time monitoring, especially in large halls where visibility might be obstructed, a television or monitor is installed for every Symbol Loading Unit, or SLU, which is the device used to load candidate symbols onto the ballot units. These monitors are set up in consultation with the manufacturers, typically Bharat Electronics Limited or Electronics Corporation of India Limited, allowing candidates and their representatives to simultaneously view the loading process on screens distributed throughout the hall. The hall itself must be fully sanitized, meaning a thorough sweep is conducted to ensure the room is entirely free from any unauthorized electronic components, mobile phones, or listening devices that could potentially interfere with the machines or compromise secrecy.

Access control to this secure zone is absolute and multi-layered. The hall is designed with a single entry and exit point to streamline security checks and prevent unauthorized egress or ingress. This entry point is guarded round the clock by armed police forces, and the entire area is under twenty-four-seven closed-circuit television surveillance to deter and record any untoward incidents. Entry into the hall is strictly regulated through a Door Frame Metal Detector, and admission is granted only upon the production of a valid identity card or pass issued by the District Election Officer to authorized officials. Furthermore, every individual entering the premises is subjected to proper frisking by security personnel. A zero-tolerance policy is enforced regarding electronic devices; no one is allowed to carry any mobile phone, smartwatch, or other electronic gadget inside the hall, nor is anyone permitted to take any material out of the preparation hall once inside, preventing the leakage of sensitive information or the introduction of malicious hardware. While the main commissioning often occurs at the Returning Officer's headquarters, provisions exist for decentralization; the commissioning of machines pertaining to assembly segments falling within the Returning Officer's headquarters jurisdiction may be done at the Assistant Returning Officer level under the direct supervision of the Returning Officer. Similarly, the commissioning for remaining assembly segments is delegated to the respective Assistant Returning Officers, all conducted in the mandatory presence of candidates or their representatives to ensure uniform standards of oversight across the district.

The manpower deployed for the preparation and commissioning of these sensitive machines is carefully vetted and restricted to authorized personnel only. The actual commissioning work is carried out by officials deputed specifically by the Returning Officer or the District Election Officer. Crucially, the technical handling of the machines is restricted solely to authorized engineers from the designated manufacturers, such as Bharat Electronics Limited or Electronics Corporation of India Limited. These manufacturing entities are required to share a comprehensive list of their engineers with the District Election Officer prior to the event, including details such as their identity card numbers, to facilitate verification. Only those engineers whose names appear on this authorized list and who possess valid identification are permitted to enter the preparation hall. To ensure the reliability of the machines, a separate team is assigned to each EVM to conduct a mock poll wherein exactly one thousand votes are cast. This extensive testing protocol is designed to stress-test the machine's memory and counting logic, ensuring it can handle a high volume of transactions without error before being sealed for actual election day use.

Beyond the general procedural framework, there are specific additional tasks that must be executed during the preparation of the Ballot Units and Control Units, the details of which are elaborated in the latest official manuals on EVMs and VVPATs. One of the primary additional tasks involves the rigorous checking of the Ballot Units. This inspection is not superficial but involves a deep verification of the hardware integrity, the clarity of the candidate names and symbols displayed on the unit, and the functionality of the buttons associated with each candidate. The engineers and election officials must verify that the Ballot Unit corresponds exactly to the list of candidates finalized for that particular constituency, ensuring that no names are missing, misspelled, or incorrectly ordered. The connection between the Ballot Unit and the Control Unit is tested to ensure seamless communication, and the VVPAT unit is checked to confirm that it prints the correct symbol and name on the paper slip for every button press, dropping the slip into the sealed box as designed. Every step of this checking process is documented, and any discrepancy, no matter how minor, leads to the immediate rejection and replacement of the faulty unit. The integration of these various checks, the strict access controls, the mandatory presence of political representatives, and the comprehensive videography creates a robust ecosystem of trust around the electronic voting infrastructure, aiming to eliminate doubts and ensure that the final outcome of the election reflects the true will of the electorate.

The procedural framework for the preparation and certification of Ballot Units involves a series of rigorous steps designed to ensure transparency, integrity, and verifiability in the electoral process. The initial phase requires the authorized engineers from Bharat Electronics Limited and Electronics Corporation of India Limited to physically open the outer protective casing of the Ballot Units. This action is not merely mechanical but serves a critical verification purpose, as these engineers must meticulously examine every internal component to certify that all parts are original and have not been tampered with or replaced. This certification process is conducted strictly in accordance with the specific guidelines detailed in the relevant annexure of the official manual governing Electronic Voting Machines and Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail systems. Crucially, this inspection is not performed in isolation; it must be demonstrated directly to the contesting candidates or their appointed representatives, thereby establishing a chain of trust and allowing political stakeholders to witness the authenticity of the hardware firsthand before the election proceedings commence.

Following this initial verification, separate and detailed technical instructions are issued specifically for the checking procedures to be undertaken by the engineers from Bharat Electronics Limited and Electronics Corporation of India Limited, tailored to the distinct architectures of their respective machines. These instructions also encompass a comprehensive inventory list specifying the exact equipment that must be brought into the secure preparation hall where the voting machines are assembled. This ensures that the environment remains controlled and that only authorized tools are used during the sensitive setup phase. A central component of the physical preparation involves the fixing of the ballot paper onto the Ballot Unit. The machine is engineered with a dedicated provision to secure the printed ballot paper beneath a transparent acrylic sheet, often referred to as the ballot paper screen. This transparent layer allows voters to clearly read the candidate details while preventing any physical alteration or removal of the paper once the unit is sealed. Before this fixation occurs, the Returning Officer, who is the senior election official responsible for the conduct of the poll in that constituency, must personally sign or affix their facsimile signature on the reverse side of every single ballot paper. This step acts as a primary authentication measure, ensuring that only officially sanctioned papers are loaded into the voting machines.

To facilitate the installation of the ballot paper, the Returning Officer must first access the interior of the Ballot Unit by opening its top cover. This mechanism is designed with specific safety latches to prevent accidental or unauthorized opening. The procedure requires the operator to press simultaneously towards the right side on the latches located at both the top and bottom edges on the right-hand side of the unit. Once these latches are depressed, the cover can be swung upwards, causing the top cover and the lower portion of the Ballot Unit to separate and open much like the pages of a book, revealing the internal assembly. The next step involves accessing the ballot paper screen itself, which is hinged to the top cover on the extreme left side. The release mechanisms for this screen are concealed inside the top cover to maintain security. To unlock the screen, the operator must press the internal latches simultaneously, first applying slight pressure towards the right and then pushing them downwards. This specific sequence of movements frees the screen, allowing it to be opened towards the upper side of the top cover. Throughout this delicate operation, extreme care must be exercised to ensure that the acrylic screen or its hinges do not sustain any damage, as even minor defects could compromise the visibility of the ballot or the structural integrity of the unit.

Once the screen is successfully opened, the signed ballot paper is placed into the designated space on the upper side of the top cover. The alignment of this paper is a task of paramount importance and requires meticulous attention to detail. The ballot paper must be positioned so that the name, photograph, and election symbol of each candidate align perfectly with the corresponding indicator lamp and push-button on the machine interface. Furthermore, the thick vertical lines that divide the panels for different candidates on the printed paper must match exactly with the physical grooves molded into the Ballot Unit structure. Any misalignment at this stage is unacceptable, as it could create significant confusion in the minds of voters, potentially leading them to vote for the wrong candidate or causing disputes regarding the validity of the votes cast. Such errors could result in serious administrative and legal complications post-election. After verifying that the alignment is precise, the Returning Officer must close the ballot paper screen and press it firmly into place, securing the ballot paper tightly underneath the transparent shield.

With the ballot paper firmly fixed and the screen pressed fit onto the top cover, the next phase involves sealing the assembly to prevent future tampering. This is achieved by threading a secure string through two specially provided holes located on the inner side of the screen. The thread is pulled tight and tied in a firm knot to ensure it cannot be loosened without breaking. The two loose ends of this thread are then placed over an address tag, which serves as a unified identifier for the Ballot Unit, the Control Unit, and the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail machine. The Returning Officer then applies their official seal over both the knotted thread and the address tag, effectively locking the components together. Subsequently, after unmasking the candidate buttons to make them operational, the upper cover of the Ballot Unit is sealed using another thread passed through holes at the top and bottom ends on the right side. This thread is also secured with address tags displaying the specific particulars of the election and bears the official seal of the Returning Officer.

To provide an additional layer of physical security, the plastic cabinet housing the Ballot Unit is sealed using a modified pink paper seal. This specialized seal consists of two portions that share the same unique serial number, ensuring that they function as a matched pair. The process begins by carefully splitting the seal into two separate pink paper strips and removing the gummed backing with precision to avoid tearing. One of these seals is pasted on the lower side of the Ballot Unit cabinet, and the other is pasted on the upper side, bridging the gap between the stationary base and the moving cover. This placement ensures that any attempt to open the cabinet will inevitably tear the seal, providing visible evidence of tampering. To maintain a permanent record of this security measure, a dedicated register is maintained where the unique identification number of the Ballot Unit and the serial number of the pink paper seal are clearly recorded. Candidates and their representatives are invited to sign this register as witnesses to the sealing process. Upon completion of the preparation activities, photocopies of this register are provided free of cost to all contesting candidates, ensuring that every stakeholder possesses a verified record of the security seals applied to the machines.

In scenarios where the volume of voters or the number of candidates necessitates the use of more than one Ballot Unit at a single polling station, additional identification measures are implemented to distinguish between the units. Braille stickers bearing labels such as Ballot Unit-1, Ballot Unit-2, and so forth, are printed and affixed to the corresponding machines. These stickers are placed specifically on the top middle part of each Ballot Unit. This positioning ensures that the units are easily identifiable by election officials and visually impaired voters who rely on tactile indicators, while also maintaining a standardized labeling system across the polling station. This systematic approach to labeling prevents confusion during the voting process and ensures that votes cast on multiple units can be accurately aggregated and accounted for during the counting phase. Every step described, from the initial engineering inspection to the final application of Braille labels, is designed to create a robust, transparent, and tamper-evident electoral environment that upholds the democratic principle of free and fair elections.

The procedural framework for preparing the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail unit, commonly known as the VVPAT, involves a series of precise mechanical and electronic steps designed to ensure the integrity and transparency of the electoral process. The initial phase focuses on the installation of essential power and printing components. A new battery power pack must be connected to the VVPAT unit to provide the necessary electrical energy for its operation. Once the battery is securely in place, the door of the battery compartment is shut. It is a critical instruction that this compartment should not be permanently sealed at this stage. The rationale behind leaving it unsealed is to allow for the replacement of the power pack should the battery level drop low during the election proceedings, thereby preventing any disruption in voting due to power failure. Following the power setup, a fresh roll of paper must be installed within the printer unit of the VVPAT. This installation must strictly adhere to the specific procedures outlined in the official EVM-VVPAT manual, which governs the combined operation of the Electronic Voting Machine and its paper trail counterpart.

Once the hardware components are physically ready, the system requires data configuration to reflect the specific contesting candidates for that particular election. This process involves loading the allotted serial numbers, the full names of the candidates, and their respective political symbols into the VVPAT memory. This task is executed by trained engineers utilizing a specialized device called the Symbol Loading Unit, or SLU. To maintain transparency and build trust among the participating political parties, this data loading process is accompanied by a simultaneous display of the information on a television or LCD screen, allowing candidates or their authorized representatives to visually verify that the correct details are being entered. After the data is loaded, the Returning Officer, often abbreviated as RO, or the Assistant Returning Officer, known as the ARO, must perform a verification step. They are required to check a test printout generated by the machine against the official ballot paper from the Ballot Unit, or BU. Upon confirming that the printed slip matches the official ballot exactly, the officer must sign and certify the document, creating an official record of accuracy before the machine proceeds to the next stage.

The security of the physical components is maintained through a rigorous sealing protocol, particularly concerning the paper roll compartment. After the fresh paper roll has been successfully installed in its designated compartment within the VVPAT, the latches securing this compartment must be sealed. This is achieved by passing a strong thread through specific holes provided on the latches. Attached to this thread is an Address Tag that displays the particulars of the election, such as the constituency name and election date. Crucially, this tag must bear the official seal of the Returning Officer to validate the security measure. Before executing this sealing action, it is mandatory to ensure that the Control Unit, which acts as the brain of the voting system, is switched off to prevent any accidental data alteration or mechanical movement during the sealing process. A fundamental rule governing all seals used during the commissioning of both Electronic Voting Machines and VVPAT units is that they must be signed by the Returning Officers as well as the candidates or their appointed agents. This dual-signature requirement ensures bipartisan oversight and prevents any single entity from tampering with the machine without detection.

The configuration of the Control Unit involves a specific sequence of operations to synchronize it with the Ballot Unit and the VVPAT. The process begins with installing a new power pack in the Control Unit to ensure it has independent and reliable power. Next, the connecting cables are established; the cable from the VVPAT is connected to the Control Unit, and the cable from the Ballot Unit is connected to the VVPAT, forming a complete communication chain. The operator must then unlock the paper roll knob on the VVPAT by rotating it to the position marked as Working Position, which enables the printer mechanism. Once the physical connections are secure, the Control Unit is switched on. The operator then presses the Candidate Set button located on the Control Unit interface. Upon pressing this button, the display panel on the unit will show a prompt reading Set Candidate followed by underscores, indicating it is ready to receive input. To finalize the setting, the operator must press the button corresponding to the last candidate on the Ballot Unit, which includes the None of the Above or NOTA button. This action signals the Control Unit to register the total number of contesting candidates for the election.

To verify that the entire system is functioning correctly and that the VVPAT is printing paper slips accurately for every possible vote, a Mock Poll is conducted. During this test, exactly one vote is cast for each candidate listed on the ballot. This comprehensive test ensures that the machine records the vote electronically and produces the corresponding physical paper slip for every option available to the voter. Once the mock poll confirms the accuracy of the system, the Control Unit is switched off. The paper roll knob on the VVPAT is then locked by rotating it back to the Transport Position, which secures the paper roll and prevents unauthorized access or jamming during transit. The final security measures involve sealing the latches of the paper roll compartment on the VVPAT, as well as the battery and candidate set compartments on the Control Unit. These are secured using thread seals and address tags similar to the earlier sealing process. Candidates or their agents are then invited to affix their signatures along with their party abbreviations on these Address Tags, providing a final layer of witness verification before the machines are deployed to polling stations.

After the number of contesting candidates has been successfully set in the Control Unit through the methods described above, it is imperative to clear any residual data stored in the machine's memory. Specifically, any data recorded during the mock poll, which was conducted as part of the first-level checking of the Electronic Voting Machines, must be erased to ensure the count starts from zero for the actual election. To achieve this, the operator must locate the button marked Clear within the Result Section of the Control Unit and press it. When the Clear button is activated, the internal logic of the machine automatically resets all vote counts to ZERO. The display panels on the Control Unit will subsequently update to show that the number of votes recorded for each contesting candidate is explicitly zero, confirming that the machine is clean and ready for the genuine polling process. Following this data clearance and the successful configuration of the Control Unit according to the specific number of candidates in the election, the power supply to the unit should be switched off. Finally, the Control Unit and the Ballot Unit or units must be delinked by physically removing the interconnecting cable from the Control Unit. This separation is a standard safety and security protocol before the final sealing of the Candidate Set Section and Battery Section of the Control Unit with thread and address tags, ensuring the machine remains in a secure, inactive state until it reaches the polling booth.

Following the finalization of the candidate list, a critical security procedure must be executed regarding the Control Unit of the Electronic Voting Machine. The cover of the Candidate Set Section on the Control Unit must be securely sealed to prevent any unauthorized access or tampering with the candidate data stored within. This sealing process involves passing a strong thread through a specifically designed hole located on the right side of the unit. Attached to this thread are address tags that clearly display the specific particulars of the election, such as the constituency name, polling station number, and date. Crucially, the seal of the Returning Officer, which serves as the primary authority figure responsible for the conduct of the election in that area, must be affixed to these tags to validate the integrity of the seal. Once the Ballot Units, which are the interfaces where voters cast their votes, the Control Units, which store and count the votes, and the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail machines, which provide a physical paper record of each vote, have been fully prepared and commissioned, they must be placed immediately into their respective dedicated carrying cases. These cases are designed to protect the sensitive electronic equipment during transport. To ensure clear identification and prevent mix-ups, address tags similar to those used on the machines themselves must also be attached to the exterior of these carrying cases. It is a mandatory requirement that every single seal applied during this commissioning phase bears the signatures of both the Returning Officer and the candidates contesting the election or their authorized agents. This dual-signature protocol ensures transparency and mutual trust among all political stakeholders regarding the initial state of the machines.

The management and identification of reserve units, which are extra Ballot Units, Control Units, and VVPAT machines kept on standby to replace any malfunctioning equipment on polling day, require strict adherence to visual identification protocols. To distinguish these reserve machines from the primary set, adhesive stickers bearing the prominent inscription RESERVE must be pasted onto the carrying cases of the reserve Ballot Units, Control Units, and VVPATs at the time of commissioning. In addition to these visual stickers, an additional layer of security is applied by sealing the carrying cases of these reserve units with thread and address tags. This double-locking mechanism is essential to eliminate any doubt or suspicion regarding the potential misuse of reserve equipment while they are being transported or stored prior to poll day. If a situation arises where a primary machine is rejected due to a technical fault and needs to be replaced at a polling station, the thread and address tag securing the reserve unit must only be removed in the direct presence of the polling agents representing the various candidates. This ensures that the deployment of a reserve machine is witnessed and verified by all parties. Furthermore, Sector Officers, who oversee multiple polling stations, will be supplied with specific adhesive stickers inscribed with the words MOCK POLL REPLACED. These stickers are to be affixed on the carrying cases of any Ballot Unit, Control Unit, or VVPAT that was used during a mock poll and subsequently replaced, ensuring a clear audit trail of machines that have been tested and taken out of the active pool. The specifications for these adhesive stickers are precise; they must be pink in color for elections to the Legislative Assembly constituencies and white in color for elections to the Parliamentary constituencies. The physical dimensions of these stickers are standardized at fifteen centimeters in length and ten centimeters in width to ensure visibility and uniformity across all polling locations.

The safe custody of the Symbol Loading Unit, a specialized device used to load candidate names and symbols onto the VVPAT machines, is governed by a strict chain of custody protocol. The manufacturing agencies, referred to as BEL or ECIL, are required to provide a comprehensive list of all Symbol Loading Units to the District Election Officer concerned. When engineers from these agencies bring the Symbol Loading Units to the constituency for the purpose of loading symbols during the commissioning of VVPATs, these devices must remain under the direct physical custody of the Returning Officer. In the case of Parliamentary Constituency elections, an Assistant Returning Officer may assume this responsibility if required. This custody arrangement lasts continuously from the moment the units arrive in the constituency until the commissioning process is fully completed. Once the commissioning of all Electronic Voting Machines and VVPATs is finished, the supervisors from BEL or ECIL must formally hand over all Symbol Loading Units to the concerned District Election Officer. This handover must be accompanied by a detailed list containing the serial numbers of every Symbol Loading Unit transferred. The District Election Officers are then responsible for keeping these units in their secure safe custody. These devices are not to be returned to the manufacturing agency supervisors or their authorized engineers until the day after the polling concludes, known as P-plus-one day, ensuring that no unauthorized symbol changes can occur during the election period.

To guarantee the accuracy and reliability of the voting systems, a higher rate mock poll is conducted as part of the pre-election testing regimen. This process begins with a comprehensive check of the VVPAT machines. In scenarios where one hundred percent of the machines are equipped with VVPAT, a test vote is cast for every single candidate listed on the ballot, including the option for None of the Above. The purpose of this exercise is to verify that the VVPAT printer is accurately printing the paper slip with the correct candidate symbol and name for each vote cast. Following this individual check, a more extensive mock poll involving one thousand votes is performed on five percent of the Electronic Voting Machines and their associated VVPATs, selected randomly to ensure statistical validity. The electronic results generated by the Control Units are then meticulously tallied against the physical count of the paper slips produced by the VVPAT machines to confirm that the digital and physical records match perfectly. Candidates or their authorized representatives are permitted to personally select the machines to be used for this random sampling, fostering confidence in the randomness of the selection. Moreover, these representatives are allowed to conduct the mock poll themselves, casting the test votes personally. Upon completion, they must sign a register, as specified in the appropriate annexure of the EVM-VVPAT manual, serving as a formal token and legal record that they have personally witnessed and participated in the mock poll. Throughout this entire commissioning and testing process, extreme care must be taken to ensure that the pink paper seal applied to the Control Unit during the First Level Checking is not damaged, torn, or compromised in any manner. After the higher mock poll of one thousand votes has been successfully cast on specific machines, new paper rolls must be installed in the VVPATs and new power packs must be fitted in both the VVPATs and the Control Units that were subjected to this intensive testing, ensuring they are fresh and fully powered for actual polling day.

The entire preparation process is conducted under strict supervision to maintain integrity. Either the Returning Officer or one of the Assistant Returning Officers must personally supervise the preparation of the Electronic Voting Machines and VVPATs. This supervision is not merely observational but is recorded through videography, creating an immutable visual record of the procedures followed, the personnel present, and the steps taken to secure the machines. Special attention is given to accessibility features within the Electronic Voting Machine to facilitate visually impaired electors in exercising their franchise independently. The Ballot Unit is equipped with Braille signage features, specifically embossed serial numbers of the candidates located to the right of the blue voting button. It is strictly prohibited to paste any sticker over this Braille area, as doing so would obscure the tactile information needed by blind voters to identify the candidate buttons. Immediately after the final list of contesting candidates is finalized, this list must be sent without delay to organizations such as the Devnar Foundation for Blind or the National Blind Association, or any other available source capable of producing specialized materials. These organizations are tasked with preparing Dummy Ballot Sheets. These sheets contain the serial numbers of the contesting candidates and their names arranged in the exact same sequential order as they appear on the actual ballot paper of the Ballot Unit. To provide complete information, the party affiliation of each candidate is indicated by printing the name of the relevant political party next to the candidate's name. In cases where a candidate is running as an Independent, the word Independent is printed in place of a party name. These Dummy Ballot Sheets are prepared in regional languages, matching the language printed on the actual ballot paper, in addition to English, to ensure comprehension by all voters. A copy of these sheets is supplied to each Polling Station. If a polling station utilizes more than one Ballot Unit, separate dummy ballot sheets must be printed for each specific unit to reflect the unique candidate allocation on that machine. The preparation of these Dummy Ballot Sheets involves assigning serial numbers in a sequential format from zero one to sixteen on each sheet, corresponding to the maximum number of candidates that can be accommodated on a single Ballot Unit page, thereby providing a complete reference guide for visually impaired voters to navigate the voting process with dignity and accuracy.

In the intricate administration of electoral processes, specific protocols are established to ensure that every citizen, including those with visual impairments, can exercise their democratic right to vote with dignity and independence. When an election contest involves a total of nineteen candidates, a figure that includes the option for None of the Above, often abbreviated as NOTA, the preparation of auxiliary voting aids requires careful numerical sequencing across multiple ballot units. A ballot unit is the electronic interface that displays the names and symbols of the candidates to the voter. In scenarios where the first ballot unit, referred to as BU-1, accommodates only the first sixteen candidates, the serial numbers on the initial dummy ballot sheet must correspond precisely to the physical layout of that first unit. Consequently, the serial numbers on this first dummy sheet are labeled from zero one to sixteen, mirroring exactly what appears on the actual ballot paper of the first ballot unit. This dummy ballot sheet serves as a tactile reference guide, allowing visually impaired voters to familiarize themselves with the order of candidates before entering the voting compartment. For the remaining contesting candidates who occupy serial positions seventeen through nineteen on the second ballot unit, known as BU-2, the numbering sequence on the subsequent dummy ballot sheet resets. This means the second dummy sheet begins its serial numbering again from zero one, corresponding to the seventeenth candidate overall but the first candidate listed on the second physical unit. This reset ensures that the tactile guide matches the local numbering system of each specific electronic unit the voter will interact with.

To further facilitate identification and proper usage when more than one ballot unit is deployed at a single polling station, distinct labeling protocols involving Braille are mandated. Braille is a tactile writing system used by people who are blind or visually impaired, consisting of patterns of raised dots that can be read by touch. On the top middle section of the first dummy ballot sheet, the identifier BU-01 must be printed in Braille. Similarly, the second dummy ballot sheet must bear the Braille inscription BU-02 on its top middle part, and this pattern continues sequentially for any additional units required. Complementing these sheets, physical Braille stickers bearing the labels BU-01, BU-02, and so forth, must be affixed to the corresponding actual ballot units. These stickers are placed on the top middle part of each unit to ensure that a visually impaired voter can independently identify which unit contains which set of candidates by simply running their fingers over the device. The production of these critical accessibility tools, specifically the dummy ballot sheets in Braille signage, is a specialized task. These documents are to be printed by recognized institutions such as the Devnar School for the Blind or the National Association for the Blind, or by any other competent source available for this specific purpose. However, to maintain the integrity and accuracy of the electoral material, this entire printing process must occur under the strict supervision of a responsible officer who has been explicitly appointed by the Chief Electoral Officer of the respective State or Union Territory. Additionally, to ensure clarity for all election officials and observers, the name of the electoral constituency must be written in normal, visible script at the very top of each dummy ballot sheet.

The validation of these accessibility measures is a rigorous process designed to guarantee accuracy. The Commissioner for Disabilities of the concerned State, or an authorized representative designated by them, plays a pivotal role in this verification. This individual must be an official who possesses knowledge of Braille to effectively perform their duties. Their responsibility is to examine and formally certify every single dummy ballot sheet prepared for every polling station within their jurisdiction, ensuring that the tactile information is correct and matches the official candidate list. Beyond the physical preparation of materials, a comprehensive educational strategy is required to ensure these facilities are utilized correctly. Detailed training and briefing sessions must be conducted for Presiding Officers and Polling Officers, who are the staff members managing the polling stations on election day. These sessions must thoroughly explain the facilities available for visually impaired electors, including how to handle the dummy ballot sheets, how to assist voters without compromising their secrecy, and the specific procedural steps involved. To ensure that the beneficiaries of these measures are aware of their rights, wide publicity campaigns must be arranged. This involves placing advertisements in newspapers, broadcasting messages on television, and utilizing other media channels to inform visually impaired voters about the special arrangements made for them, thereby encouraging their participation in the electoral process.

On the day of the poll, the execution of these rules takes place at the polling station, where the rights of visually impaired persons are actively protected. In accordance with Rule 49N of the Conduct of Elections Rules, 1961, visually impaired individuals are permitted to bring a companion of their choice to assist them during the voting process. Furthermore, if a visually impaired voter prefers to cast their vote with the aid of a tactile guide, they may request a dummy ballot sheet in Braille. Upon receiving this request, the polling officials must provide the sheet, allowing the voter to study the contents at their own pace. This study period enables the voter to memorize the serial number associated with their candidate of choice. Once the voter has reviewed the dummy sheet and identified their preferred candidate, they are allowed to proceed to the voting compartment. Inside the compartment, the voter uses the information gained from the dummy sheet to locate the numeric sticker fixed on the Electronic Voting Machine, or EVM. The EVM is the electronic device used to record votes, and it features buttons corresponding to each candidate, marked with serial numbers. By reading the serial number on the tactile guide, the elector can confidently press the correct button on the machine to cast their vote. After the vote has been successfully cast, it is mandatory for the polling staff to ensure that the dummy ballot sheet provided to the voter is returned to the Presiding Officer. This step prevents the removal of electoral materials from the polling station and maintains the security of the process.

Following the close of the poll, the handling of these used dummy ballot sheets follows a strict chain of custody. The returned sheets must be sealed in a separate cover that is clearly superscribed with the label Dummy Ballot Sheet for visually impaired. This sealed cover is then sent to the Returning Officer, who is the senior official responsible for the conduct of elections in a constituency, along with other non-statutory forms. This ensures that there is a record of the materials used without compromising the secrecy of the individual votes cast. Parallel to these voter-facing procedures, there are stringent protocols regarding the technical maintenance and commissioning of the voting machines themselves, specifically concerning the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail, or VVPAT. The VVPAT is a method used to provide feedback to voters using a ballotless voting system, printing a paper slip that shows which candidate the vote was cast for. During the commissioning phase, which is the testing and setup of the machines before the election, VVPAT paper slips are generated to verify functionality. These test slips contain no actual voter data and must be destroyed securely. The protocol dictates that these slips be disposed of using a paper shredding machine on a daily basis. This destruction must take place in the presence of the Returning Officer or the Assistant Returning Officer to ensure transparency and prevent any potential misuse of the test materials.

In the event that technical issues arise during the commissioning of the EVMs and VVPATs, specific replacement procedures are enforced to maintain the integrity of the equipment pool. If any unit, whether it is a Ballot Unit, a Control Unit, or a VVPAT machine, is found to be non-functional during testing, it must be immediately replaced with a reserve unit. The replacement logic is modular and precise. If only the Ballot Unit is defective, only the Ballot Unit is swapped out, leaving the rest of the system intact. Similarly, if the Control Unit, which is the brain of the voting machine that controls the Ballot Unit and records the votes, is found non-functional, only the Control Unit is replaced. In cases where the VVPAT printer mechanism is faulty, only the VVPAT unit is replaced. This targeted approach ensures that functional components are not unnecessarily discarded while guaranteeing that every piece of equipment used on election day is fully operational. To maintain transparency and inform stakeholders about these rigorous testing standards, a poster detailing the Commissioning of EVMs and VVPATs must be displayed. This poster shall be pasted prominently in the Commissioning Hall, serving as a public declaration of the procedures being followed to ensure the reliability and security of the electronic voting infrastructure.

The protocols governing the deployment and management of Electronic Voting Machines and Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail systems during a re-poll constitute a critical framework designed to ensure the integrity, transparency, and auditability of the electoral process. When an election authority determines that a re-poll is necessary for a specific polling station due to irregularities, natural calamities, or other valid reasons, strict procedures must be followed regarding the selection and identification of the voting equipment. The Electronic Voting Machine, which consists of a Control Unit that acts as the brain of the system to record votes, and one or more Ballot Units that present the candidate options to the voter, along with the separate VVPAT device that prints a paper slip for voter verification, must all be sourced exclusively from a pre-designated reserve list. This ensures that the machines used in the re-poll have not been previously activated or tampered with in the original polling event. Upon selecting these reserve units, the unique identification numbers assigned to the Control Unit, each Ballot Unit, and the VVPAT machine must be formally communicated in writing to all contesting candidates and their authorized agents. This written notification serves as an official record that allows political representatives to track exactly which hardware is being deployed for the fresh voting exercise.

To prevent any confusion between the equipment used in the original poll and that used in the re-poll, rigorous physical labeling measures are mandated. Great care must be exercised to ensure that the address tags attached to the Control Unit, the Ballot Units, and the VVPAT explicitly state that these specific devices are designated solely for use in the re-poll. These tags must also clearly indicate the scheduled date of the re-poll and the specific polling station number where they will be utilized. In addition to the standard address tags, a distinct visual identifier is required in the form of a pre-printed self-adhesive sticker. These stickers, bearing the clear inscriptions Re-poll EVM and Re-poll VVPAT, must be affixed directly onto the hardware itself, covering the Control Unit and all associated Ballot Units, as well as the VVPAT device. Furthermore, these same identifying stickers must be placed on the carrying cases or transport boxes used to move this equipment. This dual layer of labeling on both the machines and their containers ensures that at every stage of logistics, from storage to transport and setup, there is no possibility of mixing up the re-poll equipment with general stock or equipment from other stations.

Once the re-poll voting process has concluded, the secure storage of the used machines becomes paramount to maintain the chain of custody. The strong room, which is a highly secured facility designed for storing election materials, must be reopened specifically for the deposit of the EVMs and VVPATs used in the re-poll. This reopening cannot happen in secret; it must occur in the physical presence of the contesting candidates, their appointed agents, and an independent observer appointed by the election commission. This witness requirement guarantees that the insertion of the machines into the vault is transparent and uncontested. Inside the strong room, the newly used re-poll EVM and VVPAT must be stored together with the respective old EVM and VVPAT that were utilized during the original, now-invalidated poll. To distinguish the legal status of these two sets of machines, specific tags must be applied prominently. The old machines from the original poll, which are no longer valid for vote counting, must be tagged with a label reading Not to be counted. Conversely, the new machines used in the re-poll, which contain the valid votes to be tallied, must be tagged with labels reading Re-poll EVM – to be counted and Re-poll VVPAT – to be counted. To authenticate these designations and prevent unauthorized alteration, the Returning Officer, who is the senior official responsible for the conduct of the election in that constituency, must personally sign every single one of these tags. Additionally, the unique identification numbers of the Control Units, Ballot Units, and VVPATs used in the re-poll must be digitally entered into the EVM Management System, a centralized software database that tracks the lifecycle and status of every voting machine in the inventory.

The safe preservation of prepared voting machines before they are distributed to polling stations is governed by equally stringent security protocols designed to prevent tampering, theft, or unauthorized access. All voting machines that have been prepared and configured for use in an election, including those kept in reserve for emergencies, must be stored in a strong room under constant surveillance and protection. This facility must be guarded by armed security personnel operating twenty-four hours a day, seven days a week, with a minimum deployment of one full section of security forces to ensure robust physical deterrence. Complementing the human guard force, the strong room must be under continuous closed-circuit television coverage, creating a digital record of all activity near the entrance and interior of the storage area. The room itself must operate under a double-lock system, meaning two separate locks must be engaged to secure the doors, adding a layer of redundancy to the physical security. The seals of the Returning Officer must be affixed to these locks to indicate if they have been tampered with. To further enhance trust in the process, contesting candidates and their agents are permitted to apply their own personal seals to the locks if they so desire, giving them direct ownership over the security integrity of the storage facility.

Access to this high-security strong room is strictly controlled and time-bound. The room is to remain sealed and inaccessible until the appointed date and time when the machines are scheduled to be supplied to the polling parties just before they depart for their respective polling stations. This minimizes the window of opportunity for any potential breach. Transparency is maintained by requiring that all contesting candidates or their election agents receive prior written notice specifying the exact date and time when the strong room will be opened. This allows them to arrange to be present during the distribution process. To create an immutable audit trail, a proper logbook must be maintained at the strong room. This logbook must contain detailed entries recording every instance of the room being closed and opened, including the timestamps and the identities of the officials present. In rare scenarios where an emergent or unavoidable reason necessitates opening the strong room before the officially appointed date and time, strict procedural safeguards are triggered. The Returning Officer must immediately send a notice to the candidates or their authorized representatives informing them of the urgent need to open the room. The room may only be opened in the physical presence of these representatives. Once the urgent purpose is fulfilled, the room must be closed and re-sealed immediately. Every detail of such an unscheduled opening and closing, including the reason for the breach of schedule, must be accurately and fully recorded in the logbook. Furthermore, the entire process of this emergency access must be videographed to provide visual evidence of the proceedings and ensure that no malpractice occurred during the interim access.

Maintaining comprehensive and accurate records of all Electronic Voting Machines and VVPAT devices is a fundamental responsibility of the Returning Officer to ensure accountability throughout the election cycle. The Returning Officer is required to maintain a complete register documenting every Control Unit, Ballot Unit, and VVPAT utilized during the election. This record must be granular enough to show clearly the specific number of Control Units, Ballot Units, and VVPATs deployed at each individual polling station, accompanied by the unique serial numbers of each such unit. This level of detail allows for precise tracking of every piece of hardware from the warehouse to the specific booth where it was used. The record must also account for the inventory prepared for use but kept in reserve, listing the quantities and serial numbers of these standby units. If any of these reserve Control Units, Ballot Units, or VVPATs are eventually put into use, perhaps to replace a malfunctioning machine or to facilitate a re-poll, a complete supplementary record must be generated. This supplementary record must document exactly where each such reserve unit was deployed and, crucially, must state the specific reasons why the use of a reserve unit became necessary. This explanatory requirement ensures that the deployment of reserve stock is justified and not arbitrary.

To facilitate the maintenance of this complete record of voting machines, a master register must be kept by the Returning Officer. This master document serves as the central ledger for the constituency's election hardware and must contain several specific particulars. First, it must list the total number of Control Units currently in the stock of the Returning Officer. Second, it must specify the total number of Ballot Units in that same stock. Third, it must record the total number of VVPAT devices available in the stock. Fourth, the register must state the total number of polling stations established within the constituency. Fifth, it must calculate and display the total number of Control Units required for use at the polling stations, which is derived by multiplying the total number of polling stations by one, since each station requires exactly one Control Unit. Sixth, the register must show the total number of Ballot Units required for use at the polling stations, calculated by multiplying the total number of polling stations by the specific number of Ballot Units needed at each station, as this number can vary depending on the number of contestants and the layout of the polling booth. Finally, the register must indicate the total number of VVPATs required for use at the polling stations, ensuring that the paper audit trail capability is matched to the voting infrastructure needs. This meticulous bookkeeping creates a verifiable mathematical balance between the machines owned, the machines required, and the machines actually deployed, leaving no room for discrepancy or unaccounted hardware.

The meticulous documentation of electoral equipment is a foundational pillar of transparent and credible elections, requiring a comprehensive register that captures every detail regarding the inventory and deployment of voting machinery. This register must explicitly record the total number of Control Units held in reserve stock, which are the master devices used by election officials to initialize and close the voting process. Similarly, it must list the total count of Ballot Units kept in reserve, these being the interfaces where voters actually cast their votes by pressing buttons corresponding to their chosen candidates. The inventory must also include the precise number of Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail units, commonly known as VVPATs, stored in reserve; these machines print a paper slip for each vote cast, allowing the voter to verify their choice before the slip drops into a sealed box for potential audit purposes. Beyond mere counts, the register must provide a granular breakdown of every polling station within the constituency, listing both the unique identification number and the official name of each location. For every single polling station listed, the document must specify the exact serial number of the Control Unit assigned for use at that specific site, ensuring a one-to-one mapping between the location and the primary controlling hardware. Furthermore, the serial numbers of all Ballot Units deployed at that same polling station must be recorded, as multiple ballot units may be required depending on the volume of voters. The serial numbers of the VVPAT machines utilized at each station must also be logged with equal precision. To maintain an unbroken chain of custody and technical integrity, the register must note the serial number of the physical ballot paper sheet that is fixed onto each Ballot Unit, linking the electronic component to its physical printing element. Finally, a complete ledger of the serial numbers for every Control Unit, Ballot Unit, and VVPAT remaining in the reserve stock must be maintained separately to distinguish them from those already deployed. Crucially, this register must include a dedicated remarks column adjacent to the details of each polling station. This space is reserved for officials to indicate whether any piece of equipment, be it a Control Unit, Ballot Unit, or VVPAT, had to be drawn from the reserve stock during the election day. If such an event occurred, the official must document the specific reason for the replacement, such as a mechanical failure or a power issue, thereby creating an auditable trail of any deviations from the original deployment plan.

The procedure for the dispersal and supply of Electronic Voting Machines and VVPATs to polling parties is governed by strict protocols designed to ensure accountability and prevent tampering. Voting machines are to be handed over exclusively to the Presiding Officer, who is the senior-most election official responsible for conducting the poll at a specific station. This transfer must take place on the designated dispersal day and must be formalized through the issuance of a proper receipt. To facilitate this, the Returning Officer, who oversees the entire constituency, is required to maintain a specialized register distinct from the general inventory logs. This supply register must detail the number and name of each polling station, the full name of the Presiding Officer receiving the equipment, and the specific serial numbers of the Control Unit, the associated Ballot Units, and the VVPAT being supplied. The Presiding Officer must affix their full signature in this register as a legal token of having received these sensitive units, acknowledging their responsibility for the security and proper functioning of the machines until they are returned. The protocol extends equally to the return process after the conclusion of the poll. The officer designated to receive the machines back from the Presiding Officers must meticulously verify and record the serial numbers of every Control Unit, Ballot Unit, and VVPAT being returned. This officer must also append their full signature to certify the receipt of these units, effectively closing the loop of custody. In the unfortunate event that any machine is returned in a damaged condition, a comprehensive and detailed record must be created for that specific unit, documenting the nature and extent of the damage to facilitate investigation and repair. To handle emergencies that may arise during the actual voting hours, reserve machines must be strategically positioned at a central location or multiple centralized points within the constituency. The objective of this positioning is to ensure that if an emergent need arises at any polling station requiring the immediate replacement of a faulty EVM or VVPAT, the spare unit can be delivered with the least possible delay, minimizing disruption to the voting process. These critical reserve assets must be placed under the direct charge and supervision of an Assistant Returning Officer or another senior officer specifically nominated for this task by the Returning Officer. It is imperative that once the poll concludes, these reserve machines, as well as any machines used solely for training purposes, are not moved from their storage locations. This restriction is vital to prevent any unnecessary suspicion or doubt among the public, political parties, or observers who might otherwise misconstrue the movement of machines as an attempt to shift polled EVMs and VVPATs illicitly. A comprehensive list detailing all various items of polling materials required for stations utilizing EVMs and VVPATs is typically provided in an annexure to guide logistical preparations.

Following the commissioning of the equipment, the storage of EVMs and VVPATs becomes a high-security operation demanding rigorous safeguards. Commissioned machines, which are those ready for use, must be stored in a designated strong room, a highly secured vault-like facility. This storage process must occur in the presence of candidates or their authorized representatives to ensure transparency and build trust in the system. The entire procedure of storing these machines must be captured under videography, creating a visual record that can be reviewed if questions arise later. The security of this strong room is paramount, requiring twenty-four hours a day, seven days a week coverage by Closed-Circuit Television cameras to monitor all activity continuously. Additionally, the facility must be guarded by at least one section of armed security personnel to deter and respond to any physical threats. A log book must be maintained at the entrance of the strong room, recording every entry and exit, the identity of individuals, and the purpose of their visit. The movement of these machines to and from polling stations involves the use of vehicles equipped with Global Positioning System tracking technology or mobile application-based tracking systems. This ensures that the end-to-end movement of every GPS-enabled vehicle carrying EVMs and VVPATs, including those transporting reserve units, is monitored in real-time at all times. To oversee this complex logistical network, an EVM Control Room must be established at the level of the District Election Officer as well as at the Chief Electoral Officer level. These control rooms function as command centers for round-the-clock monitoring and tracking through GPS monitors and other related information technology infrastructure and applications. Sector Officers, who are responsible for specific geographic zones, are strictly prohibited from removing EVMs and VVPATs from their authorized transport vehicles unless it is absolutely necessary for the election process itself or for safe storage at designated secure places. The District Election Officer bears the responsibility of notifying the registration numbers and detailed descriptions of all vehicles carrying reserve EVMs and VVPATs, along with the names of the Sector Officers in charge, to all political parties, candidates, and election observers within the district. This notification ensures that all stakeholders are aware of the legitimate transport movements. The District Election Officer is ultimately accountable for the effective end-to-end GPS-based tracking and monitoring of the entire fleet. The General Observer, an independent overseer appointed to ensure fairness, shall monitor the compliance of these stringent instructions and must record an entry confirming this oversight in the General Observers' diary, adding another layer of external verification.

The polling event itself stands as the most critical and defining moment in the entire election process, serving as the practical manifestation of democratic will. In a democracy, the poll is the mechanism through which electors, or eligible voters, formally express their choice regarding which candidate should represent them in government. It is the culmination of weeks or months of campaigning, nomination, and preparation, translating citizen preference into political mandate. Because of its supreme importance, any irregularity, procedural lapse, or misconduct during the conduct of the poll has the potential to vitiate, or legally invalidate, the entire election result for that constituency. Therefore, it is an absolute imperative that the poll is conducted strictly in accordance with the letter of the law and the prescribed procedural guidelines issued by the election commission. Deviation from these standards cannot be tolerated as it undermines the legitimacy of the outcome. If the operation of conducting the poll has been planned well in advance, adhering to a comprehensive District Election Plan that anticipates challenges and allocates resources efficiently, and if all necessary logistical and security arrangements are made accordingly, then a reasonably free, fair, and peaceful poll will take place within the constituency. This level of planning ensures that voters can exercise their franchise without fear, intimidation, or obstruction, and that the counting of votes reflects the true intent of the electorate. The integrity of the polling process relies on the seamless coordination of human resources, technological tools like the EVMs and VVPATs, and strict adherence to the chain of custody and security protocols previously outlined, creating an environment where the voice of the people is heard clearly and accurately.

The legal framework governing the conduct of elections in Parliamentary or Assembly Constituencies where electronic voting machines are utilized is firmly established within the Representation of the People Act of 1951. Specifically, the statutory authority for these procedures is found in Sections 56 through 62 of this landmark legislation, with Section 61A serving as a particularly critical provision that explicitly validates the use of such technology. To align operational guidelines with these statutory mandates, necessary amendments were introduced to the Conduct of Election Rules of 1961 via the Conduct of Election Amendment Rules of 1992. This regulatory update involved the insertion of an entirely new chapter, designated as Chapter II within Part IV of the rules, which encompasses a comprehensive series of regulations numbered from 49A to 49X, placed immediately after the existing Rule 49. Furthermore, to address the specific mechanics of tallying votes recorded electronically, Rule 66A was added following Rule 66, thereby creating a dedicated legal pathway for the counting of votes cast via voting machines. The administrative documentation required for these processes was also expanded, with the introduction of Forms 17A, 17B, and 17C, which are compiled in Annexure 54 and appended to the Conduct of Election Rules of 1961 to ensure standardized recording and reporting across all constituencies.

Regarding the physical infrastructure required for voting, the establishment of polling stations is a rigorous process that must be completed well in advance of the election date. The District Election Officer holds the primary responsibility for setting up these stations, but this action cannot be taken unilaterally; it requires the prior approval of the Election Commission, acting under the authority granted by Section 25 of the Representation of the People Act, 1951. The Returning Officer, who manages the election affairs for a specific constituency, must proactively engage with the District Election Officer to obtain a complete and verified list of all polling stations within their jurisdiction. This coordination must occur significantly before the actual date of the poll to allow sufficient time for verifying that all logistical arrangements at the Commission-approved locations are fully operational. Transparency is a cornerstone of this phase, mandating that a copy of the updated list of polling stations be provided free of cost to every candidate contesting the election. This distribution must take place within three days following the final deadline for candidates to withdraw their nominations. It is imperative to understand that the location and buildings designated as polling stations are fixed once approved; no alterations to the building or the geographic location of any polling station are permissible without obtaining fresh, explicit approval from the Election Commission. If a poll is conducted at any location other than those officially approved, the entire voting process at that site is considered null and void from the very beginning, legally necessitating a recommendation for a re-poll to ensure the integrity of the electoral outcome.

The human element of the election process relies on the formation and preparation of polling parties, which are groups of officials appointed by the District Election Officer to manage operations at individual polling stations. The Returning Officer bears the duty of ensuring that these appointments are made with absolute certainty and that the arrangement is foolproof. It is essential that formal appointment orders are served to every member of the polling personnel well in advance of the election day.
