Given the technical nature of modern voting, it is mandatory that intensive, hands-on training regarding the operation of Electronic Voting Machines, commonly known as EVMs, and the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail, or VVPAT, be imparted to all members of the polling parties. This training is especially critical for Presiding Officers, who lead the station, and Senior Polling Officers, who are specifically designated in their appointment letters to assume the role of Presiding Officer should the original appointee be unable to attend due to unforeseen circumstances such as illness or emergency. This high level of competency is not limited to the polling station staff; similar comprehensive training must also be provided to Sector Magistrates, Zonal Magistrates, and Micro-observers, who play vital roles in monitoring and supervising the fairness of the process across different areas.

Logistical preparedness extends to the management of polling materials, for which the Returning Officer must ensure that every polling party is fully equipped with all necessary items, including the voting machines themselves. A standard inventory of these materials is detailed in Annexure 8 of the relevant guidelines. The Presiding Officers must be explicitly instructed to exercise the utmost care regarding the safety and security of the EVMs and VVPAT units throughout the process. It must be clearly communicated to these officers that they will be held personally liable and responsible for any damage incurred to these machines if such damage results from their negligence. To streamline operations at the dispatch center and to guarantee that all required statutory and non-statutory forms and envelopes are correctly supplied to the polling parties, a color-coding system has been implemented for easy identification. Statutory forms and their corresponding covers, which are legally mandatory documents, are colored white, while non-statutory forms and covers, which serve administrative purposes, are colored yellow. All forms within these statutory and non-statutory groups must be bundled separately and provided in a customized manner according to the latest instructions issued by the Election Commission. Additionally, these documents are pre-perforated to facilitate easy detachment, allowing the Presiding Officer to quickly place properly filled forms into the relevant envelopes after the poll concludes. For the sake of clarity and organizational efficiency, various forms, formats, and other materials used at the polling station are categorized into six distinct sets of envelopes. The first packet contains EVM papers, which are documents kept with the machine, and is white in color, except for Assembly Constituency elections where it is pink. The second packet holds scrutiny documents, which are stored separately from the EVM strong room, and is white. The third packet contains statutory covers and is white, while the fourth packet contains non-statutory covers and is yellow. The fifth packet, colored brown, includes handbooks, instructions from the Election Commission of India, indelible ink used to mark voters' fingers, and stamp pads. The sixth and final packet, colored blue, contains all other miscellaneous materials required for the smooth functioning of the polling station.

Finally, the maintenance of law and order is a prerequisite for conducting free and fair elections. The Returning Officer must ensure, in close consultation with local law enforcement and other concerned authorities, that proper order is maintained in and around all polling stations. This vigilance is necessary to create an environment where voters can cast their ballots without fear or intimidation. Adequate security measures must be implemented to provide full protection to the polling personnel, the electronic voting machines, the voter verifiable paper audit trails, and all other election-related materials. This security protocol remains in effect continuously from the start of the polling process until the polling parties have safely reached the designated Receiving Centers after the close of the poll. The secure transport and handling of these materials are crucial to preventing tampering and ensuring that the final count accurately reflects the will of the electorate, thereby upholding the democratic principles enshrined in the constitution.

The Returning Officer bears a fundamental and non-delegable responsibility to guarantee that all standing instructions and specific directions issued by the Election Commission are rigorously implemented to foster and maintain a tranquil atmosphere within the constituency on the actual day of polling. This duty extends beyond mere administrative oversight and requires proactive identification of sensitive areas and polling stations that may be prone to disruption or violence. The officer must meticulously map out these locations, often referred to as critical polling stations or vulnerable pockets, which are geographic zones where historical data or current intelligence suggests a high risk of electoral malpractice, intimidation, or public disorder. Once these areas are identified through a process known as vulnerability mapping, special preventive and security measures must be immediately enacted to neutralize potential threats and prevent any untoward incidents that could compromise the integrity of the election. The concept of vulnerability mapping involves a detailed analysis of past election records, crime statistics, and local political tensions to categorize different parts of the constituency based on their risk levels, ensuring that resources are allocated efficiently to protect the democratic process. It is imperative that the district election administration works in tandem with the police administration to enforce a strict protocol regarding the movement of political actors. Specifically, all political functionaries and party workers who have traveled from outside the constituency solely for the purpose of campaigning, and who are not registered voters within that specific area, must be mandated to leave the constituency immediately upon the conclusion of their campaign activities. The continued presence of such external agents after the campaigning period has officially ended poses a significant risk to the sanctity of the election, as their lingering influence can intimidate voters, incite violence, or undermine the conditions necessary for a free and fair poll.

The Returning Officer is further tasked with ensuring strict adherence to a comprehensive set of regulatory restrictions designed to minimize chaos and ensure public safety during the electoral process. These regulations include prohibitions on the plying of unauthorized vehicles on the day of the poll to prevent the transportation of booth captors or illicit materials, as well as a complete ban on the sale of liquor during a specified period that encompasses both the day of polling and the day of counting, thereby reducing the likelihood of alcohol-fueled disturbances. Additionally, there is a mandatory requirement for all licensed arms holders to deposit their firearms with designated authorities for the duration of the election period, coupled with aggressive operations to unearth and seize any unlicensed arms and weapons that could be used to threaten voters or election officials. To facilitate unhindered participation, the day of the poll is officially declared a holiday within the constituency, ensuring that employment obligations do not prevent citizens from exercising their franchise. All these standing instructions and directional mandates are compiled in official publications released by the Commission, specifically titled the Compendium of Instructions on Conduct of Election and the Compendium of Instructions on Election Expenditure Monitoring, which are also made accessible via the Commission's official website. Election officials are required to study the latest editions of these compilations with extreme care to ensure full compliance, as these documents serve as the definitive guide for managing the logistical and security aspects of the election. Throughout this period, a strict vigil must be maintained over the movement of undesirable elements and suspicious vehicles, requiring constant surveillance and rapid response capabilities to address any emerging threats before they escalate.

In areas identified as sensitive, the deployment of security forces follows a specific hierarchy and operational doctrine to maximize effectiveness and deterrence. Wherever feasible, Central Armed Police Forces, which are paramilitary organizations under the direct control of the central government, should be deployed instead of local state police to ensure impartiality and superior tactical capability. Furthermore, the staffing of polling stations in these high-risk zones should prioritize employees of the Central Government and Central Government Public Sector undertakings, as they are perceived to be less susceptible to local political pressures compared to state-level employees. The personnel from the Central Armed Police Forces are not merely present for show but are required to actively monitor the proceedings inside the polling stations to ensure that no coercion or fraud takes place. In every polling station where such central forces are deployed, at least one member of the force must be stationed permanently at the entrance of the facility. This positioning is strategic, intended to provide the security personnel with an unrestricted and clear view of the entire polling procedure, allowing them to observe the interaction between voters and election officials without obstructing the flow of people. Should any untoward incident occur that threatens the free and fair nature of the poll, the Presiding Officer, who is the highest authority within the polling station, is empowered to invoke Section 131 of the Representation of the People Act of 1951. This legal provision grants the Presiding Officer the authority to command the assistance of the Central Armed Police Forces or State Police to restore order, remove disruptive individuals, or even suspend voting temporarily if the situation demands it. Effective execution of these powers relies heavily on seamless coordination and communication between the Presiding Officer and the security personnel posted at the station, ensuring that decisions are made swiftly and actions are taken in unison to protect the electoral process.

The documentation and verification of security arrangements have become an integral part of the modern electoral framework, utilizing technology to create an auditable trail of compliance. If a video camera or digital camera is deployed at a polling station, its operation must commence before the actual polling begins. In addition to recording standard procedural events such as the mock poll, which is a trial run to test the electronic voting machines, and the formal commencement of the poll, the equipment must be used to capture photographic evidence of the security presence. Specifically, a photograph must be taken showing the Central Armed Police Force or police personnel standing clearly in front of the notice board of the polling station, serving as visual proof that adequate security was in place at the start of operations. In scenarios where dedicated video or digital cameras are not available at a specific location, the Presiding Officer or a Micro Observer, who is an independent observer appointed to monitor the election, is authorized to use a mobile phone equipped with a camera to fulfill this requirement. They must click a photograph of the security arrangements and either transmit the image immediately to the central control room or physically hand over the device or image file at the time when the Electronic Voting Machines are handed over to the receiving centers after the poll concludes. If neither fixed cameras nor mobile devices are available at the polling station itself, the responsibility shifts to the sector officer, a supervisory official who visits multiple stations; this officer must capture an image of the polling station along with the deployed security personnel during their inspection visit, ensuring that no station goes undocumented.

In instances where webcasting is implemented as a civil measure to enhance transparency and allow remote monitoring of the election, strict technical and positional guidelines must be followed to ensure the footage is useful and compliant with voter secrecy laws. The camera used for webcasting must be installed at a sufficient height, typically recommended to be between seven to eight feet above the ground, to provide an elevated perspective that covers a wider area without being easily obstructed by the crowd. This camera must be mounted on a stable and sturdy platform or secured on a wall-mounted stand and kept in a fixed position throughout the duration of the poll to prevent shaky or manipulated footage. The angle and position of the camera are critical and must be calibrated to capture a broad view of several key aspects of the election proceedings while strictly adhering to privacy norms. The transmitted feed must clearly show the process of voter identification conducted by the Polling Officer, where the identity of the voter is verified against the electoral roll. It must also capture the application of indelible ink on the finger of the voter, a crucial step that prevents multiple voting by marking those who have already cast their ballot. The footage should further record the initialization of the Control Unit of the Electronic Voting Machine by the Presiding Officer, which occurs only after the satisfactory identification of the voter, signaling that the machine is ready to accept a vote. Additionally, the camera must capture the voter's movement into the voting compartment to cast their vote on the Ballot Unit of the Electronic Voting Machine, but extreme care must be taken to ensure that the cover face of the Ballot Unit is never shown, thereby preserving the secrecy of the voter's choice under all conditions as mandated by law. The broadcast should also include the presence of Polling Agents, who represent various political parties and oversee the fairness of the process, to the possible extent allowed by the space. Finally, the webcast must cover the closing procedures, including the distribution of slips or tokens to voters who are still in the queue or peripheral area at the time of closing, ensuring they are allowed to vote, and the subsequent sealing of the Electronic Voting Machine components, including the Ballot Unit and Control Unit, as well as the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail units. The final act captured should include the handing over of copies of Form 17-C, which is a detailed account of the votes polled, to the polling agents, completing the chain of custody and transparency for the election day activities.

The deployment of surveillance technology at polling stations represents a critical component of modern election integrity protocols, requiring precise logistical execution beginning one day prior to the actual voting event. On this preparatory day, designated as P-minus-one, every web-camera or laptop intended for live transmission must be physically installed and configured within the boundaries of the assigned polling station. Before the system goes live for public viewing, a comprehensive dry run of the entire webcasting process must be conducted. This trial operation is not merely a technical check but a formal procedural requirement that must take place under the direct and personal supervision of the Returning Officer, who holds the primary responsibility for the conduct of elections in that constituency. To ensure transparency and provide constant context to remote viewers, a physical label containing the specific name and unique identification number of the polling station, along with the official date of the poll, must be affixed to a location that remains permanently within the field of view of the camera lens. This ensures that anyone watching the feed can immediately identify the location and time of the broadcast without ambiguity. Following the successful completion of these setup and testing phases, the Booth Level Officer and the relevant Sector Officer are mandated to issue a formal certificate for each individual polling station. This document serves as an official attestation that the webcasting arrangements have been established exactly according to specifications and are fully functional. These individual certificates form the basis upon which the District Election Officer compiles a consolidated report, which is then transmitted to the Chief Electoral Officer for final submission to the Election Commission, thereby creating a verified chain of accountability from the local booth level up to the highest electoral authority.

Public awareness regarding the presence of surveillance is maintained through standardized signage protocols designed to be highly visible and unmistakable. The Returning Officer is responsible for procuring or creating signs with specific dimensions of thirty inches by eighteen inches. These signs must adhere to a strict color scheme consisting of black lettering printed on a fluorescent yellow background to maximize contrast and visibility under various lighting conditions. The text on these signs must explicitly state that the area is under web-camera or closed-circuit television surveillance. These notices are not to be hidden or placed inconspicuously; rather, they must be displayed prominently at multiple strategic locations both inside the polling booth where voting occurs and outside the premises where voters queue or gather. This dual placement ensures that all individuals entering the electoral environment are duly informed of the monitoring measures in place. Furthermore, the technical framework used to transmit these video feeds is subject to strict content regulations. It is imperative that the software or platform utilized for webcasting does not display any form of commercial advertisement, promotional material, or unrelated graphical overlays. The purity of the broadcast feed is essential to maintain the solemnity and neutrality of the electoral process, ensuring that the focus remains solely on the conduct of the election without external commercial distractions.

To manage the vast amount of data and communication generated during an election, the District Election Officer is required to establish a centralized command hub known as the District Control Room. This facility is not a single room but a complex operational center composed of three distinct yet interconnected functional components. The first component is the Communication Control Room, which serves as the nerve center for connectivity. It must be equipped with robust communication infrastructure capable of handling high volumes of traffic and must house dedicated teams assigned to monitor specific districts or groups of districts. The personnel for these teams are drawn effectively from various government offices to ensure adequate manpower for continuous poll-day monitoring and seamless communication flow. The second component is the Media Monitoring Control Room, a specialized unit tasked with tracking news coverage across national and state-level media outlets, with a particular emphasis on local media channels that report on ground-level events. This allows authorities to stay informed about public perception and emerging narratives in real-time. The third component is the Webcasting Control Room, which is dedicated exclusively to monitoring the live video feeds and data streams from all polling stations throughout the entire duration of the voting period. This tripartite structure ensures that communication, media sentiment, and visual surveillance are managed simultaneously but with specialized focus. At a slightly lower administrative level, the Returning Officer, or the Additional Returning Officer in the case of a Parliamentary Constituency, must establish a Constituency or Segment Control Room. This localized hub facilitates immediate communication, manages logistics such as transport and equipment distribution, and coordinates emergency response efforts specific to that electoral segment.

The flow of information from the field to the command centers follows a rigorous reporting matrix based on cumulative voter turnout data. This schedule dictates exactly when data must be entered and reported by the Returning Officer to ensure timely updates on election progress. The first reporting window covers the period from the start of polling until nine o'clock in the morning, with the data entry deadline set between nine-zero-zero and nine-thirty AM. Subsequent intervals follow a similar pattern: turnout from the start until eleven AM must be reported between eleven-zero-zero and eleven-thirty AM; data covering the period up to one PM is entered between one-zero-zero and one-thirty PM; the interval ending at three PM requires reporting between three-zero-zero and three-thirty PM; and the period concluding at five PM must be logged between five-zero-zero and five-thirty PM. A final major update occurs after the polls close, with data entry taking place between seven PM and midnight. A concluding report is submitted on the day following the poll, known as P-plus-one. Adherence to this timeline is critical for maintaining an accurate and up-to-date picture of voter participation across the district.

Sector Officers play a pivotal role as the eyes and ears of the election administration on the ground, required to visit all polling stations under their jurisdiction at the shortest possible intervals to ensure continuous oversight. These officers are bound by mandatory reporting protocols that require them to communicate specific milestones and incidents to the Returning Officer or the Constituency Control Room without any delay. The first such report concerns the completion of the mock poll, a trial run conducted before actual voting begins to verify machine functionality; this report must confirm the issuance of Mock Poll Certificates and the subsequent deletion of all mock poll data, including the physical removal of mock poll slips from the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail units. The next mandatory report marks the official commencement of the poll. During the voting process, the Sector Officer must immediately report any instances where Electronic Voting Machines or VVPAT units require replacement, whether during the mock poll or the actual voting hours. They are also responsible for transmitting approximate voter turnout figures at the prescribed intervals through the appropriate communication channels. As the day concludes, the officer must report the pressing of the CLOSE button on the machines, the sealing of the equipment, and the departure of the polling parties, ensuring they travel only in designated vehicles along pre-approved routes to reach the Reception Center safely. Additionally, any unwarranted events, disturbances, or irregularities must be reported instantly, along with details of the immediate corrective measures taken. The Sector Officer must also fulfill any other reporting duties assigned by the District Election Officer or Returning Officer. To document these activities formally, Sector Officers and Magistrates must make a physical entry in a Visit Sheet maintained by the Presiding Officer at each station. Special attention is mandated for polling stations where the mock poll had to be conducted in the absence of political party agents, as these locations require heightened scrutiny. The movement of Sector Officers follows a predetermined route from booth to booth, and after inspecting each location, they must verbally communicate the prevailing situation to the designated control room via telephone before proceeding to the next station, ensuring a continuous loop of information.

To handle unforeseen challenges and ensure rapid resolution of issues, Quick Response Teams are formed with the specific mandate to address situations promptly and ascertain facts on the ground quickly. These teams act as the rapid intervention force of the election machinery. The overall functioning of the District Control Room and these Quick Response Teams is supervised by a senior officer of the district, ensuring that decision-making authority rests with experienced leadership. Regarding the logistics of personnel deployment, the Election Commission has issued strict directives concerning the arrival of polling parties at their assigned stations. Each team of polling officials must be dispatched in such a manner that they arrive at their designated polling station on the day preceding the poll, which is one clear day before the voting commences. This early arrival allows sufficient time for setup and familiarization with the locale. However, the protocol acknowledges that certain exceptions may be necessary due to difficult geographical terrain, such as remote mountainous regions or islands, or due to specific law and order problems that pose safety risks. In such exceptional cases, if polling parties need to be dispatched earlier than the standard P-minus-one schedule, the matter must be discussed with the observer appointed by the Commission. Following this consultation, specific approval must be obtained from the Chief Electoral Officer, who will make the final decision in consultation with the Election Commission. Once the polling party reaches the location, regardless of whether it is on the standard schedule or an advanced timeline, they must proceed to set up the polling station properly. This setup is not done in isolation but is carried out with the assistance of the sector or zonal magistrate and local officials such as the Booth Level Officer or any other designated official present for the purpose, ensuring that the station is established securely and in full compliance with all regulatory requirements.

The establishment and configuration of the voting compartment within a polling station are governed by strict specifications prescribed by the Election Commission to ensure the sanctity and secrecy of the democratic process. The voting compartment, which serves as the private enclosure where an elector records their choice, must be positioned in a specific corner of the room. This placement is critical because it guarantees that the voter can cast their ballot in complete secrecy, shielded from the view of other individuals inside the hall as well as observers outside the building. Great care must be taken to ensure that the compartment is never placed directly in front of or in close proximity to a window. The rationale for this restriction is to prevent any person standing outside the polling station from seeing the voter while they are in the act of casting their vote, which would constitute a breach of voting secrecy. For visual guidance on the optimal arrangement of furniture and equipment, the Commission provides a model layout for both single elections and simultaneous elections in Annexure 26, which serves as a blueprint for setting up the physical space.

Lighting conditions around the voting compartment require meticulous attention due to the sensitive electronic nature of the equipment used. Specifically, high-voltage incandescent bulbs or tube lights must not be installed directly over or immediately in front of the voting compartment. This precaution is necessary because excessive light intensity or heat generated by such fixtures can interfere with the optical sensors and internal mechanisms of the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail, commonly known as VVPAT, potentially causing the machine to enter an error mode and cease functioning correctly. Consequently, the placement of the voting compartment must satisfy four concurrent conditions. First, there must be sufficient ambient light available inside the compartment so that the voter can clearly see the candidates and symbols on the display. Second, no direct lighting source should be aimed at or placed above the compartment to avoid technical malfunctions. Third, the arrangement must strictly preserve the secrecy of the vote, ensuring no line of sight exists from outside the compartment to the voter's actions. Fourth, the compartment must remain distant from any windows or doors that could compromise privacy or security.

The spatial relationship between the voting compartment and the administrative area is also dictated by technical constraints regarding the connecting hardware. The voting compartment houses the Ballot Unit, which is the interface the voter touches, and the VVPAT machine, which prints the paper slip. These devices must be connected via a cable to the Control Unit, which is kept on the table of the Presiding Officer. The distance between the location of the Ballot Unit and VVPAT and the Presiding Officer's table must not exceed three meters. This limitation exists because the standard interconnecting cable provided for this purpose has a total length of five meters, and a buffer of two meters is required to allow for slack, proper routing, and safety. It is imperative that all items within the polling station are arranged so that no voter, while walking toward or away from the voting compartment, is forced to step on or tread over this interconnecting cable. Stepping on the cable could damage the internal wiring or disconnect the units, disrupting the election. Furthermore, the entire length of this cable must remain visible to the polling agents representing the various candidates throughout the duration of the poll. This visibility ensures transparency and prevents any allegation that the cable was tampered with or swapped during the voting process.

On the day of the poll, rigorous timelines must be adhered to by the entire polling party. Every member of the polling team is required to report to the polling station and assume their designated positions at least one hour before the official time fixed for the commencement of voting. This early arrival allows for final checks and preparations. The Electronic Voting Machine, known as the EVM, and the associated VVPAT unit must be set up and ready well before the time scheduled for the Mock Poll. A Mock Poll is a trial run conducted in the presence of candidates' representatives to demonstrate that the machines are functioning correctly and recording votes accurately before actual voting begins. Under no circumstances should these machines be left unattended or unprotected in the polling station on the night preceding the day of the poll unless adequate security measures are in place. If the Presiding Officer does not reside at the polling station overnight, it is mandatory to ensure that the EVM and VVPAT are kept under constant and adequate security surveillance at all times to prevent tampering. It is important to note that unnecessary handling of the EVM should be avoided, as frequent operation can drain the batteries prematurely. A depleted battery during the poll could stop the voting process and create unwarranted doubts or suspicions in the minds of candidates and electors regarding the integrity of the election.

The successful conduct of the poll relies heavily on the defined roles and responsibilities of the Presiding Officer and the Polling Officers. Typically, a Presiding Officer can manage the polling process in an orderly fashion with the assistance of three Polling Officers when EVMs and VVPATs are utilized. In this standard configuration, the Presiding Officer personally retains charge of the Control Unit, which is the master device that enables voting and stores the final results. However, the Commission recognizes that in certain scenarios, the Presiding Officer may be unable to devote undivided attention to the Control Unit. This situation may arise if the officer is occupied with other critical duties, such as conducting inquiries into challenged votes where an elector's identity is disputed, completing formalities to provide assistance to blind or infirm electors who cannot vote independently, or issuing tendered ballot papers for specific categories of voters. In such cases, the responsibility for the Control Unit may be delegated to a senior Polling Officer. When this delegation occurs, the polling party structure expands to include three Polling Officers in addition to the Presiding Officer, making a team of four. To facilitate the storage of the paper slips generated by the VVPAT after the poll concludes, an adequate number of black paper envelopes must be procured in advance. These opaque envelopes ensure that the printed slips remain confidential until they are counted. For a comprehensive and detailed description of the duties assigned to the Presiding Officer and each individual Polling Officer, personnel are directed to consult the latest Handbook for Presiding Officers, a separate publication issued by the Commission.

The distribution of duties among the three Polling Officers follows a logical workflow designed to streamline the movement of voters. The First Polling Officer is primarily in charge of the identification of electors. This officer verifies the identity of each person arriving to vote against the marked copy of the electoral roll, which is the official list of registered voters for that specific polling station. Once identification is confirmed, the elector proceeds to the Second Polling Officer. The duty of the Second Polling Officer involves the application of indelible ink on the left forefinger of the elector. This ink is a special formulation that cannot be easily washed off and serves as a physical mark indicating that the person has already voted, thereby preventing double voting. Additionally, the Second Polling Officer is responsible for maintaining the Register of Voters, officially designated as Form 17A, which logs every individual who has cast a ballot. The Third Polling Officer is tasked with managing the Control Unit of the EVM. This officer operates the button that releases the ballot for the next voter after verifying that the previous voter has completed the process. In polling stations where the total number of assigned voters is small, the workload may be lighter, and in such instances, the duties typically assigned to the Third Polling Officer can be performed directly by the Presiding Officer himself. Regardless of the specific allocation of tasks, it is mandatory that intensive hands-on training be provided to all Presiding Officers and Polling Officers. This practical training ensures that every official is fully conversant with their duties, familiar with the operation of the electronic equipment, and capable of handling any contingencies that may arise during the poll.

The physical seating arrangements inside the polling station are designed to enforce a systematic and unidirectional flow of electors to maintain order and prevent confusion. The Presiding Officer should be seated in a strategic location within the room that offers a clear and unobstructed view of the entire proceedings. From this vantage point, the Presiding Officer must be able to observe the activities at all stations, monitor the behavior of the crowd, and oversee the polling agents to ensure compliance with rules. The Polling Officers must be seated in a linear sequence that guides the elector through the voting process without deviation. Upon entering the polling station, an elector should proceed straightway to the First Polling Officer for identification. After clearing this stage, the elector moves directly to the Second Polling Officer for the ink marking and register entry. From there, the elector proceeds to the Third Polling Officer or the Control Unit to cast their vote. Finally, the elector may approach the Presiding Officer if any final verification or exit formalities are required. This linear progression is essential, and no crisscross movement of electors should be permitted under any circumstances. Allowing voters to move back and forth or skip stations would create congestion, increase the risk of impersonation or double voting, and undermine the orderly conduct of the election. By enforcing this strict sequential path, the polling station operates efficiently, securely, and transparently.

The role of a polling agent is fundamental to the integrity of the democratic process, serving as the eyes and ears of a political candidate within the polling station on the day of the election. When examining the legal framework governing these individuals, it is important to note that the law does not prescribe any specific professional or educational qualifications for a person to be appointed as a polling agent. This lack of rigid qualification criteria is intentional, allowing candidates the flexibility to choose representatives who can effectively safeguard their interests during the voting process. The primary expectation placed upon a polling agent is to monitor proceedings at the polling station to ensure fairness and transparency on behalf of their assigned candidate. While there are no strict legal barriers regarding who can serve, electoral guidelines strongly suggest and prefer that the appointed individual be a local person. Ideally, this person should be a registered elector and an ordinary resident of the same polling station or, at the very most, a resident of a neighboring polling station. This preference for locality ensures that the agent is familiar with the community and the specific dynamics of the area. However, recognizing that finding such local representatives may sometimes present logistical challenges, the Election Commission has relaxed these norms. In cases where it is difficult to appoint a local resident from the immediate or neighboring polling station, the rules now permit the appointment of any registered elector from within the broader Assembly Constituency area. Regardless of their place of residence, every polling agent must possess a valid Elector Photo Identity Card, commonly known as an EPIC, or any other alternative identification document that has been officially prescribed by the Commission. This identity verification is crucial for establishing the legitimacy of the agent. Furthermore, there are no restrictions preventing local elected officials from serving in this capacity; specifically, individuals holding positions such as Gram Panchayat Pradhan, Gram Panchayat Sarpanch, Panchayat members, Councillors, or Members of a Municipal Corporation or Municipality are fully eligible to act as polling agents, provided they do not fall under specific disqualification categories related to higher government service.

To maintain a level playing field among all competing candidates and political parties, the legal framework explicitly outlines certain categories of individuals who are disqualified from serving as either a primary polling agent or a relief polling agent. These restrictions are designed to prevent the misuse of official power, government resources, or administrative influence during the election. The list of disqualified persons includes sitting Ministers from either the Union Government or any State Government, as well as members of the legislative bodies, such as Members of Parliament, Members of the Legislative Assembly, and Members of the Legislative Council. The prohibition extends to high-ranking local officials including Mayors of Corporations and Chairpersons of Municipalities, Zila Parishads, or Panchayat Unions. Additionally, chairpersons and members of Central Public Sector Undertakings, State Public Sector Undertakings, and various government bodies or corporations are barred from acting as agents. The restrictions also cover individuals who receive any form of honorarium from the government or those working on a part-time basis in any government or government-aided institutions. Specific professions deemed to have significant government interaction or influence, such as paramedical and healthcare staff working in government or government-aided facilities, Fair Price Shop dealers, and Anganwadi employees, are also included in this disqualification list. Most critically, any person currently in the service of the government is strictly prohibited from acting as a polling agent. Appointing a government servant to this role is considered a criminal offense under Section 134-A of the Representation of the People Act, 1951. Individuals found guilty of such an appointment, whether the appointee or the appointer, are punishable with imprisonment for a term that may extend to three months, or with a fine, or with both penalties. The regulations further clarify that any person who has been provided with security cover by the state due to threat perception or official status will not be allowed to surrender or waive that security cover for the purpose of acting as a polling agent during an election. This rule prevents potential security lapses and ensures that the presence of high-profile individuals does not disrupt the polling environment.

The operational procedures for polling agents are rigorous and designed to eliminate any possibility of mischief or impersonation. A polling agent must possess a valid EPIC to establish their identity beyond doubt. Upon arriving at the designated polling station, the agent must report to the Presiding Officer, who is the official in charge of conducting the poll at that specific location. The Presiding Officer is mandated to check the agent's appointment letter, which must have been issued by the candidate or their election agent. Crucially, the Presiding Officer will verify the signature on this document against the specimen signature provided by the Returning Officer, who oversees the election for the entire constituency. This verification step is essential to ensure that only authorized representatives are granted access to the sensitive areas of the polling station. Once verified, the polling agents are required to display their appointment order and their EPIC prominently on their person throughout their presence in the polling station. To avoid any last-minute controversies or confusion, the Returning Officer is responsible for communicating these requirements in advance to each contesting candidate or their election agent through written communications. These communications also intimate that polling agents should reach the polling station at least ninety minutes before the time fixed for the commencement of the poll. This early arrival is critical because during this ninety-minute window, the Presiding Officer prepares the Electronic Voting Machines, known as EVMs, and the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail units, known as VVPATs. The Presiding Officer will also conduct a mock poll, which is a trial run of the voting machines to demonstrate to the polling agents that the machines are functioning correctly and recording votes accurately. If a polling agent arrives late, they will miss these critical actions. The Presiding Officer cannot wait for any agent to arrive, as they are legally bound to prepare and commission the EVM and VVPAT systems well before the exact time voting begins. Therefore, tardiness results in the loss of the opportunity to witness the setup and testing of the voting equipment.

While each candidate is entitled to appoint one additional polling agent to serve as a relief agent, strict rules govern their presence within the polling station. At any given point in time, only one agent representing a specific candidate, whether the primary agent or the relief agent, is allowed inside the polling station. Both cannot be present simultaneously. This rule ensures that the polling station does not become overcrowded and that the monitoring process remains orderly. Furthermore, under no circumstances is a polling agent allowed to take their copy of the electoral roll, which lists the registered voters, outside the premises of the polling station before the conclusion of the poll. This restriction is vital to protect the privacy of voters and prevent data misuse. However, the regulations do acknowledge basic human needs; polling agents may be permitted to leave the polling station temporarily, even after 3:00 PM, to attend to nature's call or similar necessities, provided they return promptly. It must be ensured that during such absences, only the designated polling agent or their authorized substitute is present inside the station, maintaining the one-agent-per-candidate limit. The Presiding Officer is also responsible for briefing the polling agents on their duty to remain present in the polling station until the voting process is completely over. Their presence is required to oversee the sealing procedure of the EVMs and VVPATs, which secures the recorded votes, and to sign the necessary declaration forms that certify the conduct of the poll. To regulate the movement and presence of these agents effectively during poll hours, the Election Commission has prescribed an Entry Pass system. Every polling agent or their relief agent must display this pass on their body while inside the polling station. The Presiding Officer issues one Entry Pass to one polling agent of each candidate using a standardized format. This pass includes specific details such as a serial number, the name and number of the Assembly Constituency, the number and name of the polling station, the name of the candidate being represented, the name of the polling agent, and the name of the relief agent if one has been appointed. This comprehensive documentation and procedural strictness ensure that the election process remains transparent, secure, and fair for all participants.

The Presiding Officer at every polling station bears the critical responsibility of maintaining a precise and verifiable account of all entry passes issued to polling agents, which serves as a foundational element for ensuring transparency and order during the electoral process. This account must be kept in a specific format that meticulously records the number and name of the Assembly Constituency, which is a geographic area represented by a single member in the state legislative assembly, as well as the number and name of the specific Polling Station where voting takes place. The document must also list the total number of candidates contesting the election within that constituency and detail the exact quantity of entry passes that were received along with the official poll materials before the voting commenced. A comprehensive ledger is required to track the details of every entry pass issued, including the name of the candidate who appointed the agent, a confirmation of whether the pass was actually issued, and the signature of the polling agent or their relieving counterpart who received it. Any unused entry passes must be securely accounted for and authenticated with the signature and official seal of the Presiding Officer to prevent misuse or unauthorized entry into the sensitive voting area.

In addition to the entry pass ledger, a dedicated movement sheet for polling agents and relieving agents must be provided to each polling station to monitor the presence and activity of these representatives throughout the day. Every single polling agent is legally required to sign this sheet upon arrival, clearly indicating the exact time they entered the premises, and must sign again upon departure to record the time they left. This continuous log ensures that there is an unbroken chain of observation and accountability. Observers appointed by the Election Commission, Sector Magistrates who oversee multiple stations, and other senior officers who visit the polling station during the course of the poll are mandated to verify that this movement sheet is being properly maintained and updated in real time. The proforma for this sheet includes serial numbers, the number and name of the Parliamentary Constituency which elects a member to the national lower house, the number and name of the Assembly Constituency, the name of the candidate, the name of the political party they represent, the full name of the polling agent or relieving agent, and columns for recording the time of entry with a corresponding signature and the time of exit with another signature, all finally attested by the signature of the Presiding Officer.

To facilitate immediate grievance redressal and ensure rapid communication in case of any irregularities, the telephone numbers of key officials must be prominently displayed at all polling stations. This list includes the contact details of Returning Officers who manage the election in a constituency, Assistant Returning Officers who assist them, key police officers responsible for security, Sector Officers who supervise a group of polling stations, and the central Control Rooms that coordinate overall election operations. The purpose of this display is to empower polling agents to establish contact instantly if they have any complaints, thereby enabling immediate intervention by higher authorities to resolve issues before they escalate. Furthermore, polling agents are required to bring specific materials with them to the polling station to perform their duties effectively and legally. These mandatory items include the original letter of appointment issued in Form 10, which serves as their official credential, and a copy of the latest integrated electoral roll specific to that polling station, which contains the list of all eligible voters. Agents may also choose to bring a small brass seal if they wish to affix their personal seal on the carrying cases of the Ballot Units, Control Units, and Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail machines before these devices are transported to the Counting or Receipt Centre after the poll closes. Additionally, agents must carry their own pen, paper, and pencil for note-taking, as well as detailed information regarding the specific Electronic Voting Machines and VVPAT units assigned to their station, as provided by the Returning Officer or the candidate they represent.

The physical arrangement of the polling station must include suitable seating arrangements for polling agents to ensure they can effectively observe the democratic process without obstruction. Their seats must be strategically positioned to provide them with an adequate opportunity to identify electors as they arrive, view the entire operational procedure taking place at the table of the Presiding Officer or the Third Polling Officer where the Control Unit of the voting machine is kept, and clearly see the movement of voters from the identification table to the voting compartment and their subsequent exit after recording their vote on the Ballot Unit located inside the secure booth. This visibility is crucial for agents to detect any potential malpractice or procedural errors. When allocating these seats, the Presiding Officer must follow a strict order of priority based on the status of the candidates. The highest priority for seating is given to candidates of recognized National parties, followed by candidates of recognized State parties. Next in line are candidates of recognized State parties from other states who have been granted permission to use their reserved symbols in the current constituency, followed by candidates of registered but unrecognized parties, and finally, independent candidates who do not belong to any registered party. This hierarchy ensures that major political entities with broader mandates are afforded primary observational vantage points while still accommodating all legitimate participants.

On the day of the poll, the Presiding Officer is required to compile a comprehensive document known as the Presiding Officer's Report, which is divided into five distinct parts to capture various stages and incidents of the voting process. Part One of this report is the Mock Poll Certificate, which must be filled out immediately after the completion of the mock poll conducted on the morning of election day to verify that the voting machines are functioning correctly; since this certificate is now integrated into Part One, there is no longer a need to issue a separate standalone document for the mock poll. Part Two of the report is dedicated to recording any instance where the power pack of the Control Unit is replaced during the day, ensuring a log of hardware maintenance. Part Three requires the officer to certify the pressing of the close button after the actual poll has concluded, marking the official end of voting. Part Four is an EVM and VVPAT replacement report that must be filled out during the mock poll phase if any Ballot Unit, Control Unit, or VVPAT machine had to be swapped out due to malfunction. Similarly, Part Five serves as the replacement report for any such equipment changes that occur during the actual polling hours. Once completed, Parts One, Two, and Three of the Presiding Officer's Report must be sealed together in a dedicated envelope. This envelope must bear pre-printed details including the name of the election, the number and name of the Assembly Constituency or Assembly Segment, and the number and name of the Parliamentary Constituency, ensuring that the document is securely identified and tracked throughout the post-poll handling and counting processes.

The procedural framework governing the conclusion of polling activities mandates strict adherence to chain-of-custody protocols for all election materials. Upon the closure of polls, the Presiding Officer, who serves as the primary authority responsible for the conduct of voting at a specific station, is legally obligated to secure the sealed envelope containing critical documents alongside the Electronic Voting Machine, commonly known as the EVM, and the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail device, referred to as the VVPAT. These items, collectively categorized as election material, must be physically transported and deposited at a designated Collection Centre. This step ensures that all voting hardware and associated records are centralized under secure supervision immediately after the voting process ends. Furthermore, specific sections of the official documentation, identified as Part-IV and Part-V of the Presiding Officer's Report, require special handling procedures whenever a replacement of equipment occurs during the voting day. In such instances of equipment substitution, these report sections are not retained by the Presiding Officer but must instead be collected directly by the Sector Officer. The Sector Officer acts as an intermediate supervisory role responsible for overseeing multiple polling stations within a defined geographic sector. Once the Sector Officer has gathered these reports from various stations where replacements took place, they are duty-bound to submit the compiled documents to the Returning Officer. The Returning Officer holds the highest administrative responsibility for the entire constituency and requires these reports to maintain a comprehensive audit trail of any technical interventions that occurred during the election.

When completing Part-IV or Part-V of the aforementioned report, the Presiding Officer must exercise extreme diligence in recording technical anomalies with precision. This involves selecting and entering the correct error codes that correspond to specific mechanical or electronic failures encountered by the voting machines. For instance, if a cutting mechanism within the paper trail printer malfunctions, the officer must record error code 2.6, which denotes a cutter error. Similarly, if there is an issue with the paper falling correctly into the collection box, error code 2.7, known as a fall error, must be utilized. Beyond numeric codes, the report must also include descriptive observations regarding the auditory and visual signals emitted by the Control Unit, often abbreviated as CU. The officer must explicitly note whether a beep sound was heard upon button activation and whether the busy lamp, an indicator light on the Control Unit that signals the machine is processing a vote, remained illuminated as expected. These details are crucial for post-election forensic analysis to determine if the machines functioned within normal parameters. Failure to comply with these detailed reporting requirements, including the accurate logging of error codes and status indicators, is considered a serious breach of protocol and will result in disciplinary action being taken against the negligent Presiding Officer.

Following the completion of the polling process across all stations, the Returning Officer assumes the responsibility of reviewing the aggregate Presiding Officer's Reports before the vote counting phase begins. A critical component of this review involves identifying any polling stations where the mock poll data was not successfully cleared. A mock poll is a trial run conducted before actual voting starts to verify that the machines are functioning correctly and to ensure that the memory of the devices is reset to zero. If the data from this test run remains in the system, it could corrupt the final vote tally. Therefore, the Returning Officer must scrutinize the reports to flag any such irregularities well in advance of the counting of votes, allowing time for corrective measures or investigative procedures to be initiated without delaying the declaration of results.

The physical arrangement of the voting environment is designed fundamentally to protect the secrecy of the ballot, a cornerstone of democratic elections. To achieve this, both the Ballot Units and the VVPAT devices must be housed within a structure known as the Voting Compartment. The placement of this compartment is subject to strict spatial constraints; it must not be positioned near a window or the door of the polling station to prevent outsiders from observing the voter's actions or the machine's display. The Voting Compartment itself is constructed with three covered sides to create a private enclosure for the voter while leaving one side open for entry and exit. Inside this compartment, the Ballot Units and the VVPAT are placed upon a table. The positioning of the Ballot Unit is critical; it must be arranged ergonomically so that voters of all ages and physical abilities can record their votes without difficulty or strain. Simultaneously, the VVPAT printer must be oriented such that the paper trail, which displays the symbol of the candidate selected by the voter, is clearly visible to the elector. This visibility is essential for the voter to verify that their intent has been correctly recorded by the machine before the paper slip automatically drops into the sealed drop box of the VVPAT device.

The cabling infrastructure connecting these devices requires careful management to balance functionality with security and safety. The connecting cable, which is permanently attached to the VVPAT to prevent tampering, must exit the Voting Compartment through a specifically cut aperture located at the bottom of the rear panel. This design ensures that wires do not clutter the voter's space while maintaining the integrity of the device connection. However, the dimensions of this aperture are strictly regulated; it must not be cut too wide, as an excessive opening could potentially allow someone outside the compartment to glimpse the internal workings or the paper trail, thereby violating the secrecy of voting. Furthermore, the interconnecting cables linking the Ballot Units, the Control Unit, and the VVPAT must be routed along paths that do not obstruct the movement of voters within the polling station. This prevents tripping hazards and ensures a smooth flow of electors entering and exiting the voting area, which is vital for maintaining order and efficiency during peak voting hours.

When installing the Ballot Units and VVPAT within the voting compartment, the overarching principle that must be upheld without fail is the absolute preservation of voting secrecy. Standard operating procedures dictate that the VVPAT device should be placed to the left side of the first Ballot Unit to establish a consistent layout across all polling stations, reducing voter confusion. The construction material of the Voting Compartment is also specified in detail; it must be fabricated from corrugated plastic sheet, often referred to as flex-board, which is steel grey in color. This specific color and material choice is mandated because the steel grey hue is opaque, ensuring that no light passes through the walls to reveal silhouettes or movements inside, and the material is durable enough to be reused in future elections. The geometric design of the compartment consists of three folds. When a single Ballot Unit is employed, each fold must measure twenty-four inches in length, twenty-four inches in width, and thirty inches in height. The table supporting this entire assembly must have a height of exactly thirty inches to align with the compartment dimensions and provide a comfortable working level for the voter.

In scenarios where the volume of candidates necessitates the use of more than one Ballot Unit at a single polling station, the dimensions of the Voting Compartment must be adjusted accordingly. Specifically, the width of the compartment may be increased by twelve inches for every additional Ballot Unit installed. This modular expansion ensures that all necessary interface buttons remain accessible to the voter without compromising the enclosed nature of the booth. Additionally, informational details regarding the election, such as instructions or candidate lists, must be affixed to three sides of the Voting Compartment using self-adhesive stickers. The font size and style of these stickers are regulated to ensure they are legible and readable not only by the human eye but also by closed-circuit television, known as CCTV, and webcasting cameras that monitor the polling station remotely. This visibility allows remote observers to verify that proper procedures are being followed. Finally, adequate electricity arrangements must be established at the polling station to power the electronic devices throughout the day. However, a specific lighting restriction applies: no direct lighting fixtures should be placed immediately over the Voting Compartment. This prohibition prevents glare on the machine screens and eliminates shadows that could inadvertently reveal the voter's hand movements or choices, thus safeguarding the sanctity of the secret ballot.

The Election Commission of India oversees the intricate and highly regulated process of conducting free and fair elections across the nation, a task that relies heavily on the precise setup and verification of electronic voting machinery within designated voting compartments. Before any voter casts a ballot, a rigorous series of pre-poll checks must be executed by the Presiding Officer to ensure the integrity, security, and functionality of the equipment. This procedure begins with an examination of the Ballot Unit, which is the interface where voters select their preferred candidates. It is important to understand that the Ballot Unit arrives at the polling station already fully prepared and configured by the Returning Officer, a senior official responsible for the conduct of the election in that specific constituency. Consequently, the Presiding Officer does not need to perform any complex programming or setup on the device itself on the day of the poll. The primary physical action required regarding the Ballot Unit is the careful connection of its interconnecting cable to the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail, commonly known as the VVPAT unit. This VVPAT machine serves as a critical transparency mechanism, generating a physical paper slip for every vote cast, allowing the voter to verify that their electronic selection has been recorded correctly before the slip is stored in a sealed box. In scenarios where a polling station serves a large number of voters or a constituency with many candidates, more than one Ballot Unit may be necessary. In such cases, these multiple units must be linked together in a strict sequential order to ensure that the candidate list flows logically from one unit to the next. When multiple Ballot Units are employed, only the very first unit in the chain is connected directly to the VVPAT machine, while the subsequent units feed into the first one. Despite the pre-configuration, the Presiding Officer must visually inspect the Ballot Unit to confirm that the ballot paper, which displays the names and symbols of the candidates, is securely fixed behind the protective screen in the display panel. Furthermore, the officer must verify the integrity of the security seals affixed by the Returning Officer. These seals, typically made of pink paper, are placed at both the top and bottom portions on the right-hand side of the Ballot Unit. The presence of these intact seals is crucial evidence that the machine has not been tampered with since it left the secure custody of the Returning Officer. Additionally, the thumbwheel switch, a mechanical dial used to set the number of candidates on the unit, must be checked to ensure it is correctly positioned according to the election requirements.

Following the inspection of the Ballot Unit, the Presiding Officer must proceed to check the VVPAT unit with equal diligence. This device contains several components that require verification to guarantee it will function throughout the long hours of polling. The officer must examine the compartment housing the paper roll, which is the supply of thermal paper used to print the voter slips. This compartment must be properly sealed on both sides using official address tags to prevent unauthorized access or replacement of the paper. On the rear side of the machine, the battery compartment must be inspected to ensure that the VVPAT power pack, which provides the necessary electrical energy for operation, is correctly installed. Another critical mechanical check involves the paper roll knob located on the back of the unit. Before the machine is powered on, this knob must be in the horizontal position, which acts as a lock to secure the paper roll during transport and setup. Once the voting begins, this knob will be turned to a vertical unlock position to allow the paper to feed through the printer. The next phase of the pre-poll protocol involves the Control Unit, which acts as the brain of the entire electronic voting system. The Control Unit houses the logic that records votes and manages the communication between the Ballot Unit and the VVPAT. The Presiding Officer must first verify that the security seals placed by the Returning Officer on specific sections of the Control Unit remain unbroken. Specifically, the seals on the Candidate Set Section, where candidate data is loaded, and the Battery Section must be intact. There is also a distinct pink paper seal that bridges the gap between the Candidate Set Section and the Result Section; both parts of this seal must be present and undamaged. These seals serve as a physical audit trail, providing immediate visual confirmation that the internal settings of the machine have not been altered after the final certification by the Returning Officer.

Once all individual units have been inspected and verified, the process of connecting the Electronic Voting Machine components and the VVPAT begins. The Presiding Officer must arrange the physical layout of the equipment within the voting compartment to maintain the secrecy of the vote, a fundamental principle of democratic elections. The Ballot Unit or units and the VVPAT machine are placed inside the private voting compartment, while the Control Unit is positioned on the table of the Presiding Officer or polling officer, outside the compartment but within clear view. The VVPAT machine should be situated to the left side of the first Ballot Unit to facilitate the correct cabling path. The connection process involves plugging the interconnecting cable from the Ballot Unit, or the first Ballot Unit if multiple are used, into the designated socket located in the rear compartment of the VVPAT. Subsequently, the interconnecting cable extending from the VVPAT is plugged into the corresponding socket in the rear compartment of the Control Unit. After establishing these connections, it is vital to manage the cabling to prevent accidental disconnection or damage during the voting process. The connecting wires should be secured to the leg of the table using transparent adhesive tape. This taping must be done carefully so that the wires do not hang loosely in the air, as the weight of hanging wires could strain the connecting switches on the Ballot Unit and VVPAT, potentially causing a malfunction. The tape used must be transparent and half an inch in width, ensuring that the wires remain visible for inspection while being held firmly in place. Crucially, the tape must be applied in a manner that allows for easy removal if a unit needs to be replaced due to a technical fault, ensuring that maintenance can be performed quickly without damaging the equipment or the wires.

With the physical connections secured, the system is ready to be powered on. The Presiding Officer must first rotate the paper roll knob on the back of the VVPAT from the horizontal lock position to the vertical unlock position, enabling the paper mechanism to operate. Next, the main Power switch located in the rear compartment of the Control Unit is toggled to the ON position. Upon activation, the system will emit a distinct beep sound, serving as an auditory confirmation that the power supply is active and the self-diagnostic systems are engaging. Simultaneously, the ON lamp on the display section of the Control Unit will illuminate in green, providing a visual signal that the machine is ready for operation. The Presiding Officer must watch the display carefully during this startup sequence to ensure no error codes or warning lights appear. Once these checks are complete, the rear compartment of the Control Unit is closed. To keep it firmly shut without permanently sealing it, a piece of thin wire is threaded through two provided holes and twisted tightly. It is explicitly noted that this compartment is not to be sealed with an official seal at this stage, because it must be accessible after the close of polls to switch off the power and disconnect the VVPAT for the storage of election materials. The final preparatory step before the actual election begins is the Mock Poll. This is a trial run designed to demonstrate to the polling agents and candidates that the machine is recording votes accurately and that the VVPAT is printing the correct slips. The Presiding Officer is mandated to commence this Mock Poll ninety minutes before the official start of voting. To ensure transparency and build trust, polling agents representing at least two different candidates must be present to witness this procedure. However, recognizing that agents may sometimes be delayed, the rules provide a contingency: if agents for two candidates are not present at the scheduled time, the Presiding Officer may wait for an additional fifteen minutes. If the agents still fail to arrive after this grace period, the Presiding Officer is authorized to proceed with the Mock Poll to avoid unnecessary delays in the commencement of the democratic process, ensuring that the election schedule is adhered to while maintaining the highest standards of verification and readiness.

It is further clarified within the official election procedures that after a mandatory waiting period of fifteen minutes has elapsed, there exists a possibility that only a single polling agent may be present at the polling station. In this specific scenario, where full representation from all contesting parties is not physically available, the Presiding Officer is strictly instructed to proceed without delay and initiate the mock poll process. This ensures that the verification of voting machines is not hindered by the absence of agents, maintaining the schedule and integrity of the election day operations. In such cases where the mock poll proceeds with limited agent presence, it is imperative that the Presiding Officer makes a specific and detailed mention of this circumstance in Part-One of the Presiding Officer's official report. This documentation serves as a critical legal record confirming that the procedure was conducted transparently despite the reduced number of observers, thereby safeguarding the process against future disputes regarding the validity of the machine verification.

The mock poll itself is a rigorous testing procedure designed to validate the functionality of the electronic voting infrastructure before actual voting commences. This test must involve the casting of at least fifty votes to ensure statistical reliability in verifying the machine's counting mechanism. Furthermore, these votes must be distributed equally among every unmasked button on the ballot unit, which corresponds to each contesting candidate including the option for None of the Above, often abbreviated as NOTA. To facilitate this exercise, the physical arrangement of the equipment is strictly defined. The Ballot Unit, which is the interface where voters press buttons to select their choice, and the VVPAT unit, which stands for Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail and serves as a printer that generates a paper slip for each vote cast, must be placed inside the voting compartment. This compartment is the private area where voters mark their choices. Meanwhile, the Control Unit, which acts as the brain of the system connecting to the ballot unit and storing the vote data, must be positioned on the table of the Presiding Officer or the designated Polling Officer who will operate the controls. This separation ensures that the person operating the machine is distinct from the voters, maintaining a clear chain of custody and observation.

During the execution of the mock poll, one polling officer must be stationed inside the voting compartment alongside whatever polling agents are present. The primary duty of this officer is to closely watch the operations occurring on the Ballot Unit and to observe the ballot slips being printed and dropped by the VVPAT unit. This polling officer is also responsible for maintaining a manual record of every vote cast during this test phase, creating an independent ledger against which the electronic results can be compared. Before any votes are cast, the Presiding Officer must take steps to satisfy both themselves and the observing agents that the Electronic Voting Machine, known as the EVM, and the associated VVPAT unit are in perfect working order. Crucially, they must demonstrate that no votes have been previously recorded in the machines from any prior testing or error. To achieve this transparency, the Presiding Officer must show all persons present that the drop box within the VVPAT unit, which collects the paper slips, is completely empty. Additionally, the officer must press the 'Clear' button located in the Result Section of the Control Unit. This action resets all internal counters, and the display panels on the machine will visibly update to show that the number of votes recorded for each candidate is exactly zero, providing visual confirmation of a clean slate.

Once the machines are verified as empty and functional, the mock poll conduct begins in earnest. The procedure dictates that the polling agents present should vote at random for each of the contesting candidates to simulate a real-world voting pattern. A total of at least fifty votes must be polled during this phase to meet the regulatory threshold. In the event that polling agents for certain candidates are absent, the rules provide a contingency wherein one of the Polling Officers or other available polling agents may record votes on behalf of those missing candidates. This ensures that every candidate receives an equal number of test votes, which is essential for verifying that the machine counts correctly for every option on the ballot. The Polling Officers situated in the Voting Compartment bear the responsibility of ensuring that the distribution of these test votes is perfectly equal for each contesting candidate, including the NOTA option. Once the required number of votes has been cast, the operator must press the 'CLOSE' button on the Control Unit to officially end the mock poll session and lock the data for retrieval.

Following the conclusion of the voting phase of the mock poll, the Presiding Officer must ascertain the results to verify accuracy. This is done by pressing the 'RESULT' button on the Control Unit, which triggers the machine to display the total count of votes received by each candidate. The officer must then carefully tally these electronic results against the manual record kept by the polling officer during the voting process. Simultaneously, the physical verification takes place. The VVPAT paper slips must be counted by physically removing them from the drop box in the full view of the polling agents. This step confirms that the result displayed on the Control Unit matches the actual count of paper slips generated for each candidate, thereby validating the integrity of the paper trail system. It is important to note that the slips removed from the VVPAT drop box during this process will include not only the mock vote slips but also slips generated from self-test reports. These self-test report slips are easily identifiable because they do not contain the name or symbol of any candidate. While these diagnostic slips must be preserved along with the mock poll slips as part of the official record, they are explicitly excluded from the vote count to prevent skewing the data.

After the verification and counting processes are complete, all mock poll data stored in the Control Unit must be permanently erased by pressing the 'CLEAR' button once again. Concurrently, the empty status of the VVPAT drop box must be re-verified by the Polling Agents to ensure no test slips remain inside the machine before actual voting begins. The physical mock poll VVPAT paper slips that were removed are then subjected to a specific sealing protocol. Each slip must be stamped on its back side with a rubber stamp bearing the inscription "MOCK POLL SLIP" to distinguish them from actual election ballots. These marked slips are then placed into an envelope made of thick black paper, which provides opacity and security. The envelope is sealed with the official seal of the Presiding Officer. Both the Presiding Officer and the polling agents must affix their signatures to the envelope to certify its contents. Detailed identification information must be written on the exterior of this envelope, including the number and name of the polling station, the number and name of the Assembly Constituency, the date of the poll, and the specific words "VVPAT paper slips of Mock Poll."

To ensure the highest level of security and tamper-evidence, this envelope must be further sealed with a pink paper seal placed all around the opening in such a manner that any attempt to open the envelope would require breaking the seal, thus leaving visible evidence of interference. The identifying details, including the polling station number and name, constituency information, and the date, must also be written on this pink paper seal. The Presiding Officer and Polling Agents are required to sign across the pink paper seal as well.
