This securely sealed envelope is then kept alongside other critical documents relating to the election for safekeeping and potential future audit. Subsequent to these physical security measures, the Presiding Officer will prepare Part-One of the Presiding Officer's Report, which includes the Mock Poll Certificate. At this stage, the Control Unit and the VVPAT unit are formally sealed to prevent any further access before the actual poll starts. The Presiding Officers are mandated to list the names of all Polling agents and the candidates they represent, including their party affiliations, and must obtain the signatures of these agents on the certificate upon completion of the mock poll. Finally, other polling officers who witnessed the entire mock poll process must also certify in the Part-One report that the mock votes have been successfully erased from the Control Unit and that the mock poll VVPAT slips have been removed from the VVPAT unit, confirming that the machines are reset and ready for the genuine election process to commence.

The meticulous recording of date and time constitutes a foundational element in the integrity of the electoral process, specifically occurring at the conclusion of the Mock Poll conducted at each Polling Station. The Presiding Officer, who serves as the chief administrator responsible for the conduct of voting at the station, is mandated to perform a critical verification procedure involving the Control Unit, which is the central electronic device that records and stores vote data. This officer must carefully examine the digital display on the Control Unit to note the specific date and time shown by the machine's internal clock. Simultaneously, the officer must compare this digital reading against the actual, real-world date and time. Any discrepancy, no matter how minor, between the machine's displayed time and the actual time must be explicitly documented in Part-One of the Presiding Officer's Report, a formal logbook that tracks all significant events and observations during the election day. This step ensures that there is an immutable record of the machine's status before real voting begins. Following this verification, it is absolutely imperative that the Presiding Officer ensures the complete eradication of all data generated during the Mock Poll from the memory of the Control Unit. This is achieved by physically pressing the button labeled CLEAR on the interface of the Control Unit. This action wipes the temporary test votes from the system, ensuring that the machine starts the actual election with a zero count. In parallel, the officer must verify that every single mock poll slip, which are the paper receipts generated by the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail or VVPAT machine during the test, has been physically removed from the VVPAT drop box. The drop box is the secure compartment where these paper records fall after being printed for voter verification. Failure to remove these slips or clear the electronic data would compromise the accuracy of the final results, making this cleaning phase a non-negotiable prerequisite before the commencement of the Actual Poll.

Once the Mock Poll data is cleared and the environment is reset, the process moves to the critical security phase of sealing both the Control Unit and the VVPAT machine to prevent any tampering before the actual voting starts. The sealing protocol follows a rigorous, multi-step sequence designed to create multiple layers of physical security and accountability. The first action requires the Presiding Officer to switch the main Power switch of the Control Unit to the OFF position, effectively shutting down the device to prepare it for physical securing. The next major component involves the application of a specialized Green Paper Seal intended to secure the Result Section of the Control Unit, which is the protected area containing the buttons and mechanisms that could alter vote counts or print results. Modern iterations of this Green Paper Seal have been engineered with self-adhesive stickers located at both ends on the white side of the strip, marked distinctly as side A and side B. Because these seals possess inherent adhesive properties, the older method of using an additional outer paper strip to hold the seal in place is no longer necessary, streamlining the process while maintaining security. Inside the Result Section, specifically on the inner side of the door leading to the inner compartment, there is a dedicated frame constructed to hold this paper seal. Before the seal is inserted into this frame, strict authentication protocols must be followed. The Presiding Officer must sign their full name immediately below the unique serial number printed on the paper seal. Furthermore, to ensure bipartisan or multi-party oversight, the officer must invite any candidates present or their appointed polling agents, who act as representatives for the competing political parties, to also affix their signatures on the seal if they so desire. This collaborative signing process creates a chain of custody witnessed by all stakeholders. The Presiding Officer bears the responsibility of verifying that the signatures provided by the polling agents on the paper seal match exactly with the signatures found on their official letters of appointment, thereby preventing impersonation or unauthorized access.

Continuing with the sealing procedure, if any Green Paper Seal becomes damaged, torn, or compromised during the handling process, it must strictly not be used for securing the machine. Instead, it must be replaced immediately with a fresh, intact seal. However, the damaged seal cannot simply be discarded or thrown away; it retains evidentiary value and must be preserved. The damaged seal must be placed inside an envelope, which is then itself sealed and returned to the Returning Officer, the senior official overseeing the election in that constituency, after the polling concludes. Another vital procedural detail requires the Presiding Officer to instruct all present polling agents to record the specific Serial Number of the paper seal being used in their personal logs. This allows each party representative to independently track the specific security device used on the machine, facilitating future verification. The physical insertion of the Green Paper Seal into the frame on the inner door must be executed with precision. The seal should be positioned such that when the inner door is closed, a specific Green Color Mark located on the white side of the seal aligns directly over the Result button, and a Red Color Mark aligns over the Print button. This alignment ensures that if anyone attempts to press these buttons without breaking the seal, the tampering will be immediately evident. The inner door is then closed in a manner that allows the two loose ends of the paper seal to protrude outwards from the sides of the inner compartment, making them accessible for the next stage of sealing. Once the inner door is secured, it must be further sealed with a device known as a Special Tag. Before applying this tag, the Presiding Officer is required to write the unique serial number of the Control Unit onto the Special Tag and sign the back side of the tag. Just as with the paper seal, candidates and polling agents present are invited to sign below the officer's signature on the back of the tag if they wish to do so. The officer must also read aloud the pre-printed serial number on the Special Tag and request that all present agents note this number for their records. It is paramount that a spoiled or torn Special Tag is never utilized, as its integrity is essential for the security of the unit.

The final stages of securing the Control Unit involve closing the outer door of the Result Section. As this door is shut, care must be taken to ensure that the loose ends of the Green Paper Seal, which were left protruding from the inner compartment, now extend out from both sides of the closed outer door. This configuration means that opening the outer door would necessarily tear or break the Green Paper Seal, providing a clear visual indicator of any breach. The Presiding Officer then secures this outer door using a thread and an address tag, tying them tightly around the door handles or locking mechanism. To maintain transparency, candidates or their polling agents are permitted to affix their own signatures or personal seals onto this address tag alongside the official seal of the Presiding Officer, creating a multi-signature lock on the exterior. Subsequently, the adhesive backing of the Modified Green Paper Seal is activated. The wax paper protecting the adhesive on side A is peeled off, and this side is firmly pasted onto the outer door of the Result Section. Next, the wax paper from side B is removed, and this end is pasted on top of the already adhered side A. This overlapping application is done in a specific way such that the serial number of the seal remains clearly visible on the very top layer. This layering technique ensures that removing the seal would require destroying the serial number or tearing the paper, thus making covert tampering impossible. With the Control Unit fully secured, attention turns to the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail machine. After the entire Mock Poll process is complete and the Control Unit is sealed, the Presiding Officer must once again verify that the drop box of the VVPAT is completely empty of any test slips. Once confirmed empty, the drop box is sealed using a thread and an Address Tag, mirroring the security measures applied to the Control Unit. This comprehensive sealing regimen ensures that from the moment the Mock Poll ends until the actual counting of votes begins, the electronic voting machines and their paper audit trails remain in a pristine, untampered state, verified and witnessed by the election officials and representatives of all participating political entities.

The procedural framework governing the operation of electronic voting machines and voter verifiable paper audit trail systems requires strict adherence to a sequence of authentication, verification, and contingency protocols to ensure the integrity of the electoral process. Before the actual polling commences, a critical security step involves the physical sealing of the drop box within the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail unit. This sealing process is not merely a mechanical action but a legal attestation; both the Presiding Officer, who is the senior official in charge of the polling station, and the designated polling agents representing various political candidates must affix their signatures to the address tag used for this seal. This dual signature requirement serves as a mutual verification that the machine has not been tampered with prior to use. Once this sealing is complete and verified, the machine is deemed ready for the actual election proceedings. The next operational step involves activating the Control Unit, which acts as the central processing brain of the voting system, by switching it to the on position. Following activation, a rigorous monitoring phase known as the mock poll tracking begins. The Returning Officer, a higher-ranking election official responsible for a broader constituency, utilizes a network of Sector Officers and established communication links to monitor polling stations and mobile teams. The objective of this communication is to track the conduct and status of the mock poll, a test run designed to verify that the machines are recording votes correctly. The Returning Officer must ascertain the status of this mock poll within a strict thirty-minute window. If confirmation of a successful mock poll is not received within this timeframe, it is treated as a significant anomaly. Such non-confirmation may indicate procedural negligence by the Presiding Officer or a technical malfunction within the Electronic Voting Machine or the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail unit, necessitating immediate intervention by the Returning Officer to rectify the situation before real voting begins.

Upon the conclusion of the polling day, a detailed scrutiny process is initiated based on the certification reports submitted by Presiding Officers regarding their mock polls. The Returning Officer is mandated to compile a specific list of polling stations where irregularities occurred during the testing phase. This list specifically includes stations where the mock poll was conducted without the presence of required polling agents, or situations where agents representing more than one rival candidate from recognized political parties were absent during the test. These identified polling stations are flagged for special attention during the subsequent scrutiny of election papers to ensure no foul play occurred. Furthermore, independent Observers appointed by the election commission, as well as zonal or sector magistrates who conduct field visits, are instructed to pay heightened attention to these specific stations. The election commission has issued stringent directives stating that any deviation from these compliance instructions will be viewed with extreme seriousness. In instances of non-compliance, the District Election Officer is empowered and required to take suo-moto disciplinary action, meaning action taken on their own initiative without waiting for a formal complaint, against any Presiding Officer who fails to follow the protocol. To ensure uniformity and understanding, Sector Officers bear the responsibility of verifying that all Presiding Officers clearly understand these instructions. Additionally, to aid the Presiding Officers in adhering to these complex steps, a large instructional poster detailing the mock poll procedure must be displayed. This poster must have minimum dimensions of three feet by two feet and is to be handed over to each Presiding Officer during the distribution of the voting machines. It is mandatory that this poster be pasted directly in front of the Presiding Officer's desk inside the polling booth, serving as a constant visual reference guide during the conduct of the mock poll on election day. Sector Officers are also sensitized to physically check that this poster has been correctly pasted inside every polling station. These procedures are grounded in official directives, specifically referenced under document number 51/8/T&A/2019-EMPS dated the fifth of April, 2019.

A crucial aspect of maintaining the chain of custody and security of the voting equipment involves the meticulous accounting of paper seals. The Presiding Officer is required to maintain a precise and accurate record of the green paper seals supplied to the polling station. These seals are specialized security tags used to lock and secure the Control Unit of the voting machine, preventing unauthorized access to its internal components. The account of these seals must distinguish between the total quantity supplied and the specific number actually utilized for sealing the equipment. This record-keeping is not done in an arbitrary manner but must follow a specific format prescribed by law, specifically Item 10 of Part I of Form 17C, which is appended to the Conduct of Elections Rules of 1961. Transparency is further ensured by granting candidates or their polling agents who are present at the station the right to observe and note down the serial numbers of the paper seals. They may record the serial numbers of the seals provided to the officer as well as those actually applied to the machines, creating an independent verification layer that complements the official log maintained by the Presiding Officer.

In the event that technical issues arise during the testing phase, a clear replacement protocol is established to handle errors efficiently while maintaining auditability. All error messages generated by the Balloting Unit, the Control Unit, or the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail system are centralized and displayed exclusively on the screen of the Control Unit. The Presiding Officer must carefully read these diagnostic messages and act according to the specific instructions provided. A fundamental safety rule in this protocol is that the Control Unit must be switched off before attempting any sort of hardware replacement to prevent electrical damage or data corruption. Following every mock poll, regardless of whether issues were encountered, Part I of the Presiding Officer's Report must be prepared to document the proceedings. If any units are found to be non-functional and are replaced before the start of the actual poll, these defective units are strictly prohibited from being left behind at the polling station. Instead, they must be secured and transported away by the Sector Officer. To clearly identify these items, a sticker bearing the label "MOCK POLL REPLACED" must be affixed to the carrying case of each non-functional unit before removal. Specific procedures exist for power failures; if the power pack of the Control Unit malfunctions or indicates a low battery level during the mock poll, the actual poll, or even after the poll has concluded, it must be replaced. This replacement of the Control Unit's power pack must be performed in the direct presence of the polling agents and the Sector Officer to ensure transparency. Once the new power pack is installed, the Battery Section of the Control Unit must be re-sealed using a fresh Address Tag, and the signatures of the witnessing agents and officers must be obtained again. In this specific scenario involving only a battery replacement, Part II of the Presiding Officer's Report is prepared, and there is no requirement to conduct a new mock poll since the core voting logic remains unchanged.

The protocols for replacing units differ slightly depending on whether the issue is detected during the mock poll or the actual voting period. If a unit is discovered to be non-functional before the actual poll begins, only that specific defective unit is replaced. Once the new unit is installed, the mock poll process is completed using the new hardware to verify its functionality. Following this successful test, Part IV of the Presiding Officer's Report is prepared and submitted to the Sector Officer. However, if a Balloting Unit or Control Unit fails during the actual poll when voters are already casting ballots, the response is more comprehensive to ensure total system integrity. In such cases, the entire set comprising the Balloting Unit, the Control Unit, and the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail machine must be changed out as a complete package. After installing this new set, a fresh mock poll must be conducted immediately. This emergency mock poll involves casting one test vote for every candidate listed on the ballot, including the option for None of the Above, to confirm the new set is recording accurately. Upon completion, both Part I and Part V of the Presiding Officer's Report are prepared, with Part V specifically forwarded to the Sector Officer. There are also streamlined procedures for minor power issues during the actual poll; if the Control Unit displays a "Low Battery VVPAT" warning, only the power pack of the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail unit needs to be replaced. In this instance, no mock poll is required, and no separate report needs to be generated. Similarly, if the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail unit itself malfunctions while the rest of the system works, only that specific unit is replaced without the need for a full mock poll. For this specific replacement, Part V of the Presiding Officer's Report is prepared and handed over to the Sector Officer to document the change.

In the event that the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail, commonly known as VVPAT, fails to print a paper slip or if the printed slip remains attached to the roll without being cut, specific procedural protocols must be strictly followed to maintain the integrity of the electoral process. When encountering a case where the paper slip is uncut, election officials are expressly forbidden from attempting to manually remove or cut the hanging slip from the paper roll, and no effort whatsoever should be made to force it into the drop box designated for collected ballots. The slip must be allowed to remain in its hanging position, as this physical state serves as a critical indicator that the vote has not been successfully recorded in the electronic Control Unit, which is the primary device that captures and stores the voter's choice electronically. Because the vote was not registered in the system, this particular slip is not to be counted during the final tallying of printed paper slips. To ensure full accountability and transparency, the details of such an occurrence must be clearly and meticulously recorded in the official diary maintained by the Presiding Officer, who is the senior official responsible for managing the polling station. This record must follow a prescribed format that includes the specific date and time when the malfunction occurred, the full name of the affected voter along with their serial number as listed in the relevant section of the electoral roll, and a clear statement indicating whether the voter was permitted to cast their vote after the VVPAT machine was replaced or if they chose to leave without voting. Furthermore, the entry must document the total number of votes that had been successfully cast prior to this incident and confirm that the last voter was only allowed to proceed after the replacement of the faulty VVPAT unit, thereby ensuring a complete audit trail of the disruption.

Before the actual voting process begins, the Presiding Officer is required to conduct a comprehensive demonstration for all polling agents present at the station to establish trust and verify the readiness of the equipment and documents. This demonstration involves presenting the marked copy of the electoral roll that will be utilized within that specific polling station, which is the official list of eligible voters for the area. The officer must also display the list of Classified Services Voters, often referred to as CSVs, and identify any proxies who have been appointed to vote on their behalf, ensuring that all special category voters are accounted for according to regulations. Additionally, the Register of Voters, officially designated as Form 17A, must be shown to the agents to prove that the register is completely blank and that no entries have been made regarding any elector prior to the start of the poll. Before the very first voter signs this Form 17A, the Polling Officer number one must collaborate with the Presiding Officer to physically check the electronic Control Unit and record in permanent ink within Form 17A the statement that the total count in the Control Unit has been checked and found to be zero, confirming that the machine is fresh and contains no residual data. Following these technical checks, the Presiding Officer must read aloud the formal declaration prescribed by the Election Commission under Section 128 of the Representation of the People Act of 1951, which sets the legal framework for conducting elections in the country.

To further ensure that the Presiding Officer has duly carried out all foregoing instructions regarding the demonstration of the marked electoral roll, the verification of the Register of Voters, and the obtaining of signatures from candidates or polling agents on the green paper seal used to secure the machines, a formal declaration must be recited before the commencement of the poll. These steps act as necessary safeguards to guarantee a free and fair election by preventing tampering and ensuring all parties witness the initial state of the election materials. The Presiding Officer is mandated to read this declaration aloud so that it is heard by every person present in the polling station, creating a public record of the proceedings, and must then sign the declaration document, which is typically found in Annexure-51 Part-I of the election manual. The officer must also solicit the signatures of any polling agents who are present and willing to affix their signature to the document as witnesses to the procedure. In the event that any polling agent declines to sign the declaration, the Presiding Officer is required to explicitly record the name of that specific agent within the declaration itself, noting their refusal while proceeding with the election, thus maintaining a record of all attendees and their level of cooperation with the procedural formalities.

Prior to the start of voting, the Presiding Officer must also take the opportunity to explain to everyone present the legal provisions contained in Section 128 of the Representation of the People Act of 1951, which specifically addresses the duty of all individuals to maintain the absolute secrecy of the vote. This explanation serves as a formal warning regarding the severe penalties imposed for any breach of voting secrecy, reinforcing the principle that how an individual votes must remain confidential and that disclosing this information or attempting to discover how others voted is a punishable offense. Once these warnings and formalities are complete, the Presiding Officer must commence the poll exactly at the stroke of the hour fixed for the purpose, ensuring that all preparatory activities are concluded before this designated start time. If, due to any unforeseen circumstances or compelling reasons, the officer finds themselves unable to begin the poll at the appointed hour, they possess no authority to extend the overall polling hours; the poll must still close at the originally scheduled closing time. However, a crucial exception exists for voters who are already present within the polling station premises at the moment the closing time arrives; all such electors must be allowed to cast their votes, even if this necessitates continuing the polling process for some additional time beyond the official closing hour to accommodate those who have already queued.

Regarding the security and access control of the polling environment, strict rules dictate exactly which persons shall be admitted inside the polling station by the Presiding Officer. Access is generally restricted to the electors themselves, who are the registered voters entitled to participate, and the Polling Officers who are deployed to manage the station. Furthermore, each contesting candidate is permitted entry, along with their designated election agent and one polling agent representing each candidate at any given time, ensuring that all political interests have observation rights without overcrowding the facility. Entry is also granted to persons specifically authorized by the Election Commission, public servants who are on official duty related to the election, and a child in arms who may be accompanying an elector, recognizing the practical needs of parents. Special provision is made for a person accompanying a blind or infirm voter who cannot move or vote without assistance, ensuring that disabilities do not disenfranchise voters. Finally, the Presiding Officer retains the discretion to admit such other persons as may be necessary from time to time for the specific purpose of identifying voters or otherwise assisting in the conduct of the poll. In cases where identity verification is deemed necessary, the Presiding Officer has the authority to demand the production of photo identity cards from contesting candidates bearing their photographs to confirm their identity before granting them access to the sensitive areas of the polling station.

In the rigorous framework governing electoral procedures, specific protocols are established to verify the identity and authorization of individuals acting as election agents for various candidates. These agents are mandated to present valid identification documents at the polling station to prove their legitimacy. Specifically, they must produce an attested duplicate copy of their official appointment letter. This document serves as the primary proof of their role, and the attestation process, which validates the authenticity of the copy, is carried out exclusively by the Returning Officer. The Returning Officer is the senior official responsible for the conduct of elections in a particular constituency, and their signature on the duplicate copy confirms that the agent has been formally recognized by the election authorities. This step is crucial to prevent impersonation and ensure that only authorized representatives are present within the sensitive environment of the polling station.

A critical distinction in election law concerns the definition of public servants who are considered to be on duty during the electoral process. It is explicitly clarified that the expression public servants on duty does not encompass high-ranking government officials such as Ministers, State Ministers, and Deputy Ministers at both the central and state levels. Despite their status as public servants, these officials are subject to strict restrictions regarding their presence at polling stations. According to the binding instructions issued by the Election Commission, these ministers cannot be appointed as election agents or polling agents under any circumstances. The rationale behind this prohibition is rooted in security protocols. Ministers and similar high-profile figures are typically entitled to security cover, meaning they are accompanied by armed security guards. Since security personnel are generally prohibited from entering the interior of a polling station to maintain the neutrality and sanctity of the voting process, allowing a minister to enter would create an unavoidable conflict. If the minister enters, their security detail would either have to remain outside, violating security protocols, or attempt to enter, which violates election rules. Therefore, to avoid this logistical and security impasse, such officials are barred from serving in any agency capacity within the polling station.

This restriction extends beyond just ministers to include any individual who is provided with security cover. No person enjoying such protection can be appointed as an Election Agent, Polling Agent, or Counting Agent. The logic remains consistent: the presence of armed or uniformed security personnel inside a polling station is incompatible with the requirement for a peaceful and intimidation-free voting environment. Consequently, anyone whose safety requires a visible security detail is ineligible to perform duties that would necessitate their presence inside the voting area. Furthermore, the definition of public servants on duty normally excludes police officers as well. Whether these officers are dressed in their official uniforms or are in plain clothes, they are generally prohibited from entering a polling station. This rule is designed to prevent any perception of coercion or influence over the voters. However, this prohibition is not absolute. The Presiding Officer, who is the head official in charge of a specific polling station, retains the discretionary power to allow police officers inside if unavoidable circumstances arise. Such circumstances are strictly limited to situations requiring the maintenance of law and order or other similar urgent purposes where the safety of the election process is compromised. Without such a direct invitation from the Presiding Officer, police presence inside the voting booth is forbidden.

The regulations regarding security personnel are equally stringent. No security personnel accompanying a candidate, an election agent, or even a regular voter or elector are permitted to cross the threshold into the polling station. This universal ban ensures that the act of voting remains free from external pressure or the intimidating presence of armed guards. There is, however, a singular and highly specific exception to this rule. Security personnel assigned to protect individuals categorized as Z plus protectees, which denotes the highest level of security threat assessment in the country, are allowed to enter a polling station. Even in this exceptional case, strict conditions apply. These security personnel must be dressed in plain clothes to blend in and avoid drawing attention, and any firearms they carry must be kept in a concealed manner. This ensures that while the high-value protectee receives necessary safety coverage, the visual atmosphere of the polling station remains calm and devoid of overt displays of force that could disturb the voters.

To maintain transparency and accountability regarding the movement of authorized officials and observers within the polling station, the Election Commission has instituted a mandatory tracking mechanism. A formal directive requires that a visitor sheet be maintained to record the entry and exit of specific categories of individuals. This includes high-level officials such as the Commission's observers, Sector Magistrates, Zonal Magistrates, and micro-observers deployed by the Commission to monitor the fairness of the election. It also applies to media personnel who possess a valid Media Pass issued by the Election Commission of India, as well as candidates, their election agents, the Returning Officer, Assistant Returning Officers, District Election Officers, and any other authorized persons. Every time any of these individuals visits the polling station, they are required to sign this visitor sheet. This creates an auditable trail of all official visits, ensuring that the presence of outside officials is documented and that their activities can be reviewed if necessary.

Once the polling process concludes for the day, the completed visitor sheet becomes a vital part of the election records. The Presiding Officer is responsible for placing this sheet inside a sealed cover. This sealing process ensures that the data cannot be tampered with after the fact. The sealed envelope containing the visitor sheet is then handed over at the designated Receiving Centre along with other election materials. This procedure integrates the monitoring logs into the secure chain of custody for election documents. The visitor sheet itself is a structured document designed to capture comprehensive details about each visit. It records the day of the poll and identifies the specific electoral unit by listing the name and number of the Assembly constituency. It further specifies the name and unique number of the polling station being visited. To provide context on the scale of the operation, the total number of electors registered at that station is also noted.

The core of the visitor sheet consists of a detailed log where each entry captures the serial number of the visit, the full name, and the official designation of the visiting officer. The designations recorded can vary widely, including Observers, District Election Officers, Returning Officers, Electoral Registration Officers, Sector Magistrates, Zonal Magistrates, or Patrolling Magistrates. For each visit, the exact time of arrival is logged to establish a timeline of oversight. The visiting officer is also required to provide a brief description of the polling process as observed at that moment, noting whether the proceedings were peaceful or if any incidents occurred. Quantitative data is also captured; the officer must record the number of votes that have been polled up to the time of their visit and calculate the percentage of the total electorate that has voted by that point. Finally, there is a section for any additional remarks the officer wishes to make, followed by the mandatory signature of the visiting officer, which validates the accuracy of the reported information. This systematic documentation serves as a real-time snapshot of the election's progress and integrity, providing the Election Commission with granular data on how the poll is unfolding across different locations and times.

The formal administration of an election concludes with the signature of the Presiding Officer accompanied by the current date, which serves as the official certification that all procedures were conducted according to the law. Regarding the regulation of voter entry into polling stations, strict organizational measures are implemented to ensure order and fairness throughout the voting process. There must be distinct and separate queues established for male voters and female voters to facilitate smooth movement and respect cultural norms regarding gender segregation in public gatherings. The officials responsible for managing these queues, often referred to as queue regulators or polling personnel, are tasked with controlling the flow of people entering the building. These regulators will permit only three or four voters to enter the polling station at any given time, adhering strictly to the specific directions issued by the Presiding Officer who holds ultimate authority over the station's operations. All other voters who are waiting to cast their ballots must remain outside the building, standing in an orderly line until it is their turn to proceed inward. Special consideration and priority access are granted to certain vulnerable groups to ensure inclusivity; specifically, infirm voters who may have difficulty standing for long periods and women voters who are carrying babies in their arms are allowed to move ahead of others in the queue. Furthermore, to maintain a balanced flow between the gender-separated lines, the regulations stipulate a ratio where one male elector is admitted for every two female electors, ensuring that neither queue becomes disproportionately delayed.

Security and neutrality within the polling environment are paramount, leading to specific restrictions on attire and electronic devices. On the day of the poll, individuals are prohibited from wearing any items such as caps, shawls, scarves, or other garments that display the name, symbol, or slogan of any political party. This rule is designed to prevent any form of campaigning or influence peddling inside the sacred space where citizens exercise their franchise. However, the regulations clarify that plain headwear, such as a simple cap without any political markings, symbols, or slogans, is not prohibited and may be worn comfortably. In terms of communication technology, specific exceptions have been made by the Election Commission for officials performing critical duties. The Presiding Officers are explicitly permitted to carry their mobile phones inside the polling station, provided that the devices are switched to silent mode to avoid disrupting the proceedings. Similarly, Micro Observers, who are independent monitors deployed to various polling stations to ensure transparency and integrity, are also authorized to carry mobile phones on the condition that they remain on silent mode. To facilitate effective coordination during emergencies, the contact number of the central control room must be saved in the mobile phones of these Micro Observers. This protocol ensures that if a situation arises requiring immediate attention, the observer can step outside the polling station to make the necessary call, thereby ensuring that their conversation does not interfere with the quiet and orderly conduct of voting inside.

Provisions are also made for media coverage while strictly safeguarding the secrecy of the ballot. Photographers and press representatives are generally allowed to take pictures of voters as they line up outside the polling station, provided that peace and public order are maintained and no disruption occurs. However, the extent of this access, including the number of journalists allowed and the manner in which they operate, is not automatic but is determined by the Returning Officer based on the specific local situation, crowd size, and logistical circumstances of that particular constituency. A strict boundary is drawn at the entrance of the voting area; under no circumstances is anyone, including publicity officials from the State Government, permitted to enter the interior of a polling station unless they possess a specific letter of authority issued directly by the Election Commission. The most critical restriction concerns the act of voting itself; it is absolutely forbidden to take any photograph of a voter while they are recording their vote inside the voting compartment. This rule protects the constitutional right to a secret ballot. The consequences for failing to enforce these rules are severe; if these instructions are violated, the most stringent disciplinary action will be taken against the Presiding Officer responsible for the station, emphasizing the gravity of maintaining electoral integrity.

The identification of electors is a rigorous process designed to prevent fraud while ensuring eligible citizens can vote. Every elector is required to produce their Electoral Photo Identity Card, commonly known as EPIC, or an alternative photo identity document that has been officially approved to establish their identity beyond doubt. For those electors who have not yet been issued an identity card, or for those who are unable to produce their card due to loss, damage, or other valid reasons, the regulations provide a safeguard. These individuals must present one of the alternative documents of identification that are specifically permitted by the Election Commission. The Commission issues formal orders detailing which alternative documents are acceptable at the time of every election cycle, and these lists can vary. It is the mandatory duty of the Returning Officer to refer to the latest order issued by the Commission and to bring this information to the notice of all Presiding Officers under their supervision. Furthermore, the Returning Officer must provide a copy of this order during the training sessions for polling staff and must also inform all contesting candidates and political parties about the accepted forms of ID. To ensure widespread awareness, wide publicity campaigns utilizing both print media like newspapers and electronic media like television and radio should be conducted to inform the electorate about these requirements.

The actual verification process at the polling station involves a detailed examination by the polling staff. For the compulsory identification of electors through the EPIC or alternative documents, the polling officer who is specifically in charge of the identification desk must satisfy themselves regarding the true identity of the elector. This involves carefully examining the presented EPIC or alternative document and comparing the photograph and details with the person standing before them. In cases where the officer harbors any doubt regarding the identity of the individual, the elector must be directed to present themselves directly before the Presiding Officer. The Presiding Officer is then obligated to conduct a further and more thorough enquiry to satisfy themselves about the identity of the elector before allowing them to proceed. If, after this investigation, the person is proved to be an impersonator attempting to vote under a false name, the Presiding Officer must immediately hand over the individual to the police along with a written formal complaint to initiate legal proceedings. As an additional measure to prevent bogus voting and clean up the electoral rolls, the Commission has directed the District Election Officer, known as the DEO, to prepare specific lists using Booth Level Officers, or BLOs, and other necessary officials. These lists categorize absentee voters, deceased voters, electors who have shifted residences but retain family links in the area, and electors who have shifted without any family linkage.

If the name of an elector appears on any of these special sensitivity lists, their identity must be verified very thoroughly by the Presiding Officer. Once the Presiding Officer is satisfied with the identity of such a flagged voter, a photograph of the elector will be taken by a camera person, if one has been posted at that specific polling station, to create a record of the event. The Presiding Officer is required to maintain a separate account or logbook specifically for these voters to ensure an audit trail exists. Practical challenges may arise where the photo roll, which is the official list of voters containing their photographs used at the polling station, does not contain the photo of a particular elector, or there may be stray cases where the photo on the document does not perfectly match the voter due to aging or other factors. In such instances, the production of the EPIC or alternate documents containing clear photographs becomes crucial, as it helps the Presiding Officer establish the identities of the electors concerned despite the discrepancies in the main roll. It is imperative to note that for the sole purpose of identifying an elector, the Presiding Officer and their entire team of Polling Officers are authorized to rely only on the EPIC and the specific alternative documents prescribed by the Commission. No other document, regardless of its official nature, can be accepted for the purpose of voter identification, ensuring a uniform and secure standard across all polling stations.

Although Sub-rule one of Rule 49H technically grants a Presiding Officer the broad authority to employ any persons they deem fit at a polling station to assist in the identification of electors or to provide general support during the voting process, this discretionary power is significantly narrowed by specific directives issued by the Election Commission regarding the use of the Electors Photo Identity Card, commonly known as EPIC. Consequently, the Presiding Officer should only resort to employing additional identifying personnel under this rule in very specific circumstances, primarily when pardanashin women are present. Pardanashin women refer to female voters who observe strict purdah or seclusion traditions and may not be comfortable interacting with male officials or revealing their faces to men outside their family. In such cases, the services of a local lady are required to respectfully and effectively identify these electors without violating cultural norms. Furthermore, under Sub-rule two of Rule 49-G, the Returning Officer or the Presiding Officer holds the explicit power to appoint a woman to serve as a dedicated attendant at any polling station. The role of this woman attendant is twofold: first, to provide assistance specifically to women electors throughout the voting process, and second, to aid the Presiding Officer generally in matters concerning female voters. This attendant also plays a critical security role by assisting the Presiding Officer in frisking or conducting a physical search of any woman elector if such a measure becomes necessary for maintaining order and preventing malpractice. Regarding other personnel, such as a village chowkidar, which is a local watchman or guard, or a Revenue Officer who might be employed by the Presiding Officer to act as an identifying officer, strict positioning protocols apply. These individuals should normally be stationed outside the entrance of the polling station to manage the flow of people and verify identities before entry. They are permitted to enter the interior of the polling station only when their specific skills are required to identify a particular elector or to assist the Presiding Officer with a specific task directly related to the conduct of the poll. Additionally, special provisions exist for overseas electors, meaning citizens living abroad who have returned to vote. The identification of these overseas electors at the time they cast their votes at the relevant polling station must be conducted exclusively on the basis of their original passports produced by them, ensuring a high standard of identity verification for those not residing permanently within the constituency.

The sequence of events that unfolds inside a polling station once an elector enters is a highly structured and sequential process designed to ensure accuracy, transparency, and the integrity of the election. When an elector first steps into the polling station, their initial point of contact is the First Polling Officer. This official has the primary responsibility of verifying the voter's eligibility. The First Polling Officer will first check whether the name of the person standing before them appears in the electoral roll, which is the official list of registered voters being used for that specific poll. Following this, the officer must inspect the elector's EPIC or any other identity document that has been prescribed by the Election Commission for use at the time of voting. It is crucial to note again that for overseas voters, this verification step relies solely on checking the particulars contained in their original passports. Once the documents are inspected, the First Polling Officer is required to read aloud the name and the serial number of the elector. This public announcement serves a vital function, allowing polling agents, who represent various political candidates, to note down these details in their own copies of the electoral roll for cross-verification and record-keeping. After the elector's identity has been successfully established, the First Polling Officer will put a cross mark in the box containing the name and particulars of that elector within the electoral roll to indicate that they have presented themselves. In the specific case of a woman elector, the serial number is not just crossed but is circled. This distinct marking is implemented to facilitate an accurate count of the number of women voters who have participated in the election, providing valuable demographic data post-poll.

After clearing the first checkpoint, the elector proceeds to the Second Polling Officer, whose duties focus on documentation and the application of indelible ink. The Second Polling Officer will note down the serial number from the electoral roll of that specific elector, matching the number that was just read aloud by the First Polling Officer. The next critical step involves the application of indelible ink on the voter's left-hand forefinger. This ink is applied using a brush in the form of a line that extends from the top end of the fingernail down to the bottom of the first joint of the left forefinger. This mark serves as a permanent, visible indicator that the individual has already voted, thereby preventing double voting. Strict rules govern this procedure; if any elector refuses to allow their left forefinger to be inspected or marked with the indelible ink, or if they already possess such a mark indicating they have voted previously, or if they perform any act with the intention of removing or obscuring the ink mark, they shall immediately be disqualified and not allowed to vote. Once the ink has been duly and correctly applied, the Second Polling Officer must obtain the elector's signature or thumb impression in the relevant column of the Register of Voters, which is officially designated as Form 17A. In column three of this Form 17A, the last four digits of the identification document presented by the voter must be recorded. However, there is a simplification for those voting on the basis of the EPIC; in such cases, it is sufficient to mention the letters EP, which denote EPIC, in the relevant column, and it is not necessary to write down the full alphanumeric number of the EPIC card. Conversely, for those electors who vote based on any alternative documents other than the EPIC, the last four digits of that specific document must be written down, and the type of document produced must also be explicitly mentioned in the register. Finally, the signature or thumb impression of the voter is obtained by the Second Polling Officer in column four of that same register, and the officer prepares a voter's slip containing all required particulars as outlined in the relevant procedural paragraphs.

Thereafter, the elector moves to the third stage, encountering either the Third Polling Officer or the Presiding Officer, who is in charge of the Control Unit of the electronic voting machine. This official performs a final verification before the vote is cast. They will first recheck that the indelible ink mark is clear and visible on the finger of the elector. If it is found that the mark is not clear, has faded, or appears to have been wiped off, the officer is mandated to apply the ink once again to ensure the record of voting is secure. Following this verification, the officer takes the voter's slip prepared by the previous official and allows the voter to proceed to the voting compartment where they will cast their vote. To enable this, the officer presses the Ballot button on the Control Unit, which activates the voting machine for a single vote. It is a fundamental principle of the polling process that the voter shall be allowed to vote exactly in the same sequence in which their details were recorded in the Register of Voters, Form 17A, ensuring a strict chronological order that matches the physical register with the electronic count. Finally, the voter enters the privacy of the voting compartment to record their vote by pressing the button corresponding to their chosen candidate or symbol, completing the democratic process. This entire sequence, from the initial identity check to the final casting of the ballot, is designed to be seamless yet rigorous, balancing the need for accessibility with the imperative of electoral integrity.

The voting process incorporates a critical verification mechanism known as the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail, or VVPAT, which serves as a physical record of the electronic vote cast. When an elector presses the button corresponding to their chosen candidate on the electronic voting machine, the system triggers the VVPAT unit to print a paper slip. This slip contains specific identifying information, including the serial number assigned to the candidate, the full name of the candidate, and the unique election symbol associated with that candidate. The design of the VVPAT unit includes a transparent window positioned in such a way that the voter can clearly view this printed slip while it is still held in place, before it is automatically cut and dropped into a sealed drop box within the machine. This visual confirmation allows the voter to verify that their intent has been accurately recorded by the machine. Once the voter has observed the slip through the transparent window and confirmed its accuracy, they are required to immediately exit the voting compartment to maintain the flow of the election and ensure secrecy for subsequent voters. Before the voter fully leaves the polling station area, a member of the polling staff stationed there may perform a final check to ensure that the voter's finger has been marked with indelible ink, which is a primary method for preventing multiple voting attempts by the same individual.

The reliability of the electronic voting equipment depends heavily on the power supply provided by the Control Unit, which operates using a removable Power Pack or battery. In the event that this Power Pack malfunctions, fails to operate correctly, or displays an indication of low battery charge, the Presiding Officer, who is the senior official responsible for managing the polling station, must take immediate and specific corrective actions. The first step involves notifying the Sector Officer, a supervisory official overseeing multiple polling stations, to arrange for the replacement of the faulty Power Pack. To facilitate rapid resolution of such issues, additional spare Power Packs are pre-distributed to Sector Officers specifically for this purpose. The actual replacement of the battery must be conducted transparently; the Presiding Officer is mandated to swap the Power Pack in the direct presence of the Polling Agents, who represent the various political candidates, and the Sector Officer. This witnessing requirement ensures that no tampering occurs during the maintenance procedure. Immediately after replacing the battery, the Presiding Officer must reseal the battery compartment of the Control Unit using a secure thread and an Address Tag. To further validate the integrity of this action, the Presiding Officer must obtain the signatures of the polling agents on the tag itself. Finally, the Presiding Officer is required to document the entire incident and the actions taken by submitting a formal report using the specific format prescribed in the latest instructions issued by the Election Commission.

A distinct procedural avenue exists for addressing doubts regarding the identity of individuals attempting to vote, known as challenged votes. Polling agents, who act as observers for political parties, possess the authority to challenge the identity of any person claiming to be a specific registered elector if they suspect impersonation or fraud. To initiate such a challenge, the polling agent must deposit a cash sum of two rupees with the Presiding Officer for each individual case they wish to contest. This financial deposit acts as a deterrent against frivolous or malicious challenges. Upon receiving the deposit and the challenge, the Presiding Officer is obligated to conduct a summary inquiry, which is a quick but formal investigation into the validity of the claimant's identity. If, after reviewing the evidence and questioning the individual, the Presiding Officer determines that the challenge has not been substantiated or proven, the officer must allow the challenged person to cast their vote without further hindrance. Conversely, if the Presiding Officer concludes that the challenge is valid and that the person is indeed an imposter, the officer must debar that individual from voting. In such cases of established fraud, the Presiding Officer must hand over the offender to the police authorities along with a written complaint detailing the offense. It is imperative that all such complaints filed with the police are pursued vigorously by the authorities, leading to the launch of prosecutions against the offenders to ensure they are convicted and receive suitable punishment under the law. The Presiding Officer is also required to maintain a comprehensive record of all such incidents in a specific document known as Form 14, which is appended to the Conduct of Elections Rules of 1961.

The application of indelible ink is a fundamental safeguard enshrined in Rule 49K of the election regulations, designed to prevent impersonation and ensure that no elector votes more than once in the same election. Every elector whose identity has been satisfactorily verified by the Presiding Officer or the concerned Polling Officer must be marked with this special ink, which is resistant to washing and remains visible for several days. The standard procedure dictates that the ink be applied to the left forefinger of the voter. However, the rules provide a clear hierarchy of alternatives to accommodate voters with physical differences. If an elector does not have a left forefinger, the ink should be applied to any other available finger on their left hand, starting with the index position and moving sequentially. If the voter possesses no fingers at all on their left hand, the ink must then be applied to the right forefinger. Similarly, if the right forefinger is missing, the ink is applied to the next available finger on the right hand. In the rare circumstance where an elector has no fingers on either hand, the indelible ink is to be applied to the extremity or stump of whichever hand, left or right, that the person possesses. This meticulous attention to detail ensures that every eligible voter can be marked and identified, regardless of physical disability, thereby maintaining the integrity of the one-person-one-vote principle.

Administrative record-keeping at the polling station is governed by Rule 49L of the Conduct of Elections Rules, 1961, which mandates the maintenance of a Register of Voters at every polling location. This register, prescribed in the format of Form 17A, serves a function analogous to the counterfoil of a traditional paper ballot, acting as a verifiable trail of who has participated in the election. The Presiding Officer is responsible for entering the electoral roll serial number of each elector into this register, matching the number found on the marked copy of the electoral roll used at the station. Crucially, the officer must also obtain the signature or thumb impression of the elector directly in this register immediately after they have voted. This dual record of the serial number and the biometric or written signature creates a robust audit trail. The physical specifications for this Register of Voters are strictly defined to ensure durability and uniformity; it must be produced in book form and printed on high-quality A4 size paper. The layout of Form 17A includes columns for the serial number, details of identification documents produced by the elector, and a dedicated space for the signature or thumb impression, along with a column for any necessary remarks. The form also requires the signature of the Presiding Officer to authenticate the entries. To maximize utility and conserve resources, both sides of each page in the register book are utilized for recording entries. These registers are prepared and bound in advance in two standard sizes to accommodate varying voter turnout: one version containing twenty-five sheets and another containing fifty sheets. This systematic approach to documentation ensures that there is a permanent, physical record of every vote cast, linking the voter's identity to their participation in the democratic process while adhering to the legal frameworks established for the conduct of free and fair elections.

The electoral registration system relies on specific physical documents known as Registers, which are designed to maintain an accurate and verifiable record of voter participation. These Registers are manufactured in two primary configurations to accommodate varying sizes of polling stations. The first configuration consists of a book containing twenty-five individual sheets, which provides sufficient space to record the electoral roll numbers and obtain the signatures or thumb impressions of up to five hundred electors. The second, larger configuration contains fifty sheets and offers similar recording provisions for a capacity of one thousand electors. To ensure the integrity and sequential tracking of every page within these books, each page must be machine numbered during the printing process. This mechanical numbering prevents the insertion or removal of pages and ensures a continuous, unbroken chain of custody for the records. Depending on the total number of voters assigned to a specific polling station, each polling party will be supplied with one or more of these Registers to ensure that there is adequate space for all potential voters without running out of recording room during the election day proceedings. It is the critical responsibility of the Returning Officer, who acts as the chief administrator for the election in a particular constituency, to arrange for these Registers to be printed in sufficient quantities well in advance of the election date to avoid any logistical shortages.

The procedure for recording voter attendance is strict and follows a defined protocol to prevent fraud. The signature or thumb impression of every single elector must be obtained in the fourth column of the Register of Voters. This entry must be made directly against the specific row where that voter's details have already been listed, and this task is specifically assigned to the Second Polling Officer. If an elector refuses to provide either their signature or their thumb impression on the Register of Voters, they shall not be permitted to cast their vote under any circumstances. In such instances where a voter refuses to authenticate their presence, an explicit entry must be made in the Remarks column of the Register stating clearly that the individual refused to sign. Furthermore, the third column of the Register serves as a documentation log for identity verification. In this column, particulars of the identity documents produced by the voter must be mentioned. For electors who vote based on the EPIC, which stands for Electors Photo Identity Card, the process is streamlined; it is sufficient to write the letters EP in the relevant column to denote the use of an EPIC, and it is not necessary to write down the full alphanumeric number of the card. However, for those voters who choose to vote based on any alternative acceptable documents, the existing instructions remain in force, requiring the recording of the last four digits of the document number. Additionally, the specific type of alternative document produced must also be explicitly mentioned in the register to maintain a clear audit trail. Election Observers, Zonal Magistrates, and Sector Magistrates, during their field visits to monitor the election process, are instructed to give special attention to this aspect of documentation to ensure compliance.

To clarify the legal and procedural understanding of authentication, a signature is formally described as the act of a person writing their name on a document with the specific intention of authenticating that document and confirming their identity. A literate person, meaning someone who can read and write, while signing on the Register of Voters, will typically sign using their full name or their recognized initials. In cases where a literate person refuses to sign their name and instead attempts to put a simple mark as their signature, the polling officials are required to take their thumb impression instead to ensure a biometric record is captured. If such a person further refuses to give their thumb impression, they shall not be allowed to vote, as the law requires a definitive method of identification. Regarding the meaning and application of a thumb impression, the procedures conform strictly with Rule 49K subsection 4 of the Conduct of Elections Rules established in 1961. If the left thumb of the voter is missing due to injury or congenital reasons, then the impression of the right thumb should be taken. If both thumbs are missing, the impression of one of the fingers of the left hand, starting sequentially from the forefinger, should be taken. If there are no fingers available on the left hand, the impression of one of the fingers of the right hand, again starting from the forefinger, should be recorded. In the extreme scenario where no fingers are available at all, the voter, being physically unable to record their vote independently, will necessarily have to seek the assistance of a companion under Rule 49N of the said rules. In that specific case, the signature or thumb impression of the accompanying person must be taken on both the Register of Voters and in Form 14A to document the assistance provided.

It has been observed in a number of cases that there is a tendency among some polling staff to obtain thumb impressions from literate voters simply with a view to enhance the speed of voting and reduce the time taken per person. This practice is fundamentally unacceptable and must be actively discouraged, as it undermines the preference of literate voters to sign their names and may lead to disputes regarding the authenticity of the vote. The Returning Officer, along with Observers and Sector Magistrates, must rigorously check for this malpractice during their visits to polling stations and take corrective action if such shortcuts are detected. The issuance of voters slips is another critical component of the workflow designed to maintain order. The record of every elector who comes to vote at a polling station and is subsequently allowed to vote shall be meticulously maintained in the aforementioned Register of Voters. The first column in that Register will show the precise order in which the electors have been identified and allowed to vote after successfully putting their signatures or thumb impressions on the register. It is absolutely essential that these voters record their votes in the electronic voting machine exactly in the same sequence in which they have been entered in the Register of Voters. This synchronization between the physical register and the electronic vote count is vital for reconciliation and auditing purposes. In order to ensure this strict sequence is maintained, each elector, immediately after they have signed or put their thumb impression on the Register of Voters, should be given a voter's slip by the Polling Officer who is in charge of maintaining the Register. After the electoral roll number of an elector has been entered in the Register and their signature or thumb impression has been obtained, the Second Polling Officer should prepare the voter's slip for that specific elector. This voter's slip is a small piece of paper containing specific particulars that serve as a token authorizing the voter to proceed to the voting compartment, thereby linking their physical presence in the register to their actual casting of the ballot.

The electoral process relies on a strict sequence of actions to ensure the integrity and accuracy of every vote cast, beginning when an eligible elector approaches the Presiding Officer or the specific Polling Officer who is responsible for managing the Control Unit of the electronic voting machine. This officer acts as the gatekeeper of the voting procedure and will only permit the individual to proceed with casting a ballot upon the presentation and verification of a valid voter's slip, which serves as the official credential confirming the person's registration and eligibility at that specific polling station. It is fundamentally critical that voters record their votes in the voting machine following the exact numerical order in which their names appear in the Registers of Voters, a master document that lists all eligible participants in a predetermined sequence. To maintain this chronological integrity, the Presiding Officer or the third Polling Officer stationed at the Control Unit must strictly enforce the rule that a voter enters the private voting compartment only according to the serial number explicitly mentioned on their voter's slip. This adherence to serial order prevents confusion, ensures accurate accounting, and maintains the chain of custody for the voting data. However, recognizing that real-world scenarios can be unpredictable, the procedures account for exceptional circumstances or unforeseen and unavoidable reasons that might disrupt this exact serial progression. If it becomes impossible to follow the standard order for any specific elector, the election officials are mandated to make a suitable and precise entry in the remarks column of the Register of Voters. This entry must clearly state the exact serial number at which the individual actually voted, and similar explanatory entries must be recorded for all subsequent voters whose position in the queue was consequently disturbed, thereby creating a transparent audit trail that explains any deviations from the standard sequence.

Once all preliminary procedural requirements have been successfully completed, which include the rigorous identification of the voter, the application of indelible ink on the voter's left forefinger to prevent multiple voting attempts, the obtaining of the voter's signature or thumb impression on the Register of Voters as a physical record of participation, and the final verification of the voter's slip by the authorized officer, the elector is formally directed to enter the voting compartment. This secluded area houses the Ballot Unit, which displays the candidates, and the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail, commonly known as VVPAT, which provides a physical paper record of the electronic vote. To initiate the voting capability for this specific individual, the Presiding Officer or the designated third polling officer must press the 'Ballot' button located on the Control Unit, a device that manages the flow and authorization of votes. The act of pressing this button electronically activates the Ballot Unit inside the compartment, preparing it to accept a single vote. Upon this activation, a visual signaling system engages to indicate the status of the machine; specifically, the 'Busy' lamp on the Control Unit will illuminate in red, signaling that the machine is currently occupied and processing a voter, while simultaneously, the 'Ready' lamp on the Ballot Unit within the voting compartment will begin to glow green, indicating to the voter that the system is active and ready to receive their input. The voter then exercises their democratic right by pressing the blue button situated directly adjacent to the name, photograph, and election symbol of the candidate they wish to support.

The moment the voter presses the button corresponding to their chosen candidate, immediate feedback mechanisms are triggered to confirm the action has been registered. The small lamp located on the Ballot Unit next to the selected candidate's name, photograph, and symbol will instantly light up in red, providing visual confirmation of the selection, while the general green 'Ready' light on the Ballot Unit will extinguish, indicating that the voting window for that specific transaction is closing. Concurrently, an audible beep sound will emit from the Control Unit, serving as an audio cue that the vote has been successfully cast and recorded in the machine's memory. A crucial component of this modern voting system is the transparency provided by the VVPAT mechanism. While casting the vote, the elector is able to look through a transparent window on the VVPAT device to visually verify that a paper slip has been printed displaying the correct serial number, name, and symbol of the candidate for whom they just voted. This paper slip remains visible behind the glass for a few seconds, allowing the voter to confirm the accuracy of the electronic record before the machine automatically cuts the slip and drops it into a secure sealed drop box within the VVPAT unit, creating a permanent physical audit trail. After a brief interval of a few seconds, the system resets itself; the beep sound ceases, the red light next to the candidate's name on the Ballot Unit turns off, and the red 'Busy' lamp on the Control Unit also extinguishes. These combined visual and audio signals serve as definitive indicators that the voter inside the compartment has fully completed the recording of their vote and that the machine is no longer engaged.

Following the completion of the voting act and the resetting of the machine indicators, the elector is required to exit the voting compartment immediately and leave the polling station to make way for the next participant. This entire detailed procedure is designed to be repeated systematically each time a new voter is authorized to record their vote, ensuring consistency and security throughout the polling day. It is imperative that election officials enforce the rule that only one voter is allowed inside the voting compartment at any given time to guarantee the secrecy of the ballot and prevent intimidation or coercion. Furthermore, strict discipline must be maintained regarding the operation of the Control Unit; the 'Ballot' button must never be pressed to authorize a new vote until the previous voter has completely exited the voting compartment, thereby preventing any overlap or confusion in the voting sequence. In situations where a discrepancy arises, specific legal protocols such as Rule 49MA govern the procedure for handling complaints regarding the particulars printed on the VVPAT paper slip. If an elector, after having recorded their vote, alleges that the paper slip generated by the VVPAT displayed the name or symbol of a candidate different from the one they intended to vote for, the Presiding Officer must take immediate and formal action. The officer is required to obtain a written declaration, often referred to as Annexure-55, from the elector detailing the allegation, but only after issuing a stern warning to the elector about the serious legal consequences of making a false declaration, which could include penal charges for misleading election officials.

If the elector persists and provides the required written declaration, the Presiding Officer must then make a second, distinct entry related to that specific elector in Form 17A, which is the official register used to track such incidents and irregularities. Following this documentation, the officer shall permit the elector to record a test vote in the voting machine under strict supervision. This test vote must be conducted in the direct presence of the Presiding Officer and, if available, in the presence of the candidates or their appointed polling agents who are observing the proceedings at the polling station. The purpose of this test is to objectively observe the paper slip generated by the VVPAT during a controlled instance to determine if the machine is malfunctioning or if the initial allegation holds merit. If the investigation reveals that the allegation is true and the machine indeed displayed incorrect information, the Presiding Officer is obligated to report these facts immediately to the Returning Officer, the senior official responsible for the conduct of the election in that constituency. Upon reporting, the Presiding Officer must stop any further recording of votes using that specific machine to prevent additional errors and must await and act strictly according to the further directions provided by the Returning Officer, ensuring that the integrity of the election is preserved and that any technical faults are addressed according to the highest legal standards.

In the event that an allegation regarding a voting irregularity is investigated and subsequently determined to be false, yet the physical paper slip generated by the machine coincides perfectly with the test vote that was recorded by the elector during the verification process, specific procedural steps must be followed by the Presiding Officer to document this outcome accurately. The Presiding Officer is required to make a formal written remark directly against the second entry corresponding to that specific elector in the official document known as Form 17A, which serves as the Register of Voters. This remark must explicitly state that the allegation was found to be baseless while confirming the match between the slip and the test vote, and it must include the serial number and the full name of the candidate for whom the test vote was cast. Following the recording of this remark, the Presiding Officer must obtain either the signature or the thumb impression of the elector in question directly next to these remarks to serve as legal acknowledgment and verification of the event. Furthermore, the officer must ensure that all necessary details regarding this specific test vote are entered into item five located in Part One of another critical document called Form 17C, which is used for accounting and reconciliation purposes. This rigorous documentation ensures transparency and provides an audit trail should any future disputes arise regarding the integrity of the voting machinery or the conduct of the election officials.

Regarding the ongoing monitoring of voter turnout, there exists a protocol for the periodic tallying of the number of votes polled to ensure that the electronic records match the physical admission of voters.
