At any point during the polling hours, if there is a need to ascertain the cumulative total of votes that have been cast up to that precise moment, the Presiding Officer or an authorized polling official must press the button labeled Total on the Control Unit of the Electronic Voting Machine system. Upon activation of this command, the display panel situated on the Control Unit will immediately illuminate to show the aggregate number of votes recorded since the commencement of polling. It is imperative that this action be performed at regular intervals throughout the day rather than waiting until the close of polls. The figure displayed on the machine must be carefully compared and tallied against the number of individuals who have been officially allowed to enter the voting booth as reflected in the Register of Voters. In no circumstance should this verification be neglected; specifically, the Presiding Officer is mandated to ascertain and tally the number of votes polled during every two-hour interval without fail. Once this count is verified, the exact number of votes polled must be recorded in the relevant columns of the Presiding Officer's diary, creating a time-stamped log that allows for the detection of any discrepancies between the number of people who entered the booth and the number of votes actually registered by the machine.

The maintenance of the secrecy of voting is a fundamental principle that every elector permitted to vote must uphold with absolute strictness while within the confines of the polling station. Every individual casting a ballot is legally bound to observe the established voting procedure, which includes entering the voting compartment alone, selecting their candidate in private, and exiting without revealing their choice to others. If an elector refuses to adhere to these procedures even after receiving a clear warning from the Presiding Officer, the officer possesses the authority to deny that individual the right to vote. This denial can be executed directly by the Presiding Officer or by a polling officer acting under the specific direction of the Presiding Officer. In scenarios where an elector has already been issued a voter's slip but is subsequently barred from voting due to a violation of procedure, the slip must be physically retrieved from the individual and officially cancelled to prevent its misuse. When an elector is disqualified from voting for such a violation, the Presiding Officer is required to make a specific remark in the remarks column of the Register of Voters, also known as Form 17A, against the entry pertaining to that elector. This remark must clearly state that the voting procedure was violated. Additionally, the Presiding Officer must affix their full signature below this entry to validate the decision. It is important to note that while the voter is denied the franchise, it is not necessary to alter or renumber the serial number of that disqualified elector, nor is it required to change the serial numbers of any succeeding electors in the first column of the Register of Voters, thereby maintaining the sequential integrity of the voter list.

Special provisions have been established to accommodate blind and infirm voters as well as proxy voters to ensure that physical disabilities do not disenfranchise any citizen. If the Presiding Officer is satisfied that an elector, due to blindness or another significant infirmity, is unable to recognize the symbols on the Ballot Unit or cannot record their vote by pressing the appropriate button without assistance, the officer shall permit that elector, under Rule 49N of the election regulations, to bring a companion into the voting compartment. This companion must be at least eighteen years of age and acts solely to record the vote on behalf of the elector and strictly in accordance with the elector's expressed wishes. However, a distinction is made for infirm voters who retain the cognitive and physical ability to press the button of their chosen candidate on the Ballot Unit of the Electronic Voting Machine independently. Such voters are permitted to have an authorized companion accompany them only up to the entrance of the Voting Compartment within the polling station, but the companion is strictly prohibited from entering the compartment itself. This rule applies specifically to cases where the nature of the physical infirmity requires assistance only for mobility and movement to the booth, but not for the actual act of voting. The determination of whether a voter falls into the category requiring a companion inside the booth or merely outside rests entirely with the discretion of the Presiding Officer based on their assessment of the voter's capabilities.

To further assist visually impaired voters, the Electronic Voting Machines are equipped with accessibility features including Braille signage located on the extreme right side of the Ballot Unit, which indicates the serial number of each candidate in raised dots that can be read by touch. Additionally, depending on the requirements assessed by the Election Commission, dummy Braille ballot sheet papers will be supplied to polling stations. These dummy sheets are not valid ballots but are training aids that display the names of the candidates, their political affiliations, and their corresponding serial numbers in both standard text and Braille. In cases where a visually handicapped voter requests it, the Presiding Officer will hand over this dummy ballot sheet to the voter. This allows the voter to privately note down the serial number of their preferred candidate by feeling the Braille or reading the large print before approaching the machine. Armed with this knowledge, the voter can then cast their vote independently by locating the correct Braille serial number on the machine and pressing the associated button, thus eliminating the need to depend on a companion and preserving the secrecy of their ballot. Once the vote is cast, the dummy ballot paper must be returned to the Presiding Officer so that it may be sanitized and reused by subsequent visually handicapped voters. At the conclusion of the polling process, these dummy ballot papers are treated as official polling materials and must be deposited at the collection center along with all other election equipment. To prevent fraud and ensure that the system of companionship is not abused, strict limits are imposed on who can serve as a companion. No single person is permitted to act as the companion for more than one elector at any given polling station on the same day. This restriction is enforced under the first proviso to sub-rule one of Rule 49N, which explicitly states that one person cannot represent multiple voters. To facilitate the polling staff in ensuring compliance with this provision and to create a permanent record that an individual has already served as a companion, the application of indelible ink, which is typically applied to voters, shall also be applied to the finger of the companion immediately after they have assisted a voter.

The application of indelible ink serves as a critical security measure in the electoral process to prevent multiple voting by the same individual or their associates. In scenarios where an elector requires assistance due to physical incapacity or illiteracy, they are permitted to bring a companion into the voting compartment to help record their vote. Specific protocols govern the marking of such companions to ensure integrity. The indelible ink must be applied strictly on the right index finger of the companion. This is distinct from the standard procedure for the elector themselves, who, even in these assisted cases, continues to have the ink marked on their left index finger in accordance with existing regulations that apply to all voters. Before any companion is allowed to enter the private voting area with the elector, the Presiding Officer, who is the official in charge of the polling station, must conduct a mandatory inspection of the companion's right index finger. The purpose of this check is to verify that the finger is clean and free of any prior indelible ink markings. If the inspection reveals that the companion's right index finger is already marked with ink, it indicates that the person has likely already assisted another voter or voted elsewhere on that same day. Consequently, such an individual is immediately disqualified from acting as a companion under Rule 49N, which is the specific regulation governing assisted voting. This prevents a single person from influencing multiple votes across different booths.

Furthermore, before being authorized to act as a companion, the individual must formally declare their commitment to electoral secrecy and exclusivity. This declaration requires the companion to affirm two key points: first, that they will maintain strict confidentiality regarding how the elector they are assisting chooses to vote, ensuring the sanctity of the secret ballot is preserved even when a third party is involved in the mechanical act of marking the paper; and second, that they have not already served as a companion for any other elector at any other polling station on that specific election day. This dual affirmation is designed to prevent coercion and the trafficking of voting assistance. The Presiding Officer is legally obligated to obtain this declaration from the companion using a specific form prescribed by the Election Commission, which is the apex body responsible for administering elections. Once the voting process involving a companion is concluded, administrative record-keeping becomes essential for transparency. Sub-rule 2 of Rule 49N mandates that the Presiding Officer maintain a dedicated record of all instances where electors cast their votes with the aid of a companion. This record is kept in a document known as Form 14A. It is crucial to understand the scope of this form; it must include every case where a companion physically enters the voting compartment to assist in the actual recording of the vote. However, the regulations make a clear distinction for individuals who may accompany an elector only to provide physical support for movement, such as helping a wheelchair user navigate the polling station, but who do not enter the voting compartment to influence or execute the voting act. These purely logistical assistants are not required to be listed in Form 14A, as they do not pose the same risk to the secrecy or integrity of the ballot marking process. The Presiding Officer must diligently compile this list for all relevant cases.

The importance of Form 14A extends beyond simple record-keeping on election day; it plays a vital role in post-election scrutiny. On the day immediately following the poll, various officials, including Observers appointed to monitor fairness and the Returning Officer who oversees the constituency, conduct a detailed examination of polling station documents. These documents typically include Form 17A, which is the final register of voters who actually cast their ballots, and the daily diaries maintained by Presiding Officers. During this review, Form 14A is specifically analyzed to detect any anomalies. The scrutinizing officers look for polling stations that report an unusually high number of companions assisting voters compared to other stations or historical averages. A statistically significant spike in assisted voting could indicate potential malpractice, such as organized groups bringing in proxies to manipulate votes under the guise of assistance, which would raise serious suspicions about the fairness and freedom of the election in that area. This layer of oversight ensures that the provision for assisted voting is not exploited to undermine the democratic process.

In addition to assisted voting, the electoral framework provides for voting by proxy, particularly for Service Voters, often referred to as CSVs, who include members of the armed forces and government employees posted away from their home constituencies. A proxy is a person appointed by the service voter to cast a vote on their behalf. This proxy must go to the specific polling station to which the service voter is assigned. This assigned polling station is determined based on the home address of the service voter, ensuring that the vote is cast in the correct geographical constituency where the voter holds registration. The proxy exercises the franchise in the exact same manner as any regular elector assigned to that polling station, following all standard procedures for identification and ballot casting. A notable procedural difference exists regarding the application of indelible ink for proxies. While ordinary voters and electors using companions receive the ink mark on their left index finger, the rule specified under Rule 37 dictates that for a proxy, the indelible ink must be applied to the middle finger of their left hand. This distinct marking allows election officials to easily distinguish between a person voting in their own capacity and a person voting as a proxy. Furthermore, the regulations clarify that serving as a proxy does not disenfranchise the individual from voting for themselves. If the proxy is also a registered elector in the same constituency and is normally assigned to a polling station, they retain the full right to cast their own personal vote in addition to the vote they cast on behalf of the service voter. This ensures that fulfilling a civic duty as a proxy does not come at the cost of one's own democratic participation.

The electoral laws also firmly protect the right of an elector to abstain from voting even after they have arrived at the polling station and completed the initial identification formalities. There are situations where an elector, after having their serial number entered into the Register of Voters, commonly known as Form 17A, and after providing their signature or thumb impression on that register as proof of presence, may decide for personal or political reasons not to record a vote. The law explicitly states that no elector shall be forced or compelled to cast a vote against their will. The decision to abstain at this late stage is entirely voluntary and must be respected by the polling officials. To document this event officially, the Presiding Officer is required to make a specific remark in the remarks column of the Register of Voters next to that elector's entry, stating clearly that the individual decided not to record their vote. Following this notation, the elector must sign or place their thumb impression against this specific remark to authenticate their decision. This procedure is governed by Rule 49O of the Conduct of Election Rules, 1961. It is important to note the administrative limitation of this action; while the remark is added, there is no need to alter the serial numbers in the first column of the Register of Voters. The sequence of numbers for that elector and all succeeding electors remains unchanged, preserving the structural integrity of the register while accurately reflecting the voter's choice to abstain.

Finally, the system includes a robust mechanism to handle cases of impersonation or double-voting attempts, known as tendered votes. This situation arises when a person arrives at a polling station claiming to be a specific registered elector, only to discover that someone else has already cast a vote in that elector's name. This could be the result of fraud, error, or identity theft. In such instances, the Presiding Officer must take immediate steps to verify the true identity of the person standing before them. The officer will ask a series of questions related to the elector's identity, details of which are usually available in the electoral roll or supplementary lists. If the Presiding Officer is satisfied that the person present is indeed the genuine elector based on their satisfactory answers, the officer is authorized to allow them to vote, but through a special method. Instead of using the standard Electronic Voting Machine, which cannot accept a second vote for the same serial number, the elector is issued a tendered ballot paper. This is a physical paper ballot used exclusively for such disputed cases. Under Rule 49P, the design and language of this tendered ballot paper are strictly controlled by the Election Commission. The Commission has mandated that the tendered ballot paper must match the design of the ballot paper that is displayed on the Ballot Unit of the voting machine at that specific polling station, ensuring that the options and layout are identical to what other voters see. To distinguish it from regular ballots and prevent it from being mixed into the main count prematurely or improperly, the words "tendered ballot paper" are prominently stamped on the reverse side of the document. These tendered votes are sealed separately and counted only under specific judicial or commission orders after the election, ensuring that legitimate votes are not lost due to prior fraudulent activity while maintaining the security of the overall count.

The Returning Officer holds a critical responsibility in the electoral process regarding the preparation and distribution of special ballot papers known as tendered ballot papers. Specifically, the Returning Officer must ensure that every single polling station is supplied with exactly twenty printed ballot papers designated for use within the Ballot Units of the voting machines. These specific papers are reserved for scenarios where an elector's identity or eligibility is challenged at the polling station, requiring a manual vote rather than an electronic one. In situations where the initial allocation of twenty papers proves insufficient due to a higher than anticipated number of challenges or disputes at a particular location, a mechanism exists to supply additional materials. If more tendered ballot papers are needed, they must be procured and delivered to the Presiding Officer of that specific polling station upon request. This logistical chain operates through the zonal officer who is in charge of overseeing that polling station, ensuring that the flow of materials remains controlled and documented. Once the Presiding Officer receives these documents, whether in the initial batch or as supplementary supplies, they are legally obligated to maintain a precise and accurate accounting record. This tracking is formalized in Item 9 of Part I of a document known as Form 17C. In this form, the officer must meticulously log three distinct categories of data: the total number of tendered ballot papers received for potential use, the exact number of those papers actually issued to electors who successfully claimed them, and the quantity of unused papers that are eventually returned to the Returning Officer at the conclusion of the voting day.

Beyond the general inventory control found in Form 17C, the Presiding Officer must also maintain a separate, detailed register specifically for the transaction of issuing these papers to voters, which is recorded in Form 17B. This form serves as a legal receipt and verification log. Before handing a tendered ballot paper to an elector, the Presiding Officer is required to obtain a verifiable mark of identity from that individual. This is achieved by having the elector sign their name or provide a thumb impression in column 5 of Form 17B. This step is crucial for preventing fraud and ensuring that only eligible voters who have been properly challenged and verified receive a tendered ballot. Once the elector has completed this administrative requirement and received the paper, they proceed to the voting compartment to cast their vote. Inside the privacy of the compartment, the elector marks their choice by placing a cross mark, represented by the symbol X, on or near the graphical symbol of the candidate they wish to support. It is mandatory that this mark be made using a specific tool: an arrow cross mark rubber stamp. This is the same instrument used in traditional paper-based voting systems where physical ballot boxes are utilized, ensuring consistency in how votes are visually recorded regardless of whether the primary system is electronic or manual. Special provisions exist for electors who are unable to perform this action independently due to blindness or physical infirmity. In such cases, the Presiding Officer is authorized to permit the elector to bring a companion into the voting compartment to assist them. This assistance must follow the strict procedural guidelines established for companions, ensuring that the voter's intent is respected without compromising the secrecy or integrity of the ballot. After the vote has been successfully cast on the tendered ballot paper, the document is not placed in a standard ballot box. Instead, it is sealed inside a dedicated envelope clearly labeled or superscribed with the words Tendered Ballot Paper. This sealed envelope is then securely handed over to the designated Collection Centre for safekeeping and subsequent counting procedures.

There are circumstances during the polling process where the Presiding Officer must exercise authority to enter the screened voting compartment to preserve the integrity of the election. This action is taken if the officer has a suspicion or reasonable grounds to believe that the Ballot Unit, which is the electronic machine used for recording votes, is malfunctioning or not operating correctly. Furthermore, entry is justified if there is reason to suspect that an elector currently inside the compartment is tampering with the equipment, interfering with the Ballot Unit or the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail device, or if an elector has remained inside the compartment for an unreasonably long period, which could indicate disruption or confusion. Under Rule 49Q of the relevant election rules, the Presiding Officer possesses the explicit legal right to enter the voting compartment in these specific instances. The objective of such entry is to take whatever steps are deemed necessary to prevent tampering, ensure the equipment functions as intended, and guarantee that the polling process continues smoothly and in an orderly fashion. To maintain transparency and trust in this sensitive operation, whenever the Presiding Officer decides to enter the voting compartment, they must offer the polling agents present at the station the opportunity to accompany them. Polling agents are representatives of the candidates or political parties, and their presence serves as a witness to ensure that the Presiding Officer's actions are appropriate and that no undue influence is exerted on the voter or the machine during the intervention.

Maintaining a chronological and accurate record of all events at the polling station is a fundamental duty of the Presiding Officer, fulfilled through the maintenance of an official diary. Every Presiding Officer is required to keep this diary to document the proceedings connected with the poll as they unfold throughout the day. The instruction is clear that entries must be made continuously, recording relevant events at the moment they occur rather than reconstructing them later. The specific format for this diary is provided in the Handbook for Presiding Officers, which serves as the guiding manual for election officials. To prevent forgery or the alteration of records, all Presiding Officer Diaries must be serially numbered using a mechanical numbering machine rather than being numbered by hand. This machine-generated numbering provides a higher level of security and authenticity to the documents. Presiding Officers are strictly mandated to record their observations and accounts only in these machine-numbered diaries and are prohibited from using any other notebooks or unofficial logs for official poll business. Furthermore, a rigorous account must be kept of every single diary supplied to a Presiding Officer to ensure none are lost or substituted. Election authorities have observed a problematic practice in many past instances where Presiding Officers fail to make entries in the diary columns at regular intervals. Instead, some officers wait until the end of the polling day to fill in all the entries at once, effectively recreating the day's events from memory. This practice is considered highly objectionable and a serious violation of protocol because it compromises the accuracy and reliability of the record. It must be strongly impressed upon all Presiding Officers that any lapse in maintaining the diary properly at all points in time during the poll will be viewed with great severity and may result in disciplinary action. To enforce this standard, Observers, Zonal Magistrates, and Sector Magistrates who conduct field visits to polling stations are instructed to pay special attention to the maintenance of these diaries, checking that entries are being made in real-time. In addition to the diary, the Presiding Officer is also required to submit an additional inputs report, the format for which is also available in the Handbook for Presiding Officers, providing further data on the conduct of the election at that station.

To enhance the transparency and fairness of the electoral process, the Election Commission has directed the deployment of Micro-observers as a key civil measure, particularly in polling stations identified as critical or sensitive. These Micro-observers are typically officials drawn from central government offices or Central Government Public Sector Undertakings, ensuring they are impartial and experienced in administrative duties. Their role is to closely monitor the conduct of the poll at the grassroots level to detect and report any irregularities. These observers are required to compile and submit their findings using a specific reporting format known as Annexure 28. Stakeholders and election officials are advised to refer to the latest instructions issued by the Commission regarding the deployment and duties of these Micro-observers, as guidelines may be updated to address emerging challenges. Finally, the procedure for the close of poll is strictly defined to ensure uniformity across all locations. The polling process must be terminated exactly at the hour fixed for its conclusion. This rule applies even in situations where the commencement of the poll was delayed due to unavoidable reasons. If a polling station opens later than the appointed start time because of logistical issues or emergencies, the closing time does not shift accordingly. The poll must still end at the originally scheduled hour. This ensures that the duration of voting remains consistent and that the results are not influenced by extended voting periods in specific areas, thereby upholding the principle of equal opportunity for all electors within the mandated timeframe.

At the conclusion of the voting period, a critical procedural safeguard ensures that every eligible voter who has arrived at the polling station by the officially appointed closing time is granted the opportunity to cast their ballot, even if this necessitates extending the operational hours of the polling station beyond the scheduled end time. This provision is designed to protect the democratic right of citizens who have made the effort to arrive before the deadline from being disenfranchised due to administrative cutoffs. To manage this extension in an orderly and verifiable manner, the Presiding Officer, who acts as the chief administrator for the polling station, must implement a specific queue management protocol. As soon as the clock strikes the prescribed hour for the end of polling, the officer must identify all electors currently standing in line and distribute pre-numbered slips to them. This distribution must begin with the very last person in the queue at that exact moment, creating a definitive record of who was present and eligible to vote during the extended period. Once every individual holding a slip has successfully recorded their vote, the polling process must be formally terminated to prevent any further ballots from being cast. This termination involves a technical action on the Electronic Voting Machine, specifically requiring the Presiding Officer to press the button labeled Close on the Control Unit, which serves as the central processing component of the voting system.

Upon the activation of the Close button, the voting machine enters a locked state where it becomes technically impossible to record any additional votes, thereby securing the integrity of the election data collected up to that point. Simultaneously, the display panels located on the Control Unit will illuminate to show the aggregate total number of votes that have been recorded within the machine since the opening of the poll. It is important to note that this display provides only the cumulative count and does not reveal the breakdown of votes for individual candidates, preserving the secrecy of the ballot until the official counting phase. The Presiding Officer must immediately transcribe this total figure into Item 6 of Part I of a specific statutory document known as Form 17C, which serves as the primary ledger for vote accounting. Because pressing the Close button renders the machine permanently inactive for the duration of that polling day, the Presiding Officer must exercise extreme caution and absolute certainty before executing this command. They must verify visually and physically that no elector who was present at the fixed closing time remains unattended or waiting to vote, as any error at this stage would irreversibly deny a citizen their franchise. After confirming that all eligible voters have cast their ballots and the queue is entirely empty, the officer proceeds to finalize the closure by pressing the Close button on the Control Unit. Following this technical closure, the officer must take a red pen and draw a horizontal red line immediately after the signature entry of the last voter in Register 17A, which is the official logbook of voters. Next to this red line, the date and precise time of the poll closure must be inscribed. To enhance transparency and allow for independent verification by political representatives, all polling agents present at the station are invited to affix their signatures next to this final entry if they so desire. Subsequently, the Presiding Officer must proceed to draft Part III of the Presiding Officer's Report, which documents the events and statistics of the polling day immediately following the cessation of voting activities. At this same juncture, the officer is required to record the exact date and time displayed on the Control Unit screen into their personal official diary, creating a redundant timestamp for audit purposes.

Following the formal closure of the poll, the Presiding Officer is legally mandated under Rule 49S to compile a comprehensive account of the votes recorded within the voting machine. This accounting process must be executed using Part I of Form 17C, a standardized government form designed for this specific statistical reconciliation. The regulation requires that this document be prepared in duplicate, meaning two identical copies must be generated to ensure there is a backup record in case one is lost or compromised. The original copy of the account of votes recorded in Form 17C must be retained by the Presiding Officer and placed inside a separate envelope that is securely sealed. On the exterior of this sealed cover, the words Account of Votes Recorded must be clearly written or stamped as a superscription to identify its contents without needing to open it. The second copy of this form is handled differently; it is placed into another envelope that remains unsealed at this stage and is intended for submission to the reception center where election materials are aggregated. In addition to preparing these internal records, Rule 49S imposes a further obligation on the Presiding Officer to provide transparency to the observing political parties. The officer must furnish a true and attested copy of the account of votes recorded, exactly as prepared in Form 17C, to every single polling agent who is present at the station at the time of closure. A polling agent is a representative authorized by a candidate or political party to witness the election proceedings. Before handing over these copies, the Presiding Officer must obtain a signed receipt from each agent to prove delivery. Consequently, the officer must anticipate the number of agents present and prepare a corresponding number of additional copies of Form 17C in advance. Crucially, these copies must be distributed proactively to every attending agent regardless of whether they explicitly request them, ensuring that all stakeholders have immediate access to the vote totals.

To guarantee strict adherence to these requirements regarding the distribution of vote accounts, the Election Commission has instituted a mandatory declaration procedure detailed in Annexure 51 Part-II. At the close of the poll, the Presiding Officer must formally make this declaration, which serves as a sworn statement confirming that copies of the account of votes recorded have been duly furnished to all present polling agents. This step adds a layer of legal accountability to the administrative process. Once the documentation and distribution phases are complete, the physical security of the voting equipment becomes the paramount concern. The Electronic Voting Machine, commonly referred to as the EVM, and the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail unit, known as the VVPAT, which produces a paper slip for each vote cast, must be sealed and secured for safe transportation to the designated counting or collection center. The sealing protocol begins with powering down the system; the power switch on the Control Unit must be turned to the OFF position to cut electrical supply. Simultaneously, the knob controlling the paper roll mechanism in the VVPAT unit must be rotated to the lock position to prevent any movement or extraction of the paper trail. After these safety measures are engaged, the Ballot Units, which are the interfaces where voters press buttons, along with the Control Unit and the VVPAT, must be disconnected from one another.

In a move designed to ensure the integrity of the paper audit trail in the presence of witnesses, the Presiding Officer is required to remove the power pack, which functions as the battery source, from the VVPAT unit while polling agents observe the action. This prevents any potential tampering with the VVPAT memory or paper feed after the poll has closed. Once the components are powered down, disconnected, and the battery removed, the Ballot Units, the Control Unit, and the VVPAT are carefully returned to their respective protective carrying cases. The final stage of securing the equipment involves a physical sealing process that binds the cases shut. A strong thread is passed through two specialized holes provided on both sides of each carrying case. The ends of this thread are then tied together and secured with a thread seal that bears the unique impression of the Presiding Officer's official seal. This seal is attached to a common address tag that displays essential particulars of the election, such as the constituency name, and the specific details of the polling station. To further bolster confidence in the security of the transport, any candidates or their polling agents who are present and wish to do so are permitted to apply their own personal or party seals alongside the official seal on the thread. This multi-signatory sealing method ensures that the equipment cannot be opened en route to the counting center without breaking multiple seals, thereby providing clear evidence of any unauthorized access attempts.

At the conclusion of the voting process at any polling station, a critical phase of documentation and security verification begins, centered on the integrity of the electronic voting infrastructure. The Presiding Officer, who acts as the chief administrator for the polling station, is legally mandated to record specific details regarding the physical security seals applied to the equipment. Specifically, the names of all candidates or their authorized polling agents who have placed their personal seals on the carrying cases containing the Ballot Units, the Control Units, and the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail machines must be meticulously noted. This record-keeping occurs within a formal declaration document that the Presiding Officer completes immediately after the poll closes. The structure and required content of this declaration are strictly defined in the official Handbook for Presiding Officers, ensuring uniformity across all polling locations. This step serves as a foundational chain-of-custody measure, creating a transparent audit trail that links specific individuals to the physical security of the voting machines before they leave the polling booth.

Following the closure of voting and the completion of the initial declarations, the Presiding Officer must proceed to secure all associated election paperwork. This involves placing various documents into separate packets and sealing them in strict accordance with established rules and administrative instructions. To enhance transparency and build trust among political participants, the law permits polling agents who are present at the station to affix their own seals onto these envelopes and packets, supplementing the primary seal applied by the Presiding Officer. The documents requiring this dual-sealing protection include the marked copy of the electoral roll, which indicates which registered voters actually cast their ballots, and the Register of Voters. Additionally, voter slips issued during the day, tendered ballot papers used by voters whose names were not initially found on the roll but were later verified, and the corresponding list of such tendered voters recorded in Form 17B must be secured. The protocol also covers unused tendered ballot papers, the official list of challenged votes where a voter's eligibility was questioned, and any paper seals that were either unused or damaged during the proceedings. Furthermore, appointment letters for the polling agents and any other specific papers that the Returning Officer has explicitly directed to be kept under seal must be included in these protected packets. This comprehensive sealing process ensures that every piece of documentary evidence generated during the election is tamper-evident and accounted for.

Once the Electronic Voting Machines, the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail devices, and all sealed election papers are secured, the logistical phase of transmission to a central collection point commences. The Presiding Officer is responsible for delivering these items, or arranging for their delivery, to a location designated by the Returning Officer. The Returning Officer holds the overarching responsibility for organizing safe transportation and secure custody of these sensitive materials as they move from individual polling stations to the collection centre. This centre serves as the interim storage facility where all voting machines and documents will be held until the official counting of votes begins. To maintain the highest standards of security and efficiency, several key operational instructions must be followed. Ideally, the storage centre should be located at the same venue where the vote counting will eventually take place. This strategic co-location minimizes the handling of the sealed machines, ensuring that each unit is moved only once between the polling station and the counting table, thereby reducing the risk of accidental damage or unauthorized interference.

Proper planning is essential to ensure that the receipt of Electronic Voting Machines, Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail units, their power packs, and related paperwork proceeds efficiently and without chaos. The Returning Officer must communicate the logistics plan well in advance to all contesting candidates or their election agents. This communication should include the schedule for the collection parties being dispatched and the specific route charts they will follow to retrieve materials from the polling stations. This advance notice allows candidates the opportunity to deploy their own agents to accompany the official collection teams if they wish to observe the transport process firsthand. However, strict separation protocols apply to this observation; while candidate agents may follow the convoy, they must arrange their own transportation. Under no circumstances are these agents permitted to travel in the same vehicles carrying the voting machines, election materials, or the official polling staff. This separation prevents any possibility of collusion or unauthorized access during transit. All parties involved must adhere to the detailed instructions issued by the Election Commission regarding the management of these Receiving Centres to ensure procedural compliance.

Upon arrival at the collection centre, the intake process must be managed with precision to avoid bottlenecks or idle resources. A sufficient number of reception counters should be opened, and the assignment of polling stations to these counters should be distributed evenly. The goal is to balance the workflow so that no single counter becomes overcrowded while others remain unused. However, exceptions are made for specific scenarios that require heightened scrutiny. For instance, if a mock poll, which is a test run conducted before actual voting begins, was performed at a station without the presence of any party candidates, or if a serious complaint was lodged during the voting day at that station, a dedicated special counter must be established for materials from that location. In such cases, the presence of an independent observer and the Returning Officer is mandatory to oversee the handover and ensure that any potential issues are documented and addressed immediately.

The final stage of post-poll management involves the categorization and storage of the voting machines in specialized strong rooms. After the poll concludes, Electronic Voting Machines and Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail devices fall into four distinct categories based on their status and usage. Category A consists of polled machines, meaning those that were successfully used to record votes. Category B includes polled machines that are non-functional, as well as all Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail units regardless of function, as these are treated with high priority for verification. These two categories, A and B, are stored together in a designated Polled EVM Strong Room. Category C comprises non-functional machines that were never polled, typically those that failed during the pre-election mock poll. These units are stored in a separate Repair Strong Room, which must be located away from the room holding the polled machines to prevent any confusion or mixing of data-bearing devices with defective hardware. Category D includes unused reserve machines that were kept on standby but never activated. These are stored in a Reserve Strong Room, also situated separately from the polled machine storage.

Strict segregation rules govern these storage facilities to maintain the integrity of the election data and the utility of the equipment. It is impermissible under any circumstances to store Category C or Category D machines in the same strong rooms designated for Category A and B machines. This physical separation is crucial for two main reasons. First, it ensures that when the counting process begins, only the correct Control Units containing actual vote data are brought to the counting tables, eliminating the risk of introducing empty or faulty machines into the count. Second, it protects the unused reserve machines from being unnecessarily blocked or tagged as used, preserving them for future elections or redeployment. Before the election, written information detailing the locations and protocols for all categories of strong rooms and their associated storage materials must be provided to all contesting candidates, and their receipt of this information must be formally acknowledged. Finally, the storage and security protocols for every category of machine must be executed in full compliance with the latest edition of the Manual on Electronic Voting Machines, which serves as the definitive guide for technical and procedural standards in the electoral process.

In the comprehensive administration of electoral processes, the secure custody of election materials is a foundational pillar that ensures the integrity, transparency, and legal validity of any democratic exercise. Upon the conclusion of polling activities at various voting stations, a critical logistical phase begins wherein specific election papers, along with the polled Electronic Voting Machines and their associated Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail units, are transported to a designated facility known as the Receipt Centre. This centre serves as the primary hub for the initial intake, verification, and subsequent segregation of sensitive electoral assets before they are moved to their final long-term storage locations. The management of these items requires a highly structured protocol that distinguishes between different categories of documents and hardware based on their function, sensitivity, and the stage of the electoral cycle in which they will be needed, particularly distinguishing between items required for immediate post-poll accounting and those reserved for potential future scrutiny or legal challenges.

The infrastructure for storing these materials is not monolithic but is instead divided into specialized secure environments referred to as strong rooms, which are essentially high-security vaults designed to prevent unauthorized access, tampering, or environmental damage. The first category of storage is designated specifically for the Polled EVM Strong Room, which must be clearly identified with proper signage to avoid any confusion during the chaotic and high-pressure period immediately following the close of polls. This specific strong room is tasked with housing the contents of what is officially termed the First Packet, often colloquially referred to as the EVM papers packet. The items contained within this first packet are of immediate operational importance for the counting process and the initial validation of results. Inside this packet, officials will find an unsealed envelope that contains the account of votes recorded, a formal document widely known as Form 17C. This form is crucial because it provides a detailed numerical breakdown of the total votes cast at that specific polling station, serving as the primary record against which the machine totals are verified. Alongside this financial-style account of votes, the same unsealed envelope also holds the Presiding Officer Report, which is segmented into three distinct parts labeled I, II, and III. Part I of this report specifically includes the Mock-Poll Certificate, which is a vital documentary evidence proving that the voting machine was tested and found to be recording zero votes before the actual election commenced, thereby establishing the baseline integrity of the device. Furthermore, the First Packet must also contain the printed paper slips generated by the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail unit during that pre-election mock poll. These slips, which demonstrate the machine's ability to print a voter's choice correctly, are treated with extreme caution and must be sealed inside a black-colored envelope before being placed in the packet, ensuring that these test prints are not accidentally mixed with actual ballot records or misinterpreted as real votes.

Distinct from the storage of active voting machines and their immediate accompanying documents is the second category of storage, which is dedicated to the Election Papers Strong Room. It is a mandatory procedural requirement that this strong room be physically separate from the Polled EVM Strong Room to maintain a clear chain of custody and to segregate materials based on their usage timeline. Just like its counterpart, this secondary strong room must also bear proper signage indicating its specific purpose, ensuring that election officials can quickly and accurately deposit the correct materials without error. This facility is designated to receive the Second Packet, which is formally known as the Scrutiny Cover. The term scrutiny in this context refers to the detailed examination of election records that may be ordered by a court or an election tribunal in the event of a dispute or an election petition filed by a candidate or party. Consequently, the documents stored within this second packet are not needed for the immediate counting of votes but are preserved as legal evidence. While the specific list of documents in the Scrutiny Cover can vary slightly depending on local regulations, it generally includes spare ballot papers, used ballot papers if applicable in hybrid systems, tendered ballot lists, and other administrative logs that detail the conduct of the poll beyond the raw vote counts. By separating the Scrutiny Cover into a different strong room, the election commission ensures that the core voting machines and their primary result sheets remain untouched and isolated from materials that might only be accessed months or even years later during a legal review. This dual-strong-room system exemplifies the depth of planning involved in election logistics, where physical separation acts as a security control to minimize the risk of mass tampering and to streamline the retrieval process depending on whether the need is for immediate result declaration or long-term judicial scrutiny. The entire procedure, from the sealing of the black envelopes for mock poll slips to the distinct signage on the heavy doors of the strong rooms, is designed to create an auditable trail that withstands public scrutiny and legal challenge, reinforcing the confidence of the electorate in the sanctity of the voting process.

The meticulous management of election materials following the conclusion of polling is a cornerstone of democratic integrity, requiring a structured approach to the sealing, categorization, and storage of various documents and equipment. The process begins with the organization of specific packets, each designated to hold distinct types of records essential for verifying the election's conduct. The first set of materials includes an unsealed envelope containing the Presiding Officer's Diary, which serves as a chronological log of all events and decisions made at the polling station throughout the voting day. Alongside this is a sealed envelope housing the Register of Voters, officially known as Form 17A, which acts as the definitive record of every individual who cast a ballot at that specific location. Another unsealed envelope holds Form 14-A, a specialized list identifying blind and infirm electors along with the formal declarations of their companions, ensuring that assistance was provided in accordance with legal provisions. This group also includes an unsealed envelope containing the Visit Sheet, a document used to record inspections by higher authorities. These items collectively form the initial layer of documentation that validates the procedural adherence of the polling station.

Proceeding to the third packet, which comprises statutory covers, the protocol dictates the secure containment of critical voting instruments. This packet includes a sealed envelope holding the marked copy of the electoral roll, a version of the voter list where entries are physically marked to indicate participation, thereby preventing duplicate voting. It also contains a sealed envelope with voter's slips, which are counterfoils or stubs issued to voters, and a sealed envelope dedicated to unused tendered ballot papers. Tendered ballots are special paper ballots issued to voters whose names do not appear on the roll or who have already been marked as voted but claim otherwise; the unused portion must be secured separately. Furthermore, a sealed envelope within this packet contains the used tendered ballot papers accompanied by Form 17-B, a register specifically maintained to track these exceptional votes. Also included is a sealed envelope containing Form 14, the official list of challenged votes, which records instances where a voter's identity or eligibility was questioned at the time of casting their ballot. These sealed components ensure that the core mechanisms of vote recording and verification remain tamper-proof until the counting phase.

The fourth packet is designated for non-statutory covers, encompassing administrative and logistical documents that support the election process but are not part of the primary statutory record of votes. This section starts with an unsealed envelope containing copies of the electoral roll other than the officially marked master copy, serving as reference material. It includes an unsealed envelope with appointment letters in Form 10 and the associated accounts for polling agents, who are representatives appointed by candidates to observe the polling process. An unsealed envelope holding the election duty certificate in Form 12B is also present, documenting the service of officials. Additionally, this packet contains unsealed envelopes with declarations made by the presiding officer, a receipt book and any cash held in respect of challenged votes, and unused or damaged seals and special tags used for securing equipment. There is also an unsealed envelope for unused voter's slips and another for declarations obtained from electors regarding their age, including a list of those who refused to make such declarations. This packet further houses specific forms such as the declaration by an elector under Rule 49MA for test votes, declarations for electors listed in the ASD list, and any letters of complaint addressed to the Station House Officer of the local police, ensuring a comprehensive audit trail of administrative actions and grievances.

The fifth packet focuses on instructional materials and consumables necessary for the operation of the polling station. It contains the Handbook for the Presiding Officer, which provides comprehensive guidelines on conducting elections. Included are detailed instructions regarding the Electronic Voting Machine and the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail system, often referred to as VVPAT. These instructions are supplemented by educational posters explaining how to cast a vote using these machines, brochures specifically designed for presiding officers on the operation of the equipment, and troubleshooting guides to address technical issues that may arise during polling. A crucial component of this packet is a sealed envelope containing the indelible ink set, where each phial is securely stoppered and sealed with molten candle wax or similar material to prevent leakage or evaporation, ensuring the ink remains viable for marking voters' fingers. This envelope also holds the used self-inking pads, which are the devices employed to apply the indelible ink to voters, thereby maintaining a physical record of participation that complements the electronic data.

The sixth and final packet captures all other miscellaneous materials used during the election. This includes the used list of contesting candidates known as Form 7A, which details the individuals who stood for election at that station, and a used photocopy of the signatures of these candidates, which were likely used for verification purposes. The packet also secures any other unused forms that were distributed but not utilized, an arrow cross-mark rubber stamp specifically designated for marking tendered ballot papers to distinguish them from regular ballots, and the cup used for setting or holding the indelible ink. In scenarios where simultaneous elections are held, such as when voting for both state assembly and national parliament occurs on the same day, strict segregation protocols are enforced. The materials mentioned above must be packed separately and stored in distinct strong rooms designated specifically for Assembly Elections and Parliamentary Elections. These locations must be clearly identified to prevent any mixing of materials between the two different electoral contests, and candidates must be informed of these arrangements well in advance to ensure transparency and trust in the process.

The safety and security of these election materials are paramount, governed by rigorous directives issued by the Election Commission. The District Election Officer retains overall responsibility for the safe custody of the Electronic Voting Machines, VVPAT units, and all election papers. To facilitate this, a control room adjacent to the strong room locations must be operational around the clock, serving as a command center for monitoring security. A Gazetted officer, representing the civil administration, alongside a police officer, must be deployed on a twenty-four-hour basis to oversee security arrangements. This surveillance includes comprehensive coverage via Closed-Circuit Television cameras, which must be installed with sufficient storage capacity to retain footage for the required duration. The camera placement is strategic, covering the sealed doors of both the Polled EVM Strong Room and the Election Paper Strong Room, as well as security checkpoints and corridors. A robust system for backing up this video footage must be established, with the resulting data remaining in the exclusive custody of the District Election Officer. Furthermore, videography is mandatory during the opening and closing of these strong rooms, and proper logbooks must be maintained to record every entry and exit. The Chief Electoral Officer retains the authority to inspect these strong rooms after the poll, particularly if the counting of votes is delayed due to multi-phase election schedules.

When polled Electronic Voting Machines, VVPATs, and election papers need to be temporarily stored in strong rooms at a Receipt Centre that is located far from the final Counting Centre, a specific protocol regarding the classification of strong rooms is followed. There must be a distinction between the Polled EVM Strong Room, which stores the voting machines, VVPATs, and the first packet of election papers, and the Election Papers Strong Room, which houses the second through sixth packets of documents. The transportation of these sensitive materials from the polling stations to the Receipt Centre is conducted under proper escort and security detail to prevent tampering or loss. To ensure transparency, candidates and their authorized representatives are permitted to follow the vehicles transporting the EVMs, VVPATs, and election papers. During temporary storage, these materials are kept under continuous videography and stringent security arrangements. Once all polling stations have delivered their materials to the Receipt Centre, the entire consignment is moved from the temporary strong rooms to the designated permanent strong rooms at the Counting Centre. This final leg of transportation is secured by Central Armed Police Forces, and a senior officer is specifically designated to supervise the entire movement of these election materials, ensuring that the chain of custody remains unbroken and verifiable from the moment the polls close until the counting begins.

Strict measures must be implemented to ensure that under no conceivable circumstances are the security seals affixed to election materials broken during the transportation phase. This absolute prohibition on seal tampering is fundamental to maintaining the integrity of the electoral process from the polling station to the counting center. To foster transparency and build trust among all stakeholders, candidates participating in the election and their authorized representatives are granted the explicit right to physically follow the vehicles responsible for transporting Electronic Voting Machines, Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail devices, and all associated election papers. This provision allows for direct observation of the chain of custody while the materials are in transit. Furthermore, the entire procedure of depositing these critical items into the designated strong rooms, which are specialized high-security storage facilities, must be recorded through videography. This visual record serves as an immutable account of the handover process. Following this deposition, the District Election Officer is mandated to compile a formal report detailing the completion of these activities and transmit it to the Chief Election Officer. Throughout this transportation and deposition timeline, independent observers appointed to monitor the election are required to keep a close and continuous watch on the proceedings to detect and prevent any irregularities. The rights of candidates extend beyond mere transportation tracking; they and their representatives are also permitted to witness the specific process of placing these materials into the designated strong rooms located within the Counting Centre. To add an additional layer of personal accountability and verification, candidates or their election agents may be allowed to affix their signatures directly onto the seals of the double lock system securing the storage areas. These signatures serve as a personal attestation of the seal's integrity at the time of locking. Additionally, these signatures must be formally recorded in a dedicated register or log-book maintained specifically for this purpose, creating a written trail that complements the physical seals and video evidence.

Once the polled Electronic Voting Machines, Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail units, and election papers arrive at the Counting Centre, either through the aforementioned transportation process or directly at a Receipt Centre, they must be immediately stored in the designated strong rooms known as the Polled EVM Strong Room. A rigorous protocol governs the storage of these polled machines and the first packet of election papers, which typically contains documents related to the voting process itself. Inside the strong rooms, separate spaces must be demarcated in a grid pattern, forming square or rectangular boxes, with each distinct area assigned to a specific polling station. It is imperative that the ballot units, control units, and Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail devices used at a single polling station remain grouped together and are never separated. These items from one station must invariably be kept in the single earmarked box allocated for that specific station to preserve the logical link between the machine and its origin. The first packets containing election papers must be kept attached to the polled control units belonging to Category A and B, which generally refer to machines used in the primary election contest. The arrangement of these stored items should strictly follow the serial number sequence of the polling stations to facilitate easy retrieval and verification. In scenarios where the strong room faces space constraints, these machines and packets shall still be organized by polling station but may be placed in fabricated multi-layer storage racks made of steel, iron, or wood within the Polled EVM Strong Room, ensuring that the grouping logic remains intact despite vertical stacking.

In the ideal scenario involving a single election event, all polled Electronic Voting Machines, Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail devices, and the first packet of election papers should be housed within one unified Polled EVM Strong Room, organized either by Assembly Constituency or by Assembly Segment as applicable to the specific administrative division. However, recognizing that physical infrastructure varies, if space constraints prevent housing everything in a single room, these materials may be distributed across multiple strong rooms provided they are adjacent to each other, located in the same building, and preferably situated on the same floor to minimize logistical complexity and security risks. To ensure clarity and prevent confusion regarding the contents of each storage area, a formal notice containing specific particulars of the elections, including the Assembly Constituency or Assembly Segment number and name, along with the serial numbers of the polling stations stored within, must be pasted prominently outside the door of each strong room. If the physical dimensions of a Polled EVM Strong Room are exceptionally large, regulations dictate that the room should not be utilized for storing materials pertaining to more than two Assembly Constituencies or Assembly Segments. This limitation is designed to reduce the risk of cross-contamination or mixing of records between different electoral zones. In such instances where a large room is shared, it is mandatory to ensure that proper physical bifurcation or partitioning of the room is executed before any materials are introduced. This separation ensures that items from different constituencies do not get mixed up. Each bifurcated section must function as an independent unit, equipped with its own separate door and an independent double lock system, effectively treating each partitioned zone as a distinct strong room with its own security protocols.

A critical distinction in storage protocol involves the categorization of Electronic Voting Machines. All machines falling under categories C and D, which may include spare machines, machines used for testing, or those from different phases of polling, must be kept entirely separate from the strong rooms designated for Category A and B machines. Furthermore, these Category C and D machines should not be stored in the immediate vicinity of the Category A and B strong rooms to eliminate any possibility of accidental substitution or confusion. Under no circumstances should any election material other than the first packet of papers be admitted into the Category A and B strong rooms, preserving the purity of the primary vote data environment. Distinct from the machine storage, the post-poll storage of remaining election papers follows a separate track. Documents contained in packets numbered two through six, which often include voter lists, tendered votes, and other administrative records, must be secured in a separate facility known as the Election Paper Strong Room. This room must be established for each Assembly Constituency or Assembly Segment and must adhere to the same high-security standards described previously. It is strictly prohibited to place these paper packets inside the Polled EVM Strong Room, ensuring a physical separation between the electronic voting hardware and the ancillary paper documentation. The Election Paper Strong Room is not a static vault; it shall be opened and closed specifically for the purpose of retrieving the second packet, known as Scrutiny Papers, for the counting process, and subsequently returning them. Every instance of opening this room must follow a due protocol that includes providing advance information to candidates and their election agents, who must acknowledge receipt of this notice. This ensures that no access occurs without the knowledge of the political stakeholders.

The security architecture of the Election Paper Strong Room relies on a prescribed double lock system, requiring two distinct keys or combinations to gain entry, thereby preventing unilateral access. A separate log-book must be maintained exclusively for this room. In the event that the strong room is opened, a detailed entry must be made recording the date, the exact time of opening, the duration for which the room remained open, and the names of all persons who entered. The purpose for opening the strong room must also be explicitly stated in this log. Beyond the log-book, other stringent protocols for opening strong rooms must be scrupulously followed. These include providing prior intimation to all contesting candidates to allow them to be present if they choose, and mandating the videography of all activities occurring within the strong room. This video surveillance must cover the entire sequence of events, including the breaking of seals, the opening of locks, the removal or insertion of materials, and the re-sealing of the room upon closure, creating a comprehensive visual audit trail. Regarding the safety and security arrangements for the Polled EVM Strong Rooms at the Counting Center, these designated facilities shall be established in a secured location, preferably within the district headquarters where the administrative machinery is most robust. In special cases where logistics demand it, they may be set up in sub-divisional headquarters, but only in locations where the actual counting of votes is scheduled to take place. The physical construction of these strong rooms demands extreme fortification. The room shall possess only a single point of entry and exit to control access flow. All other potential points of ingress, such as additional doors or windows, must be permanently sealed using brick masonry or concrete, rendering them impassable. Finally, reinforcing the access control, every strong room must be equipped with a double lock system, ensuring that two authorized individuals must cooperate to open the facility, thus embedding a system of checks and balances into the very physical structure of the election storage infrastructure.

The secure storage of election materials, specifically Electronic Voting Machines and Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail units, relies on a rigorous dual-lock system designed to ensure that no single individual can access the contents without authorization. In standard operational procedures, all keys corresponding to the first lock, designated as Lock-1, must remain in the exclusive custody of the Returning Officer who is responsible for the specific constituency. Simultaneously, all keys for the second lock, known as Lock-2, are to be held by the Assistant Returning Officer assigned to that particular Assembly Constituency or Assembly Segment. This separation of duties creates a necessary check and balance, requiring the presence of two distinct authorized officials to open the strong room. However, specific adjustments are mandated for Parliamentary Constituencies where the Counting Centre is established in a district different from the headquarters of the Returning Officer. In such exceptional geographical scenarios, the protocol shifts so that all keys for Lock-1 are kept with the respective Assistant Returning Officer, while all keys for Lock-2 are entrusted to officers who have been formally nominated by the District Election Officer for each individual Assembly Segment. This ensures that local oversight remains intact even when the primary Returning Officer is based elsewhere.

The physical infrastructure housing these critical voting machines must adhere to strict electrical and safety standards to prevent accidental damage or tampering. The main electrical switch controlling the power supply to these strong rooms must be installed outside the physical boundaries of the room itself. Once the strong rooms have been sealed with the voting machines inside, the electricity supply to the room must be completely disconnected. This measure is primarily intended to eliminate the risk of short-circuiting, which could potentially cause a fire or damage the sensitive electronic components of the voting machines. Furthermore, under no circumstances should any external powered unit be installed or kept inside the strong room during the storage period. To protect against natural disasters, comprehensive arrangements for fire and flood safety must be established. This includes the mandatory installation of fire extinguishers both inside and immediately outside the strong room to ensure rapid response capability regardless of whether personnel can enter the room. An adequate fire alarm system must also be put in place to provide early warning of any combustion events. Despite the disconnection of internal power to prevent shorts, it is absolutely essential that there is an uninterrupted power supply available at the strong room locations for the entire duration that the Electronic Voting Machines and Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail units are stored. This power is likely needed for external monitoring systems, lighting, or climate control in the surrounding area. To guarantee this continuity, appropriate arrangements involving backup generators must be made to ensure that power supply remains unbroken in the event of any grid outages or utility failures.

Security around these storage facilities is maintained through a robust two-tier guarding system designed to create layered defense perimeters. The innermost perimeter, which directly surrounds the strong room doors, shall be guarded exclusively by personnel from the Central Armed Police Forces. The outer perimeter, which encompasses the broader secured campus, is to be guarded by the State Armed Police. For the purpose of maintaining this high level of security, a minimum of one platoon of Central Armed Police Forces personnel must be provided to guard the strong room on a twenty-four hours a day, seven days a week basis, ensuring round-the-clock vigilance. The District Election Officer bears the responsibility of certifying that these specific security arrangements have been fully complied with in their jurisdiction. Subsequently, the Chief Electoral Officer of the State must obtain this certificate from each District Election Officer to verify statewide adherence. Additionally, independent Observers appointed by the Election Commission shall inspect the sites and confirm that these instructions have been properly implemented.

To ensure transparency and build trust in the electoral process, specific facilities are provided to allow candidates to monitor the security of the strong rooms. All contesting candidates must be intimated in writing, with a proper acknowledgement of receipt, instructing them to depute their authorized representatives to keep a close watch on the security arrangements of these strong rooms. These representatives are permitted to stay outside the outermost security perimeter within a rain-proof tent that is officially erected for this specific purpose. The location of this tent must enable the representatives to have a clear view of the entry points of the strong room. Essential facilities such as proper shade, drinking water, and toilet amenities must be provided to these watchers to ensure they can maintain their vigil comfortably. In situations where the physical layout prevents a direct line of sight to the strong room, Closed-Circuit Television cameras must be arranged at the location. These cameras should feed live video to monitors accessible to the candidate representatives, allowing them to observe all activities at the strong room door. In cases where CCTV is used, the representatives may be taken periodically in organized batches to the inner perimeter to physically see, verify, and satisfy themselves regarding the integrity of the strong room security. To facilitate communication during any emergencies or concerns, the phone numbers of the Chief Electoral Officer, Additional Chief Electoral Officers, the control rooms established by the Chief Electoral Officer, District Election Officer, and Returning Officer, as well as the concerned District Election Officer and Superintendent of Police, must be provided to the candidates. The candidates may then share these contact details with their people keeping vigil at the strong room locations, enabling them to contact the relevant officials immediately in case of any need. Furthermore, the media shall be briefed from time to time by the Chief Electoral Officer regarding the detailed safety and security arrangements in these strong rooms and Counting Centers. This proactive dissemination of information serves to inform the general public and effectively counter any fake news or misinformation that may circulate, particularly on social media platforms.

Strict protocols govern access to the inner perimeter of the Polled Electronic Voting Machine Strong Room to prevent unauthorized entry. No individual, regardless of their status or position, should be allowed to reach the inner perimeter without strictly following the established protocol. A proper logbook must be maintained by the Central Armed Police Forces for every entry and exit, as well as for the duty roster of security personnel. Detailed information including the date, time, duration of the visit, and the name of every person crossing the inner perimeter must be entered into this logbook, and a signature must be obtained in each case to validate the record. This logging requirement applies universally and includes visits by Observers, District Election Officers, Superintendents of Police, candidates, their agents, or any other persons. To further enhance accountability, video cameras must be provided to the Central Armed Police Forces to record all visits made by such visitors, creating a visual audit trail alongside the written log. Regarding vehicular movement, no vehicle is allowed inside the secured campus where the Electronic Voting Machines, Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail units, and election papers are stored. This prohibition applies equally to vehicles belonging to officials, ministers, or any other political functionaries. A specific alighting point for all vehicles must be marked clearly ahead of the outer security perimeter itself. Beyond this designated drop-off point, the area must function as a pedestrian zone only, ensuring that no motorized transport can approach the immediate vicinity of the stored materials.

Supervision of these facilities is carried out through a structured schedule of visits by senior election officials. Returning Officers are required to visit the storage campus, proceeding up to the inner perimeter only, every day in the morning and every day in the evening. During these visits, they must check the logbook and review the videography records, subsequently sending a report to the District Election Officer on the status of the facility every day. In the case of strong rooms located within the district headquarters, the District Election Officer must perform the same daily inspection routine. Where the strong rooms are situated outside the District Headquarters, the District Election Officer is required to visit the site as frequently as possible, with a mandatory minimum frequency of once every three to four days. Both the District Election Officers and the Superintendents of Police shall be held personally responsible for the security of the strong rooms within their respective districts and for the meticulous implementation of all prescribed protocols, ensuring that the integrity of the electoral material is preserved until the counting process begins.

The administrative protocols governing the security and custody of election materials require Chief Executive Officers to submit a distinct and detailed report to the Zonal Secretaries. This report must explicitly outline the nature of security arrangements established by the local administration to guard strong rooms, which are specialized high-security storage facilities used during elections.
