The report should specify whether the security personnel deployed include members of the Central Armed Police Forces who are already stationed in the area or if additional forces have been requisitioned specifically for this purpose. Furthermore, any supplementary measures implemented to enhance the protection of these strong rooms must be clearly documented and communicated. These rigorous instructions are not limited to the period immediately following the poll but apply with necessary modifications to the interim phase between the initial counting of votes and any potential recounting that may be ordered. During this critical interval, the voting machines must remain under the same level of stringent security to ensure the integrity of the electoral process is maintained without compromise.

The procedure for sealing strong rooms is designed to maximize transparency and prevent any possibility of tampering through multi-party oversight. The sealing of all categories of strong rooms must be conducted in the joint presence of key stakeholders, including the respective General Observer, the Returning Officer who manages the election for the constituency, the contesting candidates, and their appointed election agents. This collective witnessing ensures that no single entity has unilateral control over the securing of the facility. In scenarios where multiple strong rooms are established within the same premises or across the same town, the scheduling and sequencing of their closure and sealing must be meticulously coordinated. The primary objective of this coordination is to guarantee that at least one General Observer is physically present at the moment each individual strong room is closed and sealed, without any deviation from this rule. When a General Observer is assigned responsibility for more than one Assembly Constituency or Assembly Segment, and the strong room locations are geographically dispersed throughout the district, the senior-most General Observer must consult in advance with the District Election Officer and the Returning Officer. Together, they will determine which specific Observer will be stationed at which strong room during its closure to ensure comprehensive coverage.

In situations of exigency or unexpected logistical challenges, the senior-most General Observer holds the authority to nominate an Expenditure Observer to represent a General Observer for the specific task of sealing a strong room. Additionally, in exceptional circumstances where standard staffing is insufficient, senior micro-observers may be selected from the list of personnel already deployed for poll day duties. These micro-observers can be authorized by the senior-most General Observer to act as representatives for the sealing process, provided such authorization is formally granted. It is imperative that information regarding these assignments and the identity of the representing observers be made available to the contesting candidates or their election agents well in advance to maintain trust and allow for proper verification. Similarly, recognizing that candidates or their primary agents may occasionally be unavailable during the sealing proceedings, provisions exist to allow the candidate to send an authorized representative. This substitute representative must possess a written letter of authority from the candidate, carry a valid photo identification card, and must be a registered voter within the relevant Parliamentary Constituency or Assembly Constituency to ensure they have a legitimate stake in the local electoral outcome.

Regarding the safe custody of Electronic Voting Machines and Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail units after the polling has concluded, strict timelines and procedures are enforced. All Presiding Officers, who manage individual polling stations, or the designated Collecting Parties responsible for transporting materials, must deposit the EVMs, VVPATs, election papers, and related materials at the designated storage centers without any avoidable delay. Any officer who fails to adhere to this timeline or defaults in their duty regarding the timely deposition of these materials will render themselves liable to disciplinary action, underscoring the seriousness of maintaining the chain of custody. To facilitate organized storage, the Returning Officer may pre-designate specific areas inside the storage room or building. This involves marking out specified parts of the floor space in the form of squares, allocated in advance for stacking the EVMs and VVPATs received from particular polling stations. The arrangement of these stacks must strictly follow the serial number sequence of the polling stations to ensure logical order and easy retrieval.

A fundamental rule of this storage protocol is that all Ballot Units, Control Units, and VVPAT machines received from a single polling station must invariably be kept together at one location on the same marked square. This co-location prevents the mixing of equipment from different stations and preserves the integrity of the data associated with each specific polling booth. Furthermore, one copy of Part I of Form 17C, which is the account of votes recorded filed by the concerned Presiding Officer of each polling station, must be placed on top of the Control Unit pertaining to that specific station. This ensures immediate visual verification of the vote count record associated with the machine. The first packet, typically white in color, containing essential EVM papers such as Part I of Form 17C, the mock poll certificate found in Part I of the Presiding Officer's report, and the mock poll VVPAT slip enclosed in a black envelope, must be kept inside the strong room along with the EVM itself. However, distinct separation is required for other sensitive documents. The duplicate copy of the account of votes recorded and the paper seal account must be kept under the personal safe custody of the Returning Officer. These documents, along with the Presiding Officer's Diary and other records like the Register of Voters known as Form 17A, reports from Sector or Zonal magistrates, and additional inputs provided by the Presiding Officer as per Election Commission instructions, must never be placed in the strong room where the polled EVMs are stored. Under no circumstance should these specific papers, records, or the VVPAT power packs be put into the strong room housing the voted machines, thereby creating a system of separated custody to reduce risk.

The physical arrangement of the machines within the strong room requires careful planning to accommodate potential irregularities in the arrival sequence of equipment. Sufficient space must be left between the rows of voting machines as they are initially stacked. This buffer zone is necessary so that if machines are received subsequently out of turn, meaning they arrive in an order that does not match the pre-assigned serial number sequence of the polling stations, they can still be placed in their appropriate allotted space. This foresight eliminates the necessity of having to shift or disturb any of the voting machines that were received and stacked earlier, which could otherwise raise suspicions of tampering. To further enhance security and confidence in the process, if any of the contesting candidates so desires, they must be permitted to post an agent to keep a continuous watch at the location where the voting machines are stored during the period before counting begins. This agent is also allowed to affix their own seals to the doors and windows of the building in which the voting machines have been stored. These candidate-affixed seals are in addition to the official seals that are affixed by the Returning Officer, creating a dual-layer security mechanism. Finally, it must be ensured that immediately after all the voting machines have been received and securely stored, the room is locked forthwith. Once locked, no person is allowed to enter the facility until the morning of the day fixed for the counting of votes, ensuring an unbroken chain of custody and a secure environment throughout the interim period.

In the event that an unavoidable circumstance arises during the designated storage interval necessitating the opening of a secure room where voting materials are kept, strict procedural safeguards must be immediately enacted to preserve the integrity of the electoral process. The Returning Officer, who is the senior official responsible for conducting the election in a specific constituency, is mandated to issue a formal written notice to all contesting candidates or their duly authorized representatives well in advance of any such entry. This requirement ensures transparency and prevents any suspicion of tampering by guaranteeing that political stakeholders are present as witnesses. Once these representatives arrive, the room may only be opened in their direct presence. The moment the specific task requiring access is completed, the officials must allow the candidates or their agents to reapply their personal seals to the door locks and windows, thereby re-establishing the chain of custody under joint supervision. Furthermore, whenever it becomes necessary to enter the repository housing the voting machines, a comprehensive and contemporaneous record must be maintained in an official logbook. This log serves as a legal audit trail and must meticulously document the full names and designations of every individual entering the facility, the precise purpose of their visit, the exact time of entry, the exact time of exit, and must bear the signatures of the security guards on duty to verify the accuracy of the recorded times. These rigorous protocols regarding access control, witness presence, and documentation apply with equal force and necessary modifications to the storage phase occurring between the initial counting of votes and any subsequent recount that may be ordered, ensuring continuous security throughout the entire post-poll period.

To further reinforce the security architecture surrounding electronic voting infrastructure, a joint inspection protocol has been established involving two key oversight figures: the General Observer and the Police Observer. These officials are required to conduct a collaborative visit to the strong rooms specifically earmarked for the storage of polled Electronic Voting Machines, known as EVMs, and Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail units, known as VVPATs, within three days of their arrival in their assigned constituencies. An EVM is an electronic device used to record votes cast by citizens, while a VVPAT is a printer attached to the EVM that generates a physical paper slip showing how the vote was recorded, allowing the voter to verify their choice before it is dropped into a sealed box. The purpose of this joint visit is to physically verify that all logistical and security measures are in place before the sensitive equipment is stored. On the very same day as the inspection, these observers must submit a detailed report via a centralized digital system known as the Observer Portal. This report formally documents the name of the state and district, the number and name of the Assembly Constituency, and the specific address of the strong room visited. The observers must jointly certify that they have inspected the facility and confirm that all necessary arrangements for storing the polled EVMs and VVPATs for the ongoing general or bye-election have been successfully implemented. They must also explicitly state that all instructions issued by the Election Commission regarding the storage of these devices have been fully complied with.

The certification submitted by the observers includes a granular checklist of safety and security parameters that must be evaluated and marked as either compliant or non-compliant, with mandatory remarks provided if any deficiency is found. The first critical factor assessed is whether the strong room possesses sufficient spatial capacity to accommodate the entire volume of polled EVMs and VVPATs without overcrowding, which could hinder movement or monitoring. The structural integrity of the room is then examined to ensure it features a single point of entry and that there are no unsealed windows or ventilators that could serve as potential breach points for unauthorized access. Electrical safety is another paramount concern; the inspection verifies that the main electrical switch for the room is installed outside the perimeter of the strong room. This specific arrangement is designed to prevent anyone inside the room from manipulating the power supply to cause an electric short circuit or to intentionally disrupt the functioning of the stored machines. The locking mechanism is scrutinized to confirm the existence of a double lock system, which typically requires two different keys held by separate authorities to open, thereby eliminating the risk of a single individual gaining unilateral access. Additionally, the observers must verify that arrangements for an uninterrupted power supply are in place at the storage location to ensure that any electronic monitoring systems or environmental controls remain functional at all times.

Continuing the detailed assessment, the observers must certify that the keys to the strong room locks are being custodied in strict accordance with specific Election Commission instructions, referenced by the official file number 51/8/7/2018-EMPS dated November 13, 2018, which outlines the hierarchy and procedure for key management. The presence of modern surveillance technology is mandatory, requiring confirmation that Closed-Circuit Television, or CCTV, cameras have been installed to provide continuous visual monitoring of the strong room interior and exterior. Security personnel deployment is equally critical; the report must confirm that round-the-clock police security has been arranged as per norms prescribed in a separate Commission letter dated August 29, 2018, ensuring that armed guards are present at all times to deter and respond to any threats. Fire safety protocols are checked by verifying the availability of adequate fire extinguishers suitable for electrical fires, and contingency planning is reviewed by confirming the existence of alternate lighting arrangements, such as emergency generators or battery-backed lights, to maintain visibility during power outages. The security framework must also include a two-tier security arrangement operating twenty-four hours a day, creating layered defense perimeters around the stored EVMs and VVPATs. To maintain an accurate record of personnel movement, a dedicated logbook must be provided to the Central Armed Police Forces, or CAPF, deployed at the site, enabling them to record details of every visitor. Furthermore, a video camera must be available to the CAPF to visually record the visits of any permitted officials, adding a secondary layer of audiovisual evidence to the written log.

Transparency toward political parties is ensured by verifying that there is a designated location outside the inner security perimeter where candidate representatives can stay. This area must be positioned to enable these representatives to have a clear, direct view of the strong room's entry point, allowing them to monitor who goes in and out. If the physical layout of the building prevents a direct line of sight, the observers must confirm that alternative CCTV arrangements have been planned and implemented to allow these representatives to watch the main gate of the strong room remotely, ensuring no loss of oversight. Operational command and control are validated by checking whether a round-the-clock control room has been established at the storage center to coordinate security responses and monitor feeds. The democratic right of candidates to participate in the security process is upheld by confirming that all candidates have been formally intimated in writing, inviting them to depute their own representatives to keep watch over the security arrangements after the polling has concluded. Finally, the observers must declare their overall satisfaction with the arrangements; if they are not satisfied, they must detail the specific shortcomings in the remarks section. The report concludes with the signatures, names, unique codes, and constituency allocations of both the Police Observer and the General Observer, serving as a binding legal attestation of the facility's readiness.

At the administrative level, a parallel reporting hierarchy ensures accountability up to the highest levels of the election machinery. The District Election Officers, who are the chief administrators of elections at the district level, are required to submit a formal Certificate in a prescribed format to the Chief Electoral Officer of the state on a weekly basis. This certificate serves as a recurring affirmation that the District Election Officer, along with the Returning Officer and Assistant Returning Officer, are personally visiting the aforementioned strong rooms twice daily, once in the morning and once in the evening. During these visits, they must satisfy themselves that the storage and security arrangements remain intact and effective. The certificate details specific operational checks, including a verification report that lists the date and time of each visit. The officers must explicitly confirm that there is an uninterrupted power supply at the strong room locations and that alternate arrangements, such as backup generators, are functional and ready to take over immediately if the primary supply fails. They must also reaffirm that the strong rooms continue to utilize a double lock system, ensuring that the physical security measures have not degraded over time. Upon receiving these weekly reports from the various District Election Officers, the Chief Electoral Officer is tasked with compiling a consolidated Certificate in the same prescribed format. This consolidated report must be submitted on the very next day following the receipt of the district-level data, ensuring that the Election Commission receives timely, aggregated information regarding the security status of all strong rooms across the state for their kind perusal and official records. This multi-layered system of daily inspections, joint observer certifications, and weekly administrative reporting creates a robust framework designed to eliminate any possibility of tampering with the voting machines and to maintain public confidence in the sanctity of the electoral outcome.

To ensure the utmost integrity and security of electoral materials, a comprehensive and multi-layered security protocol has been established for the strong rooms where voting machines are stored. Two distinct cordoned security arrangements have been implemented to operate continuously around the clock, ensuring that the strong rooms are never left unguarded or vulnerable. This perpetual surveillance is bolstered by a minimum security deployment consisting of one full platoon of security personnel dedicated specifically to the protection of these facilities. A platoon typically represents a significant military or paramilitary unit, usually comprising several dozen trained officers, which provides a robust physical deterrent against any unauthorized access or potential threats. To further enhance transparency and build trust in the electoral process, all candidates contesting the election have been formally notified in writing. This notification instructs them to appoint their own authorized representatives who are empowered to maintain a close and constant watch over the security arrangements surrounding the strong rooms. This measure ensures that political stakeholders have direct oversight, thereby reducing the possibility of malpractice or suspicion regarding the handling of electoral equipment.

In addition to physical guarding, advanced technological surveillance has been integrated into the security framework through the installation of Closed Circuit Television, commonly known as CCTV, systems. These cameras provide real-time visual monitoring of the strong room interiors and perimeters. To make this surveillance accessible to the candidate representatives mentioned earlier, the live feed from the CCTV cameras is displayed on a large television screen situated in a designated area specifically earmarked for these observers. This setup allows the representatives to monitor the activities within and around the strong room continuously without needing to be physically inside the secured zone, thus maintaining the sanctity of the inner security cordon while ensuring full visibility. To maintain an audit trail of who accesses this monitoring capability, a formal logbook has been instituted. Every individual who is permitted or deputed to view the CCTV coverage must have their details recorded in this book, creating a verifiable record of observation. Complementing this visual monitoring is the operation of a dedicated control room located adjacent to the strong room, which functions twenty-four hours a day. This control room serves as the nerve center for coordinating security responses and managing the flow of information and personnel.

Safety measures extend beyond security against human threats to include protection against accidental hazards. Accordingly, adequate arrangements for fire extinguishers have been made both near the entrances and inside the strong rooms themselves. This ensures that in the event of a fire outbreak, immediate suppression capabilities are available to protect the sensitive electronic voting equipment from damage. The hierarchy of responsibility for these security arrangements is clearly defined, with a Gazetted Officer, who is a high-ranking government official authorized to sign official documents and certify proceedings, paired with a senior police officer. Both officials are placed on duty round the clock to jointly monitor and supervise the entire security apparatus of the strong rooms. Their presence adds a layer of administrative and law enforcement accountability. Furthermore, the Central Armed Police Forces, often referred to as CAPF, which are federal paramilitary organizations tasked with internal security, maintain a separate but equally rigorous logbook. In this register, detailed entries are made regarding every visit to the facility, capturing specific data points such as the date, time, duration of the visit, the name of the visitor, and the specific purpose of their presence. To create an immutable visual record of these movements, the CAPF contingent has been equipped with video cameras to record all visits made by individuals, ensuring that every entry and exit is documented both in writing and on video.

The procedures for the storage of electronic voting machines and voter-verified paper audit trails, specifically those that are non-functional and were not used for polling, as well as unused reserve units, follow strict categorization guidelines post-poll. These items are classified into Category C for non-functional un-polled units and Category D for unused reserve units. In the context of elections to the House of the People, which refers to the lower house of the national parliament, the non-functional un-polled machines from all assembly segments within a district may be consolidated and stored at a central district warehouse. Similarly, the un-polled reserve machines from all assembly segments falling under a single parliamentary constituency can be stored in one warehouse, provided they are properly categorized and segregated according to their specific assembly constituency segments. For elections to the State Legislative Assembly, a similar logic applies where non-functional un-polled machines from all assembly constituencies within a respective district are stored at the district warehouse. The unused reserve machines from these constituencies are also stored in a single warehouse with clear categorization by assembly constituency. It is imperative that the deposit of these Category C and Category D machines occurs simultaneously with the deposit of Category A and Category B machines, which include polled and non-functional polled units. This synchronized deposition must take place in the strong room under intimation to the candidates and must be fully videographed to ensure transparency. Candidates are informed in writing well in advance of this schedule so they can depute representatives to witness the storage of these specific categories of machines. The warehouse or strong room housing these units is guarded by a minimum security force equivalent to half a section, which is a smaller tactical unit than a platoon but sufficient for static guard duties.

Specific standard operating procedures have been prescribed by the Election Commission for the dispatch of polling parties on days prior to the poll, known as P-2 and P-3, and their return on the day after the poll, known as P+1. For the dispatch phase, a government building is identified and earmarked at a cluster point to serve as a gathering location for polling parties and their equipment. Returning Officers, who are the officials responsible for conducting the election in a constituency, must inform all contesting candidates in writing about the location of these cluster points. It is explicitly mandated that police stations cannot be used for these purposes to maintain neutrality and avoid the perception of state machinery bias. At these designated government buildings, a specific room is set aside to function as a strong room for storing the electronic voting machines and voter-verified paper audit trails belonging to the polling stations scheduled for dispatch on those days. All such equipment must be kept in this strong room and sealed in the presence of Sector Officers, who supervise groups of polling stations. The entire sealing process is subject to mandatory videography. Contesting candidates are permitted to nominate representatives to observe this procedure if they wish, reinforcing the principle of multi-party oversight.

In scenarios where the strong room at a cluster point involves equipment from more than one assembly segment, physical segregation is required. A sturdy partition must be constructed within the strong room to create separate compartments for each assembly segment. These partitions must be secure enough to support a double-lock system or official seals, ensuring that units from one assembly segment are never mixed with those from another under any circumstances. This separation is critical to prevent cross-contamination of electoral materials between different administrative boundaries. When the electronic voting machines and voter-verified paper audit trails need to be removed from these assembly segment strong rooms for dispatch to polling stations or upon their return, standard precautions apply. The date and time of opening the strong room must be communicated in writing to the candidates or their agents beforehand. All other instructions issued by the Election Commission, particularly those detailed in Chapter 9 of the Commission's Manual on Electronic Voting Machines and Voter-Verified Paper Audit Trails, must be strictly adhered to without deviation. To maintain a chronological record of access, a logbook is maintained specifically for recording every instance of opening and closing the strong room. These same rigorous processes are replicated for the arrival of polling parties on the day after the poll. Throughout the storage of polled electronic voting machines and voter-verified paper audit trails, including those that became non-functional after use, a minimum of half a section of Central Armed Police Forces personnel is deployed to guard the intermediate strong room. For the physical organization of these stored machines, the use of slotted angle racks, which are adjustable metal shelving units, or wooden racks is recommended to ensure orderly and safe stacking of the equipment.

In the administration of elections, strict protocols govern the storage and handling of Electronic Voting Machines, commonly known as EVMs, and Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail machines, referred to as VVPATs. These devices are the core technological infrastructure used to record votes and provide a physical paper record for verification. It is a mandatory requirement that any EVMs or VVPATs which have not been used for voting, including those specifically designated for training purposes or public awareness campaigns, must not be stored in the same secure facility, known as a strong-room, where the machines that have actually recorded votes are kept. This separation also applies to any machines that were replaced before the polling process began and other miscellaneous materials related to the election that do not contain actual vote data. The directive explicitly states that the warehouse or strong-room designated for storing these voting machines must contain only EVMs and VVPATs. No other materials, whether they are unrelated to the election process or are election-related items not specifically prescribed by the Election Commission to be kept with the polled machines, are permitted inside this secure area. This ensures the integrity and security of the voted machines by preventing contamination or confusion with non-essential items. Furthermore, regarding the physical infrastructure used for temporary storage, any building utilized as a temporary strong-room must be restored to its original condition immediately after the election duties are concluded. This restoration includes removing any modifications made for security purposes, such as sealing windows or reinforcing doors, and must be done as soon as possible even if another election is anticipated within the following few months. This rule prevents the permanent alteration of public buildings and ensures they remain available for their primary functions.

Specific Standard Operating Procedures, or SOPs, have been established to manage situations where EVMs or VVPATs are damaged or broken. Such damage may occur due to the actions of miscreants, who are individuals acting with criminal intent to disrupt the process, or due to accidental reasons. These procedures differ based on when the damage occurs relative to the mock poll and the actual polling day. A mock poll is a trial run conducted before the actual voting begins to demonstrate that the machines are functioning correctly and to build confidence among polling agents. If an EVM or VVPAT is damaged after it has been dispersed for this mock poll but before the actual voting starts, the machine must be stored in a strong-room designated for non-functional, unpolled machines. This specific category of storage is often classified as Category C, which holds machines that have never recorded actual voter data and are not working. However, if the damage occurs during the actual polling day or before the counting of votes, the handling procedure depends on whether the vote data can still be retrieved. If the machine contains polled data but is physically damaged, and if the votes can still be retrieved or if the paper slips generated by the VVPAT are intact enough to determine the result during counting, the machine must be kept in the strong-room reserved for polled machines. This room typically houses Category A machines, which are functional polled units, and Category B machines, which are non-functional polled units where the data remains recoverable. Conversely, if the polled votes cannot be retrieved because the internal memory is corrupted or if the VVPAT paper slips are destroyed or not intact, the damaged machine must be moved to the strong-room holding unpolled, non-functional machines, categorized as Category C. This distinction is critical because it separates machines that still hold valid electoral mandates from those that have lost their data integrity.

Upon identifying any damaged or broken machines, the District Election Officer, who is the senior administrative official responsible for conducting elections in a district, must immediately mark the status of these machines as damaged within the EVM Management System. This digital tracking system ensures a transparent audit trail of every machine's status throughout the election cycle. In all instances involving damage caused by malicious acts or suspicious circumstances, it is mandatory to file a First Information Report, or FIR, with the local police. An FIR is the initial document recorded by law enforcement upon receiving information about a cognizable offense. This report must cite relevant sections of the Representation of the People Act, 1951, which is the primary legislation governing the conduct of elections in the country. Specifically, the FIR should reference Section 129, which prohibits election officers from acting on behalf of candidates or influencing voting behavior; Section 132, which prescribes penalties for misconduct at polling stations; Section 134B, which forbids individuals from going armed near polling stations; Section 135, which addresses the illegal removal of ballot papers; Section 135A, which deals with the serious offense of booth capturing where voters are intimidated or prevented from voting; and Section 136, which covers other offenses and their corresponding penalties. In addition to these specific election laws, the authorities must also consider applicable sections of the Indian Penal Code, or IPC, which is the main criminal code of the country. Relevant IPC sections include Section 171C regarding undue influence at elections, Section 506 for punishment related to criminal intimidation, and Section 425 which defines and penalizes mischief or willful damage to property. A detailed report accompanied by a copy of the filed FIR must be submitted to the Election Commission without delay. The Chief Electoral Officer of the state or union territory is then responsible for following up on the FIR with the concerned Police Department to ensure a thorough investigation.

The legal framework further restricts the inspection of these damaged machines to protect the secrecy and sanctity of the vote. For machines damaged during the actual poll where data might still be retrievable, inspection is permitted only upon a specific order from a competent court. This restriction is based on Rule 93, subsection 1A, of the Conduct of Elections Rules, 1961. This rule stipulates that control units of EVMs, which are sealed under Rule 57C and kept in the custody of the District Election Officer, shall not be opened, inspected, or produced before any person or authority unless ordered by a court of law. This judicial oversight prevents tampering and ensures that any examination of the machine is conducted under strict legal supervision. However, for machines damaged during the mock poll or those damaged during the actual poll where the data is completely unrecoverable, these units may be provided for investigation purposes if required by law enforcement agencies. Even in such cases, the physical custody of the machine must remain with the District Election Officer to maintain the chain of custody and prevent unauthorized access.

Strict guidelines also apply to the safe custody of specific election materials such as metal seals and rubber stamps. The metal seal used by the Presiding Officer, who is the officer in charge of a specific polling station, and the arrow cross mark rubber stamp used for marking tendered ballot papers, which are special ballots issued to voters whose names are already marked as voted due to impersonation attempts, must be handled with care. According to instructions issued by the Commission, the Presiding Officer must place these items inside separate small envelopes first. The outside of these small envelopes must clearly display the names of the articles contained within. These small envelopes are then placed inside a second, larger envelope that contains other non-statutory items, which are materials not mandated by law to be sent directly for counting but are necessary for administrative records. The Returning Officer, who oversees multiple polling stations within a constituency, is responsible for collecting these packets containing the seals and stamps. They must arrange for these items to be delivered to the District Election Officer for safe custody as soon as it is practically possible after the polling concludes. These articles, once returned by the Presiding Officers and duly accounted for in the records, can be reused in any subsequent election provided they are still in serviceable condition. This practice promotes resource efficiency while maintaining security. Finally, regarding the paper slips generated by the VVPAT during the mock poll, these are placed in sealed black envelopes. These specific envelopes must be stored in the strong-room alongside the Category A and Category B machines, meaning they are kept with the machines that recorded actual votes or non-functional machines with recoverable data. This ensures that all records related to the voting process, including trial runs, are secured in the highest level of containment available.

When specific allegations of a serious nature are formally submitted in writing against the conduct of a Presiding Officer or any member of the polling staff during the course of an election, a strict procedural protocol must be followed to ensure the integrity of the democratic process. The Returning Officer, who serves as the primary administrator for the constituency, is legally obligated to initiate an inquiry into such matters as soon as it is practically feasible to do so. This immediate investigation is crucial to determine the validity of the claims and to preserve evidence while the events are still fresh. Upon completing this inquiry, the Returning Officer must compile all relevant documents, including the written complaint, witness statements if any, and a comprehensive report detailing the findings, and forward this entire dossier to both the Chief Electoral Officer of the state and the central Election Commission. These higher authorities rely on this information to make final determinations regarding disciplinary action or legal proceedings. However, the procedure differs significantly when the complaint received is anonymous, meaning it lacks the signature or identity of the accuser. In such instances, the Returning Officer is granted discretionary power to evaluate the merit of the allegation. The officer must carefully assess whether the content of the anonymous complaint suggests a genuine threat to the election's fairness or if it appears to be a frivolous attempt to disrupt proceedings. Based on this judgment, the officer decides whether to launch a formal inquiry or to dismiss the matter without further action, thereby balancing the need for accountability with the prevention of malicious harassment of election officials.

On the actual day of polling, the physical presence and accessibility of the Returning Officer or the Assistant Returning Officer are of paramount importance for the smooth management of the election. These senior officials are required to be stationed at a specific, predetermined location within the constituency or, if logistical constraints exist, at a place as geographically close to the constituency as possible. This fixed headquarters serves as the central command post for the day's operations. It is imperative that a clear communication channel be established and maintained so that key stakeholders, including the Election Commission, the Chief Electoral Officer, appointed Observers who monitor the fairness of the poll, contesting candidates, Presiding Officers in charge of individual polling booths, and zonal officers supervising clusters of booths, are fully aware of exactly where the Returning Officer can be reached. This contact information must include both landline telephone numbers and mobile phone details to ensure redundancy and reliability. The purpose of this constant availability is to allow for the rapid transmission of instructions, the resolution of emergent crises, and the coordination of resources whenever necessary, ensuring that any disruption to the voting process can be addressed immediately by the highest authority present in the field.

The legal framework governing elections has undergone significant evolution regarding the tragic circumstance of a candidate dying before the polling process is completed. Historically, the law provided for the countermanding, or complete cancellation, of an election upon the death of any candidate. However, the Representation of the People Act of 1951 was substantially amended by the Representation of the People Amendment Act of 1996, which narrowed the scope of this provision to prevent unnecessary delays in the democratic schedule. Under the current amended Section 52 of the Act, the adjournment or postponement of a poll is mandated only in very specific scenarios involving the death of a candidate who was officially set up by a recognized political party. The first scenario occurs if the candidate passes away at any time after eleven o'clock in the morning on the final date designated for filing nominations, provided that their nomination paper has subsequently been found valid during the scrutiny process conducted under Section 36 of the Act. The second scenario applies if the candidate's nomination was validated during scrutiny, the candidate did not voluntarily withdraw their candidature under Section 37, and they subsequently die, with the critical condition that a formal report of this death is received by the authorities before the official list of contesting candidates is published under Section 38. The third scenario covers situations where the individual dies while already listed as a contesting candidate, provided the report of death reaches the authorities before the actual commencement of the polling process.

Once the Returning Officer is satisfied and has verified the fact of the candidate's death through appropriate documentation or official reports, they are empowered and required to issue an order adjourning the poll. This order suspends the voting process indefinitely until a new date is notified by the Election Commission. Simultaneously, the Returning Officer must immediately report this development to the Election Commission and to the appropriate governmental authority. The specific authority depends on the type of election being held; for elections to the House of the People, which is the lower house of the national parliament, the report must be sent to the Government of India, specifically the Ministry of Law, Justice and Company Affairs. Conversely, for elections to the Legislative Assembly of a state, the report must be directed to the respective State Government. It is important to note a procedural nuance regarding the first scenario mentioned earlier, where death occurs after the nomination deadline but before scrutiny is fully complete. In such cases, the order to adjourn the poll cannot be issued until the scrutiny of all nomination papers, including that of the deceased candidate, has been fully concluded. This ensures that the decision to postpone is based on a confirmed valid nomination.

A critical determinant in whether an election is adjourned is the status of the political party fielding the deceased candidate. For the purposes of Section 52, a candidate is deemed to have been set up by a recognized political party only if all requirements outlined in paragraph 13 of the Election Symbols Reservation and Allotment Order of 1968 have been strictly fulfilled by the time of the candidate's death. These requirements typically involve the submission of specific intimation forms, known as Form A and Form B, by the political party to the election authorities, officially declaring the candidate as their representative. If the political party fails to submit these prescribed forms by the time the candidate dies, the individual is not considered to have been set up by a recognized party for the purpose of adjourning the poll. In such instances, any contention or argument by the party that they intended to furnish the required intimation by the statutory deadline, which is usually three o'clock in the afternoon on the last day of nominations, will not be accepted by the authorities. The law demands actual compliance rather than promised compliance. Furthermore, the definition of a recognized political party is geographically specific. For the application of Section 52, a recognized party refers either to a party recognized as a National party across the country or a party recognized as a State party within the specific state where the election is taking place. A political party that holds recognition as a State party in one state does not automatically enjoy that status in other states. Even if such a party has been granted concessions under paragraph 10 of the Symbols Order to use its distinctive symbol in other states where it is not recognized, it is treated merely as a registered but unrecognized party in those other jurisdictions. Consequently, if a candidate set up by such a party dies in a state where the party lacks specific state-level recognition, the poll will not be adjourned, and the election will proceed as scheduled.

Upon receiving the official report from the Returning Officer confirming the death of a candidate who was validly set up by a recognized political party, the Election Commission initiates the process to fill the vacancy. The Commission will formally call upon the concerned political party to nominate a replacement candidate to contest the election in place of the deceased individual. This step is designed to maintain the competitive balance and ensure that the electorate still has the opportunity to vote for the preferred party. The political party is legally bound to make this new nomination within a strict timeframe of seven days from the date the Election Commission issues the notice requesting the substitution. This limited window ensures that the electoral timeline is not unduly extended while providing sufficient time for the party to select a new contender and complete the necessary paperwork. The entire process, from the verification of death to the nomination of a substitute, is tightly regulated to uphold the sanctity of the election while accommodating unforeseen tragedies, ensuring that the democratic mandate is neither compromised by procedural rigidity nor delayed without just cause.

When a candidate passes away during the election process, the nomination of a replacement candidate must be carried out using the specific official documents designated for this purpose, which are known as form 2A or form 2B depending on the particular circumstances of the case. This new nominee is also required to submit a fresh security deposit, which is a sum of money paid to ensure the seriousness of the candidacy and prevent frivolous nominations. The submission of this nomination form cannot stand alone; it must be accompanied by an official communication from the political party involved, specifically using Form A and Form B as outlined in paragraph 13 of the Election Symbols Reservation and Allotment Order of 1968, a regulation that governs how political parties are assigned their identifying symbols on the ballot. Beyond these specific requirements for replacing a deceased candidate, all other standard legal procedures regarding the initial nomination, the detailed scrutiny or verification of the nomination papers by officials, and the potential withdrawal of candidature must be strictly followed for this new individual just as they would be for any original candidate. It is important to note that a person who had previously submitted a formal notice to withdraw their own candidacy under subsection one of section 37 of the Representation of the People Act of 1951, provided this was done before the polling was adjourned, retains the eligibility to be nominated again to fill the vacancy left by the deceased candidate. If the official list of candidates contesting the election had already been made public under section 38 prior to the adjournment of the poll, the Returning Officer, who is the senior official responsible for conducting the election in that constituency, must prepare and publish a completely new list of contesting candidates that includes the name of the newly nominated individual replacing the deceased one. Following these administrative updates, the subsequent stages of the election process will proceed according to a revised timetable that is officially announced by the Election Commission.

The legal framework also provides specific provisions for the adjournment of polling, which refers to the temporary suspension or postponement of voting activities at a specific location. Under section 57 subsection one of the Representation of the People Act of 1951, the Presiding Officer, who is the official in charge of a specific polling station on election day, is granted the authority to halt the voting process under several critical conditions. These conditions include the occurrence of a natural calamity such as a flood, heavy snowfall, a severe storm, or similar disastrous weather events that make voting unsafe or impossible. Another valid reason for adjournment is the non-receipt, loss, or physical damage to essential polling materials, which includes Electronic Voting Machines commonly known as EVMs, traditional ballot boxes, paper ballot papers, or the authentic copy of the electoral roll that lists eligible voters. Furthermore, if there is a significant disturbance of peace at the polling station, such as violence or rioting, that renders it impossible to conduct the poll safely, the officer may adjourn the proceedings. Adjournment is also permitted if the polling party, consisting of the staff and officials needed to run the station, fails to arrive at the location due to obstructions on the travel route or any other serious difficulty, as well as for any other cause deemed sufficient by the officer. Whenever a poll is adjourned at any polling station for these reasons, the Presiding Officer is legally obligated to immediately report the incident to the Returning Officer, who must then without delay inform the Election Commission to seek further directions on how to proceed. It is also noted that for cases involving the use of EVMs and Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail units known as VVPATs during a re-poll, additional guidance can be found in item 12.6 of chapter 12 of the relevant manual.

To properly complete a poll that has been adjourned, the Returning Officer must follow a strict and sequential procedure to ensure legality and transparency. The first step requires the Returning Officer to immediately report the specific circumstances of the adjournment to the Election Commission, the Chief Electoral Officer of the state or union territory, and the appropriate authority. The term appropriate authority is defined in section 2 of the Representation of the People Act of 1951 to mean the Central Government when the election is for either House of Parliament, and the State Government when the election is for the Legislative Assembly or Legislative Council of a state. It is a mandatory legal obligation placed upon the Returning Officer to communicate these details to both the appropriate authority and the Election Commission. Following this report, the Returning Officer must seek formal approval from the Election Commission regarding the proposed date, the specific hours of operation, and the location for holding the resumed or adjourned poll. Once this approval is granted, the officer must formally fix the date, time, and polling station as authorized. The next step involves informing all contesting candidates or their appointed election agents in writing about these fixed arrangements, but this communication can only take place after the formal approval from the Commission has been received. To ensure public awareness, the Returning Officer must affix a visible notice on their official notice board detailing the fixed date, hours, and polling station. Additionally, an announcement must be made in the concerned polling area, traditionally done by the beat of a drum or through other effective means, to inform the general electorate of the new schedule. For the sake of convenience, uniformity, and completeness in reporting to the Commission under section 57 subsection 2, a specific format for such reports has been prescribed in Annexure 41. The Returning Officer is required to send a single consolidated report using this format covering all polling stations where the poll was adjourned, as the Commission will not accept partial or piece-meal reports for these incidents. When suggesting the hours for the adjourned poll, the Returning Officer may propose any duration sufficient to complete the voting process for the remaining voters. It is crucial to understand that section 57 subsection 2 operates independently of section 56, meaning that when fixing the hours for the adjourned poll, it is not necessary to deduct the number of hours during which voting had already taken place before the adjournment occurred.

When the poll resumes at a station where it was previously adjourned under the provisions of subsection one of section 57, the voting process will recommence exactly from the stage at which it was interrupted. This means that only those electors or eligible voters who had not already cast their votes before the poll was adjourned will be permitted to vote during the adjourned session; those who voted prior to the stoppage cannot vote again. To facilitate this continuation, the Returning Officer must provide the Presiding Officer of the affected polling station with specific sealed packets containing the marked copy of the electoral roll and the Register of Voters in Form 17A, which serves as the official record of who has voted. Furthermore, the officer must supply a new Electronic Voting Machine and a new Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail unit to ensure that the voting equipment is secure and untampered for the resumed polling, thereby maintaining the integrity of the election process from the point of interruption to its final conclusion.

Before the actual voting process begins at any polling station, a specific and critical protocol must be followed regarding the documentation required to verify voter eligibility. The Presiding Officer, who is the official in charge of the polling station, is responsible for reopening sealed packets that contain the marked copy of the electoral roll and the Register of Voters. This action cannot be done in private; it must take place in the presence of the candidates contesting the election or their authorized agents if they are present at the location. This transparency ensures that all parties trust the integrity of the voter list being used. It is imperative that this specific marked copy, which has been officially opened and verified, is the exact same document utilized if the poll needs to be resumed at a later time, known as an adjourned poll. An adjourned poll occurs when voting starts but is paused and continued on a different day or time. To ensure consistency and legal compliance, the provisions outlined in Rules 28 and 49A through 49V of the Conduct of Election Rules, 1961, which govern the standard voting process, apply with full force to these adjourned polls just as they did to the original session before it was interrupted. Furthermore, there are scenarios where voting cannot even begin at the scheduled time. If the polling party, which includes the officials and equipment necessary to run the election, fails to arrive, or if other unforeseen circumstances prevent the start of the process, the same set of rules mentioned above will govern the rescheduled or adjourned poll. Additionally, if the poll cannot commence within two hours of its scheduled start time due to a malfunction of the Electronic Voting Machine, commonly referred to as an EVM, or any other significant reason, the situation must be escalated, and a recommendation for an adjourned poll should be sent to the Election Commission, the supreme body overseeing the election.

When circumstances arise that completely invalidate the voting process at a specific location, a procedure for holding a fresh poll is initiated under Section 58 of the Representation of the People Act, 1951. This legislation empowers the Election Commission to declare the poll at a particular polling station void, meaning the results from that station are cancelled and cannot be counted. This drastic measure is taken only under specific conditions involving the voting equipment or procedural integrity. One such condition is if an Electronic Voting Machine or a Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail unit, which is a device that prints a paper slip allowing voters to verify their vote was recorded correctly, is unlawfully removed from the polling station by someone who does not have the authority to do so. Another ground for voiding a poll is if any EVM or VVPAT unit is accidentally or intentionally destroyed, lost, damaged, or tampered with, provided that the Returning Officer, the senior official responsible for the election in that constituency, is satisfied that the true result of the poll at that station can no longer be determined because of this incident. A fresh poll is also mandated if any EVM or VVPAT suffers a mechanical failure while votes are being recorded, rendering the machine unable to function properly. Finally, if any error or irregularity in the election procedure occurs that is serious enough to vitiate, or corrupt, the entire poll, the Commission may step in. Upon identifying any of these issues, the Returning Officer must immediately report the facts to both the Election Commission and the Chief Electoral Officer, who is the top election official in the state or union territory. To ensure that these reports are convenient to process, uniform in structure, and complete in detail, the Election Commission has established a specific format for such reports, designated as Annexure 42. The Returning Officer is required to send a single consolidated report using this format covering all polling stations where the poll has been compromised, rather than sending separate, piecemeal reports for each incident, as the Commission will not accept fragmented submissions. After reviewing all material circumstances presented in the report, the Commission may, if deemed necessary, officially declare the poll at the affected polling station void and formally fix a new date and specific hours for a fresh poll to be conducted.

Once the Election Commission makes the decision to hold a fresh poll, the Returning Officer receives an intimation and must immediately act to inform all relevant parties. The Returning Officer is required to send a written notification to the contesting candidates or their election agents, clearly stating the new date, time, and place fixed for the fresh poll. In addition to direct communication, a public notice detailing this information must be affixed to the official notice board of the Returning Officer. To ensure that every voter in the area is aware of the change, the Returning Officer must also make a public announcement in the concerned polling area, traditionally done by the beat of a drum or through other effective means of mass communication available locally. It is a fundamental right that all electors who were entitled to vote at the original poll at the questioned station or stations are equally entitled to cast their votes in the fresh poll, regardless of whether they voted previously. A crucial logistical detail involves the use of indelible ink, which is a special ink applied to a voter's finger to indicate they have already voted and to prevent double voting. For the fresh poll, any marks of indelible ink made during the original, now-voided poll must be ignored. To clearly distinguish the votes cast in the fresh poll from those attempted in the original session, the Election Commission directs that the indelible ink mark for the fresh poll must be applied specifically to the voter's left middle finger. Furthermore, entirely new equipment and documents must be used; this includes a new Electronic Voting Machine, a new Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail unit, and a new marked copy of the electoral roll. The legal framework governing this fresh poll remains identical to the original, meaning all provisions of the Representation of the People Act, 1951, the Conduct of Elections Rules, 1961, and any orders issued under them apply to the fresh poll in exactly the same manner as they did to the first attempt. Finally, strict preservation protocols must be followed for the equipment used in the original poll at stations where a fresh poll has been ordered; all such EVMs and VVPATs must be carefully preserved, kept unopened, and maintained with their security seals intact to serve as evidence if needed.

In cases involving severe misconduct known as booth capturing, a distinct but related procedure is triggered under Section 58A of the law. Booth capturing is a serious offense defined in Section 135A, generally referring to situations where unauthorized persons seize control of a polling station, intimidate voters, or forcibly take over the voting process. If booth capturing occurs at a polling station to such an extent that the actual result of the poll at that station cannot be accurately ascertained, the Returning Officer has a duty to report the matter to the Election Commission immediately without delay. Upon receiving this report, the Election Commission will analyze all material circumstances surrounding the incident. Based on this assessment, the Commission has two primary courses of action. First, it may declare the poll at that specific polling station void and issue a directive for a fresh poll to be held on a date appointed by the Commission, allowing the legitimate voters to cast their ballots in a secure environment. Second, if the Commission is satisfied that booth capturing has occurred across a large number of polling stations to such a degree that the overall result of the entire election is likely to be affected or skewed, it possesses the authority to countermand the election entirely. Countermanding an election means cancelling the election process for the whole constituency or region, necessitating a completely new election cycle rather than just a re-poll at specific stations. This power ensures that the democratic outcome reflects the true will of the electorate and is not compromised by widespread violence or fraud.

To enable the Election Commission to make informed and appropriate decisions in situations where booth capturing has occurred at any polling station, a specific reporting protocol has been established. Booth capturing refers to the illegal seizure of a polling station by individuals or groups who intimidate voters, staff, or officials to manipulate the voting process. In such critical scenarios, the Returning Officer, who is the senior official responsible for conducting the election in a particular constituency, is required to submit a report to the Commission using a prescribed format. This standardized format, which ensures consistency and completeness in reporting, is provided in Annexure 43 of the relevant election manual. It is imperative that this report be consolidated, meaning it must cover all polling stations within the constituency that have been affected by booth capturing in a single, comprehensive document. The Commission will strictly reject any reports that are submitted in a piece-meal fashion, where information is sent gradually or separately for different locations, as this hinders the ability to assess the full scale of the disruption and make a unified decision. If, after reviewing the consolidated report and other available evidence, the Commission directs that a fresh poll be conducted at any specific polling station, the Returning Officer must immediately proceed to organize and execute this new voting process. This action must be carried out in strict accordance with the detailed instructions outlined in the preceding regulatory paragraphs, ensuring that the re-polling is secure, fair, and adheres to all procedural safeguards. In more severe cases where the integrity of the entire election in a constituency is compromised due to widespread booth capturing, the Commission possesses the authority to countermand the election. Countermanding an election means officially canceling the ongoing electoral process for that area. When such a drastic measure is taken, all proceedings related to that specific election are deemed null and void, effectively resetting the entire process. Consequently, a fresh notification calling for a new election will be issued in due course, initiating a completely new cycle of nominations, campaigning, and voting to ensure that the will of the electorate can be expressed without coercion or fraud.

The handling of Electronic Voting Machines, commonly known as EVMs, during incidents of booth capturing is governed by specific legal provisions, notably Rule 49X of the Conduct of Election Rules, 1961. An EVM typically consists of two main units: the Control Unit, which is held by the Presiding Officer to authorize the recording of votes, and the Ballot Unit, which is placed inside the voting compartment where the voter casts their vote. Under Rule 49X, if the Presiding Officer, who is the officer in charge of a specific polling station, forms the opinion that booth capturing is actively taking place, they are empowered and obligated to take immediate defensive action. The first step is to immediately close the Control Unit of the EVM. Closing the Control Unit effectively freezes the machine, ensuring that no further votes can be recorded or tampered with while the incident is unfolding. Following this, the Presiding Officer must physically detach the Ballot Unit or Units from the Control Unit. This separation is a crucial security measure designed to preserve the sanctity of the votes already cast and to prevent any electronic manipulation of the data stored within the machine. As soon as reasonably possible after closing the EVM under the authority of Rule 49X, the Presiding Officer must prepare a detailed report containing all factual circumstances surrounding the incident and submit it to the Returning Officer. Upon receiving this initial report, the Returning Officer has the duty to relay the full facts of the case to the Election Commission using the fastest means of communication available, such as dedicated secure lines, radio, or encrypted digital channels, to ensure minimal delay in decision-making.

Once the Election Commission receives the report from the Returning Officer regarding the closure of an EVM due to booth capturing, it will carefully evaluate the situation by taking into account all material circumstances, including the severity of the incident, the number of voters affected, and the evidence provided. Based on this assessment, the Commission has two primary courses of action. First, if the Commission is satisfied that the polling process up to the point of adjournment was not vitiated, meaning the votes cast before the incident were free from corruption or influence, it may decide to have the adjourned poll completed. In this scenario, the voting process resumes from the exact stage where it was interrupted. To facilitate this, a new EVM and a Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail, or VVPAT, device will be provided. The VVPAT is a printer attached to the EVM that generates a paper slip for each vote cast, allowing the voter to verify that their vote was recorded correctly before it drops into a sealed box. Alternatively, if the Commission is satisfied that the poll was vitiated, implying that the integrity of the voting process was fundamentally compromised even before the machine was closed, it will declare the poll at that specific polling station as void. A void poll is one that is legally invalid and cannot be counted. In such cases, the Commission will direct that a fresh poll be conducted at that polling station. Regardless of whether the decision is to complete an adjourned poll or conduct a entirely fresh poll, the subsequent steps must be executed in strict adherence to the procedures prescribed in paragraphs 13.58.1 through 13.59.7 of the election manual, which detail the logistical and security arrangements for such exceptional voting events.

Maintaining a continuous flow of information between the field officials and the central authority is vital for the smooth conduct of elections, and therefore, the Commission mandates that it be kept fully informed of the progress of the poll throughout the polling day. To achieve this, specific instructions require each Returning Officer to submit three comprehensive reports that provide details of every significant event occurring within their constituency. These reports must follow a standardized format found in Annexure 29, ensuring that the data received is uniform and easily analyzable. The timing of these reports is critical and strictly enforced. The first report must reach the Commission by 1300 hours, or 1:00 PM, on the day of the poll, providing an mid-day update on turnout and any early incidents. The second report is required by 1900 hours, or 7:00 PM, immediately after the close of polling activities at all stations, summarizing the final turnout and any issues encountered during the day. The third and final report must be submitted by 0700 hours, or 7:00 AM, on the morning immediately following the polling day, offering a conclusive overview of the sealing of machines and the transport of materials. These reports must be transmitted via the quickest available means of communication to ensure real-time situational awareness for the Commission. It is explicitly stated that failure to send any of these reports within the stipulated timeframes will be viewed by the Commission as a gross failure of statutory duty on the part of the concerned Returning Officer. Such negligence is treated with extreme seriousness and can lead to severe disciplinary consequences, including potential removal from office or legal action, as the timeliness of data is essential for national-level decision-making.

The submission of these timely reports is not merely an administrative formality but a prerequisite for the next major phase of the election: the counting of votes. It is only after the Election Commission has received and reviewed these mandatory reports that it will decide whether to allow the counting of votes to commence in each individual constituency. While current regulations do not make it mandatory for a Returning Officer to obtain prior explicit approval from the Commission before starting the count, the practical reality is that the failure to furnish the required reports can and likely will lead to the postponement of the counting process in that constituency. If the Commission does not have the necessary data to verify that the polling was conducted fairly and that all security protocols were followed, it cannot responsibly authorize the opening of the EVMs and the tabulation of results. Therefore, the Returning Officer must ensure that all reports reach the Commission well in advance of the scheduled counting time to enable the Commission to make a prompt and informed decision regarding the initiation of the count in their specific jurisdiction. This mechanism serves as a critical check and balance, ensuring that no constituency moves to the result-declaration stage without the central oversight body having a complete picture of the polling day events.

A rigorous process of scrutiny regarding Form 17A and other associated documents is established to safeguard the integrity of the electoral outcome. Form 17A is a critical statutory document maintained at each polling station that records the total number of voters who actually cast their votes, serving as a primary audit trail to cross-verify the counts shown on the EVMs. The purpose of scrutinizing Form 17A along with other documents created during the poll process is to effectively and thoroughly identify any instances where the poll may have been vitiated at any polling station. Vitiation in this context refers to the corruption or invalidation of the election process due to malpractice, errors, or fraud. This exercise of document verification is not to be done casually; it shall be carried out under the close and direct supervision of Observers and Returning Officers. Observers are independent officials appointed to monitor the election process and ensure fairness, while the Returning Officer holds the executive responsibility. The Commission desires to ensure that even subtle and concealed electoral malpractices, which might not be immediately obvious during the voting process, are detected through this meticulous document scrutiny process. By comparing the entries in Form 17A with the machine counts and other logs, discrepancies that indicate tampering or irregularities can be uncovered before the final results are declared.

To ensure transparency and build trust in the scrutiny process, there are specific provisions regarding the participation of political stakeholders. All contesting candidates, meaning every individual who is officially running for office in the election, shall be given an advance notice in writing. This notice must be delivered under proper acknowledgement, which means there must be proof that the candidate or their team received the notification. The purpose of this notice is to ensure that the candidates, or their designated election agents and authorized representatives, remain present during the scrutiny proceedings. Their presence acts as a additional layer of oversight, preventing any unilateral actions by election officials. Furthermore, candidates are granted the flexibility to authorize as many representatives as there are places fixed for scrutiny by the Returning Officer and the Observer. This means that if the scrutiny is happening in multiple rooms or locations simultaneously, a candidate can deploy a separate trusted representative to each location to watch the proceedings closely. This inclusive approach ensures that the scrutiny of critical documents like Form 17A is conducted in an open, observable, and contested environment, minimizing the risk of post-election disputes and reinforcing the credibility of the electoral administration.

When an official notice is issued regarding the scrutiny of election documents, it must explicitly state that presence during these proceedings is restricted to specific individuals to ensure order and integrity. The notice shall mention that either the candidate personally, their designated election agent, or a single representative who has been duly authorized by the candidate in writing may be present. The role of these individuals is strictly limited to observation; they are there only to watch the proceedings of the scrutiny and are not permitted to interfere, participate in the counting, or alter any documents. This limitation ensures that the verification process remains unbiased while still providing transparency to the contesting parties. The documents subject to this detailed examination include a comprehensive set of records maintained throughout the polling process. The Observer and the Returning Officer, who is the official responsible for conducting the election in a constituency, must scrutinize these materials in the presence of the contesting candidates or their agents. The primary documents include the Presiding Officers' diary, which serves as a chronological log of all events, incidents, and administrative actions that occurred at the polling station under the supervision of the Presiding Officer. Another critical document is Form 17-A, also known as the Register of Voters, which contains the official list of eligible voters who cast their ballots at that specific location. Beyond these core documents, the scrutiny extends to other related records which include but are not restricted to reports filed by Micro Observers, who are officials deployed to monitor specific booths, visitor sheets that log the entry of authorized personnel, and reports submitted by zonal magistrates or sector officers who oversee larger geographic areas of the election. The review also encompasses the final report of the Presiding Officer, still photography taken during the poll, videography recordings, closed-circuit television footage, and webcasting footages that provide visual evidence of the conduct inside and outside the polling booth. Furthermore, there is a specific provision regarding vulnerable voters. If the number of blind or infirm voters who cast their votes at a particular polling station exceeds one percent of the total number of electors registered at that station, as defined under Rule 40 of the Conduct of Election Rules 1961, then a special scrutiny of Form 14-A is mandatory. Form 14-A is the specific list that records the identities of blind and infirm voters who required assistance, and this extra layer of review ensures that assistance was provided correctly and without coercion.

To facilitate the efficient identification of polling stations where such detailed scrutiny of Form 17-A and associated documents is required, a systematic procedure for information collection is established at the Receiving Center. The Returning Officer is tasked with collecting specific data for each polling station using a prescribed format, often referred to as Table-I, at the precise moment when polled Electronic Voting Machines and Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail machines, along with other election documents, are received after the completion of voting. This immediate collection of data allows authorities to flag stations that need further review before the materials are stored. Once received, the polled Electronic Voting Machines and Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail units, together with envelopes containing the original Form 17-C, which is the account of votes recorded, and the slips generated during the mock poll, must be secured in a facility designated as the Polled Strong Room. This storage must adhere to existing strict security instructions to prevent tampering. However, the documents required for scrutiny, which vary from station to station, must be stored separately in a different strong room distinct from the one housing the voting machines. This separation is crucial for logistical convenience and security; by keeping the paperwork in a clearly demarcated area apart from the heavy electronic equipment, election officials can retrieve specific files for scrutiny without causing confusion or compromising the security of the voting machines. The physical separation ensures that the flow of personnel accessing paper records does not intersect unnecessarily with the high-security zone containing the actual vote-counting devices.

The protocol for opening and closing the strong rooms that contain Form 17-A and other sensitive documents is rigorous and designed to maintain a chain of custody that is transparent and verifiable.
