The rationale is to prevent the perception of state power being leveraged for campaign purposes. Furthermore, such individuals are prohibited from entering the counting hall entirely, regardless of whether they are accompanied by security personnel or not, ensuring that the counting environment remains free from any potential intimidation or influence associated with high-profile security details.

Regarding the number of agents a candidate may deploy, the rules are generous to ensure comprehensive monitoring. Each candidate is permitted to appoint as many counting agents as there are counting tables established for their constituency. This includes a specific allowance for an agent to be present at the table designated for the counting of postal ballots, which are votes cast by service voters, absentee voters, and others unable to vote in person. In addition to these table-specific agents, a candidate has the right to appoint one additional counting agent whose sole purpose is to watch the counting process taking place at the Returning Officer's table. This extra agent serves as a representative for the candidate or their election agent if they are unable to be personally present at the Returning Officer's station, ensuring that the final consolidation and declaration stages are also witnessed. The formal appointment of a Counting Agent is a regulated administrative process that must be executed using Form 18, which is appended to the Conduct of Elections Rules, 1961. The appointment must be made either by the candidate personally or by their authorized election agent. It is explicitly stated that any request for appointment submitted on plain paper by a candidate or election agent will not be accepted by the Returning Officer, emphasizing the necessity of adhering to the prescribed statutory format. The name and address of the proposed counting agent must be accurately filled into Form 18, and this document requires the personal signature of the candidate or their election agent to validate the nomination. Crucially, the Counting Agent themselves must also sign this form as a token of their acceptance of the appointment and their willingness to abide by the rules of conduct.

The procedural logistics for submitting these appointments involve the preparation of two copies of the completed Form 18, along with photographs of the appointed agents. The timeline for submission is strict to allow sufficient time for verification and credential issuance. One copy of the form, accompanied by the photograph, must be forwarded by the candidate or their election agent to the Returning Officer no later than 5:00 PM on the third day prior to the date fixed for the counting of votes. This deadline ensures that the election authorities have adequate time to process the applications before the counting day. The second copy of the form is retained by the Counting Agent, who must produce it before the Returning Officer on the actual day of counting as proof of their valid appointment. Upon receiving the duly filled forms and photographs, the Returning Officer is responsible for preparing photo identity cards for the Counting Agents. These identity cards are issued under the official seal and signature of the Returning Officer, serving as the primary authorization for entry into the restricted counting areas. These cards are then issued to the contesting candidate or their election agent, who must provide an acknowledgement of receipt. To streamline the process for candidates with multiple agents, the regulations permit a candidate to appoint all of their counting agents through a single letter of appointment using Form 18, rather than requiring separate forms for each individual, thus reducing administrative burden while maintaining the integrity of the appointment record.

In the intricate administrative framework governing the conduct of elections, specific procedural mandates are established to ensure the integrity, transparency, and security of the vote counting process. When an individual is designated as a counting agent, a formal acceptance protocol must be followed wherein all such agents are required to sign Form 18. This signature serves as a tangible token indicating that the individual has officially accepted the appointment and understands the responsibilities associated with the role. On the actual day designated for counting votes, these counting agents must present themselves before the Returning Officer, who is the senior election official responsible for overseeing the entire counting operation in a particular constituency. At this stage, the agents are obligated to produce two critical documents: a photo identity card that has been specifically issued by the Returning Officer to verify their identity, and the second copy of the aforementioned Form 18. Furthermore, they must sign a declaration contained within this form in the physical presence of the Returning Officer. This entire verification and signing process must be completed strictly within a defined timeframe, specifically not later than one hour before the scheduled time fixed for the commencement of counting. The regulations are rigid regarding this deadline; the Returning Officer is explicitly prohibited from accepting any appointment letter or documentation that is submitted after this cutoff time. To ensure all participants are aware of these strict requirements, the Returning Officer must inform the candidates that if their appointed counting agents fail to bring both the second copy of Form 18 and the requisite photo identity card, those agents will be summarily denied entry into the Counting Hall, thereby preventing them from observing the process.

The requirements for entry extend beyond just counting agents to include election agents, who act as the primary representatives for the candidates throughout the election process. Similar to counting agents, election agents are required to produce an attested duplicate copy of their official appointment letters. Only after the Returning Officer has verified these documents for both types of agents will permission be granted for them to enter the secure confines of the Counting Hall. The system also provides mechanisms for changes in representation prior to the start of the count. A candidate or their authorized election agent holds the power to revoke the appointment of a previously named counting agent. This revocation is executed using Form 19, which is appended to the Conduct of Elections Rules of 1961, a key piece of legislation detailing the operational procedures for elections in the region. It is crucial to note that such a revocation only becomes legally operative at the precise moment the document is formally lodged with the Returning Officer. In the event that an appointment is revoked, the candidate retains the authority to appoint a replacement counting agent to fill the vacancy. However, this substitution can only be made at any time before the actual commencement of the counting process. Once the counting of votes has officially begun, the window for appointing fresh counting agents closes permanently, and no new appointments can be made under any circumstances. Any such fresh appointment made prior to the start must follow the same rigorous procedure as the initial appointment, utilizing Form 18 of the Conduct of Election Rules 1961.

Upon the arrival of a newly appointed or original counting agent, they must present their letter of appointment and their identity card before the Returning Officer for verification. Following the successful production of these documents, the counting agent is required to sign a specific declaration contained within their letter of appointment. This declaration is a solemn affirmation regarding the maintenance of the secrecy of voting, a fundamental principle of democratic elections that ensures ballots remain confidential and free from coercion or influence. After the Returning Officer has thoroughly verified the letter of appointment, the identity card, and the signed secrecy declaration, the agent is permitted to enter the Counting Hall. To further enhance security and prevent the smuggling of unauthorized materials or devices into the sensitive area, the Returning Officer is empowered by law to order the searching of any counting agent before they are allowed entry. Once inside, each counting agent is issued a badge by the Returning Officer. This badge serves multiple purposes: it clearly indicates which candidate the agent represents, and it displays the serial number of the specific table where the agent is assigned to watch the counting process. The movement of agents within the hall is highly restricted to maintain order and prevent interference. Agents are instructed to remain seated or standing only at the table allotted to them and are strictly prohibited from moving about freely throughout the entire hall. An exception to this restriction is made for the candidate themselves, their election agent, and, in the absence of both, their counting agent stationed at the Returning Officer's table; these specific individuals are permitted to move around all counting tables to observe the proceedings comprehensively.

The physical arrangement of the Counting Hall is designed to facilitate an organized and systematic counting process. The counting is conducted on tables that are arranged in distinct rows, and the tables within each row are serially numbered to ensure easy identification and tracking. On each counting table, the seating arrangement for the various counting agents is not random but is determined by a strict hierarchy of priority based on the political status of the candidates they represent. The highest priority for seating is accorded to the counting agents of candidates belonging to recognized National parties, which are political organizations with a pan-country presence and significant electoral standing. Following them in priority are the counting agents of candidates from recognized State parties, which hold official recognition within specific states. The third tier of priority includes counting agents representing candidates from recognized State parties of other states who have been granted special permission to use their reserved symbols within the specific constituency where the counting is taking place. Next in line are the counting agents of candidates from registered but unrecognized parties, and finally, the lowest priority in terms of seating allocation is given to the counting agents of independent candidates who do not belong to any registered political party. This structured approach ensures that the limited space at each table is allocated fairly according to the established political hierarchy.

Access to the Counting Hall is severely restricted to a specific list of authorized personnel to guarantee the sanctity of the election results. The only individuals permitted inside the hall include counting supervisors and counting assistants who manage the logistical aspects of the count, micro-observers who provide granular oversight, and persons specifically authorized by the Election Commission of India, often referred to as the ECI, who must possess an authority letter duly issued by the commission. Additionally, general observers, public servants who are officially on duty in connection with the election, and the candidates along with their election agents and counting agents are allowed entry. Before the counting process begins, the Returning Officer bears the responsibility of ensuring that no unauthorized persons are present within the Counting Hall. It is important to clarify the definition of public servants on duty in this context. This expression explicitly excludes police officers. As a general rule, police officers, whether dressed in uniform or in plain clothes, are not allowed to enter the Counting Hall without exception. The only circumstance under which police officers may be admitted is if the Returning Officer specifically decides to call them in for the maintenance of law and order or for some similar urgent purpose. Furthermore, the definition of public servants on duty also excludes high-ranking government officials such as Ministers, State Ministers, or Deputy Ministers of either the Union Government or State Governments. These officials are permitted to enter the Counting Hall only if they are contesting the election as candidates themselves.

According to specific instructions issued by the Election Commission of India, ministers and high-ranking officials cannot be appointed as election agents or counting agents. This restriction exists because such dignitaries are typically escorted by security guards, and these security personnel are strictly prohibited from entering the Counting Halls. Consequently, to avoid logistical conflicts and security breaches, these officials are barred from serving in agent capacities. The regulation of entry into the counting venue must be strictly enforced as detailed in these guidelines. No other unauthorized person, regardless of their status, influence, or identity, should be allowed to enter the place of counting. This zero-tolerance policy extends to security personnel accompanying candidates or their agents; generally, no security staff are permitted to enter the Counting Halls. However, a narrow exception is made for contesting candidates who are classified as SPG protectees, referring to individuals entitled to protection by the Special Protection Group due to high-level security threats, or other similarly placed contesting candidates facing significant security risks. In such exceptional cases, the candidate may be permitted to be accompanied inside the counting hall by exactly one security personnel. This lone security guard must be dressed in plain clothes to blend in and is permitted to carry only one concealed arm, ensuring that security needs are met without compromising the neutral and controlled environment of the counting center.

In the intricate framework of election administration, specific provisions govern the conduct of candidates and their security details during the critical phase of vote counting. A contesting candidate who is typically accompanied by armed security personnel may be granted permission to enter the Counting Hall only under strict conditions. Specifically, the candidate must provide a formal written undertaking stating that their armed guards are voluntarily surrendering their weapons and security duties for the duration of their presence inside the facility. This exemption, which allows the candidate to enter while their security team disarms, is exclusively available to the contesting candidates themselves and does not extend to any other individuals who might possess personal armed security escorts. This rule ensures that the sanctity of the counting environment is maintained without compromising the safety of high-profile individuals, provided they adhere to the voluntary disarmament protocol.

The maintenance of discipline and decorum at the counting center is paramount to the integrity of the electoral process, and the Returning Officer, often abbreviated as RO, serves as the supreme authority within this domain. The Returning Officer is bound solely by the provisions of existing law and the direct instructions issued by the Election Commission while performing their official duties. It is explicitly mandated that the Returning Officer must not take orders from, nor show any favoritism toward, any superior officer in the administrative hierarchy or any political functionary. This prohibition includes, but is not limited to, government Ministers and other elected officials, ensuring that the counting process remains free from political interference or undue influence from higher authorities. To further secure the environment, if the Returning Officer harbors a reasonable doubt regarding the identity or intent of any person present in the Counting Hall, they possess the authority to order a search of that individual. This power to search remains valid even if the person in question holds a valid authority letter granting them entry to the counting premises, emphasizing that security clearance does not override the Returning Officer's immediate judgment on potential threats.

Security personnel are strategically posted at the entrance or entrances of the Counting Hall to control access rigorously. No individual is permitted to enter or leave the room without the explicit permission of the Returning Officer, creating a sealed environment where movement is tightly monitored. The Returning Officer must ensure that complete order and discipline prevail throughout the proceedings, guaranteeing that the counting of votes takes place in a professional and judicious manner. Furthermore, the counting staff, who are responsible for the physical handling and tabulation of ballots, are required to remain within the Counting Hall until the final results are officially declared. They may only leave the premises with the specific permission of the Returning Officer after this declaration has been made. To maintain a focused and hazard-free atmosphere, smoking and the use of electronic cigarettes are strictly prohibited anywhere within the counting center. Additionally, the possession of mobile phones, tablets such as iPads, laptops, or any other similar electronic devices capable of recording audio or video is forbidden inside the counting center. The sole exception to this ban applies to Observers appointed by the Election Commission, who may carry such devices for official oversight purposes. However, computers, laptops, or mobile devices that are strictly required for official use to transmit counting data through the ENCORE system, which is a dedicated technological platform for election result management, are permitted to ensure the efficient flow of data.

Surveillance and transparency are enforced through comprehensive video monitoring protocols. An uninterrupted closed-circuit television, known as CCTV, coverage must be ensured for the movement of all Control Units, referred to as CUs, Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail machines known as VVPATs, and all relevant election documents as they are transported from the Strong Rooms to the Counting Halls and back. Strong Rooms are secure storage facilities where election materials are kept under lock and key before counting begins. The live feed of this movement must be displayed on televisions or monitors inside the Counting Hall, allowing candidates and their agents to view the transit of materials in real time. The entire counting process within the Counting Halls must be captured with 360-degree CCTV coverage or videography that includes accurate date and time stamping. These recordings must be stored safely on appropriate retrievable storage devices to prevent loss or tampering. Video coverage is mandatory at every single stage of the counting operation. This extensive visual record must include the initial arrangements of the Counting Hall, the security setups both inside and outside the facility, the presence of candidates and their authorized agents, the randomization process used to assign counting staff, the opening of the Strong Rooms, the actual counting of votes, the tabulation of results at the Returning Officer's table, the counter-checking of two Control Units by the Observer, the formal declaration of results, the handing over of the Certificate of Return of Election to the victorious candidate, the transfer of VVPAT slips into thick black envelopes for secure disposal, the sealing of Control Units and VVPAT machines, the sealing of election papers after counting, and any other significant events that occur during the process.

Specific technical requirements dictate the placement of cameras for certain operations. For instance, a CCTV camera designated for the counting table earmarked for the VVPAT Counting Booth, often abbreviated as VCB, must be installed on the ceiling directly above the counting table. This installation must strictly follow the instructions contained in the latest edition of the Manual on Electronic Voting Machine and VVPAT, which serves as the authoritative guide for equipment handling. However, a critical privacy and security constraint exists: under no circumstances are the actual votes displayed on individual Electronic Voting Machines, known as EVMs, or on physical ballot papers to be video recorded. This prevents the creation of visual records that could link specific votes to specific voters or machines in a compromising way. Once the counting process concludes, the unedited video recordings must be sealed immediately. These seals must clearly label all details contained within the recording for future reference. It is imperative that the video recording of the entire counting process remains intact and unedited, secured with proper locking seals to ensure that no one can copy or alter the footage after it has been sealed. All such recordings fall under the custody of the District Election Officer and are legally treated as official records under Rule 93(1) of the Conduct of Election Rules 1961, a statutory framework governing election procedures. The Election Commission's latest instructions regarding the storage and retention periods for videography and CCTV data must be followed strictly in this context. Furthermore, there shall be no webcasting, which refers to the live transmission of video recordings over the internet, of the vote counting process to prevent unauthorized dissemination of sensitive operational visuals.

Regarding the commencement of the counting process, specific procedural timelines and reporting obligations must be met. Unless directed otherwise by higher authorities, the Returning Officer is not required to obtain any specific additional permission to begin the counting of votes. However, before initiating the count, the Returning Officer must ensure that all complaints received regarding the polling process have been duly examined and addressed, with no pending issues remaining unresolved. The Returning Officer is also obligated to keep the Election Commission informed of the progress of the poll on the day of voting. To fulfill this requirement, the Returning Officer must submit three distinct reports to the Commission. The third and final report is particularly time-sensitive and must reach the Commission by seven o'clock in the morning following the day of the poll. It is critically important that these reports, especially the third and final one, are received by the Commission within the stipulated timeframe. Failure to receive these reports on time can result in the Commission ordering a stoppage of the commencement of counting, highlighting the severe consequences of administrative delays. Consequently, the Returning Officer must commence the counting exactly at the hour fixed for this purpose, ensuring synchronization with the broader electoral schedule and adherence to regulatory mandates.

The secure handling of election materials begins with the strict protocol for opening the Polled Electronic Voting Machine Strong Room, a facility designed to safeguard voting units before the counting process commences. This critical procedure must be conducted in the direct presence of key stakeholders to ensure transparency and trust in the electoral system. Specifically, an independent Observer, the Returning Officer or Assistant Returning Officers, and the candidates themselves or their duly appointed election agents must all be physically present during the opening. Before any physical seals are broken, the officials are required to make necessary administrative entries in a dedicated log book maintained specifically for tracking access and events related to the strong room. Following these entries, the integrity of the security seal on the lock must be visually inspected and explicitly shown to the candidates and their agents to confirm that the room has not been tampered with since it was last secured. Only after this verification is complete shall the seal be broken. To create an immutable record of these events, the entire proceeding must be captured on video with a date and time stamp embedded in the footage. Furthermore, uninterrupted closed-circuit television coverage must be established and maintained to record the movement of all Counting Units, Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail machines, and all relevant election documents as they are transported from the strong room to the Counting Hall. This continuous visual monitoring ensures there is no gap in the chain of custody for sensitive election materials.

Before the actual counting of votes begins, the Returning Officer has a statutory duty to read out and explain specific legal provisions to everyone present in the hall. This includes Section 128 of the Representation of the People Act, 1951, and Rule 54 of the Conduct of Election Rules, 1961. These laws mandate the maintenance of secrecy regarding how individuals voted. The explanation serves to inform all attendees of their legal obligations and to ensure strict compliance. Every person inside the counting hall, regardless of their role, is legally required to maintain the secrecy of the vote and to actively aid in maintaining that secrecy. It is forbidden for anyone to communicate any information to any other person if that information is intended to violate the confidentiality of a voter's choice. Full cooperation with the instructions issued by the Returning Officer is mandatory for all present. Any individual who contravenes these legal provisions faces serious penalties, including imprisonment for a term that may extend up to three months, or a monetary fine, or both, as prescribed under Section 128 of the Representation of the People Act, 1951. Regarding movement within the facility, counting agents and other personnel are generally prohibited from leaving the Counting Center while the counting process is ongoing. They are ordinarily permitted to exit only after the final declaration of results. However, a specific exception exists for counting agents who are not assigned to watch the counting of VVPAT slips in the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail Control Unit; these agents may be granted permission by the Returning Officer to leave the counting hall once the counting of votes in the Counting Units and Postal Ballots is concluded.

A specialized procedure governs the counting of Electronically Transmitted Postal Ballots, commonly referred to as ETPBs, which are utilized primarily by service voters such as members of the armed forces and police deployed away from their home constituencies. The counting of these ballots takes place at the Returning Officer's table, following the same general principles applied to other postal ballots. A fundamental rule is that only those postal ballots received before the fixed hour for the commencement of counting are eligible to be counted; any ballots arriving after this deadline are excluded. Significant advance planning is required by the Returning Officer to manage the pre-counting arrangements for these electronically transmitted ballots effectively. This planning involves assessing the estimated number of ETPBs likely to be returned and calculating the resources needed, such as the number of tables and teams required for reading Quick Response codes, without causing undue delay to the overall process. Factors influencing this assessment include the total time anticipated for counting Electronic Voting Machines and the mandatory counting of Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail slips. For planning purposes, an approximate gross scanning speed of four scans per minute per team may be assumed when determining the necessary staffing and equipment levels. It is imperative that sufficient numbers of QR Code Readers are purchased well in advance and thoroughly tested to ensure they function without errors. Before the pre-counting scanning operation begins, each QR Scanner must be properly configured to interface with the election software.

The physical setup for reading QR codes should be located in a separate room adjacent to the main Counting Hall to allow for focused work while maintaining proximity for oversight. Each operational team assigned to this task should consist of one Supervisor and one Assistant working together with a single QR Code Reader. To ensure adequate supervision, one Assistant Returning Officer should be assigned to oversee a maximum of ten such QR code reading teams. Transparency is maintained by allowing one counting agent for each candidate to witness the QR code reading process, irrespective of the total number of tables set up for this purpose, and suitable seating arrangements must be provided for these agents. Operational efficiency and accuracy require the arrangement of separate trays to physically segregate valid ballots from invalid ones, specifically those associated with Form 13B, which is the covering envelope for the ballot paper. Security protocols extend to communication devices; the mobile numbers or email addresses of the Returning Officer, Assistant Returning Officers, and Counting Supervisors that are linked to the ETPB system to receive One-Time Passwords must remain unchanged until the elections are fully concluded. Additionally, it must be ensured that the Do Not Disturb mode is activated on the mobile phones of these officials to prevent interruptions from calls or notifications during the critical counting phase. When multiple tables and teams are deployed for QR code reading, each team is issued a separate login credential. This unique authentication enables the system to trace exactly which envelopes and documents were processed by which specific team, ensuring accountability. Once the QR code reading is completed according to the laid-down procedure, the sets of envelopes are assigned to the counting tables arranged for the subsequent counting of postal ballots.

Specific procedures are mandated for handling critical scenarios that may arise during the QR code reading process, particularly when technical issues occur. In the event that the QR Code available on Form 13C, which is the outer cover of the postal ballot envelope, is damaged and cannot be scanned by the machine, a manual fallback procedure must be followed. The first step in this contingency plan involves opening the outer covers, known as Form 13C, to access the inner declaration form, designated as Form 13-A. The official must then read the name of the elector directly from this declaration. Using the election software, the operator must select the name of the elector from a digital drop-down list within the Scan Form 13C tab and click on the ADD button to initiate the verification process. The software will then perform necessary valid checks to identify possible duplicates and to verify the status of the service voter. Upon successful verification, the software generates a unique serial number for that specific ballot. The Counting Supervisor is then required to manually write this computer-generated serial number directly onto the envelope being verified, thereby linking the physical document to the digital record and allowing the counting process to proceed despite the initial scanning failure.

The procedure for validating and processing postal ballots involves a rigorous, multi-step verification protocol designed to ensure the integrity of the electoral process through the use of Quick Response codes, commonly known as QR codes, and unique digital identifiers. In the standard operational scenario where all documentation is intact and free from defects, the process begins after an initial check confirms that no duplicate entries exist and that the primary verification metrics are correct. At this stage, the election official must proceed to scan two distinct QR codes located on Form 13A, which serves as the statutory declaration by the service voter. These codes are physically positioned on the lower section of the form. The scanning device reads these codes sequentially, one after the other, to authenticate the document against the central database. Upon successful reading, the computer system automatically generates a unique serial number, often referred to as a Sl. No., which acts as a digital fingerprint for that specific transaction. The official is required to manually record this computer-generated serial number directly onto Form 13A for physical audit trails. Following this, the official moves to Form 13B, which is the inner envelope containing the actual ballot paper. A single QR code located on the lower right-hand side of Form 13B must be scanned. Similar to the previous step, the system produces a unique serial number for this component, which must also be noted down on the form. If the entire sequence of QR code readings completes without triggering any software anomalies or flags indicating fraud or error, the documents are considered valid. The official then carefully places the Form 13B envelope and the signed Declaration, which is Form 13A, back inside the outer covering envelope designated as Form 13C. This reassembled package is then deposited into a specific collection tray clearly marked as VALID BALLOT, signifying that the vote is ready to be counted.

However, electoral materials may occasionally suffer from physical damage during transit or handling, necessitating alternative procedures to ensure no legitimate vote is discarded due to technical read errors. In the second scenario, where the QR codes printed on Form 13A are damaged to the extent that they cannot be scanned by the optical reader, a specific contingency protocol is activated. The process initiates not with the damaged inner forms, but with the outer cover, known as Form 13C. This outer envelope features its own QR code on the lower right-hand side. The election official scans this outer code first, allowing the QR code reader software to perform necessary validity checks, including screening for potential duplicates and verifying the identity of the service voter associated with the packet. Upon successful validation of the outer layer, the computer system assigns a unique serial number to the transaction. The Counting Supervisor, who oversees the integrity of the counting process, must then manually inscribe this computer-generated serial number onto the envelope currently under verification. Only after confirming that no duplicate records exist and that the verification is successful is the Counting Supervisor authorized to open the outer Form 13C envelope. Once opened, if the QR codes on the internal Form 13A remain unreadable due to damage, the official must switch to a manual data entry method. The supervisor selects the name of the elector from the digital interface and manually enters the 40-digit e-PBID, which stands for electronic Postal Ballot Identification number, into the designated field within the Scan Form 13A tab of the software. This long alphanumeric string serves as a backup unique identifier when visual codes fail. After entering this data and clicking the add button to register the entry, the official proceeds to scan the QR code located on the lower right side of Form 13B. Assuming this second scan is successful, the system will generate the requisite serial numbers. The official must diligently note down the computer-generated serial numbers for all the respective covers involved in this transaction. Finally, provided no anomalies are raised during this hybrid manual-digital process, the Form 13B envelope and the Declaration Form 13A are returned to the Form 13C envelope, and the complete packet is placed in the tray marked as VALID BALLOT.

A third distinct scenario addresses situations where the QR code on Form 13B, the inner ballot envelope, is damaged and unscannable, while the declaration form remains readable. The procedure begins similarly to the previous exception, starting with the scanning of the outer cover, Form 13C, which bears a QR code on its lower right-hand side. The QR code reader processes this information to conduct essential validity checks, ensuring there are no duplicate submissions and confirming the legitimacy of the service voter. The software subsequently provides a unique serial number for the session, which the Counting Supervisor must manually write on the envelope being verified. If the system confirms that there are no duplicates and the verification is accurate, the Counting Supervisor is permitted to open only the outer Form 13C envelope. Inside, the supervisor will find two critical documents: the declaration, identified as Form 13A, and the inner cover, identified as Form 13B, which houses the actual postal ballot paper. The supervisor removes both the declaration and the inner cover from the outer packaging. The next step involves scanning the two QR codes located on the lower side of Form 13A, one by one. As each code is read, the computer generates a serial number that must be recorded on Form 13A. When the official attempts to scan the QR code on the lower right side of Form 13B and finds it damaged beyond recognition, they must again resort to manual entry. The 40-digit e-PBID printed on Form 13B is typed into the specific text boxes available in the Scan Form 13B tab of the application. The official then clicks the ADD button to confirm the manual entry, effectively bypassing the need for a visual scan. Following this confirmation, the computer-generated serial numbers for all the aforementioned covers are noted down respectively. Upon the successful completion of these steps, the Form 13B envelope and the Declaration Form 13A are placed back inside the Form 13C envelope, and the consolidated package is secured in the tray designated for VALID BALLOT.

The fourth scenario deals with the most severe case of document degradation, where both the QR codes and the human-readable backup identifiers are compromised. If the QR codes on either Form 13A or Form 13B cannot be scanned, and furthermore, the 40-digit e-PBID is illegible due to smudging, tearing, or other forms of damage that render the numbers unreadable, the standard validation path is blocked. In such instances, the user operating the scanning terminal must navigate to a drop-down menu within the software interface and select the option labeled "other reason." Selecting this option prompts the user to type a detailed explanation describing precisely why the scanning or manual entry of the 40-digit e-PBID from the declaration Form 13A was impossible. This written justification creates an audit trail explaining the deviation from standard procedure. Once this reason is logged, the Returning Officer, a senior election official with the authority to make final determinations on ballot validity, must review the case. Based on the inability to verify the unique identifiers through either automated or manual means, the Returning Officer is required to mark the ballot as an INVALID BALLOT. This ensures that only votes that can be definitively authenticated are counted, maintaining the security of the election even at the cost of discarding damaged materials that cannot be verified.

The fifth and final scenario outlines the protocol for handling fraudulent or irregular submissions, specifically targeting duplicate or spurious electronic postal ballots. During the scanning process, the software continuously analyzes the data embedded in the QR codes against the central database. If the QR code reading reveals any discrepancy, such as evidence suggesting the documents are not genuine, forged, or that multiple copies of the same document have been received for a single voter, the system flags the submission immediately. Such discrepancies indicate a potential breach of electoral security, as each voter is entitled to cast only one vote. Consequently, any envelope triggering these specific alerts regarding authenticity or duplication is strictly prohibited from entering the valid count stream. Instead, these suspicious envelopes must be segregated and kept in a separate, dedicated tray explicitly meant for INVALID BALLOT. This isolation prevents accidental counting and allows for further investigation by election authorities into the source of the spurious documents. Throughout all these scenarios, the overarching principle is the meticulous cross-referencing of physical documents with digital records via QR technology and unique serial numbers, ensuring that every valid vote is captured while systematically filtering out errors, damages, and fraudulent attempts.

In the event that envelopes or documents are rejected due to issues identified during QR Code reading, the Assistant Returning Officer who is in charge of the specific team must personally examine the situation to ensure full satisfaction regarding the discrepancy shown by the QR Code scanning process that led to the rejection. This verification step is critical to prevent erroneous disqualification of votes, and once the officer is convinced of the validity of the rejection, they must record a detailed note describing the specific discrepancy on the reverse side of Form 13C, which serves as the outer covering document for postal ballots. This documentation creates an audit trail and ensures accountability in the handling of disputed materials before the process moves forward to the actual counting phase.

The counting of postal ballot papers is governed by strict regulatory frameworks, specifically under Rule 54A of the Conduct of Elections Rules established in 1961, which mandates that the counting of these special ballots must commence first at the table designated for the Returning Officer. This priority ensures that votes cast by individuals who cannot be present at polling stations, such as military personnel, government officials on duty, or those with disabilities, are integrated into the final tally before general voting papers are processed. A fundamental condition for eligibility is the timing of receipt; only those postal ballot papers that have been physically received by the Returning Officer prior to the fixed hour set for the commencement of the counting process are permitted to be taken up for scrutiny and counting. Any ballots arriving after this strict deadline are legally excluded from the count to maintain the integrity and synchronicity of the election timeline. At the precise moment counting begins, the Returning Officer is obligated to provide the Election Observer, an independent official tasked with monitoring fairness, with the most current and accurate account of the total number of postal ballot papers received. This total includes all ballots delivered through designated facilitation centers as well as those sent via the postal service, ensuring transparency from the outset.

Before the physical distribution of postal ballot envelopes to the various counting tables and before the actual scrutiny of declarations begins, the Returning Officer or one of the Assistant Returning Officers must conduct a comprehensive briefing and demonstration for all counting supervisors, assistants, and micro-observers. This training session is designed to explain and visually demonstrate the specific requirements that constitute a valid declaration, ensuring that every individual involved in the process understands the legal standards for acceptance or rejection. The Returning Officer must also actively manage the workflow to ensure there is no inordinate or unreasonable delay in the scrutiny of declarations by the Assistant Returning Officers and counting supervisors, as efficiency is vital to the timely declaration of results. Furthermore, a crucial safety mechanism exists wherein every single case of a postal ballot being rejected due to defects in the Declaration found in Form 13A must be re-verified personally by the Returning Officer before the ballot is officially categorized as rejected. This double-check system minimizes the risk of human error disenfranchising voters. The Election Observer is required to oversee this entire counting process with extreme vigilance, paying particular attention to the scrutiny of the declarations in Form 13A, which is often the most complex part of the postal voting procedure.

Upon the conclusion of the counting and the subsequent declaration of the election result, the Observer must submit a formal report that includes a highly detailed description of the procedures followed specifically for the postal ballot counting. This report is not merely a summary but a forensic account that must explicitly mention the total number of postal ballot papers received for counting, the exact number of postal ballots that were rejected, the number of tables that were allocated and utilized for the postal ballot counting operation, and the total duration of time consumed for the entire counting of postal ballots. It is imperative to note that all postal ballots received by the Returning Officer up to the fixed hour for the commencement of counting must be counted, and therefore, all such ballots must be brought before the Returning Officer for processing. Conversely, strict prohibitions apply to late arrivals; no Cover-B within Form 13C, which contains the actual postal ballot, that is received after the time fixed for the commencement of counting shall be opened or counted, as per Rule 54A subsection 2. For each such late cover received, the Returning Officer or an authorized Assistant Returning Officer must make a suitable endorsement directly on the Form 13C cover stating that it was received late and thus remains unopened. Following this endorsement, these late covers are placed into a larger master cover, which is then securely sealed before any further proceedings continue, preserving them as evidence while excluding them from the vote tally.

For the postal ballots that were received on time and are eligible for counting, a rigorous, multi-stage process must be scrupulously followed to ensure accuracy and legality. All Cover-B units within Form 13C that contain postal ballot papers and were received by the Returning Officer within the permissible timeframe are to be opened sequentially, one after another. Upon opening a Cover-B in Form 13C, two distinct documents are required to be found inside for the ballot to be considered procedurally complete. The first document is the declaration made by the voter, recorded in Form 13A, which serves as a sworn statement of identity and eligibility. The second document is the inner Cover-A, designated as Form 13B, which physically contains the marked postal ballot paper itself. Before the Returning Officer proceeds to open the inner Cover-A to reveal the voter's choice, they must first meticulously check the declaration in Form 13A. This preliminary check is decisive; the Returning Officer shall reject a postal ballot paper without even opening its inner cover, Form 13B, if any of several specific disqualifying conditions are met.

The first ground for immediate rejection without opening the inner ballot cover occurs if the declaration in Form 13A is completely missing from inside the Cover-B of Form 13C, as the absence of this legal declaration renders the ballot invalid regardless of its contents. The second ground for rejection is if the declaration has not been duly signed by the elector, meaning the voter failed to sign it, or if it has not been duly attested by an officer who is legally competent to perform such attestation, or if the document is otherwise substantially defective in a way that compromises its legal standing. The third ground for rejection arises if there is a mismatch in identification numbers; specifically, if the serial number of the ballot paper appearing on the declaration in Form 13A is different from the serial number that has been endorsed on the inner Cover-A in Form 13B. Such a discrepancy suggests that the ballot paper and the declaration do not belong to the same voting instance, necessitating rejection to prevent fraud or administrative errors.

However, the rules also provide for leniency in minor technical matters to ensure that voters are not disenfranchised for trivial reasons. A postal ballot paper shall not be rejected merely on the ground that the attesting officer failed to put their official seal on the declaration of the elector in Form 13A, provided that the attesting officer has clearly written all relevant details regarding their name and designation on that form. The presence of the written details is deemed sufficient to verify the authority of the attesting officer even in the absence of a physical stamp or seal. Additionally, a postal ballot paper shall not be rejected on the ground that the sender, who is the elector, did not put their signature on the outer Cover-B, which is Form 13C, used to return the Postal Ballot Paper. This exception applies if the identity of the sender can be clearly verified based on the information and signature provided in the internal declaration in Form 13A. This distinction recognizes that the inner declaration is the primary legal instrument for verifying voter identity, while the outer cover is primarily for logistical transport. Finally, all such covers designated as Cover-A in Form 13B, which contain the Postal Ballot Paper and have been rejected for the reasons stated above, must be suitably endorsed by the Returning Officer or the authorized Assistant Returning Officer to indicate the reason for rejection. These rejected inner covers are then returned along with their respective declarations into the larger Cover-B of Form 13C, keeping the rejected materials bundled together for secure storage and potential future review.

The procedural handling of postal ballot materials during an election count requires strict adherence to protocols designed to maintain the integrity and secrecy of the vote. When larger covers, designated as cover B, are encountered in the process, they must be segregated from other materials and placed into a distinct packet. This specific packet is then sealed by the Returning Officer or the Assistant Returning Officer who is officially overseeing the counting proceedings at that location. To ensure that this packet can be properly identified and tracked in the future, comprehensive details must be recorded on its exterior surface. These required particulars include the full name of the electoral constituency where the election is taking place, the specific calendar date on which the counting of votes is occurring, and a concise written description summarizing the contents contained within the packet. Once this separation and sealing of cover B materials is complete, the Returning Officer or Assistant Returning Officer will move forward to address the remaining envelopes known as cover A, which are associated with Form 13-B. It is important to note that this step involves only those covers that have not been previously rejected during earlier stages of scrutiny.

A fundamental principle guiding these operations is the absolute protection of voter secrecy regarding postal votes. To uphold this principle, any declaration forms completed in Form 13-A that have undergone scrutiny and been found to be in proper order must immediately be placed into a separate packet and securely sealed. This action of isolating and sealing the declaration forms is a mandatory prerequisite that must be completed before any actual ballot papers are removed from their corresponding cover A envelopes in Form 13-B. The necessity of this sequence arises from the sensitive nature of the information contained within the declarations. These documents explicitly list the names of the voters alongside the unique serial numbers assigned to their respective postal ballot papers. If the ballot papers were extracted before the declarations were secured, it would create a risk that a specific vote could be linked back to the identity of the voter who cast it, thereby violating the constitutional guarantee of a secret ballot. By removing the identifying declarations first, the subsequent handling of the anonymous ballot papers ensures that the link between the voter and their choice is permanently broken.

Following the secure storage of the declaration forms, the Returning Officer or Assistant Returning Officer will proceed to open the cover A envelopes from Form 13-B one by one in a systematic manner. As each envelope is opened, the postal ballot paper contained inside is carefully extracted. The officer must then scrutinize every single ballot paper individually to determine its validity according to established legal standards. A postal ballot paper will be declared invalid and rejected if it meets any of several specific criteria. Firstly, a ballot is rejected if no vote has been recorded on it whatsoever, meaning the voter failed to indicate a preference for any candidate. Secondly, rejection occurs if votes have been marked in favor of more than one candidate on the same paper, as the rules typically permit a voter to select only one option. Thirdly, any ballot paper determined to be spurious, meaning it is counterfeit or not an official document issued by the election authority, must be discarded. Fourthly, if a ballot paper has been so severely damaged, torn, or mutilated that its authenticity as a genuine election document cannot be confidently established, it cannot be counted.

Further grounds for rejection include instances where the ballot paper was not returned in the specific cover B that was originally sent to the elector by the Returning Officer, as this breaks the chain of custody required for validation. Additionally, a ballot is invalid if the mark indicating the voter's choice is made in such an ambiguous or unclear manner that it creates reasonable doubt regarding which candidate the voter intended to support. Finally, strict confidentiality rules dictate that if a ballot paper bears any extraneous mark, signature, or writing that could potentially allow the voter to be identified, it must be rejected to prevent any breach of secrecy. Despite these strict rules, the law does not mandate a specific type of symbol or mark that a voter must use to indicate their choice on a postal ballot. Any form of marking is acceptable provided it is placed on the ballot in such a way that the intention of the voter to vote for a particular candidate is clear beyond any reasonable doubt. Consequently, a mark made anywhere within the space specifically allotted to a candidate will be interpreted as a valid vote cast in favor of that specific individual. Furthermore, a vote recorded on a postal ballot paper shall not be rejected simply because the mark is somewhat indistinct or has been made multiple times, as long as the overall appearance of the paper clearly demonstrates that the voter intended to support one particular candidate. This flexible approach is grounded in Rule 54A subsection 10 of the Conduct of Elections Rules 1961, which prioritizes voter intent over technical perfection.

Once the scrutiny process is complete, all votes deemed valid are tallied, and the total count is credited to the respective candidates who received them. The aggregate number of postal votes secured by each candidate through this counting process is then formally entered into the official result sheet, known as Form 20. These figures are subsequently announced publicly to ensure transparency, providing the information to the candidates themselves, their appointed election agents, and the authorized counting agents who are present to observe the proceedings. After the counting and announcement phases are concluded, all physical ballot papers, both those validated and those rejected, undergo a final packaging procedure. The valid postal ballot papers are bundled together separately from the bundle of rejected postal ballot papers. Both bundles are then placed together into a single packet. This packet is sealed using the official seal of the Returning Officer. In addition to the officer's seal, the packet may also be sealed with the personal seals of any candidates, their election agents, or counting agents who wish to affix their own marks to ensure the security of the materials until they are stored or transported for archival purposes.

The procedure for counting postal ballots in a Parliamentary Constituency involves specific jurisdictional distinctions. The Assistant Returning Officer who is responsible for counting the votes polled at a specific component Assembly Segment within the larger Parliamentary Constituency does not have the authority or responsibility to count the postal ballot papers designated for the Parliamentary Constituency as a whole. This separation of duties ensures clarity in the aggregation of results across different levels of representation. According to Rule 65 of the Conduct of Elections Rules 1961, in scenarios where vote counting takes place at more than one counting center, which is the standard practice for Parliamentary elections, the detailed provisions of Rule 54A regarding the finalization of counts apply exclusively to the counting activities conducted at the last of such locations. The specific venue where the Returning Officer performs the final consolidation of votes and officially declares the election result is legally defined as the last place of counting. Throughout this entire process, elaborate instructions issued periodically by the Election Commission must be followed with scrupulous attention. These instructions cover pre-counting activities related to Electronically Transmitted Postal Ballot Systems, the protocol for intimating candidates about postal ballots received from facilitation centers and via regular post, the secure storage and transmission of these ballots, and the precise methodologies for counting and declaring the results.

Regarding the broader timeline of the counting day, there is a structured sequence for integrating different voting technologies. Specifically, the counting of votes recorded on Electronic Voting Machines, commonly known as EVMs, is scheduled to commence thirty minutes after the counting of postal ballots has begun. This staggered start allows election officials to manage the workflow efficiently. However, there are exceptions to this timing rule. The counting of votes from EVMs shall begin immediately at the originally scheduled time for counting in certain specific cases. One primary example of such a case is if there are no postal ballot papers to be counted in the constituency at all. In the absence of any postal ballots, the delay is unnecessary, and the EVM counting proceeds without waiting for the thirty-minute interval, ensuring that the declaration of results is not unduly delayed when only machine-recorded votes are present. This comprehensive framework ensures that every stage of the postal ballot and EVM counting process is conducted with uniformity, security, and respect for the democratic rights of the electorate.

In the comprehensive administration of electoral processes, specific protocols govern the segments of an assembly within a parliamentary constituency where postal ballots are not included in the immediate count. When the critical phase of vote counting commences for elections utilizing Electronic Voting Machines, it is imperative to understand that only the Control Unit of the machine deployed at a specific polling station is necessary to determine the final outcome. The Ballot Units, which are the interfaces voters interact with to cast their votes, remain at the polling stations or in secure storage, while the Control Units, which store the encrypted vote data, are transported to the counting center. Consequently, for the purpose of tallying votes recorded on these electronic devices, officials are required to bring only the Control Units to the designated counting tables, accompanied by the respective Form 17-C. This form serves as an official statutory record documenting the total number of votes polled at that specific station, acting as a primary verification tool to ensure the integrity of the digital data against the physical log maintained during the voting hours.

The systematic distribution of these Control Units across the various counting tables follows a strict serial order corresponding to the numbering of the polling stations to maintain an unbroken chain of custody and logical organization. For instance, the Control Unit that was utilized at Polling Station Number One must be assigned exclusively to Counting Table Number One, and similarly, the unit from Polling Station Number Two is directed to Counting Table Number Two, continuing this pattern sequentially for all subsequent stations. This methodical allocation prevents confusion and ensures that each table handles a distinct and verifiable set of data. Furthermore, the workflow is designed to proceed in discrete rounds to manage crowd control and observation efficiency. Only after the counting of all Control Units scheduled for a particular round has been fully completed, and these units have been physically removed from the counting hall, may the Control Units designated for the next round be introduced into the facility. This transition requires explicit verbal clearance from the Returning Officer or the Assistant Returning Officer, who are the senior officials responsible for overseeing the entire counting operation. Additionally, before the next batch of machines enters, the results of the concluded round must be publicly displayed on blackboards, whiteboards, or television monitors within the hall to ensure transparency for candidates and their agents.

Before any attempt is made to ascertain the election result from the Control Units, the counting officials must perform a rigorous series of preliminary checks to validate the security and operational status of the hardware. First, they must verify that the paper seal affixed to the Control Unit remains intact and confirm that this specific seal matches the one recorded as having been provided to that particular polling station on election day, ensuring no tampering has occurred during transport. Second, the official must ensure that the Control Unit is switched to the ON position. Third, and perhaps most critically, the total number of votes displayed on the digital panel of the Control Unit, which is revealed by pressing the TOTAL button, must be compared against the total number of votes polled as documented in Form 17-C. This cross-verification is essential to confirm that the digital record aligns perfectly with the physical register of voters who participated. Once these initial validations are successful, the process of retrieving the candidate-specific data begins. The counting official is instructed to tear the green paper seal that protects the result section and remove the address tag associated with that section. However, great care must be taken to press the Result button on the Control Unit without removing the main green paper seal that secures the internal memory compartment, thereby preserving the forensic integrity of the machine while allowing the data to be viewed.

Upon activation of the Result function, the Control Unit automatically displays the total number of votes recorded for each contesting candidate, including the option for None of the Above, on its digital display panels. To facilitate transparent observation, the Counting Assistant must lift and position the Control Unit in such a manner that the display panel is clearly visible to the Counting Supervisor, the Micro Observer, and the Counting Agents representing the various candidates. These agents are typically seated across a wire-mesh or fence that separates them from the counting tables to prevent physical interference while allowing visual access. This viewing process is not a one-time event; if any Counting Agent desires to verify the figures again, the process of displaying the results shall be repeated to satisfy their need for confirmation. After the results have been meticulously noted by the officials in Part-II of Form 17-C, the cover of the Result Section on the machine must be closed, and the Control Unit should be switched OFF to preserve battery life and secure the data. Subsequently, the Control Units, along with their paper seals, are placed inside their designated carrying cases and sent for final sealing and storage.

Contingency procedures are strictly defined for scenarios where the Control Unit fails to display results on its panel. In such instances, the malfunctioning unit is immediately placed inside its carrying case and kept under the direct custody of the Returning Officer. If a Control Unit cannot provide a digital readout, the election rules mandate that the Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail slips from the corresponding VVPAT machine associated with that polling station must be counted manually. This manual count takes place only after the counting of all other functional Control Units has been completed, ensuring that the electronic process is not unduly delayed by isolated technical issues. Another specific procedural nuance addresses situations where the Close button was inadvertently not pressed at the conclusion of the polling hours. In these cases, the Total button on the Control Unit is pressed first to view the aggregate vote count. If this total matches the figure recorded in Form 17-C, the official then presses the Close button followed by the Result button to safely ascertain the breakdown of votes. Conversely, if the total votes displayed in the Control Unit do not tally with the total votes recorded in Form 17-C, the unit is deemed compromised or erroneous; it is immediately returned to its carrying case and secured under the custody of the Returning Officer for further investigation, and its digital results are not accepted for the final tally.

Special provisions exist for handling known irregularities identified prior to the counting day, such as the non-deletion of mock poll data from the Control Unit, the failure to remove mock poll slips from the VVPAT machine, or discrepancies where the total votes in the Control Unit do not match the Form 17-C records. These issues are often identified on polling day or during the scrutiny of documents on the day immediately following the poll. In all such pre-identified cases, a comprehensive list is compiled and shared with all contesting candidates before the commencement of the counting process to ensure full disclosure. For any Control Unit falling into these categories, the protocol dictates that the machine shall not be brought into the counting hall for the purpose of retrieving results, thereby preventing potential confusion or disputes during the live counting event. It is also important to note that the counting of votes via Electronic Voting Machines can proceed independently and concurrently with the counting of postal ballots, meaning the progress of one stream does not halt the other.

To serve as a robust measure for cross-checking the correctness and integrity of the counting operation, observers are empowered to randomly select two Control Units that have been counted in each round for independent verification. This audit is conducted with the assistance of additional counting staff who were not involved in the original tabulation of those specific units. A standardized proforma, detailed in Annexure-56 of the election guidelines, is utilized to record the findings of this random check. If any discrepancy is discovered between the result originally obtained at the counting table and the result ascertained through this random checking by the observer, immediate and severe corrective actions are triggered. First, the result of that specific round for every table involved must be re-verified directly from the Control Units to identify the source of the error. Second, the counting official found to be responsible for the error or negligence is immediately removed from their duty and replaced by a fresh set of staff members. Severe disciplinary action is initiated against the erring official to uphold the sanctity of the electoral process. Finally, the results provided by the disqualified staff and their assigned tables in all preceding rounds are subjected to a thorough re-examination in the presence of the Observer, and corrected sheets are prepared wherever necessary to ensure the final declaration of results is accurate and beyond reproach.

The comprehensive procedure for compiling the results of an election begins with the meticulous documentation found in Part II of Form 17C, which serves as the foundational record for the candidate-wise outcome at each specific Counting Unit, commonly referred to as a polling station. This critical document must be formally noted and authenticated by the Counting Supervisor who is directly responsible for overseeing the tabulation process at their assigned table. To ensure transparency and mutual agreement on the recorded figures, it is mandatory to obtain the signatures of the candidates themselves or their designated counting agents who are physically present at the counting table. Before these individuals affix their signatures to Part II of Form 17C, a specific certification statement must be manually inscribed in pen within the designated space located between the signature of the Counting Supervisor and the signatures of the candidates or their agents. This handwritten declaration reads, We, hereby certify that CU No(s) is/are the same which were used in the Polling Station No, followed by the insertion of the specific polling station number, thereby legally verifying that the electronic voting machine or counting unit being tallied corresponds exactly to the one deployed at the referenced polling location. To prevent any discrepancies between records and to maintain an unbroken chain of custody, the result sheet must be prepared simultaneously in duplicate using carbon paper, ensuring that both the original and the copy contain identical information generated at the same moment. The original copy of Part II of Form 17C is immediately handed over to the Returning Officer or Assistant Returning Officer, whose duty is to compute the cumulative tally of votes round by round as the counting progresses. Conversely, the carbon copy serves a different but equally vital function; it is used to produce multiple photocopies that are distributed to the counting agents stationed at each respective table. These distributed copies must first be signed by the counting agents present to acknowledge receipt and verification, and subsequently signed by the Counting Supervisor presiding over that table to validate the transaction.

Following the initial distribution and signing process, the original second copy, which acts as the permanent duplicate record, must be returned to the Counting Supervisor of the respective table for safekeeping. The Counting Supervisor is required to retain a copy of the results from every single round of Electronic Voting Machine counting that takes place. Once the entire counting process for all rounds is concluded, the supervisor must consolidate these documents into a single envelope. This envelope must be clearly superscribed with specific details, including the label Duplicate copy of result of counting in Form 17C-Part-II, along with the specific Table number, the total number of counting rounds conducted, and the full name of the Counting Supervisor. This sealed envelope is then personally handed over to the Returning Officer or Assistant Returning Officer immediately after the completion of counting activities. Upon receipt, the Returning Officer ensures that this packet is securely sealed and stored separately alongside other crucial election documents to preserve its integrity for future reference or audit. A critical responsibility of the Returning Officer during this phase is to verify that the names of the candidates entered into these lists match exactly with the order in which they appeared on the ballot paper used by the Balloting Unit, ensuring no clerical errors have altered the sequence of candidates. Furthermore, when the tabulation sheets from each polling booth, known as Form 17C, arrive at the table of the Returning Officer or Assistant Returning Officer, it becomes the explicit duty of the Returning Officer to display these documents to the candidates, their election agents, and their counting agents who are seated at the Returning Officer's table. This display enables them to independently note down and verify the results for each candidate corresponding to each polling station, fostering an environment of open scrutiny. The Returning Officer or Assistant Returning Officer must then countersign the original filled Part-II of Form 17C, but only after satisfying themselves through rigorous inspection that the form has been properly filled out and completed in all respects without omissions or errors. Once countersigned by the Returning Officer, this validated form is transmitted to the specific officer tasked with compiling the final aggregate result and preparing the ultimate Final Result Sheet, officially designated as Form 20.

The officer in charge of compiling the final result and preparing Form 20 undertakes the complex task of aggregating data from numerous sources into a single authoritative document. This officer must make entries on Form 20 showing the votes polled by each candidate on a polling station-wise basis, adhering strictly and exclusively to the entries recorded in the Part II Result of Counting section of Form 17C for every individual polling station. Accuracy in this transcription is paramount, as it forms the basis of the declared election outcome. In addition to the standard vote counts, the number of tendered votes polled, if any, at a specific polling station must also be meticulously noted in the appropriate column in Form 20 against the concerned polling station, based on the official report submitted by the Presiding Officer. It is important to clarify that tendered votes, which are cast by eligible voters whose names are already marked as having voted due to impersonation attempts or other irregularities, are recorded for statistical and legal purposes but are not actually counted towards the final tally of any candidate. Beyond the manual compilation, the Returning Officer, relying on the duly completed Part II of Form 17C provided by the counting supervisors, must also oversee the preparation of a detailed round-wise statement formatted according to Annexure 57. A copy of this granular, polling station-wise, and round-wise breakup of votes is retained by the Election Observer in their personal folder for independent monitoring. To further safeguard against human error and ensure mathematical precision, parallel tabulation work is conducted on a computer installed within the Counting Hall using an Excel spreadsheet. This digital entry mirrors the manual process, capturing data polling station by polling station and round by round. A printout of this electronic data is generated and subjected to a comparative analysis by the Observer and the designated officer, who must sign off on the comparison to certify its accuracy.

Both the Returning Officer and the Observers must reach a state of complete satisfaction after due checking, confirming that the number of votes posted against the name of each candidate for every counting table aligns perfectly with the figures displayed in Part-II of Form 17C pertaining to that specific table. In scenarios where a single Observer is allotted to supervise counting operations across more than one Hall, Assembly Constituency, or Assembly Segment, a system of delegation is employed to maintain continuous oversight. Micro-Observers attached to the main Observer in each hall are authorized to prepare the round-wise tabulation format for randomly selected two Counting Units and sign these formats on behalf of the primary Observer during their temporary absence. However, this delegation is provisional; the primary Observer must countersign these documents immediately upon their return to validate the actions taken by the Micro-Observers. Once all checks are complete, the Observer and the Returning Officer jointly sign the candidate-wise results for that specific round. They must then ensure that the results of that round of vote counting are immediately displayed prominently on a blackboard or whiteboard visible to all stakeholders in the hall. Simultaneously, these results must be announced aloud through the public address system, ensuring that every candidate, agent, and observer present is instantly informed of the progressing tally, thereby maintaining the highest standards of transparency and public confidence throughout the electoral counting process.

Upon the conclusion of any specific round within the electoral counting process, it is a mandatory procedural requirement that a complete copy of the results generated during that round be immediately disseminated to all contesting candidates and their authorized agents. This ensures transparency and allows for real-time verification by the stakeholders directly involved in the election. Simultaneously, physical printouts of these results must be distributed to designated media facilities, specifically the media room, to facilitate accurate and timely reporting by the press. Another duplicate copy must be delivered to the communication room, where the data will be uploaded onto the ENCORE system, which serves as the central digital repository and broadcasting platform for election data. In accordance with these established arrangements, the progress of every counting round must be publicly announced, broken down by each Assembly Segment, immediately after the completion of that specific round. Furthermore, a consolidated status report for the entire Parliamentary Constituency, which aggregates the positions from the various rounds across different Assembly Segments, must also be proclaimed. These consolidated updates should be repeated at appropriate intervals over the public address system to keep the general public informed, ideally broadcast from a centralized location where a dedicated public announcement booth has been established to serve as the primary source of official information.

Regarding the specific documentation required when a Returning Officer is responsible for counting votes within an Assembly Constituency, the officer is obligated to prepare the Final Result Sheet exclusively using Part I of Form 20. This form is the statutory document used to record the final tally of votes. Within this section of the form, there is a specific provision requiring the officer to enter the number of votes secured by each candidate through postal ballot papers, which are ballots cast by voters who are unable to visit polling stations on election day, such as military personnel or those with disabilities. Once the total number of votes polled for each candidate at every individual polling station, along with the votes received via postal ballots, has been accurately entered into the Final Result Sheet, the Returning Officer must calculate and record the grand total of votes credited to each candidate. Additionally, the officer must compute and record the grand total of postal votes and tendered votes, which are ballots cast by voters whose names were not found on the register but who were allowed to vote provisionally.
